
### 4.3 Process Flow Architecture (Placement in WWTP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Sewage] --> B[Screening]
    B --> C[Grit Removal]
    C --> D[Equalization Basin]
    D --> E[Primary Clarification]
    E --> F[Biological Reactor]
    F --> G[Secondary Clarifier]
    G --> H[Filtration]
    H --> I[Disinfection]
    I --> J[Sludge Treatment Loop Back to Head of Plant]

    style D fill:#fab1a0,stroke:#d63031,stroke-width:3px
    style J fill:#a29bfe
```

> **Caption:** Equalization basin is placed **after preliminary treatment** (screening + grit removal) and **before primary sedimentation**. This protects downstream units from shock loads.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define equalization in wastewater treatment. List any four of its functions.*

**Model Answer:**
Equalization is a pretreatment unit operation that uses a holding basin to dampen variations in hydraulic flow rate and pollutant concentration of incoming wastewater, providing a quasi-steady influent to downstream treatment units.

**Four functions:**
1. Hydraulic flow smoothing
2. Concentration equalization (BOD, COD, SS)
3. pH stabilization
4. Toxic shock attenuation
5. Odour control (when aerated)
6. Provision of reaction time for partial BOD reduction

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Differentiate between in-line and off-line equalization basins.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | In-line Equalization | Off-line Equalization |
|---------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Position | All flow passes through basin | Only excess flow diverted |
| Application | Constant volume | Variable storage |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Effectiveness | High (flow + conc.) | Flow only |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Explain the **design criteria** for an equalization basin. Discuss the types of mixing systems used.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A factory discharges wastewater in a cyclic pattern over **12 hours** as tabulated:

| Time (h) | 0 | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 | 12 |
|----------|---|---|---|---|---|----|----|
| Flow (m³/h) | 100 | 200 | 300 | 250 | 200 | 150 | 100 |
| BOD (mg/L) | 300 | 500 | 800 | 700 | 600 | 400 | 300 |

Determine the **required basin volume** if the outflow is to be at the **average rate**.

**Solution:**

**(a) Design Criteria — Model Answer:**

Design criteria include:
1. **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** Typically 2–8 h for combined equalization
2. **Volume determination:** Cumulative flow curve (Rippl's method)
3. **Mixing:** Mechanical mixers OR diffused aeration — must keep solids in suspension; G = 20–80 s⁻¹
4. **Aeration:** For aerated basins, 0.015–0.025 m³ air/m³·min; prevents septicity, prevents odour
5. **Geometry:** Depth 3.5–6 m, L:B ratio 2:1 to 4:1
6. **Freeboard:** Minimum 0.5 m above maximum water level
7. **Inlet/Outlet:** Inlet with energy dissipator; outlet with overflow weir
8. **Sludge removal:** Bottom hoppers with sludge pumps (every 3–7 days)
9. **Corrosion protection:** Concrete + protective coating (industrial wastewater)
10. **Instrumentation:** Flow meters, DO probes, pH probes, level sensors

**Types of Mixing Systems:**

- **Mechanical Mixers:** Submersible propeller-type; for sensitive applications; no air contact
- **Diffused Aeration:** Coarse bubble diffusers at bottom; provides mixing + aeration; prevents septic conditions
- **Jet Mixing:** Recirculation pumps + nozzles; efficient for deep tanks

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Average Flow**

$$Q_{avg} = \dfrac{100 + 200 + 300 + 250 + 200 + 150 + 100}{7} = \dfrac{1300}{7} = 185.71 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 2: Inflow − Mean**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta Q &= (100 - 185.71, 200 - 185.71, 300 - 185.71, 250 - 185.71, \\
&\quad 200 - 185.71, 150 - 185.71, 100 - 185.71) \\
&= (-85.71, 14.29, 114.29, 64.29, 14.29, -35.71, -85.71)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Cumulative Excess**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(0) &= 0 \\
S(2) &= 0 + (-85.71)(2) = -171.42 \\
S(4) &= -171.42 + (14.29)(2) = -142.84 \\
S(6) &= -142.84 + (114.29)(2) = 85.74 \\
S(8) &= 85.74 + (64.29)(2) = 214.32 \\
S(10) &= 214.32 + (14.29)(2) = 242.90 \\
S(12) &= 242.90 + (-35.71)(2) = 171.48
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Required Volume**

$$V = S_{max} - S_{min} = 242.90 - (-171.42) = 414.32 \, \text{m}^3$$

> **Design the basin as ~ 415 m³, e.g., 10 m × 8 m × 5.2 m depth.**

---

#### **Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* With the aid of a sketch, describe the **constructional features** of an **aerated equalization basin**.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* An industrial wastewater has an average flow of **500 m³/d** and a peak BOD concentration of **1500 mg/L** lasting 2 hours during the morning shift. If the plant operates **16 hours/day** with the remaining 8 hours at 200 mg/L BOD, calculate the **equalized BOD** at the basin outlet (assume complete mixing).

**Solution:**

**(a) Constructional Features (Model Answer):**

A typical aerated equalization basin includes:

1. **Reinforced Concrete Tank** — Rectangular or circular, 3.5–6 m deep
2. **Inlet Pipe** — With diffuser / energy dissipator to prevent short-circuiting
3. **Diffuser Grid** — Coarse bubble diffusers mounted on the floor; PVC piping loop with control valves
4. **Blower Room** — Centrifugal/positive displacement blowers sized for required air supply
5. **Effluent Weir** — Adjustable overflow weir at outlet end for controlled discharge
6. **Mechanical Mixer (optional)** — Submersible propeller for additional mixing
7. **Sludge Hoppers** — Pyramidal bottom with sludge withdrawal pipes and pumps
8. **Instrumentation** — DO probe, level sensor, flow meter, pH probe
9. **Access Ladders & Ventilation** — For maintenance and worker safety
10. **Freeboard** — Minimum 0.5 m; covered tanks recommended for odour control

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Average flow = 500 m³/d (assumed uniformly distributed)
- Time 1 (peak): 16 hours at C₁ = ?; Time 2 (low): 8 hours at C₂ = 200 mg/L
- Peak BOD = 1500 mg/L for 2 hours within the 16-hour window

**Assumption:** Reinterpret — for 2 hours of peak, the remaining 14 hours are at baseline BOD. Calculate weighted average.

**Option A — Mass balance over 24 h:**

Let baseline BOD for non-peak hours = B (unknown). Use a simpler interpretation:

Mass of BOD over 24 h:
- Peak period: 2 h × (1500 mg/L) × Q = 3000Q mg·h/L
- Off-peak: 22 h × (B mg/L) × Q

Without knowing B, take the **simpler weighted approach**:
If only the 2-hour peak contributes the variability:

$$C_{out,24h} = \dfrac{(2)(1500) + (22)(200)}{24} = \dfrac{3000 + 4400}{24} = 308.33 \, \text{mg/L}$$

> **Logic Conversion:** With complete mixing and constant Q, effluent concentration equals time-weighted influent average over the cycle. Peak of 1500 mg/L reduces to ~308 mg/L → **80% peak reduction.**

> **Engineering Verdict:** Equalization dramatically smooths shock loads, but cannot achieve dilution below the time-weighted mean unless combined with biological treatment downstream.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to use $Q_{avg}$ as the controlled outflow — they use peak or minimum incorrectly. *Always outflow = average.*
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Using peak flow instead of average flow when computing HRT. *HRT = V / Q_avg, NOT V / Q_peak.*
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting units in numericals — Q must be in consistent m³/h and time in h.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing equalization with primary treatment — it is **NOT** a true treatment process; it is a **buffer**.
> 5. **Mark Loss Zone:** Sketches without labels lose up to 2 marks in 7-mark sub-questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Read Before Exam:**

- Equalization = **hydraulic + concentration** smoothing in a holding basin
- **Placement:** After screening + grit removal, **before primary clarifier**
- **HRT:** 2–8 hours; design for longer if concentration smoothing needed
- **Volume Methods:** (i) Rippl cumulative curve (ii) HRT × Q_avg (iii) Simplified triangular approximation
- **Mixing:** Mechanical mixers OR diffused aeration; G = **20–80 s⁻¹**
- **Aeration Supply:** **0.015–0.025 m³ air/(m³·min)** for aerated basins
- **Basin Depth:** **3.5–6.0 m**; L:B ratio **2:1 to 4:1**
- **Inlet:** Energy dissipator to prevent short-circuiting
- **Outlet:** Overflow weir for level control
- **Sludge Removal:** Bottom hoppers; withdraw every **3–7 days**
- **Aerated Basin Benefits:** Mixing + aeration + odour control + partial BOD reduction
- **Mathematical Identity:** $\sum Q_{in} C_{in} = \sum Q_{out} C_{out}$ (mass conservation over a cycle)
- **Concentration Smoothing Equation:** $C_{out} = \dfrac{\int Q_{in} C_{in} \, dt}{\int Q_{in} \, dt}$
- **Design Verdict Rule:** Final basin volume = **MAX** of {Rippl volume, HRT-based volume}
- **KTU Board Tip:** Always mention **both flow and concentration equalization** when asked about advantages
- **Real-World Mandate:** Required for **industrial WWTPs** (textile, dairy, distillery, pharma) and **municipal plants with industrial discharge**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sedimentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sedimentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sedimentation** is a physical unit operation in which suspended solids (heavier than the surrounding water) are removed from wastewater by gravitational settling under quiescent (or near-quiet) hydraulic conditions. It is the most widely used primary and secondary clarification process in municipal and industrial wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, sedimentation is classified as a **gravity separation process** governed by the balance between three forces acting on a discrete particle in a fluid:

1. The **gravitational force (weight)** acting downward.
2. The **buoyant force** acting upward.
3. The **hydrodynamic drag force** opposing motion.

When these forces reach **dynamic equilibrium**, the particle descends at a constant terminal velocity known as the **settling velocity (vₛ)** — the single most important design parameter in clarifier design.

> [!NOTE]
> The three standard KTU learning outcomes tied to this topic are:
> - **CO2:** Apply principles of physical unit operations for the design of primary and secondary sedimentation tanks.
> - **CO3:** Analyze settling behavior of discrete and flocculent particles.
> - **CO4:** Evaluate high-rate sedimentation systems (lamella plates, tube settlers, dissolved air flotation coupling).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pouring a glass of **turmeric-mixed water** (a real-life Indian analogy) into a tall transparent glass and leaving it perfectly still on a table:

- Within a few minutes, the heavier **turmeric dust particles** drift to the bottom.
- The clearer supernatant water rises to the top.
- This is **sedimentation in nature.**

Similarly, when muddy river water enters a settling pond:
- Big sand grains settle in minutes.
- Silt takes hours.
- Colloidal clay may take days or months.

**The intuition is this:** *Give the water enough quiet time in a wide, deep tank, and gravity alone will do 50–70% of the pollution removal work — completely free of chemicals or electricity.*

> [!TIP]
> **Standard design metrics** for municipal sedimentation:
> - **Overflow rate (v₀):** 24 – 48 m³/m²·day (primary), 16 – 28 m³/m²·day (secondary)
> - **Detention time (t):** 1.5 – 2.5 hours (primary), 2.0 – 3.0 hours (secondary)
> - **Weir loading:** ≤ 185 m³/m·day (municipal standard)

---

## 1.3 Why Sedimentation? — Engineering Justification

| Function | Typical Removal Efficiency |
|---|---|
| Removal of settleable solids | 60 – 90 % |
| Reduction in BOD₅ (primary clarifier) | 25 – 40 % |
| Reduction in TSS (primary clarifier) | 50 – 70 % |
| Sludge thickening | Concentration to 2 – 5 % solids |

Sedimentation is the **workhorse of every WWTP** because it is **low-cost, low-energy, and chemical-free.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Classical Types of Settling (Camp-Hazen Classification)

In 1904, **Thomas R. Camp** and **Allen Hazen** formalized the four regimes of sedimentation, which remain the bedrock of every clarifier design taught in KTU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification of Settling (KTU Board Hot Topic — 7-mark favourite):**

### Type I — Discrete Particle Settling
- Particles do **not flocculate**; they remain as individual grains.
- Settling velocity is **constant** (Stokes' regime).
- Typical of: **Grit chambers, sand removal, primary clarifiers for inorganic particles.**

### Type II — Flocculent Settling
- Particles **coalesce** as they fall, growing larger and settling faster.
- Settling velocity is **not constant** — it increases with depth and time.
- Typical of: **Primary clarifiers treating domestic sewage.**

### Type III — Hindered (Zone) Settling
- High concentration of particles forms a **blanket or zone**.
- The whole mass settles as a unit; liquid moves up through interstices.
- Typical of: **Secondary clarifiers, sludge thickeners, activated sludge biomass.**

### Type IV — Compression Settling
- Particles at the bottom are pressed together by the weight of the overlying sludge.
- Settling rate is **very slow** and governed by compression mechanics.
- Typical of: **Deep sludge beds, consolidation zone of thickeners.**

---

## 2.2 Governing Physics — Force Balance on a Single Particle

A discrete particle settling through a viscous fluid experiences:

$$F_g = \rho_p \, V \, g = \rho_p \, \left(\frac{\pi d^3}{6}\right) g$$

$$F_b = \rho_w \, V \, g = \rho_w \, \left(\frac{\pi d^3}{6}\right) g$$

$$F_d = C_d \, A_p \, \frac{\rho_w v_s^2}{2} = C_d \left(\frac{\pi d^2}{4}\right) \frac{\rho_w v_s^2}{2}$$

At terminal (constant) velocity, **ΣF = 0**:

$$F_g - F_b - F_d = 0$$

Substituting and solving for **vₛ**:

$$v_s = \sqrt{\frac{4 g d (\rho_p - \rho_w)}{3 C_d \, \rho_w}}$$

Where:
- $d$ = particle diameter **(m)**
- $\rho_p$ = particle density **(kg/m³)**
- $\rho_w$ = water density **(≈ 1000 kg/m³)**
- $C_d$ = drag coefficient (dimensionless, depends on Reynolds number)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity **(9.81 m/s²)**

---

## 2.3 Stokes' Law (Laminar Regime, Re < 1)

When settling is slow and particles are tiny, flow around the particle is **laminar**. In this regime, $C_d = 24 / \text{Re}$, and the equation simplifies dramatically to the famous **Stokes' Law**:

$$v_s = \frac{g (\rho_p - \rho_w) d^2}{18 \, \mu}$$

Where $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of water **(Pa·s or N·s/m²)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity of Stokes' Law:**
> - Reynolds number: $\text{Re} = \frac{\rho_w v_s d}{\mu} < 1$
> - Particle diameter: $d <$ 0.1 mm (silt and finer)
> - **Temperature dependence:** Warmer water (lower $\mu$) → faster settling.

---

## 2.4 Newton's Law (Turbulent Regime, Re > 1000)

For large, rapidly settling particles (gravel, grit), $C_d \approx 0.4$ (constant):

$$v_s = 1.82 \sqrt{\frac{g d (\rho_p - \rho_w)}{\rho_w}}$$

---

## 2.5 Transition Regime (1 < Re < 1000)

In the intermediate zone, $C_d$ varies with Re. Standard drag coefficient correlations are used:

| Regime | Re Range | $C_d$ Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Laminar (Stokes) | Re < 1 | $C_d = 24 / \text{Re}$ |
| Transitional | 1 < Re < 1000 | $C_d = 18.5 / \text{Re}^{0.6}$ |
| Turbulent (Newton) | Re > 1000 | $C_d \approx 0.4$ |

---

## 2.6 Hazen's Overflow Rate (v₀) — The Heart of Clarifier Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hazen's Ideal Sedimentation Theory (1904)** states that a particle will be **completely removed** if its settling velocity $v_s$ is **greater than or equal to the surface overflow rate** $v_0$.

$$v_0 = \frac{Q}{A_s}$$

$$A_s = \frac{Q}{v_0}$$

$$t = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{H}{v_0}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = flow rate **(m³/s or m³/day)**
- $A_s$ = surface area of clarifier **(m²)**
- $t$ = detention / retention time **(s or h)**
- $H$ = depth of effective settling zone **(m)**

> **Real-world engineering utility:** Every municipal clarifier in the world is sized using Hazen's $v_0$ as the controlling criterion, **not** the depth. This is why deeper tanks are NOT automatically more efficient.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize this table for the KTU ESE. It covers 90% of the numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_s = \dfrac{g (\rho_p - \rho_w) d^2}{18 \mu}$ | Stokes' settling velocity | m/s |
| 2 | $v_s = 1.82 \sqrt{\dfrac{g d (\rho_p - \rho_w)}{\rho_w}}$ | Newton's settling velocity | m/s |
| 3 | $v_0 = \dfrac{Q}{A_s}$ | Surface overflow rate | m³/m²·d |
| 4 | $t = \dfrac{H}{v_0} = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Detention time | hours |
| 5 | $L = \dfrac{v_s H}{v_0}$ | Length of rectangular basin (Camp) | m |
| 6 | $\text{Removal} \, \% = \left(1 - e^{-\text{const} \cdot t}\right)$ | Flocculent settling (first-order) | % |
| 7 | $\text{Weir loading} = \dfrac{Q}{L_w}$ | Weir overflow rate | m³/m·d |
| 8 | $\text{Weir length}, \, L_w = \dfrac{Q}{\text{weir loading}}$ | Effluent weir design | m |
| 9 | $\text{Surface loading factor} = \dfrac{v_s}{v_0}$ | Hazen's removal criterion | — |
| 10 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho_w v_s d}{\mu}$ | Particle Reynolds number | — |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Newton's 2nd Law to Stokes' Law

We start from the force balance on a spherical particle of diameter $d$ falling under gravity in a stagnant fluid.

**Step 1:** Identify the three forces.

- Weight (downward): $W = \rho_p \, V \, g = \rho_p \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} g$
- Buoyancy (upward): $F_b = \rho_w \, V \, g = \rho_w \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} g$
- Drag (opposes motion): $F_d = C_d \, \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \, \dfrac{\rho_w v^2}{2}$

**Step 2:** Apply Newton's second law (net force = mass × acceleration). At terminal velocity, acceleration is zero:

$$W - F_b - F_d = 0$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the expressions:

$$\rho_p \frac{\pi d^3}{6} g - \rho_w \frac{\pi d^3}{6} g - C_d \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \frac{\rho_w v_s^2}{2} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $\pi$ and group terms:

$$\frac{\pi d^3}{6} g (\rho_p - \rho_w) = C_d \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \frac{\rho_w v_s^2}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Divide both sides by $\dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$:

$$\frac{2 d g (\rho_p - \rho_w)}{3} = C_d \frac{\rho_w v_s^2}{2}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $v_s^2$:

$$v_s^2 = \frac{4 g d (\rho_p - \rho_w)}{3 C_d \rho_w}$$

$$v_s = \sqrt{\frac{4 g d (\rho_p - \rho_w)}{3 C_d \rho_w}}$$

This is the **general settling velocity equation.**

**Step 7:** Apply the laminar regime condition. In laminar flow, the drag coefficient is theoretically derived as:

$$C_d = \frac{24}{\text{Re}} = \frac{24 \mu}{\rho_w v_s d}$$

**Step 8:** Substitute $C_d$ into the general equation:

$$v_s^2 = \frac{4 g d (\rho_p - \rho_w)}{3 \left(\dfrac{24 \mu}{\rho_w v_s d}\right) \rho_w}$$

**Step 9:** Simplify the denominator:

$$v_s^2 = \frac{4 g d (\rho_p - \rho_w) \cdot \rho_w v_s d}{3 \cdot 24 \mu \cdot \rho_w}$$

$$v_s^2 = \frac{4 g d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_w) v_s}{72 \mu}$$

**Step 10:** Cancel one $v_s$ from both sides:

$$v_s = \frac{4 g d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_w)}{72 \mu}$$

**Step 11:** Reduce the fraction:

$$\boxed{\,v_s = \frac{g (\rho_p - \rho_w) d^2}{18 \mu}\,}$$

This is **Stokes' Law** — the most quoted equation in sedimentation.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU ESE Style)

> **[KTU Numerical — Module 2, Frequently Repeated Pattern]**

A wastewater treatment plant treats $Q = 25,000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$ of domestic sewage. A circular primary clarifier is to be designed with:
- Surface overflow rate $v_0 = 30 \, \text{m}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day}$
- Effective depth $H = 3.0 \, \text{m}$
- Detention time must be ≥ 2 hours.

**Required:** Find (a) Surface area, (b) Diameter, (c) Detention time, (d) Check if detention criterion is met.

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Surface Area**

$$A_s = \frac{Q}{v_0} = \frac{25{,}000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d}}{30 \, \text{m}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{d}}$$

$$A_s = 833.33 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Part (b): Diameter of Circular Clarifier**

For a circular tank: $A_s = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_s}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 833.33}{3.1416}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{1061.0} = 32.57 \, \text{m}$$

**Part (c): Detention Time**

$$t = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{A_s \times H}{Q} = \frac{833.33 \times 3.0}{25{,}000}$$

$$t = \frac{2500}{25{,}000} = 0.1 \, \text{days} = 2.4 \, \text{hours}$$

**Part (d): Check Criteria**

Required detention ≥ 2 hours. Computed detention = 2.4 hours. **2.4 h > 2.0 h** → **Design is acceptable.** ✅

**Bonus check — actual overflow rate:**

$$v_0 = \frac{25{,}000}{833.33} = 30 \, \text{m}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{d} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Type of Settling

| Question Asked | Type | Key Equation |
|---|---|---|
| "A sand grain settles in grit chamber" | Type I — Discrete | Stokes' / Newton's |
| "Sewage particles coalesce during fall" | Type II — Flocculent | First-order kinetics |
| "Activated sludge blanket settles" | Type III — Hindered | Solids flux theory |
| "Sludge compacts in thickener bottom" | Type IV — Compression | Empirical |

---

## 3.4 Long Derivation: Camp's Equation for Length of Rectangular Basin

For a horizontally flowing rectangular clarifier, **Camp** derived the critical basin length $L$ required to remove a particle of settling velocity $v_s$:

$$L = \frac{v_s \, H}{v_0 - v_s}$$

For a particle to be removed, the residence time of water $t = L/u$ (where $u$ is horizontal velocity) must equal or exceed $t = H/v_0$. Combining:

$$L \geq \frac{u H}{v_0}$$

In the critical case where $v_s = v_0$, the particle reaches the bottom exactly at the outlet weir. Hence:

$$\boxed{\,L = \frac{v_s \, H}{v_0}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For complete removal of a particle with $v_s \geq v_0$, the **surface overflow rate is the controlling parameter** — not depth. This is the cornerstone of clarifier design and a guaranteed KTU question.

---

## 3.5 Solids Flux Theory (Type III — Hindered Settling) — KTU Advanced

For thickeners and secondary clarifiers handling activated sludge, the limiting solids flux $G_L$ is:

$$G_L = C_i v_i$$

Where:
- $C_i$ = solids concentration at layer $i$ **(mg/L)**
- $v_i$ = settling velocity at that concentration **(m/d)**

The total flux is:

$$G = G_u + G_f = v_i C_i + u C_i$$

- $G_u$ = flux due to gravity settling
- $G_f$ = flux due to bulk underflow (u = underflow velocity)

The limiting flux $G_L$ occurs at the **critical concentration $C_c$** where the derivative $dG/dC = 0$. The thickener area is:

$$A = \frac{Q_f C_u}{G_L}$$

Where $Q_f$ = underflow rate and $C_u$ = underflow concentration.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Force-Balance Concept Diagram (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Particle of diameter d] --> B[Weight W = rho_p V g]
    A --> C[Buoyancy Fb = rho_w V g]
    A --> D[Drag Force Fd = Cd Ap rho v2/2]
    B --> E[Net Force = W minus Fb minus Fd]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F{Re equals 0?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Terminal settling velocity vs]
    F -- No --> H[Particle accelerates or decelerates]
    G --> I{Re less than 1: Stokes Regime}
    G --> J{Re greater than 1000: Newton Regime}
    G --> K[1 less than Re less than 1000: Transition]
```

## 4.2 Architecture of a Complete Primary Sedimentation Tank

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Influent Channel] --> INW[Inlet Well with Energy Dissipator]
    INW --> SZ[Effective Settling Zone Volume V equals As times H]
    SZ --> SL[Sludge Hopper Bottom]
    SL --> SCR[Sludge Scraper Mechanism]
    SCR --> SP[Sludge Pump to Digester]
    SZ --> EW[Effluent Weir Launder]
    EW --> OUT[Treated Effluent to Next Stage]
    SCADA[SCADA Sensors TSS pH Flow] --> CTRL[Central Control Panel]
    CTRL --> SCR
    CTRL --> SP
```

## 4.3 Settling Type Decision Flowchart (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Incoming Wastewater Sample] --> Q1{Concentration of suspended solids}
    Q1 -- Less than 1000 mg per L --> TYPE1[Type I Discrete Settling]
    Q1 -- 1000 to 5000 mg per L --> TYPE2[Type II Flocculent Settling]
    Q1 -- Greater than 5000 mg per L --> Q2{Does a clear interface form}
    Q2 -- Yes --> TYPE3[Type III Hindered Zone Settling]
    Q2 -- No --> TYPE4[Type IV Compression Settling]
    TYPE1 --> M1[Use Stokes Law or Newton Law]
    TYPE2 --> M2[Use settling column tests]
    TYPE3 --> M3[Use solids flux theory]
    TYPE4 --> M4[Use empirical compression models]
```

## 4.4 Hazen Surface Overflow Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Flow rate Q] --> DIV[Divided by Surface Area As]
    DIV --> V0[Surface Overflow Rate v0]
    V0 --> COMP{vs greater than or equal to v0}
    COMP -- Yes --> REM[Particle Fully Removed]
    COMP -- No --> ESC[Particle Escapes with Effluent]
    H[Depth H] --> T[Detention Time t equals H divided by v0]
    T --> EFF[Clarifier Efficiency Indicator]
```

## 4.5 High-Rate Sedimentation: Lamella Plate Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INF[Influent] --> INLET[Distribution Channel]
    INLET --> PLATES[Inclined Lamella Plates at 55 to 60 degrees]
    PLATES -- Particles slide down --> SLUDGE[Sludge Hopper at Bottom]
    PLATES -- Clarified water rises --> LAUNDER[Effluent Launder at Top]
    SLUDGE --> PUMP[Sludge Withdrawal Pump]
    LAUNDER --> EFF[Treated Effluent]
    PUMP --> DIG[To Sludge Treatment]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lamella plates increase the effective settling area inside the same tank footprint.** A 6 m deep conventional clarifier can be replaced with a 2 m deep lamella unit, reducing construction cost by 50% and footprint by 70%.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define sedimentation. State Stokes' Law and its significance in wastewater treatment. [3 Marks]

> **[Model Answer]**
>
> **Sedimentation** is the physical process of removing suspended solids from wastewater by gravitational settling under quiescent hydraulic conditions.
>
> **Stokes' Law** gives the terminal settling velocity of a discrete spherical particle in the laminar regime:
>
> $$v_s = \frac{g (\rho_p - \rho_w) d^2}{18 \mu}$$
>
> **Significance:** It is the foundation for designing grit chambers, primary clarifiers, and high-rate settlers. It establishes that settling velocity increases with the **square of particle diameter** and **density difference**, and decreases with **viscosity** (which is why sedimentation works better in warm summer months).

---

### Q2. Distinguish between Type I and Type II settling. [3 Marks]

> **[Model Answer]**
>
> | Feature | Type I (Discrete) | Type II (Flocculent) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Particle behaviour | Settle as individual grains | Coalesce during fall |
> | Settling velocity | Constant with depth | Increases with depth |
> | Governing equation | Stokes' / Newton's Law | Empirical first-order kinetics |
> | Typical application | Grit chamber, sand removal | Primary clarifier (sewage) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Module 2 Choice Option A) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – April 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive Stokes' Law for the settling velocity of a discrete spherical particle settling in a viscous fluid under laminar conditions. State clearly the assumptions and validity criteria. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A grit chamber is to be designed for a sewage flow of $12,000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$. The particles to be removed have a diameter of $0.20 \, \text{mm}$ and a specific gravity of $2.65$. Water temperature is $25 \,^\circ\text{C}$ ($\mu = 0.89 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa·s}$). Determine: (i) the settling velocity of the particles, (ii) the surface overflow rate to ensure complete removal, and (iii) the required surface area of the chamber. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Assumptions:**
1. Particle is a **rigid sphere** of constant diameter $d$.
2. Settling occurs in an **infinite, stagnant fluid**.
3. **Laminar flow** around the particle (Re < 1).
4. **No interaction** between neighbouring particles.
5. Particle density $\rho_p$ is **constant**.

**Derivation:**

Net downward force at terminal velocity:

$$F_{\text{net}} = (\rho_p - \rho_w) \frac{\pi d^3}{6} g$$

This is balanced by the viscous drag (Stokes drag):

$$F_d = 3 \pi \mu d v_s$$

Equating:

$$(\rho_p - \rho_w) \frac{\pi d^3}{6} g = 3 \pi \mu d v_s$$

Solving for $v_s$:

$$\boxed{\,v_s = \frac{g (\rho_p - \rho_w) d^2}{18 \mu}\,}$$

**Validity:** Holds for $Re < 1$, i.e., $\dfrac{\rho_w v_s d}{\mu} < 1$. For larger particles, Newton's law is used.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Given:**
- $Q = 12{,}000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$
- $d = 0.20 \, \text{mm} = 0.20 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$
- Specific gravity = 2.65 → $\rho_p = 2650 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_w = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 0.89 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa·s}$
- $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

**(i) Settling velocity (Stokes' Law):**

$$v_s = \frac{9.81 \times (2650 - 1000) \times (0.20 \times 10^{-3})^2}{18 \times 0.89 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Step-by-step:

$$v_s = \frac{9.81 \times 1650 \times 4 \times 10^{-8}}{1.602 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$v_s = \frac{64.746 \times 10^{-6}}{1.602 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$v_s = 4041.6 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m/s} = 0.0223 \, \text{m/s}$$

Convert to m/day:

$$v_s = 0.0223 \times 86400 = 1927 \, \text{m/day} \approx 1927 \, \text{m/d}$$

**(ii) Surface overflow rate for complete removal:**

By Hazen's theory, $v_0 \leq v_s$ for complete removal:

$$v_0 = 1927 \, \text{m}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day}$$

**(iii) Required surface area:**

$$A_s = \frac{Q}{v_0} = \frac{12{,}000}{1927} = 6.23 \, \text{m}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students forget to convert seconds to days (multiply by 86,400) when computing $v_s$ for use with $Q$ in m³/day. This leads to an answer 1000× too small. Always check unit consistency first — it is worth 2 marks by itself.

---

### Question B (Module 2 Choice Option B) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the four types of settling classified by Camp and Hazen. Give one example of a treatment unit where each type is observed. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A circular primary sedimentation tank handles $35,000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$ of sewage. The surface overflow rate is $32 \, \text{m}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day}$ and the side-water depth is $3.5 \, \text{m}$. Calculate: (i) the diameter of the tank, (ii) the detention time in hours, and (iii) the weir loading if the effluent weir is placed along the entire circumference and weir loading is to be limited to $180 \, \text{m}^3/\text{m} \cdot \text{day}$. State whether the design is acceptable. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**The Four Types of Settling (Camp-Hazen, 1904):**

1. **Type I — Discrete Particle Settling:** Particles do not interact. Settling velocity remains constant. Each particle behaves independently.
   - *Example:* **Grit chamber** in preliminary treatment.

2. **Type II — Flocculent Settling:** Particles coalesce as they settle, becoming larger and heavier. Settling velocity increases with depth and time.
   - *Example:* **Primary sedimentation tank** for domestic sewage.

3. **Type III — Hindered (Zone) Settling:** Particle concentration is so high that the suspension settles as a mass, with clear liquid rising through channels in the blanket.
   - *Example:* **Secondary clarifier** of activated sludge process.

4. **Type IV — Compression Settling:** At very high concentrations, the weight of overlying particles compresses the lower layers. Settling rate is governed by compression mechanics.
   - *Example:* **Sludge thickener** bottom zone.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Given:**
- $Q = 35{,}000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$
- $v_0 = 32 \, \text{m}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day}$
- $H = 3.5 \, \text{m}$
- Maximum weir loading = $180 \, \text{m}^3/\text{m} \cdot \text{day}$

**(i) Diameter of tank:**

$$A_s = \frac{Q}{v_0} = \frac{35{,}000}{32} = 1093.75 \, \text{m}^2$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_s}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1093.75}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{1392.3}$$

$$D = 37.31 \, \text{m}$$

**(ii) Detention time:**

$$t = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{A_s \times H}{Q} = \frac{1093.75 \times 3.5}{35{,}000}$$

$$t = \frac{3828.1}{35{,}000} = 0.1094 \, \text{day} = 2.625 \, \text{hours}$$

**(iii) Required weir length:**

$$L_w = \frac{Q}{\text{weir loading}} = \frac{35{,}000}{180} = 194.4 \, \text{m}$$

**Circumference available:**

$$C = \pi D = 3.1416 \times 37.31 = 117.2 \, \text{m}$$

**Weir loading actually achieved:**

$$\text{WL}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{35{,}000}{117.2} = 298.6 \, \text{m}^3/\text{m} \cdot \text{day}$$

**Conclusion:**

The required weir length (194.4 m) **far exceeds** the available circumference (117.2 m). The actual weir loading is 298.6 m³/m·day, which is **higher than the permitted 180 m³/m·day**.

> **Design Verdict: NOT acceptable.** ❌

**Remedial options (must write at least one for full marks):**
- Use a **double-weir launder** (increases effective length).
- Use **multiple V-notch weirs** around the periphery.
- Reduce $D$ to increase surface overflow rate — but this conflicts with the given $v_0$.
- Install **inlet-diffuser baffles** to even out flow.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students compute the weir length and forget to **compare it with the actual circumference** of the circular tank. The question explicitly asks to "state whether the design is acceptable" — losing this 2-mark decision is a common KTU failure point.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU ESE Revision — 10 Golden Rules of Sedimentation:**

1. **Sedimentation = gravity separation** of suspended solids. It is the **most economical** treatment step.
2. **Stokes' Law** is valid only for **Re < 1**; for larger particles use **Newton's Law** ($C_d \approx 0.4$).
3. **Hazen's Overflow Rate ($v_0$)** is the **master design parameter** — **NOT** tank depth.
4. **Surface area $A_s = Q / v_0$** is the first thing to compute in any clarifier problem.
5. **Detention time** for primary clarifier should be **1.5 – 2.5 h**; for secondary, **2.0 – 3.0 h**.
6. **Weir loading** must be **≤ 185 m³/m·day** (municipal) to avoid short-circuiting and floating sludge.
7. **Type I** = discrete, **Type II** = flocculent, **Type III** = hindered (zone), **Type IV** = compression.
8. **Lamella plates** at 55–60° can **multiply effective area 5–10 times** within the same tank.
9. **Solids flux theory** governs thickener design: $G = G_u + G_f$, find $G_L$ at $\dfrac{dG}{dC} = 0$.
10. **Warmer water** (lower $\mu$) → **faster settling** in summer — a favourite viva question.
11. **Stokes' Law is parabolic in $d$** → halving particle size reduces $v_s$ to 1/4. This is why colloidal particles **never settle by gravity alone**.
12. **Surface overflow rate** has units of **m³/m²·day**; **weir loading** has units of **m³/m·day** — students frequently confuse them.
13. **Camp's equation for rectangular basin length:** $L = v_s H / v_0$.
14. **Tube settlers** operate at $v_0 = 120$–$180$ m³/m²·day — about 4× higher than conventional clarifiers.

> [!TIP]
> **5-Mark Examiner's Favourite Question to Practice:**
> *"With a neat sketch, derive Stokes' Law and discuss the factors affecting settling velocity in a sedimentation tank."*
> Always remember to list the **factors affecting settling velocity:**
> - Particle diameter $d$ ↑ → $v_s$ ↑↑ (quadratic)
> - Density difference $(\rho_p - \rho_w)$ ↑ → $v_s$ ↑
> - Viscosity $\mu$ ↑ (cold water) → $v_s$ ↓
> - Shape factor (non-spherical particles) → effective $C_d$ ↑
> - Particle concentration → at high C, hindered settling reduces $v_s$

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flotation (Oil & Grease Removal)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Flotation (Oil & Grease Removal) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Flotation** is a gravity separation process in which microscopic gas bubbles (typically air) are attached to or trapped within suspended particles, oil droplets, or grease globules, causing their **effective density to become less than that of the surrounding water**. The buoyant particles then rise rapidly to the liquid surface, where they are concentrated and removed by skimming. In the context of **Advanced Wastewater Treatment (OEBBT725)**, flotation is classified as a **physical separation unit operation** under the umbrella of **primary and tertiary treatment processes**, specifically employed for the removal of: - **Free oils and greases** (specific gravity $\lt 0.95$) - **Suspended solids** with low settling velocities - **Emulsified oils** (when combined with chemical pretreatment/Coagulation-Flocculation) - **Floatable plastics, fibers, and scum** The **Standard Hydraulic Detention Time** in flotation units typically ranges between **20 to 60 minutes**, while the **Air-to-Solids Ratio (A/S)** is maintained between **0.005 to 0.060** depending on the type of influent. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Beach Ball Principle** Imagine throwing a heavy stone into a swimming pool — it sinks immediately. But if you strap a **beach ball** (filled with air) to that stone, the combined object floats because the **average density** of the stone-ball system becomes less than water. Flotation applies this exact physics to wastewater: - The "stone" = oil droplets, grease, or fine suspended solids - The "beach ball" = microscopic **air bubbles** (10 – 100 μm diameter) - The "swimming pool" = the flotation tank By attaching tiny air bubbles to contaminants, the **buoyancy force overcomes the gravitational settling force**, and the particles rocket to the surface, where they are mechanically scraped off as a thick **float layer** (or "scum blanket"). This is fundamentally different from sedimentation, where particles sink. Here, particles **float upward**, making flotation the preferred choice for substances that are naturally lighter than water (oils) or too light to settle efficiently. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!TIP] > **Key Design Constants Used in Flotation Engineering:** > - **Density of water:** $\rho_w = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ at $4^\circ \text{C}$ > - **Density of air (STP):** $\rho_a = 1.225 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ > - **Specific gravity of typical oil:** $0.85 - 0.95$ > - **Dynamic viscosity of water at 20°C:** $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$ > - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ > - **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$ > - **Bubbly flow Reynolds Number range for flotation bubbles:** $Re \lt 1$ (Stokes regime dominant) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Three-Phase System Flotation operates as a **three-phase heterogeneous system**: 1. **Solid phase** — oil droplets, suspended particles, grease globules 2. **Liquid phase** — wastewater (continuous medium) 3. **Gas phase** — air bubbles (discrete bubble swarm) ### 2.1.1 Mechanism of Particle-Bubble Attachment The attachment of a bubble to a particle is governed by **interfacial thermodynamics** rather than just hydrodynamics. The driving force is the reduction in **Gibbs free energy** of the system, expressed through the **Young-Dupré equation**: $$\theta = \arccos\left(\frac{\gamma_{PL} - \gamma_{PG}}{\gamma_{LG}}\right)$$ Where: - $\theta$ = contact angle between bubble and particle - $\gamma_{PL}$ = interfacial tension between particle and liquid - $\gamma_{PG}$ = interfacial tension between particle and gas - $\gamma_{LG}$ = interfacial tension between liquid and gas > [!NOTE] > **Hydrophobic particles** (oils, greases) have a contact angle $\theta \gt 90^\circ$, indicating strong affinity for bubbles. **Hydrophilic particles** (clays, fine silts) have $\theta \lt 90^\circ$ and require **chemical conditioners** (surfactants, coagulants) to enhance bubble attachment. ## 2.2 Classification of Flotation Systems Flotation systems in wastewater engineering are classified based on **how the air bubbles are generated**: ### A. Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF) — Most Common in O&G Removal - Air is dissolved in water under **high pressure** (300 – 600 kPa) in a saturator - Upon depressurization at the nozzle, microscopic bubbles (10 – 100 μm) precipitate out - Bubble rise velocity: **0.5 – 2.0 mm/s** (very slow → high contact efficiency) ### B. Induced Air Flotation (IAF) - Air is mechanically induced by **impellers or rotor-stator mechanisms** - Bubble size: **400 – 1000 μm** (larger bubbles) - Used for **free oil removal** (API separator effluent polishing) ### C. Electroflotation - Hydrogen and oxygen bubbles generated by **electrolysis of water** at electrodes - Bubble size: **20 – 50 μm** (extremely fine) - High capital cost; used for specialized industrial applications ### D. Vacuum Flotation - Saturation under atmospheric pressure, then vacuum applied - Limited capacity; rarely used in modern plants ## 2.3 Factors Affecting Flotation Efficiency > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Design Parameters (KTU High-Yield)** - **Bubble size distribution** — smaller bubbles = larger surface area = better attachment - **Air-to-Solids ratio (A/S)** — typically 0.02 – 0.05 for oil removal - **Hydraulic retention time (HRT)** — 20 – 60 min for DAF - **Surface overflow rate (SOR)** — 10 – 25 m³/m²·h for DAF - **Recycle ratio (R)** — fraction of effluent recycled (typically 10 – 30%) - **Temperature** — affects viscosity and Henry's constant - **Coagulant/Flocculant dosage** — for emulsified oils - **pH** — optimum range 6.5 – 7.5 for most systems ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > **Master these formulas — they form the backbone of all KTU numerical problems on Flotation.** | **Formula Name** | **Equation** | **Variables & Units** | **Typical Range** | |---|---|---|---| | Stokes Rise Velocity | $v_s = \dfrac{g \cdot d_p^2 \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_p)}{18 \mu}$ | $d_p$ = particle diameter (m); $\rho_w, \rho_p$ = densities (kg/m³); $\mu$ = viscosity (Pa·s) | 0.001 – 0.1 m/s | | Air Solubility (Henry's Law) | $C_s = k_H \cdot P$ | $C_s$ = saturation conc. (mg/L); $k_H$ = Henry's constant; $P$ = absolute pressure (atm) | $k_H$ for O₂ ≈ 40 mg/L·atm at 20°C | | Air-to-Solids Ratio | $\dfrac{A}{S} = \dfrac{S_a \cdot (f \cdot P - 1)}{X}$ | $S_a$ = air solubility; $f$ = fraction saturation; $P$ = pressure (atm); $X$ = influent SS (mg/L) | 0.005 – 0.060 | | Surface Overflow Rate | $\text{SOR} = \dfrac{Q}{A_s}$ | $Q$ = flow rate (m³/h); $A_s$ = surface area (m²) | 10 – 25 m³/m²·h | | Hydraulic Retention Time | $\text{HRT} = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | $V$ = tank volume (m³); $Q$ = flow rate (m³/h) | 0.33 – 1.0 h | | Mass of Air Released | $M_a = S_a \cdot Q_r \cdot (f \cdot P - 1)$ | $S_a$ = solubility; $Q_r$ = recycle flow; $f$ = saturation efficiency; $P$ = pressure (atm) | — | | Bubble Reynolds Number | $Re_b = \dfrac{\rho_w \cdot v \cdot d_b}{\mu}$ | $v$ = bubble velocity; $d_b$ = bubble diameter | $Re_b \lt 1$ for flotation | | Float Removal Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | $C_{in}, C_{out}$ = influent/effluent concentrations | 70 – 95% for oils | > **Critical Rule:** Never use `|` inside the markdown tables above. For absolute value notation in equations, use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ instead. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > Flotation is **the industry standard** for oil and grease (O&G) removal in: > - **Petroleum refineries** — pre-treatment before biological systems > - **Food processing industries** — dairy, edible oil, meat rendering plants > - **Metal finishing and rolling mills** — removal of lubricants and cutting oils > - **Petrochemical complexes** — protection of downstream activated sludge units > - **Airport de-icing runoff** — removal of glycols and hydrocarbons > - **Marine bilge water treatment** — IMO compliance for offshore discharge # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stokes' Law for Bubble Rise Velocity We consider a spherical air bubble rising through quiescent water at low Reynolds number. Three forces act on the bubble: **Step 1 — Identify the Forces** The forces acting on a rising bubble are: - **Buoyancy force** (upward): $F_B = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_b$ - **Gravitational force** (downward): $F_G = \rho_b \cdot g \cdot V_b$ - **Drag force** (opposing motion): $F_D = C_D \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{\rho_w \cdot v^2}{2}$ Where: - $V_b = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_b^3}{6}$ = volume of bubble - $A = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_b^2}{4}$ = cross-sectional area of bubble - $C_D$ = drag coefficient - $\rho_b$ = density of bubble (≈ density of air) - $v$ = bubble rise velocity **Step 2 — Apply Equilibrium (Terminal Velocity Condition)** At terminal velocity, the net force = 0: $$F_B - F_G - F_D = 0$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the Force Expressions** $$\rho_w \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot d_b^3}{6} - \rho_b \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot d_b^3}{6} - C_D \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot d_b^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_w \cdot v^2}{2} = 0$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the Common Factors** Divide throughout by $\pi$ and factor: $$g \cdot \frac{d_b^3}{6} \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_b) = C_D \cdot \frac{d_b^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_w \cdot v^2}{2}$$ **Step 5 — Apply Stokes' Drag Regime Assumption** For $Re_b \lt 1$ (laminar flow around bubble), the drag coefficient is: $$C_D = \frac{24}{Re_b} = \frac{24 \cdot \mu}{\rho_w \cdot v \cdot d_b}$$ **Step 6 — Substitute Drag Coefficient** $$g \cdot \frac{d_b^3}{6} \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_b) = \frac{24 \cdot \mu}{\rho_w \cdot v \cdot d_b} \cdot \frac{d_b^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_w \cdot v^2}{2}$$ **Step 7 — Cancel Common Terms on Both Sides** The terms $\rho_w$, $v$, $d_b$ simplify as follows: $$g \cdot \frac{d_b^3}{6} \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_b) = \frac{24 \cdot \mu}{v \cdot d_b} \cdot \frac{d_b^2}{4} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2}$$ $$g \cdot \frac{d_b^3}{6} \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_b) = 3 \cdot \mu \cdot v \cdot d_b$$ **Step 8 — Solve for Velocity (v)** $$v = \frac{g \cdot d_b^2 \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_b)}{18 \cdot \mu}$$ $$\boxed{v_s = \frac{g \cdot d_b^2 \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_b)}{18 \cdot \mu}}$$ > [!NOTE] > Since $\rho_b \approx 1.225 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \ll \rho_w = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, we can approximate $\rho_w - \rho_b \approx \rho_w$ for air bubbles. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Air-to-Solids (A/S) Ratio for DAF **Step 1 — Define the A/S Ratio Conceptually** The A/S ratio is the mass of air released per unit mass of solids (or oil) to be floated. It is the **single most important design parameter** for DAF systems. $$\frac{A}{S} = \frac{\text{Mass of air released per unit time}}{\text{Mass of solids entering per unit time}}$$ **Step 2 — Express Mass of Air Released** From Henry's Law, the saturation concentration of air in water at pressure P (in atm) is: $$C_s = S_a \cdot P$$ Where $S_a$ is the saturation concentration at atmospheric pressure. **Step 3 — Account for Incomplete Saturation** Real saturators do not achieve 100% saturation. Let $f$ = fraction of saturation achieved: $$C_{actual} = f \cdot S_a \cdot P$$ **Step 4 — Determine Net Air Released on Depressurization** When water leaves the saturator at pressure $P$ and returns to atmospheric pressure (1 atm): $$\Delta C = C_{actual} - C_{atmospheric} = f \cdot S_a \cdot P - S_a \cdot 1 = S_a \cdot (f \cdot P - 1)$$ **Step 5 — Express Mass Flow Rate of Air Released** For a recycle flow rate $Q_r$ (m³/s): $$M_a = Q_r \cdot \rho_w \cdot S_a \cdot (f \cdot P - 1) \quad [\text{in mass units}]$$ Or in concentration terms: $$M_a = S_a \cdot Q_r \cdot (f \cdot P - 1)$$ **Step 6 — Express Mass of Solids to be Floated** For an influent flow $Q$ with solids concentration $X$ (mg/L): $$M_s = Q \cdot X$$ **Step 7 — Form the A/S Ratio** $$\frac{A}{S} = \frac{S_a \cdot Q_r \cdot (f \cdot P - 1)}{Q \cdot X}$$ **Step 8 — Introduce the Recycle Ratio** Let $R = Q_r / Q$ (recycle ratio, typically 0.10 – 0.30): $$\boxed{\frac{A}{S} = \frac{R \cdot S_a \cdot (f \cdot P - 1)}{X}}$$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** A Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF) unit treats a wastewater flow of **500 m³/d** containing **200 mg/L of oil**. The saturator operates at **400 kPa** (≈ 4 atm absolute) with **90% saturation efficiency**. The air solubility at 1 atm and 20°C is **22 mg/L**. The desired A/S ratio is **0.025**. Determine the required **recycle ratio (R)**. **Solution:** Given: - $Q = 500 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d}$ - $X = 200 \, \text{mg/L}$ (oil concentration) - $P = 400 \, \text{kPa} = 4 \, \text{atm}$ (absolute) - $f = 0.90$ - $S_a = 22 \, \text{mg/L}$ - $A/S = 0.025$ Step 1 — Apply the A/S equation: $$\frac{A}{S} = \frac{R \cdot S_a \cdot (f \cdot P - 1)}{X}$$ Step 2 — Solve for R: $$R = \frac{(A/S) \cdot X}{S_a \cdot (f \cdot P - 1)}$$ Step 3 — Calculate the numerator: $$(A/S) \cdot X = 0.025 \times 200 = 5.0 \, \text{mg/L}$$ Step 4 — Calculate the denominator: $$S_a \cdot (f \cdot P - 1) = 22 \times (0.90 \times 4 - 1) = 22 \times 2.6 = 57.2 \, \text{mg/L}$$ Step 5 — Compute R: $$R = \frac{5.0}{57.2} = 0.0874 \approx 0.087$$ Step 6 — Convert to percentage: $$R \approx 8.74\%$$ Step 7 — Calculate actual recycle flow: $$Q_r = R \cdot Q = 0.087 \times 500 = 43.5 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Approximately **8.74%** of the clarified effluent must be recycled and saturated to generate sufficient air bubbles to float the oil at the required A/S ratio. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study of Flotation Types | **Parameter** | **DAF** | **IAF** | **Electroflotation** | |---|---|---|---| | Bubble size (μm) | 10 – 100 | 400 – 1000 | 20 – 50 | | Bubble rise velocity (mm/s) | 0.5 – 2.0 | 150 – 300 | 1.0 – 5.0 | | HRT (min) | 20 – 60 | 5 – 15 | 15 – 30 | | Capital cost | Moderate | Low | High | | O&G removal efficiency (%) | 85 – 95 | 70 – 85 | 90 – 98 | | Energy consumption | Moderate | High (impeller) | Very High (DC power) | | Best application | Emulsified oils, food industry | Free oils, refinery | Sludge thickening, heavy metals | # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: Define the term "Dissolved Air Flotation" (DAF) and list any three advantages it has over conventional sedimentation for oil and grease removal.** **Model Answer:** **Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF)** is a physico-chemical treatment process in which **air is dissolved in water under high pressure (300 – 600 kPa)** and then released at atmospheric pressure in a flotation tank. The released air forms **microscopic bubbles (10 – 100 μm)** that attach to suspended particles, oil droplets, and grease, causing them to **float to the surface** as a concentrated scum layer that is subsequently removed by skimming. **Three Advantages over Sedimentation:** 1. **Higher removal efficiency for light materials** — DAF efficiently removes substances with specific gravity less than water (oils, greases, plastics), which cannot settle by gravity. 2. **Shorter detention time** — DAF requires only 20 – 60 minutes versus 2 – 4 hours for primary clarifiers. 3. **Higher surface overflow rates** — DAF operates at 10 – 25 m³/m²·h, producing denser sludge with less water content (typically 2 – 5% solids vs. 0.5 – 2% in sedimentation). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "DAF is faster" without quantifying detention time or surface overflow rate. **Always cite numerical ranges** to score full marks. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: Explain the role of the "Air-to-Solids Ratio (A/S)" in the design of a DAF unit. State its typical range.** **Model Answer:** The **Air-to-Solids Ratio (A/S)** is defined as the **mass of air released per unit mass of solids (or oil)** introduced into the flotation tank. It is the **single most critical design parameter** governing flotation efficiency. $$\frac{A}{S} = \frac{R \cdot S_a \cdot (f \cdot P - 1)}{X}$$ **Role in Design:** - **Determines bubble density** in the contact zone, affecting particle-bubble collision frequency - **Controls float layer thickness and stability** on the tank surface - **Governs the recycle ratio** required for a given influent solids load - **Directly influences removal efficiency** — too low A/S results in incomplete flotation; too high A/S causes turbulence and re-suspension **Typical Range:** - **0.005 to 0.060** (dimensionless, mass-based) - For **oil and grease removal**: A/S = **0.02 to 0.05** - For **sludge thickening**: A/S = **0.03 to 0.06** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students forget to mention that A/S is **dimensionless** and often confuse it with the recycle ratio (R). Remember: **R is a flow ratio; A/S is a mass ratio.** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and has **internal choice** (either Question A or Question B to be answered). Each sub-part typically carries **7 marks**. ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: (a)** Describe the working principle of a **Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF) unit** with a neat sketch. Explain the function of the **saturator**, **pressure relief nozzle**, and **skimming mechanism** in the system. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A DAF unit is to be designed to treat **750 m³/d** of wastewater from an edible oil refinery. The wastewater contains **150 mg/L of oil and grease**. The saturator operates at **450 kPa (≈ 4.5 atm)** with **85% saturation efficiency**. The air solubility at 1 atm and 25°C is **20 mg/L**. The desired A/S ratio is **0.030**. Calculate: **(i)** The required **recycle ratio (R)**, **(ii)** The **recycle flow rate**, and **(iii)** The **mass of air released per day**. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q(a):** **Working Principle:** A DAF unit operates on the principle of **differential pressure-induced air supersaturation**. The process has three functional zones: 1. **Pressurization Zone (Saturator):** A portion of the clarified effluent (typically 10 – 30%) is pressurized to **300 – 600 kPa** in a saturator vessel. Compressed air is injected into this stream, and the water is held under pressure for **1 – 3 minutes** to allow air to dissolve according to **Henry's Law**: $C_s = k_H \cdot P$. 2. **Release Zone (Nozzle):** The pressurized recycle stream is injected into the flotation tank through **pressure-relief nozzles** or needle valves. The sudden pressure drop causes the dissolved air to come out of solution as **microscopic bubbles** (10 – 100 μm diameter). These bubbles attach to oil droplets and suspended solids in the contact zone. 3. **Separation Zone:** The bubble-particle aggregates rise rapidly (rise velocity ≈ 0.5 – 2.0 mm/s) to the surface, forming a **concentrated float layer**. A **rotating skimmer blade** slowly scrapes this layer into a collection hopper. The clarified effluent exits through submerged launders at the bottom. **Function of Key Components:** - **Saturator:** Maximizes air dissolution efficiency using baffles, packing material, or internal mixing; ensures 70 – 95% saturation efficiency. - **Pressure relief nozzle:** Creates the sudden depressurization needed for fine bubble generation; typically a needle valve or pin nozzle with 1 – 3 mm orifice. - **Skimmer mechanism:** Slowly rotates (0.5 – 2.0 RPM) to remove float without disturbing the clarified water below; typically a spiral or paddle-type design. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT skip the mention of **Henry's Law** in the saturator explanation. Marks are awarded for linking physics to function. --- **Model Solution for Q(b):** **Given:** - $Q = 750 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d}$ - $X = 150 \, \text{mg/L}$ - $P = 450 \, \text{kPa} = 4.5 \, \text{atm}$ - $f = 0.85$ - $S_a = 20 \, \text{mg/L}$ - $A/S = 0.030$ **(i) Required Recycle Ratio (R):** Using the A/S equation: $$\frac{A}{S} = \frac{R \cdot S_a \cdot (f \cdot P - 1)}{X}$$ Solve for R: $$R = \frac{(A/S) \cdot X}{S_a \cdot (f \cdot P - 1)}$$ Numerator: $(0.030) \times 150 = 4.5 \, \text{mg/L}$ Denominator: $20 \times (0.85 \times 4.5 - 1) = 20 \times (3.825 - 1) = 20 \times 2.825 = 56.5 \, \text{mg/L}$ $$R = \frac{4.5}{56.5} = 0.0796 \approx 0.080$$ $$\boxed{R \approx 8.0\%}$$ **(ii) Recycle Flow Rate ($Q_r$):** $$Q_r = R \cdot Q = 0.080 \times 750 = 60 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d}$$ $$\boxed{Q_r = 60 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d}}$$ **(iii) Mass of Air Released per Day:** $$M_a = S_a \cdot Q_r \cdot (f \cdot P - 1)$$ First convert $Q_r$ to L/day: $60 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d} = 60{,}000 \, \text{L/d}$ $$M_a = 20 \times 60{,}000 \times 2.825 = 3{,}390{,}000 \, \text{mg/d} = 3.39 \, \text{kg/d}$$ $$\boxed{M_a \approx 3.39 \, \text{kg/d}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (Q-b):** Unit conversion errors are the most common reason for losing 2 – 3 marks. Always convert m³ to L (multiply by 1000) when working in mg/L units. Also, do NOT confuse absolute pressure (P) with gauge pressure. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: (a)** Compare **Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF)** and **Induced Air Flotation (IAF)** with respect to bubble size, detention time, removal efficiency, energy consumption, and typical applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a **schematic diagram**, explain the design features of a **DAF reactor**. Discuss the significance of the **contact zone**, **separation zone**, and **hydraulic retention time (HRT)** in achieving effective oil removal. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q(a):** | **Comparison Parameter** | **DAF** | **IAF** | |---|---|---| | **Bubble size (μm)** | 10 – 100 (microscopic) | 400 – 1000 (macroscopic) | | **Detention time** | 20 – 60 min (longer) | 5 – 15 min (shorter) | | **Oil removal efficiency** | 85 – 95% (higher) | 70 – 85% (moderate) | | **Energy consumption** | Moderate (compressors + pumps) | High (mechanical impeller) | | **Mechanism** | Pressure-induced air release | Mechanical agitation | | **Sludge/Scum density** | Thick (4 – 6% solids) | Thin (1 – 3% solids) | | **Capital cost** | Moderate to high | Low to moderate | | **Typical applications** | Food industry, edible oils, emulsified oils, biological sludge thickening | Refinery wastewater (API separator effluent), free oil removal, metal working fluids | | **Sensitivity to shock loads** | More tolerant | Less tolerant | **Conclusion:** DAF is preferred when **high efficiency** is required for **emulsified oils** and **fine suspended matter**, while IAF is favored when **simple, low-cost free oil removal** is the goal. --- **Model Solution for Q(b):** **Design Features of a DAF Reactor (refer to schematic in Section 4.3):** A modern DAF reactor consists of the following **internal zones**: 1. **Inlet Distribution Header:** Ensures uniform hydraulic distribution of the chemically pretreated wastewater across the tank width. Critical to avoid short-circuiting. 2. **Contact Zone:** A confined zone near the inlet where the **pressurized recycle stream** is injected through nozzles. Here, intense **turbulent mixing** occurs between the bubbles and the oil/solid particles, maximizing the **collision and attachment frequency**. Typical contact zone HRT: **30 – 60 seconds**. 3. **Separation Zone:** A large, quiescent zone where **laminar upward flow** allows the bubble-particle aggregates to rise unhindered. The clarified water moves downward and exits through submerged launders. Typical HRT in this zone: **15 – 45 minutes**. 4. **Float/Surface Skimming Zone:** A thick scum layer accumulates on the surface, which is continuously scraped off by a **slowly rotating skimmer blade** (0.5 – 2.0 RPM). 5. **Sludge Hopper:** Heavy settleable solids accumulate at the conical bottom and are periodically drained. **Significance of Key Design Parameters:** - **Contact Zone:** The **collision efficiency** $\eta_c$ is directly proportional to the contact zone residence time. Insufficient contact time results in **poor bubble-particle attachment** and incomplete flotation. - **Separation Zone:** Must provide sufficient **quiescent settling (rising) time** for the buoyant aggregates. Based on **Stokes' law**, a 50 μm bubble with attached oil droplet rises at ≈ 1 mm/s, requiring approximately **30 minutes** to traverse a 2-meter deep separation zone. - **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** Defined as $\text{HRT} = V/Q$. For DAF, the **total HRT** (contact + separation) is typically **20 – 60 minutes**. Excessive HRT promotes turbulence and float re-suspension, while insufficient HRT reduces removal efficiency. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (Q-b):** Students often forget to quantify the **contact zone HRT (seconds)** separately from the **total HRT (minutes)**. Both must be mentioned with their numerical values. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Flotation (Oil & Grease Removal)** ### Core Concepts - [ ] Flotation is a **gravity separation** process where particles are made **buoyant** by attaching **gas bubbles** - [ ] Most common method for **oil and grease removal** in industrial wastewater treatment - [ ] Operates as a **three-phase system** (solid-liquid-gas) - [ ] **Hydrophobic** particles (oils) attach to bubbles naturally; **hydrophilic** particles need chemical conditioning ### Three Main Types of Flotation - [ ] **DAF (Dissolved Air Flotation):** Pressure-induced; bubbles 10 – 100 μm; HRT 20 – 60 min - [ ] **IAF (Induced Air Flotation):** Mechanical; bubbles 400 – 1000 μm; HRT 5 – 15 min - [ ] **Electroflotation:** Electrolysis; bubbles 20 – 50 μm; high cost, niche applications ### Critical Equations (Must Memorize) - [ ] **Stokes' Rise Velocity:** $v_s = \dfrac{g \cdot d^2 \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_p)}{18 \mu}$ - [ ] **Henry's Law:** $C_s = k_H \cdot P$ - [ ] **Air-to-Solids Ratio:** $\dfrac{A}{S} = \dfrac{R \cdot S_a \cdot (f \cdot P - 1)}{X}$ - [ ] **Surface Overflow Rate:** $\text{SOR} = Q / A_s$ - [ ] **Hydraulic Retention Time:** $\text{HRT} = V / Q$ - [ ] **Removal Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ ### Typical Design Values (Quick Reference) - [ ] **DAF pressure:** 300 – 600 kPa (3 – 6 atm) - [ ] **Saturation efficiency (f):** 70 – 95% - [ ] **A/S ratio for oil removal:** 0.02 – 0.05 - [ ] **Recycle ratio (R):** 10 – 30% - [ ] **SOR for DAF:** 10 – 25 m³/m²·h - [ ] **Oil removal efficiency:** 85 – 95% for DAF ### Components of a DAF System (Know the Function of Each) - [ ] **Saturator** — dissolves air in water under pressure - [ ] **Pressure relief nozzle** — creates fine bubbles on depressurization - [ ] **Contact zone** — promotes bubble-particle collisions - [ ] **Separation zone** — allows quiescent rising of aggregates - [ ] **Skimmer mechanism** — removes concentrated float layer - [ ] **Effluent launder** — collects clarified water - [ ] **Sludge hopper** — collects heavy settleable solids ### Key Industrial Applications - [ ] Petroleum refineries, petrochemicals - [ ] Food processing (dairy, edible oils, meat rendering) - [ ] Metal finishing and rolling mills - [ ] Marine bilge water treatment - [ ] Sludge thickening in biological treatment plants ### Common Examination Mistakes to Avoid - [ ] Do NOT confuse **recycle ratio (R)** with **A/S ratio** - [ ] Do NOT use **gauge pressure** in Henry's Law — use **absolute pressure** - [ ] Always **convert m³ to L** when working in mg/L units - [ ] Always **cite numerical ranges** for design parameters - [ ] Always **label schematics** clearly in descriptive questions

topics.title: Air Stripping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Air Stripping # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Air Stripping ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Air Stripping** is a physico-chemical **mass transfer separation process** in which volatile, sparingly soluble gases, or volatile organic compounds (VOCs) dissolved in a liquid phase (typically water or wastewater) are transferred into a gaseous phase (usually atmospheric air) by establishing intimate gas-liquid contact under controlled conditions of temperature, pressure, and gas-to-liquid flow ratio. The driving force for the transfer is the **concentration gradient** of the contaminant between the bulk liquid and the gas-liquid interface, governed fundamentally by **Henry's Law of Gas Solubility**. In the context of KTU's Advanced Wastewater Treatment framework, air stripping is classified as a **Volatilization-Based Advanced Treatment Unit Operation** used predominantly for the removal of: | Pollutant Class | Common Examples | Strippability | |---|---|---| | **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)** | Trichloroethylene (TCE), Tetrachloroethylene (PCE), BTEX, Vinyl Chloride | Highly Strippable (H > 100 atm·m³/mol) | | **Reduced Inorganics** | Ammonia (NH₃), Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S), Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) | pH/Temperature-Dependent | | **Synthetic Odorants** | Mercaptans, Amines, Indoles | Variable | | **Disinfection By-Product Precursors** | Chloroform, Bromoform (THMs) | Moderate | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Explanation > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Kettle on a Stove:** > Imagine you boil a pot of water that has been left uncovered. The dissolved "stinky" gases like hydrogen sulfide (the rotten-egg smell) and carbon dioxide escape into the kitchen air long before the water actually boils. Why? Because as water heats up, the gas molecules inside it have more energy and "jump out" into the air. **Air stripping is essentially this same phenomenon, but engineered and controlled** — instead of waiting for water to boil, we force large volumes of clean air to bubble up *counter-current* to a falling stream of contaminated water. The volatile contaminants preferentially leave the water and enter the air because they are "shy" of being dissolved. **Plain-English Intuition (Three Pillars):** 1. **"Loud molecules" (volatility):** Some molecules are simply more volatile than water — they prefer to be a gas. Air stripping exploits this preference. 2. **Concentration gradient:** Wastewater is "rich" in contaminants; air is "clean." Nature always tries to balance this difference — engineers simply accelerate the balancing act. 3. **Surface area is king:** The more air-water contact surface, the faster the transfer. That's why real systems use **packing materials** (like saddles, rings, or structured media) that look like tiny crinkled sculptures — each piece creates enormous interfacial area for the molecules to "jump" across. ## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > The process is governed by the **Henry's Law Constant (H)**, measured in atmospheres-cubic meter per mole (**atm·m³/mol**), or its dimensionless form **H′ = H/(RT)**. Higher H values indicate greater strippability. > > **Standard Reference Value:** $R = 8.2057 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{atm·m}^3/(\text{mol·K})$ > > **Typical Air-to-Water Ratio (Q_a/Q_w):** ranges from **5:1 to 60:1** (volume of air per volume of water) for VOC stripping, and **2000:1 to 5000:1** for ammonia stripping. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Henry's Law Henry's Law is the **thermodynamic backbone** of air stripping. For dilute aqueous solutions, the equilibrium partial pressure of a dissolved volatile compound above the liquid is proportional to its molar concentration in the liquid. $$ P = H \cdot C $$ Where: - $P$ = partial pressure of the contaminant in the gas phase (atm) - $H$ = Henry's Law Constant (atm·m³/mol) - $C$ = aqueous phase concentration (mol/m³ or mol/L with appropriate conversion) In dimensionless form, used widely in environmental engineering: $$ y^* = m \cdot x $$ Where: - $y^*$ = equilibrium mole fraction of the contaminant in the gas phase - $x$ = mole fraction in the liquid phase - $m$ = dimensionless Henry's constant = $\dfrac{H}{R T}$ ## 2.2 The Two-Film Theory — Mechanism of Mass Transfer Air stripping follows the **Whitman Two-Film (Stagnant Film) Theory** of interphase mass transfer: > [!NOTE] > **The 'Why' Behind the Mechanism:** > When a gas molecule escapes water, it does *not* leap across instantly. It must first **diffuse through a thin, stagnant liquid film** on the water side, then instantaneously cross the interface, then **diffuse through a thin, stagnant gas film** on the air side. The slowest of these steps **controls** the overall rate. Mathematically, the steady-state flux $N_A$ (mol/m²·s) of the contaminant A is given by: $$ N_A = K_L \left( C - C^* \right) = K_G \left( P^* - P \right) $$ Where: - $K_L$ = overall liquid-phase mass transfer coefficient (m/s) - $K_G$ = overall gas-phase mass transfer coefficient (mol/m²·s·atm) - $C$ = bulk liquid concentration (mol/m³) - $C^*$ = liquid concentration in equilibrium with the gas (mol/m³) - $P$ = bulk gas partial pressure (atm) - $P^*$ = partial pressure in equilibrium with liquid (atm) ## 2.3 Packed Tower Stripping — The "HTU × NTU" Concept A **packed tower** is the most common air-stripping configuration in KTU-curriculum problems. The design is rooted in the concept of **transfer units**. - **HTU (Height of a Transfer Unit):** A measure of the difficulty of mass transfer, dependent on the packing type, gas/liquid flow rates, and the contaminant. Typical values range from **0.3 m to 1.5 m**. - **NTU (Number of Transfer Units):** A measure of the magnitude of concentration change required. $$ Z = HTU \times NTU $$ Where $Z$ = total packed height (m). ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Formula Sheet — Memorize for Board Examination** | # | Formula / Equation | Description / Application | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P = H \cdot C$ | Henry's Law (concentration form) | atm = (atm·m³/mol) × (mol/m³) | | 2 | $m = \dfrac{H}{R T}$ | Dimensionless Henry's constant | Dimensionless | | 3 | $N_A = K_L (C - C^*)$ | Two-film mass transfer flux (liquid-controlled) | mol/(m²·s) | | 4 | $Z = HTU \times NTU$ | Packed tower height equation | m | | 5 | $HTU = \dfrac{L}{\rho_w \cdot K_L \cdot a}$ | HTU from operating parameters | m | | 6 | $NTU = \left( \dfrac{r}{r-1} \right) \ln \left[ \dfrac{C_o - C^*}{C_e - C^*} \right]$ | When $r \neq 1$ (driving force varies) | Dimensionless | | 7 | $NTU = \dfrac{C_o - C_e}{C_o - C^*}$ | When $r = 1$ (constant driving force) | Dimensionless | | 8 | $r = \dfrac{Q_a / Q_w}{m \cdot \rho_w / \rho_a}$ | Stripping factor (volumetric ratio / equilibrium ratio) | Dimensionless | | 9 | $C^* = \dfrac{y \cdot P_{total}}{H \cdot \rho_w / M_w}$ | Equilibrium concentration in liquid | mol/m³ or mg/L | | 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{C_o - C_e}{C_o} \times 100$ | Removal efficiency (%) | % | | 11 | $\dfrac{[NH_3]}{[NH_4^+]} = 10^{(pH - pK_a)}$ | Ammonia speciation at given pH, $pK_a = 9.25$ at 25°C | Ratio | > **Notation Notes:** > - $C_o$ = influent concentration, $C_e$ = effluent concentration > - $L$ = liquid superficial mass velocity (kg/m²·s) > - $a$ = specific interfacial area of packing (m²/m³) > - $Q_a / Q_w$ = air-to-water volumetric flow ratio > - $\rho_w$ = water density (≈ **1000 kg/m³**), $\rho_a$ = air density (≈ **1.2 kg/m³**) > - $M_w$ = molecular weight of water (**18 g/mol**) ## 2.5 Engineering Utility and Real-World Application > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility of Air Stripping:** > 1. **Groundwater Remediation:** Removal of chlorinated solvents (TCE, PCE) from contaminated aquifer water. > 2. **Industrial Wastewater:** Treatment of petrochemical refinery effluents containing BTEX. > 3. **Municipal Water Treatment:** Removal of ammonia and CO₂ before disinfection (improves chlorine efficiency). > 4. **Odor Control:** Hydrogen sulfide removal from sewage treatment plants. > 5. **PFAS / THM Precursor Reduction:** Pre-treatment for membrane and RO systems. The **air pollution consequence** of stripping is critical: VOCs removed from water enter the off-gas, which typically requires **Off-Gas Treatment (OGT)** using activated carbon or thermal/catalytic oxidation to prevent atmospheric release. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — The Packed Tower Differential Mass Balance **Objective:** Derive the fundamental packed tower equation relating height $Z$ to the change in liquid-phase concentration. ### Step 1 — Set Up a Differential Control Volume Consider a thin horizontal slice of packing of height $dZ$ and unit cross-sectional area $A$. The contaminant is transferring from the descending liquid film to the rising air stream. **Step 1a — Liquid-phase mass balance on the slice:** The rate of contaminant entering the slice (downward) minus the rate leaving the slice = the rate transferred to the gas: $$ L \cdot C \bigg\vert_z - L \cdot C \bigg\vert_{z+dz} = N_A \cdot a \cdot A \cdot dZ $$ Where $a$ is the specific packing area (m²/m³) and $L$ is the molar flow rate of liquid per unit cross-section (mol/m²·s). **Step 1b — Recognize the differential:** $$ - L \cdot dC = N_A \cdot a \cdot dZ $$ (The negative sign is because concentration decreases as we move down the tower, against the flow convention.) ### Step 2 — Substitute the Two-Film Flux Equation Using $N_A = K_L (C - C^*)$ from the two-film theory: $$ - L \cdot dC = K_L \cdot a \cdot (C - C^*) \cdot dZ $$ ### Step 3 — Define the HTU The Height of a Transfer Unit is defined as the velocity at which the liquid moves divided by the mass transfer coefficient times the interfacial area: $$ HTU_L = \frac{L}{K_L \cdot a} $$ **Step 3a — Algebraic Justification (Why this form?):** This non-dimensional grouping naturally arises because the denominator $K_L \cdot a$ has units of m/s, the same as $L/\rho$ — making the quotient dimensionally a length. ### Step 4 — Rearrange to Obtain the Integral Form $$ dZ = HTU_L \cdot \frac{-dC}{(C - C^*)} $$ Integrating from inlet (concentration $C_o$ at top, $Z = 0$) to outlet (concentration $C_e$ at bottom, $Z = Z_{total}$): $$ Z_{total} = HTU_L \int_{C_e}^{C_o} \frac{dC}{(C - C^*)} $$ ### Step 5 — Recognize the Integral as the NTU The **Number of Transfer Units (NTU)** is, by definition: $$ NTU = \int_{C_e}^{C_o} \frac{dC}{(C - C^*)} $$ Hence, we arrive at the **fundamental packed-tower design equation**: $$ \boxed{\ Z = HTU \times NTU\ } $$ ### Step 6 — Analytical Solution for the Case $r \neq 1$ **Step 6a — Relate $C^*$ to $C$ via operating line and equilibrium:** The operating line of a counter-current stripper (mass balance over the whole column): $$ L \cdot C_o + G \cdot y_{in} = L \cdot C_e + G \cdot y_{out} $$ The equilibrium condition: $y = m \cdot C^*$. Combining gives: $$ C^* = C - \frac{L}{G \cdot m} (C - C_e) - \frac{y_{in}}{m} $$ For clean inlet air ($y_{in} \approx 0$): $$ C^* = C - \frac{L}{G \cdot m} (C - C_e) $$ **Step 6b — Define the Stripping Factor:** $$ r = \frac{G \cdot m}{L} $$ **Step 6c — Substitute into the integral:** $$ C - C^* = C - \left[ C - \frac{1}{r}(C - C_e) \right] = \frac{1}{r}(C - C_e) $$ $$ NTU = \int_{C_e}^{C_o} \frac{r \cdot dC}{(C - C_e)} = r \cdot \ln\left[ \frac{C_o - C_e}{C_e - C_e} \right] $$ Wait — the rigorous form, accounting for the proper operating line slope, yields: $$ \boxed{\ NTU = \frac{r}{r-1} \cdot \ln\left[ \frac{C_o - C^*}{C_e - C^*} \right]\ } $$ For the special case $r = 1$: $$ \boxed{\ NTU = \frac{C_o - C_e}{C_o - C^*}\ } $$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem) **Problem Statement:** A packed tower is to be used to remove **trichloroethylene (TCE)** from a contaminated groundwater. Given: - $C_o = 200 \ \mu g/L$ - Target $C_e = 5 \ \mu g/L$ (95% removal) - $H = 0.0117 \ \text{atm·m}^3/\text{mol}$ at 25°C - $Q_w = 0.05 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ - Air-to-water ratio $Q_a / Q_w = 30$ (volumetric) - $HTU = 0.9 \ \text{m}$ (typical for 1-inch Pall rings) **Find:** The required packed height $Z$. ### Step-by-Step Solution: **Step 1 — Calculate the dimensionless Henry's constant $m$:** $$ m = \frac{H}{RT} = \frac{0.0117}{(8.2057 \times 10^{-5})(298)} = \frac{0.0117}{0.02445} = 0.4786 $$ **Step 2 — Calculate the stripping factor $r$:** $$ r = \frac{Q_a / Q_w \cdot \rho_w / \rho_a}{m} = \frac{30 \times (1000/1.2)}{0.4786} = \frac{30 \times 833.33}{0.4786} = \frac{25000}{0.4786} \approx 52236 $$ **Step 3 — Since $r \gg 1$ (infinite stripping factor approximation valid):** For large $r$, the term $\frac{r}{r-1} \approx 1$, and $C^* \approx 0$ (since inlet air is clean). $$ NTU \approx \ln\left( \frac{C_o}{C_e} \right) = \ln\left( \frac{200}{5} \right) = \ln(40) = 3.689 $$ **Step 4 — Compute packed height:** $$ Z = HTU \times NTU = 0.9 \times 3.689 = 3.32 \ \text{m} $$ **Practical Design Adjustment:** Apply a safety factor of **1.2 to 1.5** → Final design height ≈ **4.0 m**. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Air Stripping | Parameter | Favorable for Air Stripping | Unfavorable — Consider Alternative | |---|---|---| | **Henry's Constant (H)** | $H > 0.01$ atm·m³/mol | $H < 0.001$ atm·m³/mol (use adsorption) | | **Aqueous Solubility** | Low to moderate (< 1000 mg/L) | Very high (> 10,000 mg/L) | | **Pollutant Type** | VOCs, NH₃, H₂S, CO₂ | Heavy metals, non-volatiles | | **Temperature** | Higher (15-25°C ideal) | Very cold (kinetics poor) | | **Off-Gas Treatment Feasibility** | On-site carbon/thermal units available | Stricter air regulations, no OGT | | **Influent Concentration** | 1 mg/L to 1000 mg/L | < 0.1 mg/L (cost-inefficient) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Air-Stripping System The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete process flow and functional architecture of a counter-current packed-tower air-stripping system, including off-gas treatment — the configuration most relevant to KTU examination questions. ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Influent Wastewater
with VOCs or NH3] --> B[Feed Sump
and Equalization Basin] B --> C[Feed Pump
with Flow Control] C --> D[Distribution Header
at Top of Tower] D --> E[Top Inlet
Y distributor] E --> F[Packed Tower
Packing 0.6 to 1.2 m height] F --> G[Support Plate
and Liquid Redistributor] G --> H[Packed Tower
Second Stage Packing] H --> I[Bottom Collector
Sump] I --> J[Stripped Effluent
to Polishing Stage] K[Atmospheric Air Intake
via Blower Fan] --> L[Air Filter
and Misting Eliminator] L --> M[Bottom Inlet
Plenum Chamber] M --> F F --> N[Off Gas Exit
Top of Tower] N --> O[Demister Pad
and Mist Eliminator] O --> P[Off Gas Treatment
Activated Carbon OR
Thermal Oxidizer] P --> Q[Clean Treated Air
