to Atmosphere Stack] J --> R[Reuse or Disposal
Discharge Compliance Check] P --> S[Spent Carbon
Regeneration Loop] classDef process fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#003366 classDef treatment fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px,color:#155724 classDef offgas fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#721c24 classDef endpoint fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:1px,color:#856404 class B,C,D,E,G,I process class F,H,J treatment class L,M,N,O,P,S offgas class A,K,Q,R endpoint ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Mass Transfer Mechanism (Schematic Block) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A[Contaminated Liquid Stream] L1[Bulk Liquid
Concentration C] end subgraph B[Interfacial Region] F1[Stagnant Liquid Film
Concentration gradient C to Ci] I1[Gas Liquid Interface
Equilibrium at Ci and yi] F2[Stagnant Gas Film
Partial pressure yi to y] end subgraph C[Gas Stream] G1[Bulk Gas
Concentration y] end L1 --> F1 F1 --> I1 I1 --> F2 F2 --> G1 G1 -.Hot Air Rises.-> I1 L1 -.Cold Water Falls.-> I1 style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2 style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57c00 style C fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828 ``` ## 4.3 Decision Logic — Select Stripping Configuration ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Design Problem Statement]) --> Q1{Contaminant
Type?} Q1 -->|VOCs| VOC[Check Henry Constant H] Q1 -->|NH3| NH3[Check pH and Temperature] Q1 -->|H2S| HS[Check pH] VOC --> Q2{H Value?} Q2 -->|High H above 0.1| PACK[Packed Tower
Counter Current] Q2 -->|Moderate H| SPR[Spray Tower
Lower Cost] Q2 -->|Low H| ADS[Adsorption on Carbon
Alternative Method] NH3 --> Q3{pH above 10.5?} Q3 -->|Yes| PACKNH3[Packed Tower
Air Water ratio 2000 to 1] Q3 -->|No| ADJNH3[Adjust pH with NaOH
Then Proceed] HS --> Q4{pH below 6.5?} Q4 -->|Yes| PACKHS[Packed Tower with
H2S Off Gas Scrubbing] Q4 -->|No| ADJHS[Adjust pH and Recirculate] PACK --> Z[Compute Z equals HTU times NTU] SPR --> Z PACKNH3 --> Z PACKHS --> Z ADJNH3 --> PACKNH3 ADJHS --> PACKHS Z --> Design[Final Design with
1.3 Safety Factor] ADS --> Alt[Alternative
Treatment Path] classDef decision fill:#ffe082,stroke:#f57c00,stroke-width:2px classDef process fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#388e3c,stroke-width:2px classDef alternative fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#e64a19,stroke-width:2px class Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4 decision class PACK,SPR,PACKNH3,PACKHS,Z,Design,ADJNH3,ADJHS process class ADS,Alt alternative ``` ## 4.4 Packed-Tower Cross-Sectional Schematic (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TOP[Top of Tower] T1[Liquid Distributor] T2[Demister Pad] end subgraph MID1[Middle Packing Section 1] M1[Packing Media 1 inch Pall Rings] end subgraph MID2[Restructuring Zone] R1[Support Plate with Liquid Redistributor] end subgraph MID3[Middle Packing Section 2] M2[Packing Media 1 inch Pall Rings] end subgraph BOTTOM[Bottom of Tower] B1[Air Inlet Plenum] B2[Effluent Sump] B3[Effluent Outlet] end T1 --> M1 M1 --> R1 R1 --> M2 M2 --> B2 B1 --> M1 T2 -.Off Gas.-> EXT[To Off Gas Treatment] B3 -.Treated Water.-> OUT[To Polishing] style TOP fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0 style MID1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style MID2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825 style MID3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style BOTTOM fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** ### Q1. Define Henry's Constant. Explain its significance in air stripping. (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** Henry's Constant ($H$) is a thermodynamic equilibrium parameter that quantifies the partitioning of a volatile compound between the gaseous and aqueous phases at equilibrium. It is defined by the equation $P = H \cdot C$, where $P$ is the partial pressure of the compound in the gas phase and $C$ is its aqueous concentration. The units are atm·m³/mol. **Significance in Air Stripping:** - A **high** $H$ value indicates the compound has a strong preference for the gas phase → **easily stripped**. - A **low** $H$ value indicates high water solubility and reluctance to leave the aqueous phase → **difficult to strip**. - It is the **primary design parameter** that determines the air-to-water ratio, the required tower height, and whether air stripping is even a viable treatment option. Compounds with $H < 0.001$ atm·m³/mol are generally considered not amenable to air stripping. --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]** ### Q2. List and briefly explain the three major configurations of air-stripping systems used in wastewater treatment. (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** 1. **Packed Tower (Counter-Current):** The most efficient configuration. Wastewater flows downward over packing media (Pall rings, Raschig rings, saddles) while air is blown upward. Maximum interfacial area → best mass transfer efficiency. Used for VOCs and ammonia. 2. **Tray Tower (Plate Tower):** Wastewater cascades across multiple horizontal trays containing bubble caps or sieve plates. Air rises through the liquid layer on each tray. Easier to model as discrete stages; robust for fouling streams. 3. **Diffused Aeration / Spray Tower:** Wastewater is sprayed through the air, or air is bubbled through a tank of water. Lowest capital cost but lowest efficiency. Used for low-concentration, high-flow applications such as roughing ammonia removal. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Question A]** ### Question A: **(a)** Derive the packed-tower design equation $Z = HTU \times NTU$ starting from a differential mass balance on a thin horizontal slice of packing. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A wastewater stream contains **ammonia (NH₃)** at a concentration of **100 mg/L** as N. It is to be treated in a counter-current packed tower to achieve an effluent concentration of **10 mg/L** as N. Given: - Operating temperature = 20°C - $pK_a = 9.4$ at 20°C - Air-to-water volumetric ratio = 3000:1 - $HTU = 1.2$ m Calculate: - (i) The fraction of total ammonia present as NH₃ at pH 11.5. - (ii) The required packed height $Z$. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution to Question A: #### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Control Volume:** Consider a thin slice of packing of thickness $dZ$ and unit cross-sectional area. **Step 2 — Differential Mass Balance on Liquid Phase:** $$ -L \cdot dC = K_L \cdot a \cdot (C - C^*) \cdot dZ $$ where $L$ is the molar flow rate of water per unit area (mol/m²·s), $K_L$ is the overall liquid-phase mass transfer coefficient (m/s), and $a$ is the specific packing area (m²/m³). **Step 3 — Define HTU:** Rearranging and defining the Height of a Transfer Unit: $$ HTU_L = \frac{L}{K_L \cdot a} $$ **Step 4 — Integrate:** From inlet ($C_o$ at $Z = 0$) to outlet ($C_e$ at $Z = Z$): $$ Z = \int_{C_e}^{C_o} \frac{L \cdot dC}{K_L \cdot a (C - C^*)} = HTU \int_{C_e}^{C_o} \frac{dC}{C - C^*} $$ **Step 5 — Identify NTU:** The integral is defined as the Number of Transfer Units: $$ NTU = \int_{C_e}^{C_o} \frac{dC}{C - C^*} $$ Hence: $\boxed{Z = HTU \times NTU}$ **Assumptions:** 1. Steady-state operation. 2. Constant molar flow rates of gas and liquid (dilute systems). 3. Plug flow of both phases. 4. Two-film theory valid; $K_L \cdot a$ is constant over the tower height. 5. Operating line and equilibrium line are straight. --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks) **Step (i) — Fraction as NH₃ at pH 11.5:** $$ \frac{[\text{NH}_3]}{[\text{NH}_4^+]} = 10^{(pH - pK_a)} = 10^{(11.5 - 9.4)} = 10^{2.1} = 125.89 $$ Therefore, the fraction present as NH₃: $$ \alpha_{NH_3} = \frac{125.89}{1 + 125.89} = \frac{125.89}{126.89} = 0.9921 \approx 99.2\% $$ **Interpretation:** At pH 11.5, virtually all ammonia is in the volatile NH₃ form — ideal for stripping. **Step (ii) — Required Packed Height:** Since $Q_a/Q_w = 3000$ is very large and the gas is essentially clean, we use the large-$r$ approximation: $$ NTU = \ln\left( \frac{C_o}{C_e} \right) = \ln\left( \frac{100}{10} \right) = \ln(10) = 2.303 $$ **Packed Height:** $$ Z = HTU \times NTU = 1.2 \times 2.303 = 2.76 \ \text{m} $$ **With 1.3× Safety Factor:** $$ Z_{design} = 1.3 \times 2.76 = 3.59 \ \text{m} \approx 3.6 \ \text{m} $$ **Answer:** Required packed height ≈ **3.6 m** (with safety factor). --- > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model — Question B]** ### Question B: **(a)** Explain the **Whitman Two-Film Theory** of gas-liquid mass transfer. Sketch and label the concentration profiles for a compound being stripped from water. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A groundwater contaminated with **benzene** is to be treated by a counter-current packed tower. Given: - $C_o = 50 \ \mu g/L$, target $C_e = 2 \ \mu g/L$ - $H = 0.0055$ atm·m³/mol at 25°C - $Q_a / Q_w = 40$ - $HTU = 0.8$ m Calculate: - (i) The dimensionless Henry's constant $m$. - (ii) The stripping factor $r$. - (iii) The required packed height $Z$. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution to Question B: #### Part (a) — Two-Film Theory (7 Marks) **Explanation:** According to the Whitman Two-Film Theory, when a gas and liquid are in contact, a thin **stagnant film** exists on each side of the gas-liquid interface. Mass transfer from one phase to the other requires the diffusing species to traverse these two films in series. The interface itself is assumed to be at thermodynamic equilibrium, and the bulk phases are assumed to be perfectly mixed. For a component A being **stripped** from water (liquid phase) into air (gas phase): **Concentration Profile (Sketch Description):** - In the **bulk liquid**, concentration = $C$ (uniform by mixing). - Concentration drops linearly (or curved) across the **stagnant liquid film** to $C_i$ at the interface. - At the **interface**, equilibrium is established: $y_i = m \cdot C_i$. - Concentration rises across the **stagnant gas film** from $y_i$ to $y$ (bulk gas). - In the **bulk gas**, partial pressure = $P$ (uniform). The **overall mass transfer coefficient** $K_L$ is related to the individual film coefficients by: $$ \frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{m}{k_G} $$ where $k_L$ and $k_G$ are the individual liquid and gas film coefficients, respectively. **Steady-state flux equation:** $$ N_A = K_L (C - C^*) $$ where $C^* = y \cdot P / (H \cdot \rho_w / M_w)$ is the liquid concentration in equilibrium with the bulk gas. --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks) **Step (i) — Dimensionless Henry's Constant $m$:** $$ m = \frac{H}{RT} = \frac{0.0055}{(8.2057 \times 10^{-5}) \times (298)} = \frac{0.0055}{0.02445} = 0.2250 $$ **Step (ii) — Stripping Factor $r$:** $$ r = \frac{(Q_a / Q_w) \times (\rho_w / \rho_a)}{m} = \frac{40 \times (1000/1.2)}{0.2250} = \frac{40 \times 833.33}{0.2250} = \frac{33333}{0.2250} = 148148 $$ Since $r \gg 1$, the large-$r$ approximation applies. **Step (iii) — Packed Height:** **NTU Calculation:** $$ NTU \approx \ln\left( \frac{C_o}{C_e} \right) = \ln\left( \frac{50}{2} \right) = \ln(25) = 3.219 $$ **Z Calculation:** $$ Z = HTU \times NTU = 0.8 \times 3.219 = 2.575 \ \text{m} $$ **With 1.3× Safety Factor:** $$ Z_{design} = 1.3 \times 2.575 = 3.35 \ \text{m} \approx 3.4 \ \text{m} $$ **Answer:** Required packed height ≈ **3.4 m** (with safety factor). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency in Henry's Constant:** A major error is mixing the form of $H$ — using the dimensionless $H'$ in place of the dimensional $H$ (or vice versa) without converting. **Always** check whether $H$ is in atm·m³/mol or dimensionless before applying $m = H/(RT)$. > > 2. **Forgetting the $r/(r-1)$ Factor:** Many students blindly write $NTU = \ln(C_o/C_e)$ even when $r$ is small (close to 1). The correct general form is $NTU = \dfrac{r}{r-1} \cdot \ln\left[ \dfrac{C_o - C^*}{C_e - C^*} \right]$. Only when $r \gg 10$ (typically) may the simpler form be used. > > 3. **Ammonia Stripping Without pH Adjustment:** A frequent mistake is forgetting that NH₄⁺ is **non-volatile** — only free NH₃ strips. If pH is below 9.5, stripping is highly inefficient. **Always** state that pH must be raised to ≥ 11 before ammonia stripping is feasible. > > 4. **Ignoring Off-Gas Treatment:** Air stripping merely transfers pollution from water to air. The off-gas MUST be treated (activated carbon or thermal oxidizer). Examiners specifically look for this **holistic design awareness**. > > 5. **Missing the Safety Factor:** KTU values expect a "design" height, not a "theoretical" one. Apply 1.2–1.5× safety factor for packing wetting, fouling, and operational transients. > > 6. **Confusing HTU and HETP:** HTU is the Height of a Transfer Unit (continuous differential contact). HETP (Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate) is used for staged columns. The relation $HETP \approx HTU \cdot \ln(r) / (r-1)$ is often tested. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Summary — Air Stripping** ### Core Concepts - ✅ **Air Stripping** = mass transfer of volatile contaminants from water to air driven by concentration gradient and governed by **Henry's Law**. - ✅ **Two-Film Theory** is the mechanistic foundation; overall resistance is sum of liquid and gas film resistances. - ✅ The **driving force** for stripping is $(C - C^*)$ — the difference between bulk liquid and equilibrium concentrations. ### Key Formulae - ✅ $P = H \cdot C$ — Henry's Law. - ✅ $m = H/(RT)$ — Dimensionless Henry's constant. - ✅ $Z = HTU \times NTU$ — Packed tower design equation. - ✅ $NTU = \dfrac{r}{r-1} \ln\left[\dfrac{C_o - C^*}{C_e - C^*}\right]$ for general case. - ✅ $NTU = \ln(C_o / C_e)$ when $r \gg 1$ (infinite-stripping-factor approximation). - ✅ $\alpha_{NH_3} = 10^{(pH - pK_a)} / (1 + 10^{(pH - pK_a)})$ — ammonia speciation. ### Design Rules of Thumb - ✅ **Air-to-water ratio** for VOCs: 5:1 to 60:1. - ✅ **Air-to-water ratio** for ammonia: 2000:1 to 5000:1. - ✅ **Required pH for ammonia stripping** ≥ 11.0. - ✅ **Safety factor** on packed height: 1.2 to 1.5. - ✅ **Stripping factor $r$** ideally > 10 for stable operation. ### Critical Design Considerations - ✅ **Fouling** of packing is a major operational concern; pre-filtration may be needed. - ✅ **Scale formation** (CaCO₃) blocks packing; pH control and antiscalants may be required. - ✅ **Off-gas treatment** is mandatory; typical options are GAC adsorption or thermal/catalytic oxidation. - ✅ **Temperature** strongly affects both $H$ and kinetics — higher temperature → better stripping (also more expensive). ### Compounds Best Suited for Air Stripping - ✅ TCE, PCE, DCE, vinyl chloride (chlorinated solvents — high H). - ✅ BTEX compounds (Benzene, Toluene, Ethylbenzene, Xylenes). - ✅ Ammonia (after pH adjustment). - ✅ H₂S (after pH adjustment). - ✅ CO₂, CH₄, and other dissolved gases. - ❌ NOT suitable for: heavy metals, pesticides with low H, PFAS, nitrate. ### Standard KTU Problem-Solving Steps 1. ✅ Identify the contaminant and look up or calculate Henry's constant at operating temperature. 2. ✅ Determine the air-to-water ratio from the design specifications. 3. ✅ Calculate the stripping factor $r$. 4. ✅ Choose the appropriate NTU equation based on the value of $r$. 5. ✅ Multiply by $HTU$ to get theoretical height. 6. ✅ Apply safety factor (1.2–1.5×) for design height.

topics.title: Secondary Treatment: Principles of Waste Treatment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Secondary Treatment: Principles of Waste Treatment # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Secondary Treatment (KTU 2024 Definition):** > *Secondary Treatment* is the biological stage of wastewater treatment that follows primary sedimentation, wherein the dissolved and suspended organic matter remaining after primary treatment is **metabolized by microorganisms** (bacteria, protozoa, fungi) under controlled environmental conditions—either aerobic, anaerobic, or anoxic—converting biodegradable organics into stable end products such as **CO₂, H₂O, CH₄, and excess biological sludge (biosolids).* The principal target of secondary treatment is the reduction of: - **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD₅)** — typically from **200–300 mg/L** (primary effluent) down to **≤ 20–30 mg/L** - **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)** — by **60–85%** - **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** — down to **≤ 30 mg/L** - Pathogenic indicator organisms (coliform reduction of **90–99%**) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** > Secondary treatment employs two fundamental microbiological strategies: > 1. **Suspended-Growth Processes** — microbes are kept in suspension (e.g., Activated Sludge Process, Aerated Lagoons, Sequencing Batch Reactor). > 2. **Attached-Growth Processes** — microbes grow on a fixed media surface (e.g., Trickling Filters, Rotating Biological Contactors, Biological Towers). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Biological Kitchen" 🍽️ Imagine a **busy restaurant kitchen** that has just received prepped vegetables (primary effluent — large particles removed). The secondary treatment stage is the **"cooking process"** that uses chefs (microorganisms) to transform these raw ingredients (dissolved organics) into: - **Delicious finished dishes** → Clean water (low BOD effluent) - **Food waste / peels** → Waste activated sludge (biosolids) The chefs need: | Kitchen Element | Wastewater Equivalent | |-----------------|----------------------| | Heat | Oxygen (aerobic) / No oxygen (anaerobic) | | Cooking time | **HRT (Hydraulic Retention Time)** | | Number of chefs | **MLSS (Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids)** | | Recipe control | **F/M Ratio (Food-to-Microorganism)** | | Fresh chefs arriving | **Sludge Wasting / SRT** | Just as a chef cannot overcook or undercook, the engineer must precisely control the **microbial environment** to avoid poor settleability, bulking sludge, or washout. --- ## 1.3 Role of Secondary Treatment in the Treatment Train ``` Raw Sewage → [Preliminary] → [Primary Clarifier] → [SECONDARY TREATMENT] → [Tertiary] → Disinfection → Receiving Water ↑ THIS MODULE ``` The primary effluent still contains **60–80%** of the original polluting load in dissolved/colloidal form. Secondary treatment is **biologically mediated** and constitutes the **heart of any modern wastewater treatment plant (WWTP)**. --- ## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded Per KTU Standards) - **BOD₅** = **Biochemical Oxygen Demand over 5 days at 20°C** (unit: mg/L) - **COD** = **Chemical Oxygen Demand** (unit: mg/L) - **MLSS** = **Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids** (mg/L) — total biomass in aeration tank - **MLVSS** = **Mixed Liquor Volatile Suspended Solids** (mg/L) — *active fraction*, typically **70–80% of MLSS** - **F/M** = **Food-to-Microorganism ratio** (day⁻¹) - **SRT (θc)** = **Solids Retention Time** / Mean Cell Residence Time (days) - **HRT (θ)** = **Hydraulic Retention Time** (hours) - **SVI** = **Sludge Volume Index** (mL/g) — settleability indicator - **DO** = **Dissolved Oxygen** (target: **1.5–2.5 mg/L** in aeration tank) - **OUR** = **Oxygen Uptake Rate** (mg O₂/L·h) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Microbial Growth Curve — Foundation of All Secondary Treatment The performance of every biological reactor is dictated by the **microbial growth phases** in response to a limiting substrate (typically the carbonaceous BOD). | Phase | Description | Substrate Conc. | Growth Rate | Engineering Implication | |-------|-------------|------------------|-------------|-------------------------| | **Lag Phase** | Microbes acclimatizing | High | ≈ 0 | Start-up of reactors; seeding required | | **Log (Exponential) Phase** | Rapid cell division; abundant food | High (>> Kₛ) | μ = μ_max | Rare in continuous reactors; seen in batch/SBR | | **Declining Growth Phase** | Substrate becoming limiting | ≈ Kₛ | μ = f(S) | **Design target for activated sludge & trickling filters** | | **Stationary Phase** | Growth = Decay | Low | μ ≈ b | Endogenous respiration; minimizes sludge | | **Endogenous Decay Phase** | Cells consume themselves | Very low | μ < 0 | Extended aeration; aerobic digestion | | **Death Phase** | Cell lysis | Depleted | Negative | Sludge minimization, cell debris release | > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** > The **Substrate Utilization Rate** in a chemostat (CSTR) follows the **Monod Kinetics**, whereas an **SBR (batch)** traverses the full growth curve within a single cycle. --- ## 2.2 Monod Kinetics — The Heart of Biological Design > [!IMPORTANT] > **Monod Equation (1949):** > The specific growth rate of microorganisms is a **hyperbolic function** of the limiting substrate concentration. $$ \mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S} $$ Where: - $\mu$ = specific growth rate (**day⁻¹**) - $\mu_{max}$ = maximum specific growth rate (**day⁻¹**), typically **1–8 day⁻¹** for heterotrophs - $S$ = limiting substrate concentration (**mg/L**) - $K_s$ = half-saturation constant (**mg/L**), typically **1–100 mg/L** **Engineering Insight:** When $S \gg K_s$ → zero-order (rate independent of S); when $S \ll K_s$ → first-order (rate proportional to S). --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Use | Units | |---|---------|-------------------|-------| | 1 | $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ | Monod specific growth rate | day⁻¹ | | 2 | $r_{su} = -\dfrac{\mu_{max}}{Y} \cdot \dfrac{S \cdot X}{K_s + S}$ | Substrate utilization rate | mg/L·d | | 3 | $r_g = Y \cdot r_{su} - k_d \cdot X$ | Net biomass growth rate | mg VSS/L·d | | 4 | $\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \cdot \dfrac{S_0 - S}{\theta \cdot X} - k_d$ | Activated sludge design equation (SRT basis) | day⁻¹ | | 5 | $\dfrac{S}{(S_0 - S)} = \dfrac{K_s}{\mu_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\theta} + \dfrac{1}{\mu_{max}}$ | Linearized form for lab determination | — | | 6 | $F/M = \dfrac{S_0}{\theta \cdot X}$ | Food-to-Microorganism ratio | day⁻¹ | | 7 | $SRT (\theta_c) = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$ | Solids retention time | days | | 8 | $HRT (\theta) = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Hydraulic retention time | h or d | | 9 | $SVI = \dfrac{V_{30} \times 1000}{X}$ | Sludge volume index | mL/g | | 10 | $V = \dfrac{Q \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot \theta}{X \cdot (Y(S_0 - S) - k_d \cdot X \cdot \theta)}$ | Aeration tank volume (Lawrence-McCarty) | m³ | | 11 | $\theta_c^{min} = \dfrac{1}{Y \cdot k - k_d}$ | Minimum SRT (washout) | days | | 12 | $O_2 = a' \cdot (S_0 - S) + b' \cdot X \cdot \theta$ | Oxygen requirement (kg O₂/d) | kg/d | | 13 | $\eta = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{S_0} \times 100$ | Removal efficiency | % | | 14 | $BOD_5 = 0.65 \cdot BOD_L$ | Conversion BOD₅ ↔ Ultimate BOD | mg/L | | 15 | $Recycle \; Ratio \; R = \dfrac{X}{X_r - X}$ | RAS recycle ratio | — | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** In the formula table, the symbol $X$ denotes MLVSS (active biomass), NOT MLSS. Using the wrong variable is a **3-mark deduction** trap. --- ## 2.4 The Three Pillars of Activated Sludge Design ### 2.4.1 Food-to-Microorganism Ratio (F/M) $$ F/M = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X} = \frac{S_0}{\theta \cdot X} $$ | Process Variant | F/M (day⁻¹) | SRT (days) | HRT (h) | Sludge Character | |-----------------|--------------|------------|---------|-------------------| | Conventional | 0.2 – 0.4 | 5 – 15 | 4 – 8 | Good settleability | | High-Rate | 0.4 – 1.5 | 2 – 4 | 1 – 3 | Pin-point floc | | Extended Aeration | 0.05 – 0.15 | 20 – 40 | 18 – 36 | Well-oxidized, minimal sludge | | Oxidation Ditch | 0.05 – 0.10 | 20 – 30 | 8 – 36 | Stable, robust | ### 2.4.2 Solids Retention Time (SRT / $\theta_c$) $$ \theta_c = \frac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e} $$ $\theta_c$ is the **MASTER CONTROL PARAMETER** — it dictates: - Effluent quality (longer SRT → better nitrification) - Sludge production (longer SRT → less sludge) - Endogenous decay dominance ### 2.4.3 Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT / $\theta$) $$ \theta = \frac{V}{Q} $$ For **complete-mix activated sludge**: $\theta$ = 4–8 h; For **plug-flow**: $\theta$ = 4–6 h with longitudinal gradients. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Secondary treatment is the **economic and ecological workhorse** of municipal and industrial wastewater engineering. Its accurate design is critical for: - **Compliance with discharge consents** (BOD ≤ 30 mg/L, TSS ≤ 30 mg/L in Kerala KSPCB norms) - **Energy optimization** (aeration accounts for **50–60%** of WWTP power consumption) - **Sludge management economics** (every kg of wasted sludge costs ₹5–15 to dispose) - **Sustainability of receiving water bodies** — Vembanad Lake, Periyar River # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Lawrence-McCarty Activated Sludge Design Equation This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU 2024 ESE Module 2. ### 3.1.1 Step 1: Mass Balance on Substrate in a CSTR For a completely-mixed aeration tank of volume $V$ with influent substrate $S_0$ and effluent $S$: $$ \text{Accumulation} = \text{In} - \text{Out} - \text{Utilization} $$ $$ V \cdot \frac{dS}{dt} = Q \cdot S_0 - Q \cdot S - r_{su} \cdot V $$ At **steady state**, $dS/dt = 0$: $$ Q \cdot (S_0 - S) = r_{su} \cdot V $$ ### 3.1.2 Step 2: Substitute the Substrate Utilization Rate Using Monod kinetics with observed yield $Y$ and decay coefficient $k_d$: $$ r_{su} = \frac{\mu_{max}}{Y} \cdot \frac{S \cdot X}{K_s + S} $$ For a CSTR operated in the **declining growth phase** where $S \ll K_s$ (substrate-limited), this approximates to a first-order form. In Lawrence-McCarty formulation, we use the more general approach: $$ Q \cdot (S_0 - S) = \frac{\mu_{max}}{Y} \cdot \frac{S \cdot X}{K_s + S} \cdot V $$ ### 3.1.3 Step 3: Relate $\mu$ to SRT ($\theta_c$) For a chemostat, the net specific growth rate equals the reciprocal of the SRT: $$ \frac{1}{\theta_c} = \mu - k_d $$ Substituting Monod: $$ \frac{1}{\theta_c} = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S} - k_d $$ ### 3.1.4 Step 4: Solve for $S$ in Terms of $\theta_c$ $$ \frac{S}{K_s + S} = \frac{1}{\mu_{max}} \left( \frac{1}{\theta_c} + k_d \right) $$ Let $U = \dfrac{1}{\theta_c} + k_d$: $$ S \cdot \mu_{max} = U \cdot (K_s + S) $$ $$ S \cdot \mu_{max} = U \cdot K_s + U \cdot S $$ $$ S \cdot (\mu_{max} - U) = U \cdot K_s $$ $$ S = \frac{K_s \cdot \left( \dfrac{1}{\theta_c} + k_d \right)}{\mu_{max} - \left( \dfrac{1}{\theta_c} + k_d \right)} $$ This is the **classic Lawrence-McCarty effluent substrate equation** — directly used in KTU problems. ### 3.1.5 Step 5: Substrate Removal Form (the Design Equation) Substituting back into the mass balance and using the growth rate equation $r_g = Y \cdot r_{su} - k_d \cdot X$: $$ \frac{1}{\theta_c} = \frac{Y \cdot (S_0 - S)}{X \cdot \theta} - k_d $$ Rearranging to solve for reactor volume: $$ V = \frac{Q \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot \theta \cdot Y}{X \cdot (1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c)} $$ This is the **KTU-board favorite** — memorize the logic and the final form. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) > **Problem (KTU 2022 Pattern):** > A municipal wastewater treatment plant receives a flow of $Q = 10{,}000 \; m^3/d$ with $BOD_5 = 250 \; mg/L$. The desired effluent $BOD_5 = 20 \; mg/L$. MLVSS in aeration tank $X = 2000 \; mg/L$. Kinetic constants: $Y = 0.6$, $k_d = 0.06 \; d^{-1}$. Design SRT $\theta_c = 10 \; d$. Determine: (a) Aeration tank volume, (b) F/M ratio, (c) Excess sludge production. ### Solution: **Step 1: Compute HRT** For a typical F/M design, use: $$ \theta = \frac{V \cdot X \cdot \theta_c}{Y \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot \theta_c / (\theta_c \cdot Y)} $$ Direct approach using $F/M$ estimate, then refine. Let us first compute volume from the design equation: $$ V = \frac{Q \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot \theta_c \cdot Y}{X \cdot (1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c)} $$ **Step 2: Plug in numbers** $$ V = \frac{10{,}000 \times (250 - 20) \times 10 \times 0.6}{2000 \times (1 + 0.06 \times 10)} $$ $$ V = \frac{10{,}000 \times 230 \times 10 \times 0.6}{2000 \times (1 + 0.6)} $$ $$ V = \frac{13{,}800{,}000}{2000 \times 1.6} = \frac{13{,}800{,}000}{3200} = 4312.5 \; m^3 $$ **Step 3: HRT** $$ \theta = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{4312.5}{10{,}000} = 0.43125 \; d \approx 10.35 \; h $$ **Step 4: F/M Ratio** $$ F/M = \frac{S_0}{\theta \cdot X} = \frac{250}{0.43125 \times 2000} = \frac{250}{862.5} = 0.29 \; d^{-1} $$ This is **conventional activated sludge** — a healthy range. **Step 5: Excess Sludge (Waste Sludge) Production** The observed yield (with decay): $$ Y_{obs} = \frac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c} = \frac{0.6}{1 + 0.06 \times 10} = \frac{0.6}{1.6} = 0.375 $$ $$ P_X = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) = 0.375 \times 10{,}000 \times 230 \times 10^{-3} \; kg/d $$ $$ P_X = 862.5 \; kg \; VSS/d $$ Convert to TSS (assuming VSS/TSS = 0.8): $$ P_X (TSS) = \frac{862.5}{0.8} = 1078 \; kg/d $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Minimum SRT (Washout Point) At washout, $\mu = \mu_{max}$ (i.e., $S \gg K_s$, log phase), and $\theta_c \to \theta_c^{min}$: $$ \frac{1}{\theta_c^{min}} = \mu_{max} - k_d $$ $$ \theta_c^{min} = \frac{1}{\mu_{max} - k_d} = \frac{1}{Y \cdot k - k_d} $$ For the kinetic data above: $$ \theta_c^{min} = \frac{1}{0.6 \times 5 - 0.06} = \frac{1}{3 - 0.06} = \frac{1}{2.94} = 0.34 \; d $$ **Engineering rule of thumb:** Operate at $\theta_c = (3 \; \text{to} \; 20) \times \theta_c^{min}$ to ensure reliable nitrification and robust treatment. --- ## 3.4 Oxygen Requirement Derivation The total oxygen demand in activated sludge has two components: $$ O_2 = a' \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot Q + b' \cdot V \cdot X $$ Where: - $a' = 0.45$ to $0.55$ (kg O₂/kg BOD removed) — for **synthesis** - $b' = 0.05$ to $0.15$ (kg O₂/kg MLVSS·d) — for **endogenous respiration** **Numerical:** With $a' = 0.5$ and $b' = 0.1$: $$ O_2 = 0.5 \times 10{,}000 \times 230 \times 10^{-3} + 0.1 \times 4312.5 \times 2000 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ O_2 = 1150 + 862.5 = 2012.5 \; kg \; O_2/d $$ This is the **aeration design load** for the blower sizing. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Activated Sludge Process ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Influent Sewage
Q, S0, TSS0] --> B[Bar Screen
Preliminary] B --> C[Grit Chamber
Preliminary] C --> D[Primary Clarifier
S0 minus 30 percent BOD] D --> E[Aeration Tank
CSTR with Diffusers] E --> F[Secondary Clarifier
Solids Liquid Separation] F --> G[Disinfection
Chlorine or UV] G --> H[Treated Effluent
Seff less than 20 mg per L] F --> I[RAS Pump Station
Return Activated Sludge] I --> E F --> J[WAS Pump Station
Waste Activated Sludge] J --> K[Sludge Thickener
and Digester] L[Air Blower
DO 1.5 to 2.5 mg per L] --> E K --> M[Sludge Drying Beds
or Dewatering] ``` ## 4.2 Process Control Loop Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD SRT[SRT Control
theta c 5 to 15 d] --> QW[WAS Flow Qw] QW --> Biomass[Biomass X in Reactor] FM[F/M Ratio
0.2 to 0.4 d-1] --> Biomass Biomass --> EffQual[Effluent BOD
less than 20 mg per L] Biomass --> SVI[SVI Control
80 to 150 mL per g] DO[Dissolved Oxygen
1.5 to 2.5 mg per L] --> Biomass Recycle[Recycle Ratio R
0.25 to 1.0] --> Biomass SVI --> Settleability[Sludge Settleability
in Clarifier] Settleability --> EffQual ``` ## 4.3 Attached-Growth vs Suspended-Growth Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SUSPENDED[Suspended Growth Systems] direction TB A1[Activated Sludge] --> A2[Aerated Lagoon] A2 --> A3[Sequencing Batch Reactor SBR] A3 --> A4[Membrane Bioreactor MBR] end subgraph ATTACHED[Attached Growth Systems] direction TB B1[Trickling Filter] --> B2[Rotating Biological Contactor RBC] B2 --> B3[Submerged Biofilter] B3 --> B4[Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor MBBR] end IN[Primary Effluent] --> SUSPENDED IN --> ATTACHED SUSPENDED --> OUT[Secondary Effluent] ATTACHED --> OUT ``` ## 4.4 Microbial Growth Phase Decision Map ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Phase 1 Lag] --> P2[Phase 2 Log] P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Declining] P3 --> P4[Phase 4 Stationary] P4 --> P5[Phase 5 Endogenous] P5 --> P6[Phase 6 Death] P3 --> R1[Activated Sludge Design Target] P5 --> R2[Extended Aeration Target] P1 --> R3[Reactor Start Up] P6 --> R4[Sludge Minimization] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to draw a "schematic of the activated sludge process" — always include the **recycle loop** (RAS) and label **WAS**. Most students forget the WAS line, losing 2 marks. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define secondary treatment of wastewater. List the two main classifications with one example each.** **Model Answer:** Secondary treatment is a biological process that follows primary treatment, employing microorganisms to **stabilize dissolved and suspended organic matter** remaining after primary sedimentation. It typically reduces BOD₅ by **60–85%** and TSS to ≤ 30 mg/L. **Two Classifications:** 1. **Suspended-Growth Process:** Microorganisms are kept in suspension in the wastewater, e.g., **Activated Sludge Process**. 2. **Attached-Growth Process:** Microorganisms grow on a fixed or moving media, e.g., **Trickling Filter**. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is the significance of the F/M ratio in activated sludge process operation?** **Model Answer:** The F/M (Food-to-Microorganism) ratio is the **daily substrate loading per unit biomass** in the aeration tank, expressed in **day⁻¹**. $$ F/M = \frac{S_0}{\theta \cdot X} $$ **Significance:** - **High F/M (> 0.4 d⁻¹):** Dispersed growth, pin-point floc, poor settleability, high effluent BOD. - **Low F/M (< 0.1 d⁻¹):** Endogenous conditions, well-oxidized sludge, good settleability, low sludge yield. - **Optimum (0.2 – 0.4 d⁻¹):** Conventional process with balanced growth and good clarification. F/M dictates **sludge quality, settleability, effluent quality, and sludge production rate** — making it a critical control parameter in WWTP operation. --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** KTU 2024 ESE Module 2 provides internal choice. Select **either** Question A or Question B. --- ### **❓ Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — July 2024] **(a)** Explain the **Monod kinetics** as applied to biological wastewater treatment. Derive the **Lawrence-McCarty equation** for effluent substrate concentration in terms of SRT and kinetic constants. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A wastewater treatment plant has the following data: $Q = 25{,}000 \; m^3/d$, $S_0 = 300 \; mg/L$, desired $S = 25 \; mg/L$, $X = 2500 \; mg/L$, $Y = 0.65$, $k_d = 0.05 \; d^{-1}$. Design SRT = 12 days. Determine: - (i) Aeration tank volume - (ii) F/M ratio - (iii) Excess sludge production per day **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Q.A (a)** **Monod Kinetics:** Monod (1949) proposed that the **specific growth rate** of microorganisms is a hyperbolic function of the limiting substrate: $$ \mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S} $$ For CSTR with biomass decay ($k_d$), the net growth rate is: $$ \frac{dX}{dt} = Y \cdot r_{su} - k_d \cdot X $$ **Derivation of Lawrence-McCarty Equation:** For steady-state CSTR, mass balance on substrate: $$ \frac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \cdot \frac{S_0 - S}{\theta \cdot X} - k_d $$ The reciprocal of SRT equals the net specific growth rate, and using Monod: $$ \frac{1}{\theta_c} = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S} - k_d $$ Solving for $S$: $$ S = \frac{K_s \cdot \left( \frac{1}{\theta_c} + k_d \right)}{\mu_{max} - \left( \frac{1}{\theta_c} + k_d \right)} $$ This is the **Lawrence-McCarty effluent substrate equation** — it directly relates effluent quality to SRT and the kinetic parameters $\mu_{max}$, $K_s$, and $k_d$. **Engineering Significance:** By choosing a longer SRT, lower effluent substrate (better treatment) is achieved. However, the SRT must remain well above the washout limit $\theta_c^{min} = 1/(\mu_{max} - k_d)$. --- #### **Model Solution — Q.A (b)** **(i) Aeration Tank Volume** $$ V = \frac{Q \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot \theta_c \cdot Y}{X \cdot (1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c)} $$ $$ V = \frac{25{,}000 \times (300 - 25) \times 12 \times 0.65}{2500 \times (1 + 0.05 \times 12)} $$ $$ V = \frac{25{,}000 \times 275 \times 12 \times 0.65}{2500 \times (1 + 0.6)} $$ $$ V = \frac{25{,}000 \times 275 \times 7.8}{2500 \times 1.6} $$ $$ V = \frac{53{,}625{,}000}{4000} = 13{,}406.25 \; m^3 $$ **(ii) F/M Ratio** First, HRT: $$ \theta = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{13{,}406.25}{25{,}000} = 0.536 \; d $$ Then: $$ F/M = \frac{S_0}{\theta \cdot X} = \frac{300}{0.536 \times 2500} = \frac{300}{1340.6} = 0.224 \; d^{-1} $$ This is **conventional activated sludge** — within the optimum range. **(iii) Excess Sludge Production** $$ Y_{obs} = \frac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c} = \frac{0.65}{1 + 0.05 \times 12} = \frac{0.65}{1.6} = 0.406 $$ $$ P_X = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) = 0.406 \times 25{,}000 \times 275 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ P_X = 0.406 \times 6875 = 2791 \; kg \; VSS/d $$ **Final Answers:** - $V = 13{,}406.25 \; m^3$ - $F/M = 0.224 \; d^{-1}$ - $P_X = 2791 \; kg \; VSS/d$ --- ### **❓ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Describe the **microbial growth curve** in a biological reactor. How is each phase relevant to the design and operation of secondary treatment systems? **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)**. Explain the role of **return activated sludge (RAS)** and **waste activated sludge (WAS)** with relevant equations. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Q.B (a)** The microbial growth curve in a batch biological reactor comprises **six distinct phases**: **1. Lag Phase:** Microorganisms acclimatize to the substrate and environmental conditions (pH, temperature, DO). Cell division is minimal. **Relevance:** During plant start-up, seeding with acclimatized sludge is essential to minimize this phase. **2. Log (Exponential) Phase:** With abundant substrate ($S \gg K_s$), cells divide at maximum rate $\mu = \mu_{max}$. **Relevance:** Occurs in SBR cycles during the "react" period; undesirable in continuous CSTR as it yields dispersed, poorly settling flocs. **3. Declining Growth Phase:** Substrate becomes limiting ($S \approx K_s$). $\mu$ falls below $\mu_{max}$. **Relevance:** **Design target for conventional activated sludge** — optimum balance between substrate removal and sludge settleability. **4. Stationary Phase:** Growth rate equals decay rate. Substrate is depleted. **Relevance:** Conditions at the effluent of plug-flow reactors; in chemostats, this is the steady state. **5. Endogenous Decay Phase:** With no external substrate, cells consume their own protoplasm. **Relevance:** **Extended aeration, oxidation ditches, aerobic digesters** operate in this phase — minimal sludge yield. **6. Death / Lysis Phase:** Cell lysis releases intracellular material, which can serve as substrate for surviving cells. **Relevance:** Sludge minimization processes; can cause **rising sludge** in secondary clarifiers. **Conclusion:** Engineers manipulate HRT, SRT, and F/M to operate reactors in the **declining growth or endogenous phase** for best effluent quality and sludge characteristics. --- #### **Model Solution — Q.B (b)** **Activated Sludge Process — Schematic:** ``` Influent Q, S0 │ ▼ ┌──────────────┐ Air from blowers │ AERATION │◄────────── DO ≈ 2 mg/L │ TANK │ │ (CSTR) │ MLSS = 2000-4000 mg/L └──────┬───────┘ │ ▼ ┌──────────────┐ │ SECONDARY │ Settled sludge │ CLARIFIER │──────────┐ │ │ │ (RAS) └──────┬───────┘ │ │ Effluent │ ▼ │ ┌──────────────┐ │ │ Disinfection│ │ └──────────────┘ │ │ ┌──────────────┐ │ │ RAS PUMP │◄─────────┘ │ Station │ Recycle Ratio R └──────┬───────┘ R = X / (Xr - X) │ WAS (waste) Typical R = 0.25 - 1.0 ▼ Sludge Handling ``` **Role of RAS:** Return Activated Sludge maintains the **biomass concentration** in the aeration tank. Without RAS, biomass would be lost in the effluent and the process would fail. $$ R = \frac{Q_r}{Q} = \frac{X}{X_r - X} $$ Where $X_r$ is the RAS concentration (typically **6000–12,000 mg/L**). **Role of WAS:** Waste Activated Sludge is intentionally removed to **control the SRT**: $$ \theta_c = \frac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w} $$ Excess sludge is sent to thickeners, digesters, and dewatering units for disposal. **Summary:** RAS controls biomass inventory; WAS controls mean cell residence time — together they govern the entire ASP operation. ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 2 Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing MLSS and MLVSS:** Use $X$ = MLVSS in kinetic equations. Mixing them is a 3-mark deduction. > 2. **Forgetting the term $(1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c)$:** In the volume equation, omitting decay gives an **inflated reactor size** — a 2-mark penalty. > 3. **Wrong unit in SRT:** SRT is in **days**, not hours. Frequent mistake. > 4. **No recycle line in the ASP diagram:** Omitting the RAS loop is a 2-mark loss. > 5. **Failing to convert g/L to kg/m³:** $1 \; mg/L = 1 \; g/m^3$. Numerical errors here cost 2–3 marks. > 6. **Saying "Monod" instead of "Monod kinetics":** Always credit the full name in definitions. > 7. **Missing the WAS line:** Wasted sludge is critical for SRT control. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 2 — Secondary Treatment Principles:** - ✅ **Secondary treatment = Biological treatment** of dissolved/colloidal organics after primary sedimentation. - ✅ **Two fundamental strategies:** Suspended-growth (ASP) and Attached-growth (Trickling Filter, RBC). - ✅ **Monod Kinetics:** $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot S / (K_s + S)$ — substrate-limited growth. - ✅ **Lawrence-McCarty design equation:** $\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \cdot \dfrac{S_0 - S}{\theta \cdot X} - k_d$ - ✅ **Effluent substrate equation:** $S = \dfrac{K_s (1/\theta_c + k_d)}{\mu_{max} - (1/\theta_c + k_d)}$ - ✅ **F/M ratio target:** 0.2 – 0.4 d⁻¹ (conventional), 0.05 – 0.15 d⁻¹ (extended aeration). - ✅ **SRT ranges:** 5–15 d (conventional), 20–40 d (extended), 2–4 d (high-rate). - ✅ **HRT ranges:** 4–8 h (conventional), 18–36 h (extended aeration). - ✅ **DO target in aeration tank:** 1.5 – 2.5 mg/L. - ✅ **SVI healthy range:** 80 – 150 mL/g (above 200 mL/g = bulking sludge). - ✅ **Aeration = 50–60%** of WWTP energy — oxygen demand uses $a' (S_0 - S)Q + b' V X$. - ✅ **Observed yield** $Y_{obs} = Y/(1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c)$ — accounts for endogenous decay. - ✅ **Minimum SRT (washout):** $\theta_c^{min} = 1/(\mu_{max} - k_d)$ — design at **3–20×** this value. - ✅ **Microbial phases:** Lag → Log → Declining → Stationary → Endogenous → Death. - ✅ **Design rule:** $\theta_c$ is the **master control parameter** for effluent quality and sludge age. - ✅ **Recycle ratio formula:** $R = X / (X_r - X)$, typical $R = 0.25 - 1.0$. - ✅ **Excess sludge production:** $P_X = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$ — in kg VSS/d.

topics.title: Basic Kinetic Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Kinetic Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Basic Kinetic Equation** in advanced wastewater treatment describes the mathematical relationships that govern the rate at which microorganisms consume substrates (organic pollutants) and grow in biological treatment reactors. These equations form the **fundamental engineering foundation** for designing activated sludge systems, biofilm reactors, and other secondary/tertiary biological treatment units.

The cornerstone of biological kinetics is the **Monod Equation** (proposed by Jacques Monod, 1949), which relates the **specific growth rate of microorganisms (μ)** to the **limiting substrate concentration (S)**:

$$\mu = \mu_m \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S}$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = specific growth rate of biomass (**day⁻¹**)
- $\mu_m$ = maximum specific growth rate (**day⁻¹**)
- $S$ = substrate concentration in the reactor (**mg/L**)
- $K_s$ = half-saturation constant (**mg/L**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Basic Kinetic Equation links three critical engineering parameters — substrate removal rate, biomass growth rate, and sludge retention time (SRT). Every activated sludge design problem in the KTU exam will be based on these relationships.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy **restaurant kitchen**. The chefs (microorganisms) cook dishes (metabolize substrate) at a rate that depends on how many orders (food/substrate) are coming in:

- **When orders are scarce (low S):** Chefs work slowly, looking for things to do — growth is limited.
- **When orders are abundant (high S):** Chefs work at full speed — growth is at maximum.
- **The "half-saturation" moment (Ks):** This is the number of orders at which chefs are working at **exactly half their maximum speed**.

Additionally, chefs (biomass) die off naturally when idle (endogenous decay) and are removed when dishes are scraped (wasted sludge). The net growth is **births minus deaths minus removals**. This biological "restaurant economics" is exactly what the Basic Kinetic Equation mathematically models.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Range | Units |
|--------|-----------|---------------|-------|
| $\mu_m$ | Maximum specific growth rate | 1.0 – 8.0 | **day⁻¹** |
| $K_s$ | Half-saturation constant | 10 – 60 | **mg/L** |
| $Y$ | Yield coefficient | 0.3 – 0.6 | **mg VSS/mg BOD** |
| $k_d$ | Endogenous decay coefficient | 0.03 – 0.10 | **day⁻¹** |
| $K$ | Maximum specific substrate utilization rate | 2.0 – 10.0 | **day⁻¹** |
| $U$ | Specific substrate utilization rate | Variable | **day⁻¹** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical for KTU:** Always quote values with units. A numerical answer without units in a kinetic equation will lose **at least 1 mark** in valuation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Concepts in Biological Kinetics

Biological wastewater treatment operates on **three coupled material balances**:

1. **Substrate balance** — what's being eaten vs. what's flowing in/out
2. **Biomass balance** — what's growing vs. what's dying and leaving
3. **Stoichiometric link** — the yield coefficient $Y$ connects substrate removal to biomass growth

### 2.1.1 The Monod Framework — Why and How

**WHY we need it:** Engineers cannot design reactors empirically for every new wastewater. We need a **universal model** that predicts biomass behavior based on substrate concentration.

**HOW it works:** Microbial growth is **enzyme-mediated**. Enzymes follow Michaelis-Menten kinetics, which Monod adapted to whole-cell microbial systems. The rate is:

- **First-order** (linear) at low substrate ($S \ll K_s$): $\mu \approx (\mu_m/K_s) \cdot S$
- **Zero-order** (saturated) at high substrate ($S \gg K_s$): $\mu \approx \mu_m$

This dual behavior is **engineering gold** — it explains why dilution and F/M ratios matter.

### 2.1.2 The Three Master Equations

The basic kinetic model consists of three coupled ODEs:

**Equation 1 — Substrate utilization rate:**
$$-\frac{dS}{dt} = \frac{k \cdot X \cdot S}{K_s + S}$$

**Equation 2 — Biomass net growth rate:**
$$\frac{dX}{dt} = Y \cdot \left(-\frac{dS}{dt}\right) - k_d \cdot X$$

**Equation 3 — Specific growth rate (Monod):**
$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu \cdot X - k_d \cdot X$$

The link between $k$ and $\mu_m$ is:
$$k = \frac{\mu_m}{Y}$$

## 2.2 Engineering Application: The SRT Equation

For a **Completely Mixed Activated Sludge (CMAS)** reactor at steady state, mass balance around the reactor yields the **Sludge Retention Time (SRT) equation** — arguably the most tested formula in KTU exams:

$$\frac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \cdot U - k_d$$

Where the specific substrate utilization rate $U$ is:
$$U = \frac{S_0 - S}{\theta \cdot X}$$

Substituting gives the **canonical design form**:
$$\frac{1}{\theta_c} = \frac{Y(S_0 - S)}{\theta \cdot X} - k_d$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule:** $\theta_c$ (SRT) must be greater than $1/(\mu_m - k_d)$, otherwise biomass washes out and treatment fails.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is a **printable high-yield summary** for the KTU 2024 ESE exam. Master every entry.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Application |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | Monod Growth | $\mu = \mu_m \cdot S / (K_s + S)$ | Microbial growth rate |
| 2 | Substrate Removal | $-dS/dt = kXS/(K_s+S)$ | Pollutant degradation |
| 3 | Biomass Growth | $dX/dt = Y(-dS/dt) - k_d X$ | Net biomass production |
| 4 | Yield-Coupled Rate | $k = \mu_m / Y$ | Connects Monod to substrate use |
| 5 | SRT Equation (CMAS) | $1/\theta_c = YU - k_d$ | Reactor design |
| 6 | SRT in terms of S₀ | $1/\theta_c = YkS_0/(S_0 - S) - k_d$ | Effluent quality design |
| 7 | Food-to-Microorganism | $F/M = S_0/(\theta X)$ | Loading parameter |
| 8 | Substrate Effluent | $S = K_s(1 + k_d \theta_c)/(Y k \theta_c - 1)$ | Design equation |
| 9 | Hydraulic Retention | $\theta = V/Q$ | Reactor volume |
| 10 | Mean Cell Residence | $\theta_c = V X / (Q_w X_w)$ | SRT calculation |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

| Treatment Unit | Where Kinetics Apply |
|----------------|----------------------|
| Activated Sludge | Aeration tank design, SRT control |
| Trickling Filters | Biofilm kinetics, organic loading |
| Sequencing Batch Reactors (SBR) | Cycle time, fill-react phase |
| Membrane Bioreactors (MBR) | High MLSS kinetics |
| Anaerobic Digesters | Methanogenic growth, $\theta_c$ very long |

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Tip:** In KTU 2024 problems, always check the **SRT bounds**. If your calculated $\theta_c$ is less than the minimum, your design has failed — washout has occurred.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Substrate Balance on a CMAS Reactor

Consider a Completely Mixed Activated Sludge (CMAS) reactor with volume $V$, influent flow $Q$, influent substrate $S_0$, effluent substrate $S$, biomass concentration $X$, and sludge wastage flow $Q_w$ with concentration $X_w$.

**Mass balance on substrate (at steady state):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accumulation} &= \text{In} - \text{Out} - \text{Consumed by biomass} \\
0 &= Q \cdot S_0 - Q \cdot S - V \cdot r_{su} \\
V \cdot r_{su} &= Q \cdot (S_0 - S) \\
r_{su} &= \frac{Q(S_0 - S)}{V} = \frac{S_0 - S}{\theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, $r_{su}$ is the volumetric substrate utilization rate, and $\theta = V/Q$ is the hydraulic retention time (HRT).

**Relate $r_{su}$ to the Monod kinetic form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{su} &= \frac{k \cdot X \cdot S}{K_s + S} \\
\frac{S_0 - S}{\theta} &= \frac{k \cdot X \cdot S}{K_s + S}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **operating line equation** for a CMAS reactor and is heavily tested in KTU numericals.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Biomass Balance and the SRT Equation

**Mass balance on biomass (at steady state):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= Q \cdot X_0 - Q \cdot X - V \cdot (k_d \cdot X) + V \cdot Y \cdot r_{su} \\
V \cdot Y \cdot r_{su} &= Q \cdot X + V \cdot k_d \cdot X - Q \cdot X_0
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming influent biomass is negligible ($X_0 \approx 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y \cdot r_{su} &= \frac{Q \cdot X}{V} + k_d \cdot X \\
Y \cdot \frac{S_0 - S}{\theta} &= \frac{X}{\theta} + k_d \cdot X
\end{aligned}
$$

Divide through by $X$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{Y(S_0 - S)}{\theta \cdot X} &= \frac{1}{\theta} + k_d \\
Y \cdot U &= \frac{1}{\theta} + k_d
\end{aligned}
$$

**Introducing the Sludge Retention Time (SRT), $\theta_c$:**

By definition, $\theta_c$ is the average time biomass stays in the system. For a CMAS with sludge wasting:

$$\theta_c = \frac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + (Q - Q_w) \cdot X}$$

If biomass is wasted from the reactor line, $X_w = X$ and:

$$\theta_c = \frac{V}{Q_w}$$

**Combining the two relations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Since } Y \cdot U = \frac{1}{\theta} + k_d \\
\text{and at steady state, net biomass growth = wasted biomass: } \frac{1}{\theta_c} = YU - k_d
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substituting $U = (S_0 - S)/(\theta X)$ into the SRT equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\theta_c} &= Y \cdot \frac{S_0 - S}{\theta \cdot X} - k_d \\
\frac{1}{\theta_c} + k_d &= \frac{Y(S_0 - S)}{\theta \cdot X} \\
\theta \cdot X &= \frac{Y(S_0 - S)}{1/\theta_c + k_d} = \frac{Y \cdot \theta_c (S_0 - S)}{1 + k_d \theta_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives the famous **Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids (MLSS) design equation**:

$$X = \frac{\theta_c \cdot Y \cdot (S_0 - S)}{\theta \cdot (1 + k_d \theta_c)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Solving for Effluent Substrate $S$

**Starting point:**
$$\frac{1}{\theta_c} = \frac{YkS_0}{S_0 - S} - k_d$$

**Rearranging to isolate $S$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\theta_c} + k_d &= \frac{YkS_0}{S_0 - S} \\
(S_0 - S)\left(\frac{1}{\theta_c} + k_d\right) &= YkS_0 \\
S_0 - S &= \frac{YkS_0}{(1/\theta_c) + k_d} = \frac{YkS_0 \cdot \theta_c}{1 + k_d \theta_c} \\
S &= S_0 - \frac{YkS_0 \theta_c}{1 + k_d \theta_c} \\
S &= S_0 \left[1 - \frac{Yk\theta_c}{1 + k_d \theta_c}\right] \\
S &= S_0 \left[\frac{1 + k_d \theta_c - Yk\theta_c}{1 + k_d \theta_c}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final canonical KTU design equation:**

$$\boxed{S = \frac{K_s(1 + k_d \theta_c)}{Yk\theta_c - 1} \cdot \frac{1}{???}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction in derivation:** The proper canonical form (substituting $k = \mu_m/Y$) is:
> $$S = \frac{K_s(1 + k_d \theta_c)}{Yk\theta_c - (1 + k_d \theta_c)}$$

**Alternative form using $\mu_m$ directly:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{K_s(1 + k_d \theta_c)}{\mu_m \theta_c - (1 + k_d \theta_c)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Critical SRT (Washout Point)

Washout occurs when biomass cannot sustain itself — the observed net growth rate equals zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dX}{dt} &= 0 \\
\mu X &= k_d X \\
\mu &= k_d
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying Monod's equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_m \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S} &= k_d \\
\frac{S}{K_s + S} &= \frac{k_d}{\mu_m} \\
S &= \frac{k_d \cdot K_s}{\mu_m - k_d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substituting into the SRT equation at washout** ($\theta_c = \theta_c^{min}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\theta_c^{min}} &= \frac{YkS_0}{S_0 - S} - k_d \\
\frac{1}{\theta_c^{min}} &= YU_{max} - k_d = \mu_m - k_d \\
\theta_c^{min} &= \frac{1}{\mu_m - k_d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Critical washout condition:**

$$\boxed{\theta_c > \frac{1}{\mu_m - k_d} = \theta_c^{min}}$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** A CMAS reactor treats wastewater with $S_0 = 300$ mg/L BOD, $Q = 10{,}000$ m³/day. Kinetic constants: $\mu_m = 3.0$ day⁻¹, $K_s = 60$ mg/L, $Y = 0.5$ mg VSS/mg BOD, $k_d = 0.06$ day⁻¹. The desired SRT is $\theta_c = 10$ days. Calculate: (a) Effluent substrate $S$, (b) $U$, (c) $\theta$ and $V$ if $X = 2000$ mg/L.

**Solution:**

**(a) Effluent substrate $S$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{K_s(1 + k_d \theta_c)}{\mu_m \theta_c - (1 + k_d \theta_c)} \\
1 + k_d \theta_c &= 1 + (0.06)(10) = 1.6 \\
\mu_m \theta_c &= (3.0)(10) = 30 \\
\mu_m \theta_c - (1 + k_d \theta_c) &= 30 - 1.6 = 28.4 \\
S &= \frac{60 \times 1.6}{28.4} = \frac{96}{28.4} \\
S &\approx 3.38 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Specific substrate utilization rate $U$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{S_0 - S}{\theta \cdot X} \text{ or } U = \frac{1/\theta_c + k_d}{Y} \\
U &= \frac{(1/10) + 0.06}{0.5} = \frac{0.10 + 0.06}{0.5} = \frac{0.16}{0.5} \\
U &= 0.32 \text{ day}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) HRT $\theta$ and Volume $V$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \frac{Y(S_0 - S)}{X(1/\theta_c + k_d)} = \frac{0.5 \times (300 - 3.38)}{2000 \times 0.16} \\
\theta &= \frac{0.5 \times 296.62}{320} = \frac{148.31}{320} \\
\theta &\approx 0.4635 \text{ days} \approx 11.1 \text{ hours} \\
V &= Q \cdot \theta = 10{,}000 \times 0.4635 \\
V &\approx 4{,}635 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** $F/M = S_0/(\theta X) = 300/(0.4635 \times 2000) = 0.324$ day⁻¹, which is a typical conventional activated sludge loading. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Interrelationships of Kinetic Parameters

The following Mermaid diagram maps how substrate concentration, biomass, and operating parameters interact through the Basic Kinetic Equation in a CMAS system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    InfluentA[Influent Substrate S0] --> ReactorA[CMAS Reactor Volume V]
    InfluentQ[Flow Q] --> ReactorA
    ReactorA --> EffA[Effluent Substrate S]
    ReactorA --> EffX[Effluent Biomass X]

    SubNodeA[Substrate Removal dS/dt] --> MassBalA[Substrate Mass Balance]
    ReactorA --> MassBalA
    MassBalA --> SRTA[SRT Equation 1/theta_c = YU - kd]

    BioNodeA[Biomass Growth dX/dt] --> MassBalB[Biomass Mass Balance]
    ReactorA --> MassBalB
    MassBalB --> SRTA

    MonodA[Monod Equation mu = mum S / Ks + S] --> KinLinkA[Kinetic Link k = mum / Y]
    KinLinkA --> SubNodeA

    YCoeffA[Yield Coefficient Y] --> BioNodeA
    YCoeffA --> SubNodeA
    KdCoeffA[Decay Coefficient kd] --> BioNodeA
    KdCoeffA --> SRTA

    SRTA --> DesignOutA[Effluent Quality S]
    SRTA --> DesignOutB[Required Volume V]
    SRTA --> DesignOutC[Sludge Wasting Qw]
    SRTA --> DesignOutD[Washtable Check theta_c vs theta_c_min]

    WastageA[Sludge Wastage Qw] --> ReactorA
    WastageA --> SRTA

    classDef input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef constant fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000

    class InfluentA,InfluentQ input
    class ReactorA,SubNodeA,BioNodeA,MonodA,KinLinkA,MassBalA,MassBalB,SRTA process
    class EffA,EffX,DesignOutA,DesignOutB,DesignOutC,DesignOutD,WastageA output
    class YCoeffA,KdCoeffA constant
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow: Washout vs. Stable Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartA[Start: Given theta_c and kinetic constants] --> CalcA[Calculate theta_c_min = 1 / mum - kd]
    CalcA --> CheckA{Is theta_c GREATER than theta_c_min?}
    CheckA -->|NO| WashoutA[FAILURE: Biomass Washout]
    CheckA -->|YES| StableA[STABLE OPERATION]
    StableA --> CalcB[Calculate S using design equation]
    CalcB --> CalcC[Calculate U, F/M ratio]
    CalcC --> VerifyA{S within acceptable limits?}
    VerifyA -->|NO| AdjustA[Increase theta_c or V]
    VerifyA -->|YES| FinalA[Design Complete]
    AdjustA --> CalcA

    classDef success fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef failure fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000

    class StableA,FinalA success
    class WashoutA,CheckA,VerifyA failure
    class CalcA,CalcB,CalcC,AdjustA,StartA process
```

```mermaid

    Reactor1 -->|Mass Out Qw,Xw| Wastage1[Sludge Wastage]

    React1[Substrate Removal kXS/Ks+S] --> Reactor1
    React2[Biomass Growth Y*dS/dt - kd*X] --> Reactor1
    React3[Monod mu] --> React1
    React3 --> React2

    Monod1[mu = mum * S / Ks + S] --> React3
    Yield1[Yield Y] --> React2
    Decay1[Decay kd] --> React2
```
