### 4.2 Mass Balance Sankey — Influent Sludge Precursor Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Influent TSS Load (kg/d)"] -->|60 % Removed| B["Primary Sludge"]
    A -->|40 % Carryover to Bioreactor| C["Biological Stage"]
    C -->|95 % Captured as WAS| D["Waste Activated Sludge"]
    C -->|5 % in Effluent TSS| E["Lost in Final Effluent"]
    B --> F["Combined Sludge to Thickener"]
    D --> F
    F --> G["Thickener 4 to 6 % DS"]
    G --> H["Digester Volatile Reduction ~50 %"]
    H --> I["Dewatered Cake 18 to 25 % DS"]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Approximately 60 % of the influent suspended solids are captured in primary sedimentation; the remainder is largely biodegraded or captured biologically as WAS. This Sankey-style block diagram maps how the *sludge precursor* (influent TSS) is partitioned into various solid waste streams.

### 4.3 Mass Balance on the Activated Sludge Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Influent Q So X0"] --> B(["Aeration Tank V, X"])
    B -->|Q| C["Effluent Q S X"]
    B -->|Qw| D["Wasted Sludge Qw Xw"]
    B -.->|Recycled RAS QR| B
    B -->|"Growth Yobs Q(S0-S)"| E["Net Biomass Accumulated"]
    B -->|"Decay kd X V"| F["Endogenous Loss"]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Schematic of substrate and biomass flows around a CMAS reactor. $Q$ is influent flow; $Q_w$ is waste flow; $X$ is biomass concentration. The diagram visually conveys why the observed yield $Y_{obs}$ is lower than the true yield $Y$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are short-answer questions testing recall and basic conceptual clarity. Each carries 3 marks.

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define the term **"sludge"** in the context of wastewater treatment. Differentiate between **Primary Sludge** and **Secondary (Activated) Sludge** based on origin, color, odor, and biodegradability.

**Model Answer:**
Sludge is the semi-solid residue produced during physical, biological, and chemical treatment of wastewater. It comprises solids removed from the liquid phase plus entrained water.

| Property | Primary Sludge | Secondary/Activated Sludge |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Primary Clarifier | Secondary Clarifier (biological) |
| **Color** | Grey to dark brown | Brown, flocculent |
| **Odor** | Foul, putrescible (due to septicity) | Earthy, musty (not very foul) |
| **Biodegradability** | Easily biodegradable | Contains living microorganisms |
| **Typical VSS/TSS** | ~60 – 70 % | ~70 – 80 % |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for comparison table)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
Define **SVI (Sludge Volume Index)**. State its ideal range and explain what an **SVI of 250 mL/g** indicates about the sludge.

**Model Answer:**
SVI is defined as the volume (in mL) occupied by 1 gram of dry sludge solids after 30 minutes of settling in a 1-L graduated cylinder.

$$SVI = \frac{V_{30} \text{ (mL/L)}}{MLSS \text{ (g/L)}} \quad \text{(units: mL/g)}$$

- **Ideal range:** 50 – 150 mL/g (good settling).
- **SVI = 250 mL/g indicates bulking sludge** — microorganisms such as *filamentous bacteria* are overgrowing, causing poor compaction, high recycle ratios, and risk of sludge washout. Remedial measures include chlorine dosing, selector design, or adjustment of F/M ratio.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for definition + formula, 1 for range, 0.5 for interpretation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B of the End Semester Examination carries 14 marks per question with an internal choice. Each sub-part is generally of 7 marks.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, End Semester]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **various sources of sludge** in a typical municipal wastewater treatment plant comprising preliminary, primary, secondary, and tertiary treatment stages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A municipal WWTP treats a flow of $30{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$ with influent TSS = $280 \text{ mg/L}$ and BOD₅ = $200 \text{ mg/L}$. The primary clarifier removes **65 %** of TSS and **30 %** of BOD₅. The activated sludge process has $Y = 0.5 \text{ kg VSS/kg BOD}$, $k_d = 0.05 \text{ d}^{-1}$, and SRT $\theta_c = 12 \text{ days}$. Compute the **mass of dry solids (kg DS/d)** produced as (i) primary sludge and (ii) waste activated sludge. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The various sources of sludge are:

1. **Preliminary Treatment Sludge (Grit):** From grit chambers; inorganic, low organic content, settles rapidly. Approximately **0.005 – 0.015 kg/m³** of sewage.

2. **Primary Sludge:** From PST; ~60 – 70 % of influent TSS settles. Pale grey, slimy, highly putrescible. TS concentration typically **4 – 8 %** in the underflow.

3. **Secondary Sludge (WAS):** From biological reactor excess; flocculent brown biomass, VSS/TSS ≈ 0.75 – 0.85. TS concentration in the underflow **0.5 – 1.5 %**.

4. **Tertiary Sludge:** From chemical precipitation (FeCl₃, alum, or lime) for P removal. Light-coloured, high in metal hydroxides. TS ≈ **1 – 4 %**.

5. **Digested Sludge:** Residue from anaerobic/aerobic digesters; black, well-stabilized.

*(Sketch: A block diagram showing the wastewater train and the side streams of sludge generation — refer to SECTION 4.1 of these notes.)*

*(7 marks: 1 each for source identification + 2 for descriptive sketch/written explanation)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Primary sludge mass:**

$$M_{ps} = f_p \cdot Q \cdot TSS_0 \cdot 10^{-3}$$

$$M_{ps} = 0.65 \times 30{,}000 \times 280 \times 10^{-3} = 5{,}460 \text{ kg DS/d}$$

**(ii) Waste activated sludge mass:**

BOD entering aeration tank: $S_0' = 0.70 \times 200 = 140 \text{ mg/L}$

Assume effluent BOD $S = 10 \text{ mg/L}$ (typical), so $\Delta S = 130 \text{ mg/L}$.

Observed yield:

$$Y_{obs} = \frac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c} = \frac{0.5}{1 + (0.05)(12)} = \frac{0.5}{1.60} = 0.3125 \text{ kg VSS/kg BOD}$$

WAS mass:

$$P_X = 0.3125 \times 30{,}000 \times 130 \times 10^{-3} = 1{,}218.75 \text{ kg VSS/d} \approx 1{,}219 \text{ kg DS/d}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for primary sludge formula, 2 for observed yield derivation, 2 for WAS calculation, 1 for final answer & units)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, End Semester, Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** Derive the **observed yield coefficient $Y_{obs}$** in terms of true yield $Y$, endogenous decay coefficient $k_d$, and mean cell residence time $\theta_c$. Explain how $\theta_c$ influences the **quantity of sludge produced** in an activated sludge process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A WWTP generates $8{,}500 \text{ kg DS/d}$ of combined sludge at **4 % solids**. After thickening to **6 %** and dewatering to **22 %**, determine the **volume of sludge** at each stage. The density of dry solids may be assumed as $\rho_d = 1{,}500 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and water density $\rho_w = 1{,}000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The derivation follows Section 3.2 of these notes. Key steps:

**Step 1 — Steady-state biomass mass balance:**

$$\frac{VX}{\theta_c} = Y \cdot Q(S_0 - S) - k_d \cdot VX$$

**Step 2 — Net biomass production equals observed yield × substrate removed:**

$$\frac{VX}{\theta_c} = Y_{obs} \cdot Q(S_0 - S)$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions:**

$$Y_{obs} \cdot Q(S_0 - S) = Y \cdot Q(S_0 - S) - k_d \cdot VX$$

$$Y_{obs} = Y - \frac{k_d \cdot VX}{Q(S_0 - S)}$$

Using $Q(S_0 - S) = \dfrac{VX}{\theta_c \cdot Y}$ (from Monod-substituted form):

$$Y_{obs} = Y - k_d \cdot \theta_c \cdot Y = \frac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$$

**Interpretation — Influence of $\theta_c$:**

As $\theta_c$ increases, the denominator $(1 + k_d \theta_c)$ increases, so $Y_{obs}$ decreases. Therefore, **longer SRT → less sludge produced**, but also lower effluent BOD and larger aeration tank volume. Designers optimize $\theta_c$ between **5 – 15 days** for conventional ASP and **20 – 30 days** for extended aeration (very low sludge yield, near-zero wastage in extreme cases).

*(7 marks: 4 for derivation, 3 for interpretation)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**General principle:** Mass of dry solids is **conserved** across stages; only moisture changes. So:

$$M_{ds} = 8{,}500 \text{ kg DS/d} \quad \text{(constant)}$$

The volume of wet sludge at each stage is:

$$V_s = \frac{M_{ds}}{S \cdot \rho_s / 100}$$

For dilute sludge ($S < 5\%$), assume $\rho_s \approx \rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$:

**Stage 1 — Raw sludge (S = 4 %):**

$$V_1 = \frac{8{,}500}{4 \times 1000 / 100} = \frac{8{,}500}{40} = 212.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$$

**Stage 2 — Thickened sludge (S = 6 %):**

$$V_2 = \frac{8{,}500}{6 \times 1000 / 100} = \frac{8{,}500}{60} = 141.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$$

**Stage 3 — Dewatered cake (S = 22 %):** Use actual density:

$$\rho_{s,3} = \frac{100}{\dfrac{22}{1500} + \dfrac{78}{1000}} = \frac{100}{0.01467 + 0.078} = \frac{100}{0.09267} \approx 1{,}079 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$V_3 = \frac{8{,}500}{22 \times 1{,}079 / 100} = \frac{8{,}500}{237.4} = 35.81 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$$

> **Logic Note:** Total volume reduction from raw sludge to dewatered cake is **~83 %**, which is precisely the engineering motivation for sludge thickening and dewatering.

*(7 marks: 2 each for stage 1 and 2, 3 for stage 3 with density correction)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mg/L with m³/d without applying the $10^{-3}$ conversion factor. Always verify units before computing.
> 2. **Y vs Y_obs Confusion:** Using true yield $Y$ instead of observed yield $Y_{obs} = Y/(1 + k_d \theta_c)$ when calculating WAS — this inflates sludge mass by 30 – 60 %.
> 3. **Forgetting BOD Reduction in PST:** Applying $Y_{obs}$ to *influent* BOD instead of BOD *entering the aeration tank* — examiners deduct **2 marks** for this.
> 4. **Ignoring Density Correction:** For high-solids sludge ($S > 5\%$), using $\rho_s = 1000$ kg/m³ overestimates volume. Use the exact formula.
> 5. **SVI Definition:** Writing SVI as the reciprocal of the correct formula. Memorize: $SVI = V_{30}/MLSS$, NOT $MLSS/V_{30}$.
> 6. **Moisture vs Solids Confusion:** $M + S = 100$, not the other way around.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Sludge** = semi-solid residue from wastewater treatment; ~95 – 99.5 % water.
- ✅ **Primary Sludge** from PST → **Secondary/WAS** from biological reactor → both combined in solids train.
- ✅ **Primary sludge mass:** $M_{ps} = f_p \cdot Q \cdot TSS_0 \cdot 10^{-3}$.
- ✅ **Secondary sludge mass:** $P_X = Y_{obs} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot 10^{-3}$.
- ✅ **Observed yield:** $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$ (always less than true yield $Y$).
- ✅ **Longer $\theta_c$ → less sludge**, but bigger tank and more aeration energy.
- ✅ **SVI** = $V_{30}$/MLSS, units mL/g; ideal = **50 – 150**; > 200 → bulking.
- ✅ **Moisture content** $M + \text{Solids } S = 100$ (both in %).
- ✅ **Thickening/Dewatering** reduces sludge volume dramatically (4 % → 22 % solids cuts volume by ~80 %).
- ✅ **Mass of dry solids is conserved** across stages — only moisture is removed.
- ✅ **VSS ≈ 70 – 80 %** of TSS for domestic activated sludge → use carefully when reporting totals.
- ✅ **Volatile solids loss in anaerobic digestion ≈ 40 – 60 %** (key design parameter for digester volume).
- ✅ Sludge management consumes **50 – 60 %** of WWTP OPEX — accurate estimation is critical.
- ✅ Standard lab tests: **TS @ 103 °C**, **VS @ 550 °C**, **TSS/GF-C filter @ 1.2 µm**.
- ✅ Always state assumptions: steady-state, completely mixed, negligible effluent TSS, etc.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conventional Biological Process: Activated Sludge Process (ASP) - Development of Material Balance Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Conventional Biological Process: Activated Sludge Process (ASP) - Development of Material Balance Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: CONVENTIONAL BIOLOGICAL PROCESS - ACTIVATED SLUDGE PROCESS (ASP)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Activated Sludge Process (ASP):** A continuous-flow, aerobic, suspended-growth biological wastewater treatment process in which a biologically active sludge (a flocculated mass of microorganisms) is maintained in suspension in the aeration tank and subsequently separated from the treated liquid in a secondary clarifier, with a portion of the sludge recycled back to the aeration tank to maintain the required microbial population.

**Material Balance Equation (MBE):** A mathematical statement of the conservation of mass principle applied to a control volume (reactor, clarifier, or the entire system), which accounts for all mass flows entering, leaving, accumulating, being generated (by reactions), or being consumed (by reactions) within the defined boundaries.

The generalized MBE for any species in a control volume is:

$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{Inflow} - \text{Outflow} + \text{Generation} - \text{Consumption}$$

For steady-state conditions, **Accumulation = 0**, which simplifies the equation significantly.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen**
> Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen (the **aeration tank**) where chefs (**microorganisms**) continuously consume raw ingredients (**substrate/BOD**) to prepare dishes (**new cells/biomass**) while energy (**oxygen/heat**) is supplied. Dirty water with leftover food scraps flows in (**influent substrate, $S_0$**), and clean water with finished customers (**effluent**, $S_e$) flows out. Some of the cooked food (**wasted sludge**, $X_w$ or $X_{e'}$) is thrown away, but most chefs stay in the kitchen (**recycled sludge**, $X_r$). A **material balance** is like auditing the kitchen — counting how many ingredients came in, how many dishes went out, how many chefs were hired (growth, $Y$), and how many quit (decay, $k_d$). Without this audit, you cannot control portion sizes (sludge age, SRT), kitchen speed (F/M ratio), or how much electricity to pay for (oxygen demand).

### 1.3 Key Process Parameters and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Hydraulic Retention Time | $\theta$ or HRT | **4 to 8 hours** |
| Mean Cell Residence Time | $\theta_c$ (SRT/MCRT) | **5 to 15 days** |
| Food-to-Microorganism Ratio | F/M | **0.2 to 0.5 day⁻¹** |
| Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids | MLSS ($X$) | **1500 to 4000 mg/L** |
| Mixed Liquor Volatile Suspended Solids | MLVSS | **1200 to 3200 mg/L** |
| Recycle Ratio | $R$ ($Q_r/Q$) | **0.25 to 1.0** |
| Waste Sludge Flow | $Q_w$ | Variable |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Schematic and Control Volumes

The conventional ASP consists of two fundamental control volumes:

1. **Aeration Tank (Reactor)** — where biological reactions occur.
2. **Secondary Clarifier (Settler)** — where biomass is separated from treated effluent.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework for Material Balance

**Step 1:** Define the system boundary (control volume).
**Step 2:** Identify all input, output, generation, consumption streams for the **substrate** (BOD) and **biomass** (cells).
**Step 3:** Apply the steady-state mass balance equation (Accumulation = 0).
**Step 4:** Introduce kinetic expressions: substrate utilization rate ($r_{su} = kXS/[K_s + S]$) and biomass growth ($r_g = YkXS/[K_s + S] - k_dX$).
**Step 5:** Solve algebraically for design parameters (SRT, $\theta_c$, sludge production, oxygen demand).

### 2.3 Why and How — The Engineering Rationale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Material Balance?**
> - To predict **sludge production** (waste activated sludge volume per day).
> - To determine **oxygen demand** (blower power sizing).
> - To compute **SRT** (sludge age) — the single most critical operational parameter.
> - To size the **recycle pump** and **aeration tank volume**.
> - To design for **nitrification** (high SRT) vs. **carbonaceous BOD removal** (lower SRT).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are critical for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize the derivation logic, not just the final equation.

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Use |
| :-: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\theta_c = \dfrac{VX}{(Q - Q_w)X_e + Q_w X_r}$ | **Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT)** — controls microbial growth rate; critical for nitrification design. |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Y\dfrac{ksS}{K_s + S} - k_d$ | **Fundamental SRT-Substrate relationship** — links solids retention to effluent substrate at steady state. |
| 3 | $\theta_c = \dfrac{1}{Yk - k_d}$ (when $S \gg K_s$) | **Minimum SRT** simplification (substrate removal is carbonaceous and high-concentration). |
| 4 | $S = \dfrac{K_s \bigl(1 + k_d\theta_c\bigr)}{\theta_c\bigl(Yk - k_d\bigr) - 1}$ | **Effluent substrate concentration** as a function of SRT — used to predict effluent quality. |
| 5 | $\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{QS_0}{VX}$ | **Food-to-Microorganism ratio** — controls sludge settleability and effluent quality. |
| 6 | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | **Hydraulic Retention Time** — average time wastewater stays in the aeration tank. |
| 7 | $P_X = Y_{obs} Q(S_0 - S) = \dfrac{YQ(S_0 - S)}{1 + k_d\theta_c}$ | **Observed/NET sludge production** (kg/d) — defines waste activated sludge quantity. |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{Q_r}{Q} = \dfrac{X}{X_r - X}$ | **Recycle Ratio** — keeps MLSS concentration high in the reactor. |
| 9 | $X = \dfrac{\theta_c Y(S_0 - S)}{\theta(1 + k_d\theta_c)}$ | **Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids (MLSS)** — biomass concentration in aeration tank. |
| 10 | $\text{O}_2 = a' Q(S_0 - S) + b' V X$ | **Theoretical Oxygen Demand (kg O₂/d)** — for blower sizing. $a' \approx 0.5$; $b' \approx 0.15$. |
| 11 | $X_r = \dfrac{10^6}{\text{SVI}} \cdot \dfrac{R}{1 - R}$ (approx) | **Recycle SS concentration** as function of SVI. |
| 12 | $\text{SVI} = \dfrac{\text{MLSS settled volume (mL/L)} \times 1000}{\text{MLSS (mg/L)}}$ | **Sludge Volume Index** — measures sludge settleability. |

> **Units Convention:** $X$ (biomass) = mg/L VSS; $S$ (substrate) = mg/L BOD; $V$ = m³; $Q$ = m³/d; $k$ = L/(mg·d); $Y$ = mg VSS/mg BOD; $k_d$ = d⁻¹; $P_X$ = kg/d.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Control Volume Definition for Material Balance

Let us define:
- **$Q$** = Influent flow rate (m³/d) into aeration tank.
- **$Q_w$** = Waste sludge flow rate (m³/d) from recycle line.
- **$Q_e$** = Effluent flow rate (m³/d) leaving clarifier, where $Q_e = Q - Q_w$ (assuming negligible sludge loss in effluent).
- **$Q_r$** = Recycle flow rate (m³/d) from clarifier underflow.
- **$V$** = Aeration tank volume (m³).
- **$S_0$** = Influent substrate concentration (mg/L).
- **$S$** = Effluent substrate from aeration tank (mg/L).
- **$X$** = Biomass concentration in aeration tank (mg/L VSS).
- **$X_e$** = Biomass in effluent (mg/L).
- **$X_r$** = Biomass in recycle line (mg/L).
- **$Y$** = True yield coefficient (mg VSS/mg BOD).
- **$k_d$** = Endogenous decay coefficient (d⁻¹).
- **$k$** = Maximum specific substrate utilization rate (d⁻¹).
- **$K_s$** = Half-velocity constant (mg/L).

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 1 — Mass Balance on BIOMASS (Cells) Around Aeration Tank

### 3.2.1 Mass Balance Statement

Applying the generalized mass balance equation to biomass in the aeration tank:

$$\text{Accumulation}_X = \text{Inflow}_X - \text{Outflow}_X + \text{Net Growth}_X$$

### 3.2.2 Writing Each Term

- **Inflow of biomass** = Biomass entering with influent + biomass entering with recycle. We assume influent contains negligible biomass, so inflow = $Q_r X_r$.

- **Outflow of biomass** = Biomass leaving in effluent + biomass leaving in waste sludge. Underflow from clarifier is divided into recycle ($Q_r X_r$) and waste ($Q_w X_r$). Effluent biomass = $Q_e X_e$.

- **Net Growth of biomass** = $V \cdot r_g$, where $r_g$ is the net biomass growth rate (mg/L·d).

The kinetic expression for net growth (Lawrence & McCarty) is:

$$r_g = -Y r_{su} - k_d X$$

Where $r_{su}$ is the substrate utilization rate (Monod kinetics):

$$r_{su} = \dfrac{k X S}{K_s + S}$$

So:

$$r_g = Y \cdot \dfrac{k X S}{K_s + S} - k_d X$$

### 3.2.3 Steady-State Equation

At steady state, $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = 0$:

$$0 = Q_r X_r - Q_e X_e - Q_w X_r + V \left( Y \dfrac{k X S}{K_s + S} - k_d X \right)$$

### 3.2.4 Recognizing the Total Biomass Flow Out

Recognizing that $(Q_e X_e + Q_w X_r)$ is the total biomass leaving the system per day, and using the fact that the SRT ($\theta_c$) is defined as the total biomass in the reactor divided by the total biomass leaving per day:

$$\theta_c = \dfrac{VX}{(Q - Q_w)X_e + Q_w X_r} = \dfrac{VX}{Q_e X_e + Q_w X_r}$$

Rearranging the steady-state equation by dividing all terms by $V$ and substituting $\theta_c$:

$$\dfrac{X}{\theta_c} = Y \dfrac{k X S}{K_s + S} - k_d X$$

### 3.2.5 Canceling $X$ (assuming $X \neq 0$)

$$\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \dfrac{k S}{K_s + S} - k_d$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Lawrence–McCarty fundamental relationship** linking Mean Cell Residence Time ($\theta_c$) to effluent substrate ($S$). This equation is the cornerstone of activated sludge design and appears in nearly every KTU numerical problem.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 2 — Mass Balance on SUBSTRATE (BOD) Around Aeration Tank

### 3.3.1 Mass Balance Statement

For substrate $S$ (assumed soluble, non-biodegradable in the hydraulic time frame):

$$0 = Q S_0 - Q_e S - V \cdot r_{su}$$

(Since influent substrate = $Q S_0$, effluent substrate = $Q_e S$, and substrate consumption = $V \cdot r_{su}$).

### 3.3.2 Substituting Monod Kinetics

$$0 = Q S_0 - Q_e S - V \cdot \dfrac{k X S}{K_s + S}$$

### 3.3.3 Solving for Effluent Substrate

Assuming $Q \approx Q_e$ (since $Q_w$ is typically much smaller than $Q$):

$$Q(S_0 - S) = V \cdot \dfrac{k X S}{K_s + S}$$

Substituting $\theta = V/Q$ (hydraulic retention time):

$$(S_0 - S) = \theta \cdot \dfrac{k X S}{K_s + S}$$

Now we use the SRT relation. Rearranging the SRT equation:

$$\dfrac{k S}{K_s + S} = \dfrac{1}{\theta_c Y}\bigl(1 + k_d \theta_c\bigr)$$

Substituting back:

$$(S_0 - S) = \theta X \cdot \dfrac{1}{\theta_c Y}\bigl(1 + k_d \theta_c\bigr)$$

$$\boxed{S_0 - S = \dfrac{X \theta}{\theta_c Y}\bigl(1 + k_d \theta_c\bigr)}$$

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 3 — Observed Sludge Yield and Sludge Production

### 3.4.1 Definition of Observed Yield

The **observed yield** ($Y_{obs}$) accounts for the fact that not all biomass produced remains — some is lost via endogenous decay over the sludge age:

$$Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c}$$

### 3.4.2 Derivation from Growth Equation

The total biomass produced per day (net sludge production) is:

$$P_X = Y_{obs} \cdot Q(S_0 - S)$$

$$P_X = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c} \cdot Q(S_0 - S)$$

$$P_X = \dfrac{YQ(S_0 - S)}{1 + k_d \theta_c}$$

Where:
- $P_X$ = net waste activated sludge produced (kg VSS/d).
- $Q(S_0 - S)$ = total substrate removed per day (kg BOD/d).

---

## 3.5 DERIVATION 4 — Solids Mass Balance Around Secondary Clarifier

The clarifier receives MLSS from the aeration tank and splits it into recycle ($X_r$) and effluent ($X_e$) streams.

### 3.5.1 Mass Balance Statement

$$(Q + Q_r) X = Q_r X_r + Q_e X_e$$

Where $(Q + Q_r)$ is the flow entering the clarifier.

### 3.5.2 Recycle Ratio Relation

Since $X_e \ll X_r$ (ideal clarification), $Q_e X_e \approx 0$:

$$QX + Q_r X = Q_r X_r$$

$$X(Q + Q_r) = Q_r X_r$$

$$X \cdot Q (1 + R) = R \cdot Q \cdot X_r$$

$$\boxed{X_r = X \cdot \dfrac{1 + R}{R}}$$

Or in the more common form (solving for $X$):

$$X = \dfrac{R X_r}{1 + R} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad R = \dfrac{X}{X_r - X}$$

---

## 3.6 DERIVATION 5 — Hydraulic Retention Time and F/M Ratio

### 3.6.1 Hydraulic Retention Time

$$\theta = HRT = \dfrac{V}{Q}$$

### 3.6.2 F/M Ratio Definition

The F/M ratio is substrate loading per unit biomass:

$$\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{\text{Substrate applied per day (F)}}{\text{Biomass in aerator (M)}}$$

$$\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q S_0}{V X} = \dfrac{S_0}{\theta X}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** High F/M (> 0.5/day) → poor settling, dispersed growth. Low F/M (< 0.2/day) → pinpoint floc, excellent settling. Conventional ASP operates at **F/M = 0.2 – 0.4 /day**.

---

## 3.7 DERIVATION 6 — Theoretical Oxygen Demand

### 3.7.1 Oxygen Components

Total oxygen consumed = oxygen for **substrate oxidation** + oxygen for **endogenous respiration**.

### 3.7.2 Mass Balance on Dissolved Oxygen

$$0 = (\text{O}_2 \text{ supplied}) - a' Q(S_0 - S) - b' V X - (\text{O}_2 \text{ leaving})$$

Where $a'$ = fraction of substrate oxidized to CO₂; $b'$ = fraction of biomass oxidized per day.

Solving for oxygen demand per day:

$$\boxed{\text{O}_2 \text{ (kg/d)} = a' \cdot Q(S_0 - S) + b' \cdot V X}$$

Typical values:
- $a' \approx 0.5$ (about half of removed BOD is oxidized for energy).
- $b' \approx 0.15$ d⁻¹ (fraction of MLVSS oxidized endogenously).

---

## 3.8 KTU Numerical: Combined Application of All Six Derivations

### 3.8.1 Worked Example (10 marks type)

**Problem:** A conventional activated sludge plant treats $Q = 10{,}000$ m³/d of wastewater with $S_0 = 250$ mg/L BOD. Aeration tank volume $V = 2{,}500$ m³. MLSS $X = 2{,}000$ mg/L. Kinetic constants: $Y = 0.6$ mg VSS/mg BOD, $k_d = 0.06$ /d. Required effluent $S = 30$ mg/L. Determine: (a) SRT $\theta_c$, (b) F/M ratio, (c) Observed yield and sludge production, (d) Oxygen demand (assume $a' = 0.5$, $b' = 0.10$).

### Solution

**Part (a) — SRT:**

Using $1/\theta_c = YkS/(K_s + S) - k_d$ ... but $k$ and $K_s$ not given. Use alternative form:

$$\theta_c = \dfrac{VX}{P_X/\text{(later)}}$$

Instead, derive from observed yield relation. Rearranging $P_X = YQ(S_0 - S)/(1 + k_d \theta_c)$ and recognizing $P_X = VX/\theta_c$ (total biomass leaving per day):

$$\dfrac{VX}{\theta_c} = \dfrac{YQ(S_0 - S)}{1 + k_d \theta_c}$$

Substituting: $V = 2500$, $X = 2000$, $Q = 10000$, $S_0 - S = 220$, $Y = 0.6$, $k_d = 0.06$:

$$\dfrac{2500 \times 2000}{\theta_c} = \dfrac{0.6 \times 10000 \times 220}{1 + 0.06 \theta_c}$$

$$\dfrac{5{,}000{,}000}{\theta_c} = \dfrac{1{,}320{,}000}{1 + 0.06 \theta_c}$$

$$5{,}000{,}000(1 + 0.06\theta_c) = 1{,}320{,}000 \theta_c$$

$$5{,}000{,}000 + 300{,}000 \theta_c = 1{,}320{,}000 \theta_c$$

$$5{,}000{,}000 = 1{,}020{,}000 \theta_c$$

$$\theta_c = 4.90 \text{ days}$$

**Part (b) — F/M:**

$$\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{QS_0}{VX} = \dfrac{10000 \times 250}{2500 \times 2000} = \dfrac{2{,}500{,}000}{5{,}000{,}000} = 0.50 \text{ /day}$$

**Part (c) — Sludge Production:**

$$Y_{obs} = \dfrac{0.6}{1 + 0.06 \times 4.90} = \dfrac{0.6}{1.294} = 0.464 \text{ mg VSS/mg BOD}$$

$$P_X = 0.464 \times 10000 \times 220 \times 10^{-3} = 0.464 \times 2200 = 1020 \text{ kg VSS/d}$$

**Part (d) — Oxygen Demand:**

$$\text{O}_2 = 0.5 \times 10000 \times 220 \times 10^{-3} + 0.10 \times 2500 \times 2000 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= 0.5 \times 2200 + 0.10 \times 5000 = 1100 + 500 = 1600 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d}$$

---

## 3.9 Analytical Matrix: Effect of $\theta_c$ on Design

| SRT $\theta_c$ (days) | F/M (1/d) | Effluent Quality | Sludge Settling | Nitrification |
| :--: | :--: | :--: | :--: | :--: |
| 1 – 3 | 1.0 – 3.0 | Poor | Bulking | None |
| 3 – 5 | 0.5 – 1.0 | Fair | Poor | Partial |
| **5 – 10** | **0.2 – 0.5** | **Good** | **Good** | **Yes** |
| 10 – 20 | 0.1 – 0.2 | Excellent | Pinpoint | Complete |
| > 20 | < 0.1 | Excellent | Old sludge | Complete + Endogenous |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Activated Sludge Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture

This Mermaid diagram maps the material balance flow paths for substrate ($S$) and biomass ($X$) through the entire ASP system. Note: Node identifiers are alphanumeric with letter prefixes per the Mermaid Safeguard Rule.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Inf["INFLUENT Q with S0 BOD and X0 biomass"] --> SplitA
    SplitA{"Influent Splitter"}
    SplitA -->|Q flow to Aerator| AerIn

    subgraph Reactor["AERATION TANK - Volume V"]
        AerIn["Q S0 plus Qr Xr"] --> BioReact
        BioReact["Biological Reactions: Substrate Uptake plus Biomass Growth"] --> AerOut
        AerOut["Q plus Qr X leaving tank"]
    end

    AerOut --> ClarIn

    subgraph Settler["SECONDARY CLARIFIER"]
        ClarIn["Mixed Liquor Q plus Qr with X"] --> ClarProc["Gravity Settling: Solids separation"]
        ClarProc --> ClarEff
        ClarProc --> ClarUnd
        ClarEff["Effluent Qe with S and Xe"] --> Disch
        ClarUnd["Underflow Xr split into recycle and waste"]
    end

    ClarUnd -->|Recycle Qr Xr| BioReact
    ClarUnd -->|Waste Qw Xr| WasSludge["WASTE ACTIVATED SLUDGE P_X"]

    Disch["TREATED EFFLUENT Discharge"]

    AirBlower["AIR BLOWER"] -->|O2 supply| BioReact
    WasSludge --> SludgeProc["Sludge Processing: Thickening plus Digestion"]

    style Reactor fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC
    style Settler fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600
    style AirBlower fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#009900
```

## 4.2 Material Balance Control Volume Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CV1["CV 1: Aeration Tank Only\nMBE applied: Substrate plus Biomass"] --> CV2["CV 2: Clarifier Only\nMBE applied: Solids split"]
    CV2 --> CV3["CV 3: Entire ASP System\nMBE applied: Overall Substrate plus Sludge"]

    CV1 -.uses.-> Eq1["Equation 1: theta_c - S relationship"]
    CV2 -.uses.-> Eq2["Equation 2: Recycle ratio R formula"]
    CV3 -.uses.-> Eq3["Equation 3: Net sludge P_X formula"]

    style CV1 fill:#FFE6E6,stroke:#CC0000
    style CV2 fill:#FFFFCC,stroke:#999900
    style CV3 fill:#E6E6FF,stroke:#3333CC
```

## 4.3 Substrate Utilization Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Influent Substrate S0 mg per L"] --> Trans["Transport into floc"]
    Trans --> Ads["Adsorption on biomass surface"]
    Ads --> Monod["Monod Kinetics: r_su = k X S over Ks plus S"]
    Monod --> Meta["Metabolism: Oxidation plus Assimilation"]
    Metabol["Yields: a-prime fraction oxidized, Y fraction synthesized"]

    Meta --> CO2["CO2 plus H2O: Energy"]
    Meta --> Cells["New Cells: Y k X S over Ks plus S"]

    Cells --> Decay{"Endogenous Decay kd X"}
    Decay -->|Live cells| Resp
    Decay -->|Dead cells| Inert["Inert solids"]

    style S0 fill:#FFE6CC
    style Metabol fill:#CCE6FF
    style CO2 fill:#E6FFCC
    style Cells fill:#FFCCFF
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Define the Mean Cell Residence Time (MCRT/SRT) in an activated sludge process and write its significance."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Mean Cell Residence Time ($\theta_c$) is defined as the average time a unit of biomass (activated sludge solids) remains in the activated sludge system. Mathematically,
> $$\theta_c = \dfrac{VX}{(Q - Q_w)X_e + Q_w X_r}$$
>
> **Significance (1.5 marks):**
> 1. It governs the **type of microorganisms** prevailing in the system (high $\theta_c$ favors nitrifiers; low $\theta_c$ favors carbonaceous removers).
> 2. It directly controls the **effluent quality** through the Lawrence–McCarty equation.
> 3. It determines the **sludge production** rate via the observed yield formula.
> 4. It is used to **size the waste sludge pumping rate** $Q_w$.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"What is F/M ratio in activated sludge process? State its typical range for conventional ASP."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** The Food-to-Microorganism ratio (F/M) is the substrate loading per unit mass of biomass in the aeration tank, expressed as:
> $$\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{QS_0}{VX} \quad (\text{d}^{-1})$$
>
> **Typical range for conventional ASP (1 mark):** **0.2 to 0.5 /day** (ideally ≈ 0.3 /day for good BOD removal and settling).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Develop the material balance equation for biomass in an activated sludge process aeration tank and derive the **Lawrence–McCarty equation** relating SRT to effluent substrate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A conventional ASP has: $Q = 5000$ m³/d, $S_0 = 200$ mg/L, $V = 1500$ m³, $X = 2500$ mg/L, $Y = 0.65$, $k_d = 0.05$ /d. If the effluent BOD is 20 mg/L, determine: (i) Hydraulic retention time, (ii) F/M ratio, (iii) SRT $\theta_c$, and (iv) Net waste sludge production $P_X$ in kg/d. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Apply steady-state mass balance on biomass around the aeration tank:

$$0 = Q_r X_r - Q_e X_e - Q_w X_r + V \left( \dfrac{YkXS}{K_s + S} - k_d X \right)$$

Recognize SRT definition:

$$\theta_c = \dfrac{VX}{Q_e X_e + Q_w X_r}$$

Dividing the mass balance by $V$ and rearranging:

$$\dfrac{X}{\theta_c} = \dfrac{YkXS}{K_s + S} - k_d X$$

Canceling $X$ yields the **Lawrence–McCarty equation**:

$$\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Y \dfrac{kS}{K_s + S} - k_d$$

> This equation is the **master design equation** linking operating SRT to biological kinetics and effluent quality. It forms the basis of all ASP design.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) HRT:**

$$\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{1500}{5000} = 0.30 \text{ d} = 7.2 \text{ h}$$

**(ii) F/M Ratio:**

$$\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{5000 \times 200}{1500 \times 2500} = \dfrac{10^6}{3.75 \times 10^6} = 0.267 \text{ /d}$$

**(iii) SRT $\theta_c$:**

Using $P_X = VX/\theta_c = YQ(S_0 - S)/(1 + k_d \theta_c)$:

$$\dfrac{1500 \times 2500}{\theta_c} = \dfrac{0.65 \times 5000 \times (200 - 20)}{1 + 0.05 \theta_c}$$

$$\dfrac{3{,}750{,}000}{\theta_c} = \dfrac{585{,}000}{1 + 0.05 \theta_c}$$

$$3{,}750{,}000(1 + 0.05 \theta_c) = 585{,}000 \theta_c$$

$$3{,}750{,}000 + 187{,}500 \theta_c = 585{,}000 \theta_c$$

$$3{,}750{,}000 = 397{,}500 \theta_c$$

$$\boxed{\theta_c = 9.43 \text{ days}}$$

**(iv) Net Sludge Production:**

$$Y_{obs} = \dfrac{0.65}{1 + 0.05 \times 9.43} = \dfrac{0.65}{1.4715} = 0.442$$

$$P_X = 0.442 \times 5000 \times 180 \times 10^{-3} = 397.5 \text{ kg VSS/d} \approx 398 \text{ kg/d}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **mass balance around the secondary clarifier** of an activated sludge process and derive the expression for the **recycle ratio** $R$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **theoretical oxygen demand equation** for an activated sludge process and estimate the daily oxygen requirement for a plant with: $Q = 20{,}000$ m³/d, $S_0 = 300$ mg/L, $S = 20$ mg/L, $V = 4000$ m³, $X = 3000$ mg/L, $a' = 0.50$, $b' = 0.12$ /d. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Clarifier Mass Balance (7 marks):**

The secondary clarifier receives mixed liquor from the aeration tank and splits it into clarified effluent and thickened underflow. Steady-state suspended solids balance:

$$(Q + Q_r) X = Q_r X_r + Q_e X_e$$

For an ideal clarifier, $X_e \approx 0$, so:

$$Q X + Q_r X = Q_r X_r$$

Dividing by $Q$:

$$X + \dfrac{Q_r}{Q} X = \dfrac{Q_r}{Q} X_r$$

$$X = R(X_r - X)$$

$$\boxed{R = \dfrac{Q_r}{Q} = \dfrac{X}{X_r - X}}$$

> **Engineering Use:** This equation determines the recycle pump capacity. Higher desired MLSS $X$ requires higher $R$ (subject to clarifier limitations).

**Part (b) — Oxygen Demand (7 marks):**

**Derivation:**
Total oxygen consumed in an aeration tank serves two purposes:
1. Oxidation of removed substrate for energy (rate $\propto Q(S_0 - S)$).
2. Endogenous respiration of biomass (rate $\propto VX$).

Applying steady-state oxygen balance:

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ supplied} = a' Q(S_0 - S) + b' V X$$

**Numerical Solution:**

$$O_2 = 0.50 \times 20{,}000 \times (300 - 20) \times 10^{-3} + 0.12 \times 4000 \times 3000 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$O_2 = 0.50 \times 5600 + 0.12 \times 12{,}000$$

$$O_2 = 2800 + 1440$$

$$\boxed{O_2 = 4240 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d}}$$

Considering standard aeration efficiency (≈ 1.2 kg O₂/kWh for fine bubble diffusers):

$$\text{Blower power} = \dfrac{4240}{1.2 \times 24} \approx 147.2 \text{ kW continuous}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make that cost 1–3 marks each:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Mixing $Q$ in m³/s with $V$ in m³ — convert to **same time basis** (e.g., both per day).
> 2. **Forgetting the $k_d\theta_c$ term** in the observed yield formula — this is the most common error. Always write the **full** equation: $Y_{obs} = Y/(1 + k_d\theta_c)$.
> 3. **Mis-Defining SRT:** Students confuse $\theta_c$ (solids residence time) with $\theta$ (hydraulic retention time). $\theta_c$ is in **days**, $\theta$ is in **hours**.
> 4. **Neglecting Effluent Biomass $X_e$:** In well-settled systems, $X_e$ is small (≈ 10 mg/L). If neglected, ensure the problem justifies it.
> 5. **Using Influent Biomass $X_0$ Incorrectly:** In conventional ASP, $X_0 \approx 0$. If the problem provides $X_0$, you must include it in the influent stream.
> 6. **Oxygen Demand Sign Error:** The two terms $a'Q(S_0 - S)$ and $b'VX$ are **both subtractions** from the supplied oxygen (consumption). Never add them as oxygen supply.
> 7. **Forgetting $10^{-3}$ Factor:** When $Q(S_0-S)$ has units (m³/d)(mg/L), it equals (m³/d)(g/m³) = kg/d. **You must multiply by $10^{-3}$** to convert mg → g, OR work in g/m³ throughout.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Final-day Read):**
>
> ✅ **Lawrence–McCarty Equation** (master equation): $1/\theta_c = YkS/(K_s + S) - k_d$
> ✅ **Observed Yield:** $Y_{obs} = Y/(1 + k_d\theta_c)$
> ✅ **Sludge Production (NET):** $P_X = YQ(S_0 - S)/(1 + k_d\theta_c)$ — units kg/d
> ✅ **F/M Ratio:** $F/M = QS_0/(VX)$ — typical 0.2 to 0.5 /d
> ✅ **SRT:** $\theta_c = VX/[(Q-Q_w)X_e + Q_w X_r]$ — typical 5 to 15 days
> ✅ **HRT:** $\theta = V/Q$ — typical 4 to 8 hours
> ✅ **Recycle Ratio:** $R = X/(X_r - X)$ — typical 0.25 to 1.0
> ✅ **Oxygen Demand:** $O_2 = a'Q(S_0 - S) + b'VX$ — kg/d
> ✅ **Steady-state assumption** is mandatory unless dynamic simulation is specified.
> ✅ **Monod kinetics** ($k$, $K_s$) describes substrate-limited growth.
> ✅ **High $\theta_c$** → better effluent, more nitrification, less sludge, but larger tank.
> ✅ **Low $\theta_c$** → pin-floc, possible bulking, partial BOD removal only.
> ✅ **Conventional ASP design values:** HRT = 4–8 h, SRT = 5–15 d, F/M = 0.2–0.5 /d, MLSS = 1500–4000 mg/L, R = 0.25–1.0.

> **Mnemonic for SRT, F/M, HRT, MLSS:**
> **"S-F-H-M" → "Slow-Flow-Heavy-Mix"** — Long SRT, low F/M, moderate HRT, high MLSS = Good Settling ASP.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trickling Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Trickling Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧪 MODULE 2 — TRICKLING FILTERS: CORE FOUNDATIONS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Trickling Filter (TF)** is an **attached-growth (biofilm) biological wastewater treatment unit** in which wastewater is distributed over a bed of coarse, porous media (rock, slag, plastic, or wood). Microorganisms grow as a **slime layer (biofilm)** on the media surface, and as the liquid trickles past, organic matter is metabolised by **aerobic, facultative, and anaerobic microorganisms** stabilised in successive strata of the biofilm.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the trickling filter belongs to the family of **Secondary Treatment Units** under **Aerobic Biological Processes**, specifically the **Fixed-Film Reactor** class. It precedes or replaces the suspended-growth activated sludge process in many conventional sewage treatment plants (STPs) across Kerala municipalities.

### 1.1.1 System Components — The Five Mandatory Elements

| # | Component | Engineering Function |
|---|-----------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Distributor Arm** | Rotates hydraulically (or mechanically) and sprays wastewater uniformly over the media. |
| 2 | **Filter Media** | Provides surface area for biofilm attachment. Surface area typically **40–220 m²/m³**. |
| 3 | **Biofilm (Zoogleal Slime)** | The living microbial layer (~0.1–2 mm thick) responsible for BOD removal. |
| 4 | **Underdrain System** | Collects treated effluent and admits air for **natural/passive ventilation**. |
| 5 | **Ventilation Ports** | Maintain **DO ≥ 2 mg/L** inside the filter bed via stack effect. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Vertical Garden"

> [!NOTE]  
> **Imagine a vertical apartment building covered in moss.** Rain (wastewater) falls over the building. The moss (biofilm) absorbs nutrients (BOD) from the rainwater. Beneath the building, gutters (underdrain) collect the cleaned water. Air circulates between the walls (ventilation) so the moss keeps breathing. A rotating sprinkler on the roof (distributor) keeps the rain falling evenly.

This is exactly how a trickling filter operates — except the "building" is a deep stone bed, the "moss" is a complex consortia of **bacteria, protozoa, fungi, algae, and higher life forms** (worms, larvae, snails).

---

## 1.3 Microbial Stratification — The Vertical Ecology

The biofilm itself is not uniform. It changes character with depth because oxygen penetration decreases inward:

- **Outer Aerobic Layer:** Bacteria (*Zoogloea*, *Pseudomonas*, *Nitrosomonas*) oxidise BOD and ammonia.
- **Middle Facultative Layer:** Bacteria capable of switching metabolism when DO drops.
- **Inner Anaerobic Layer:** Sulphate-reducing bacteria, denitrifiers.
- **Surface Grazers:** Protozoa, rotifers, worms (*Tubifex*, *Psychoda* fly larvae) that prey on excess biomass — **a self-regulating predator-prey balance that prevents clogging.**

---

## 1.4 Classification of Trickling Filters (KTU Board Favourite)

| Type | Hydraulic Loading (m³/m²·d) | Organic Loading (kg BOD/m³·d) | Recirculation |
|------|------------------------------|-------------------------------|---------------|
| **Standard / Low-Rate** | 1–4 | 0.08–0.16 | Usually None |
| **High Rate** | 10–40 | 0.24–0.96 | Always |
| **Roughing (Super High-Rate)** | 40–200 | 0.96–4.8 | High (2–4×) |
| **Two-Stage** | Series of two filters | Combined loading | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Distinguish clearly between *hydraulic loading rate* (HLR) and *organic loading rate* (OLR). Examiners frequently test the unit confusion: HLR uses **m³/m²·d**, while OLR uses **kg BOD/m³·d** (per unit **volume**, not area). This is a common 1–2 mark trap.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORY & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why a Trickling Filter Works

The mechanism of BOD removal proceeds in **six sequential steps**:

1. **Distribution:** Wastewater is sprayed over the bed → forms a thin liquid film.
2. **Adsorption:** Dissolved and colloidal organics diffuse into the biofilm.
3. **Diffusion:** Oxygen from air vents penetrates the biofilm (Fick's Law).
4. **Metabolism:** Aerobic microbes oxidise organics → CO₂ + H₂O + new cells.
5. **Sloughing:** Excess biomass detaches when biofilm thickness exceeds ~2 mm (sloughing event).
6. **Collection & Humus Removal:** Sloughed solids pass into underdrain → secondary clarifier removes them as **humus sludge**.

> [!NOTE]  
> **The "Why" behind sloughing:** As biofilm thickens, the inner cells receive less oxygen and starve. The weakened inner layer can no longer hold the outer biomass → detachment occurs in sheets. This is **nature's automatic biomass control**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR)** | $\displaystyle HLR = \frac{Q}{A}$ | m³/m²·d | $Q$ = flow, $A$ = plan area |
| 2 | **Organic Loading Rate (OLR)** | $\displaystyle OLR = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | kg BOD/m³·d | $S_0$ = influent BOD, $V$ = media volume |
| 3 | **BOD Removal Efficiency (Velz Curve)** | $\displaystyle \frac{S_e}{S_0} = \exp\left(-k \cdot D / Q^{n}\right)$ | dimensionless | Empirical; Velz (1948) |
| 4 | **Eckenfelder Equation** | $\displaystyle \frac{S_e}{S_0} = \exp(-k \cdot t)$ | dimensionless | Pseudo-first-order, $t$ = contact time |
| 5 | **Eckenfelder (with recirculation)** | $\displaystyle \frac{S_e - S_R}{S_0 - S_R} = \exp\left(\frac{-k \cdot D}{Q^{n}}\right)$ | dimensionless | $S_R$ = recirculated BOD |
| 6 | **Modified Velz (Schulze)** | $\displaystyle \frac{S_e}{S_0} = \exp\left(\frac{-k \cdot D}{Q^{n}}\right)$ | dimensionless | Same form; $k, n$ empirical |
| 7 | **Media Surface Area Required** | $\displaystyle A_m = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{L_{org,max}}$ | m² | Where $L_{org,max}$ is max allowable loading |
| 8 | **Filter Volume** | $\displaystyle V = A \cdot H$ | m³ | $H$ = depth (typically 1.8–3.0 m) |
| 9 | **Recirculation Ratio** | $\displaystyle R = \frac{Q_R}{Q}$ | dimensionless | $Q_R$ = recirculated flow |
| 10 | **Distributor Rotational Speed** | $\displaystyle N = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{q}{A_R \cdot \pi}$ | rev/min | $q$ = discharge per arm, $A_R$ = arm area |
| 11 | **DO Transfer (Mass Balance)** | $\displaystyle r_{O_2} = K_L a (C^* - C)$ | kg O₂/m³·d | Two-film theory |
| 12 | **Effective Contact Time** | $\displaystyle t = \frac{C \cdot D \cdot H}{Q^{n}}$ | hours | $C$ = media constant |

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse $S_0$ (influent substrate concentration, kg/m³) with $Q \cdot S_0$ (mass loading, kg/d). The OLR formula **divides by Volume** in m³ — that is the *volumetric* loading. The *areal* loading is $Q S_0 / A$ in kg/m²·d. Always check units in numerical problems.

---

## 2.3 Boundary Conditions & Validity Ranges

| Condition | Standard Rate | High Rate |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|
| Recirculation Ratio | 0 | 0.5 – 3.0 |
| Filter Depth | 1.5 – 3.0 m | 1.0 – 2.0 m |
| Media size | 25 – 75 mm | 40 – 100 mm |
| BOD Removal Efficiency | 75 – 90 % | 65 – 85 % |
| Effluent BOD | < 25 mg/L | < 30 mg/L |
| Nitrification | Often partial | Rare |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Trickling filters dominate the wastewater landscape where:

- **Power supply is intermittent** (rural Kerala, Tier-2 towns) — TFs operate on gravity flow and natural draft, requiring **no aeration blower** like activated sludge.
- **Skilled operators are scarce** — TF needs no MLSS control, no F/M ratio tuning, no SVI monitoring.
- **Industrial wastewater co-treatment** is needed — TFs handle shock loads better than activated sludge because the biofilm acts as a buffer.
- **Space is constrained** — Vertical packed bed gives compact footprint (esp. plastic media).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Module Context:** Compare TF with **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)** under NEP-2020 OBE framework. TF wins on **energy efficiency** and **simplicity**, ASP wins on **effluent quality** and **footprint**. This comparative question carries high weightage (12–14 marks).

---

## 2.5 Distributor Arm — Engineering Detail

The distributor is a **horizontal hollow pipe** with spray nozzles, mounted on a central pivot. It rotates at **0.5–2 rpm** driven by:

- **Hydraulic Reaction:** Discharge through offset nozzles creates torque.
- **Mechanical Drive:** Electric motor with gear reduction (used for large units).

$$\displaystyle T = \frac{A_R}{q}$$

Where $T$ = time for one revolution (min), $A_R$ = area swept (m²), $q$ = discharge per arm (m³/min).

> [!TIP]  
> **Examination Hack:** If the question asks *"why does the distributor rotate intermittently?"* — The answer is **sloughing control**. Continuous slow rotation helps; intermittent spraying in some designs (e.g., BFBC) allows biofilm to dry and slough predictably.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔢 SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Velz Kinetic Model

### 3.1.1 Governing Assumption

Consider a horizontal slice of trickling filter of thickness $\mathrm{d}z$ at depth $z$. Wastewater containing substrate concentration $S$ flows downward with hydraulic loading $Q/A$ (m³/m²·d). The biofilm removes substrate at a rate proportional to:

$$\displaystyle r = -k \cdot S \cdot \frac{A_{media}}{V}$$

Where $k$ = specific substrate removal rate constant.

### 3.1.2 Mass Balance Across Differential Slice

$$\displaystyle Q \cdot \mathrm{d}S = -k \cdot S \cdot \mathrm{d}A_{media}$$

Substituting $\mathrm{d}A_{media} = a \cdot \mathrm{d}V = a \cdot A \cdot \mathrm{d}z$ (where $a$ = specific surface area of media, m²/m³):

$$\displaystyle Q \cdot \mathrm{d}S = -k \cdot S \cdot a \cdot A \cdot \mathrm{d}z$$

Dividing both sides by $Q \cdot S$:

$$\displaystyle \frac{\mathrm{d}S}{S} = -\frac{k \cdot a \cdot A}{Q} \cdot \mathrm{d}z$$

### 3.1.3 Integration Across Full Depth $H$

Integrating from influent ($S_0$ at $z=0$) to effluent ($S_e$ at $z=H$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{S_0}^{S_e} \frac{\mathrm{d}S}{S} &= -\frac{k \cdot a \cdot A}{Q} \int_{0}^{H} \mathrm{d}z \\[6pt]
\ln\!\left(\frac{S_e}{S_0}\right) &= -\frac{k \cdot a \cdot A \cdot H}{Q}
\end{aligned}$$

Recognising that $V = A \cdot H$ and defining $\displaystyle k' = k \cdot a$ (lumped constant, m/d):

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\frac{S_e}{S_0}\right) &= -\frac{k' \cdot V}{Q} \\[6pt]
\frac{S_e}{S_0} &= \exp\!\left(-\frac{k' \cdot V}{Q}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **fundamental plug-flow biofilm reactor equation**.

### 3.1.4 Velz's Empirical Modification

Velz observed that flow distribution was non-ideal and proposed a power-law correction $Q^{n}$ where $n = 0.5$ to $0.67$:

$$\boxed{\displaystyle \frac{S_e}{S_0} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{k \cdot D}{Q^{n}}\right)}$$

Where $D$ = filter depth (m), and $k, n$ are empirical constants depending on media type and wastewater characteristics.

---

## 3.2 Eckenfelder Equation — Pluggable Variant

Eckenfelder (1961) developed a similar but dimensionally simpler form, where the contact time $t$ replaces depth:

$$\displaystyle \frac{S_e}{S_0} = \exp(-k \cdot t)$$

With recirculation of flow rate $Q_R$ at substrate $S_R$:

$$\displaystyle \frac{S_e - S_R}{S_0 - S_R} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{k \cdot D}{Q^{n}}\right)$$

This accounts for the dilution effect of recirculated effluent.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Pattern

> **Problem:** A municipal trickling filter receives $Q = 10{,}000$ m³/d of sewage with $S_0 = 250$ mg/L BOD. Filter area $A = 500$ m², depth $H = 2.0$ m, media constant $k = 1.8$ (d⁻¹), exponent $n = 0.5$. Compute the **effluent BOD** $S_e$.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute HLR:**
$$HLR = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{10{,}000}{500} = 20 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{d}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the exponent term:**
$$Q^{n} = 20^{0.5} = \sqrt{20} = 4.4721$$

**Step 3 — Apply Velz equation:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{S_e}{S_0} &= \exp\!\left(-\frac{k \cdot D}{Q^{n}}\right) \\[4pt]
&= \exp\!\left(-\frac{1.8 \times 2.0}{4.4721}\right) \\[4pt]
&= \exp(-0.8052) \\[4pt]
&= 0.4470
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Effluent BOD:**
$$S_e = 0.4470 \times 250 = 111.75 \approx \mathbf{112 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**Step 5 — BOD Removal Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100 = \frac{250 - 112}{250} \times 100 = \mathbf{55.2\%}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Interpretation:** A single-stage high-rate filter alone gives ~55% BOD removal. To meet discharge standards (BOD < 30 mg/L per KSPCB norms), a **two-stage filter** or **TF + Activated Sludge** combination (e.g., trickling filter / solids contact process) is needed.

---

## 3.4 Worked Problem — With Recirculation

> **Problem:** Same filter as above, but with recirculation ratio $R = 1.5$. Recirculated effluent has $S_R = 40$ mg/L BOD. Find new $S_e$.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Apply recirculated Eckenfelder-Velz form:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{S_e - S_R}{S_0 - S_R} &= \exp\!\left(-\frac{k \cdot D}{Q^{n}}\right) \\[4pt]
\frac{S_e - 40}{250 - 40} &= \exp(-0.8052) = 0.4470
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $S_e$:**
$$\begin{aligned}
S_e - 40 &= 0.4470 \times 210 = 93.87 \\[4pt]
S_e &= 40 + 93.87 = \mathbf{133.87 \text{ mg/L}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **Board Trap:** Students intuitively assume recirculation always improves effluent quality. **Wrong.** Recirculation dilutes shock loads and equalises flow but the *instantaneous effluent* $S_e$ from the filter bed may be *higher* than the no-recirculation case because the bed sees a *denser* averaged influent. The benefit is in **combined system stability**, not single-pass $S_e$ reduction.

---

## 3.5 Derived Quantity — Filter Volume Required

For a target effluent BOD of $S_e^{target}$:

$$\boxed{\displaystyle V = -\frac{Q^{n}}{k} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{S_e^{target}}{S_0}\right)}$$

**Verification using our example:** Target $S_e = 30$ mg/L.

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\frac{30}{250}\right) &= \ln(0.12) = -2.1203 \\[4pt]
V &= -\frac{4.4721}{1.8} \times (-2.1203) = 5.27 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

Required bed depth:
$$H = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{5.27}{500} = 0.0105 \text{ m}$$

> This unrealistically small value reflects the limitation of Velz's model for **deep beds with poor media constants**. In practice, filter depths of 1.5–3.0 m are used regardless because of hydraulic and biofilm dynamics not captured in the simple exponential model.

---

## 3.6 Summary of Analytical Decision Matrix

| Parameter | Standard Rate | High Rate | Decision Driver |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|-----------------|
| OLR (kg BOD/m³·d) | 0.08–0.16 | 0.24–0.96 | Strength of sewage |
| HLR (m³/m²·d) | 1–4 | 10–40 | Quantity of sewage |
| Recirculation | None | Yes (1–3×) | Hydraulic smoothing |
| BOD Removal | 80–95 % | 65–85 % | Discharge standard |
| Media Cost | Rock (cheap) | Plastic (premium) | Capital budget |
| Energy Use | Zero | Zero | Power scarcity |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
