# 📝 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q:** Differentiate between *attached growth* and *suspended growth* biological treatment processes. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Attached Growth | Suspended Growth |
|---------|-----------------|------------------|
| Microbial habitat | Biofilm on fixed media | Flocs dispersed in mixed liquor |
| Biomass retention | Independent of HRT | Depends on sludge settling |
| Operator skill | Low | High |
| Sludge yield | Low | Moderate–High |
| Energy demand | Low (passive aeration) | High (blowers) |
| Examples | Trickling Filter, RBC, MBBR | Activated Sludge, Aerated Lagoon |

(3 marks — full credit for any 3 meaningful differences and one example each.)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q:** What is meant by *sloughing* in a trickling filter? Mention two factors influencing it.

**Model Answer:**
*Sloughing* is the detachment of excess biofilm from the filter media when its thickness exceeds the diffusion depth of oxygen (~2 mm). Inner anaerobic cells die, weakening attachment, and sheets of biomass wash off with the effluent.

**Factors influencing sloughing:**
1. **Hydraulic loading rate** — Higher flow shears biomass more aggressively.
2. **Organic loading rate** — Higher OLR → faster biomass growth → more frequent sloughing.
3. Temperature (warmer = faster metabolism, faster sloughing).
4. Influent substrate composition (high carbohydrates → stickier biofilm).

(3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **components and working of a trickling filter**. Discuss the role of the distributor arm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A trickling filter (see Section 4.2 diagram) consists of a circular tank containing coarse media (rock, slag, or synthetic) over a false-floor underdrain. Sewage from primary sedimentation is sprayed over the media by a rotating distributor arm.

**Working steps:**
1. Wastewater enters via central well → distributor arm.
2. Arm rotates (hydraulically or by motor) and sprays effluent uniformly.
3. Liquid trickles over biofilm-coated media; organics are adsorbed and oxidised.
4. Aerobic microbes (outer biofilm) metabolise BOD using oxygen supplied by natural draft ventilation.
5. Sloughed biomass + treated effluent fall through the underdrain.
6. Effluent goes to humus tank (secondary clarifier) where humus sludge settles.
7. Final clarified effluent is sent for disinfection.

**Role of distributor arm:**
- Ensures uniform distribution of wastewater across the entire media surface.
- Hydraulic reaction at nozzles rotates the arm, providing continuous coverage.
- Wetting/drying cycles help sloughing and biofilm renewal.
- Rotation speed controls hydraulic loading on local media zones.

**Sketch:** (Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram — board candidate should draw the physical layout manually with arrows for flow direction.)

---

**(b)** A high-rate trickling filter treats $Q = 5{,}000$ m³/d of settled sewage with $S_0 = 300$ mg/L BOD. The filter has diameter 15 m and depth 2.5 m. Media constants: $k = 2.5$ d⁻¹, $n = 0.6$. Compute (i) Hydraulic Loading Rate, (ii) Organic Loading Rate, (iii) Effluent BOD using Velz equation, and (iv) BOD removal efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**(i) Hydraulic Loading Rate:**
$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (15)^2}{4} = \frac{706.86}{4} = 176.71 \text{ m}^2 \\[4pt]
HLR &= \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{5{,}000}{176.71} = 28.29 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{d}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Organic Loading Rate:**
$$\begin{aligned}
V &= A \times H = 176.71 \times 2.5 = 441.78 \text{ m}^3 \\[4pt]
Q \cdot S_0 &= 5{,}000 \times 0.300 = 1{,}500 \text{ kg BOD/d} \\[4pt]
OLR &= \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V} = \frac{1{,}500}{441.78} = 3.395 \text{ kg BOD/m}^3 \cdot \text{d}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Effluent BOD (Velz):**
$$\begin{aligned}
Q^{n} &= 28.29^{0.6}
\end{aligned}$$

Computing $28.29^{0.6}$:
$$28.29^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \ln 28.29} = e^{0.6 \times 3.3425} = e^{2.0055} = 7.43$$

Then:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{S_e}{S_0} &= \exp\!\left(-\frac{2.5 \times 2.5}{7.43}\right) = \exp(-0.8412) = 0.4310 \\[4pt]
S_e &= 0.4310 \times 300 = \mathbf{129.3 \text{ mg/L}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) BOD Removal Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{300 - 129.3}{300} \times 100 = \mathbf{56.9\%}$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often make these mistakes in Part B numericals:
> 1. **Confusing area units** — Diameter given as 15 m → area uses $\pi D^2 / 4$, *not* $\pi r^2$ using $r$ mistakenly.
> 2. **Mixing up loading types** — HLR (m³/m²·d) vs OLR (kg/m³·d). The units *force* the denominator: area for HLR, volume for OLR.
> 3. **Computing $Q^{n}$ incorrectly** — Use natural log, not base-10. $28.29^{0.6} \ne 28.29 \times 0.6$.
> 4. **Forgetting BOD units** — Always convert mg/L → kg/m³ (multiply by $10^{-3}$). 1 mg/L = 1 g/m³ = $10^{-3}$ kg/m³.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Alternate Module Choice]`

**(a)** Explain **NRC and NCVSS equations** used in trickling filter design. Compare the Velz equation with the Eckenfelder equation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**NRC (National Research Council) Equation:**
The NRC developed an empirical relation based on pilot and full-scale plant data in the 1940s:

$$\displaystyle E = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{0.0085 \cdot W}{V \cdot F}}$$

Where:
- $E$ = fractional BOD removal efficiency
- $W$ = BOD loading (lb/day)
- $V$ = filter volume (acre·ft)
- $F$ = recirculation factor

$$F = \frac{1 + R}{(1 + 0.1 R)^2}$$

Where $R$ = recirculation ratio.

**NCVSS / Normal Equation Approach:**
A more generalised form, often called the *modified Velz* or *Schulze equation*, expresses effluent quality as:

$$\displaystyle \frac{S_e}{S_0} = \exp\!\left(\frac{-k \cdot D \cdot a^{m}}{Q^{n}}\right)$$

Where $a$ = specific surface area (m²/m³), $m$ = surface-area exponent (~0.5–0.67).

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Velz Equation | Eckenfelder Equation |
|---------|---------------|----------------------|
| Primary variable | Filter depth $D$ | Contact time $t$ |
| Flow effect | $Q^n$ correction | Often embedded in $t$ |
| Recirculation | Separate variant | Built-in via $t$ |
| Empirical basis | Pilot data 1948 | Pilot data 1961 |
| Computational ease | Direct | Requires $t$ calculation |
| Use today | Common in design | Common in MBBR/RBC |

---

**(b)** Design a **two-stage high-rate trickling filter** to treat $Q = 20{,}000$ m³/d with $S_0 = 400$ mg/L BOD to meet a final effluent of $S_e = 30$ mg/L. Assume each filter stage has depth 2 m and $k = 2.0$ d⁻¹, $n = 0.6$. Total recirculation = 100% ($R = 1$, $S_R \approx 100$ mg/L average). Determine the **required area per stage**. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Effective substrate entering each filter (with recirculation):**
$$\displaystyle S_{in} = \frac{S_0 + R \cdot S_R}{1 + R} = \frac{400 + 1 \times 100}{2} = 250 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Target per stage (assume equal split — total effluent 30 mg/L, so each stage removes half on log scale):**
For two identical stages in series, we can design each to achieve $S = \sqrt{30 \times 250} = 86.6$ mg/L *as exit of first stage*, then 30 mg/L as exit of second. But this assumes no recirculation within stages; we simplify by setting per-stage target $S_{e1} = 86.6$ mg/L.

**Apply Velz to first stage:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{86.6}{250} &= \exp\!\left(-\frac{2.0 \times 2.0}{Q^{n}}\right) \\[4pt]
\ln(0.3464) &= -\frac{4.0}{Q^{n}} \\[4pt]
-1.0596 &= -\frac{4.0}{Q^{n}} \\[4pt]
Q^{n} &= \frac{4.0}{1.0596} = 3.775
\end{aligned}$$

**Solve for $Q/A$:**
$$\frac{Q}{A} = Q^{1/n} = 3.775^{1/0.6} = 3.775^{1.667}$$

Computing:
$$\begin{aligned}
\ln(3.775) &= 1.3287 \\[4pt]
1.667 \times 1.3287 &= 2.215 \\[4pt]
e^{2.215} &= 9.16
\end{aligned}$$

So:
$$\frac{Q}{A} = 9.16 \Rightarrow A = \frac{Q}{9.16} = \frac{20{,}000}{9.16} \approx 2{,}183 \text{ m}^2 \text{ per stage}$$

**Verification of OLR for first stage:**
$$\begin{aligned}
V &= A \cdot H = 2{,}183 \times 2 = 4{,}366 \text{ m}^3 \\[4pt]
OLR &= \frac{20{,}000 \times 0.250}{4{,}366} = 1.145 \text{ kg BOD/m}^3 \cdot \text{d}
\end{aligned}$$

This falls within the **high-rate range (0.24–0.96)** boundary, slightly above it — design is on the conservative edge.

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q B):**  
> 1. **Recirculation arithmetic** — Many students forget to compute $S_{in}$ via the weighted average $\frac{S_0 + R \cdot S_R}{1+R}$. Plugging in $S_0 = 400$ directly would massively over-design the filter.  
> 2. **Stage-in-series log-mean split** — Two stages don't split the *removal* evenly (50/50); they split the *log-removal* evenly. Hence the use of $\sqrt{S_e \cdot S_{in}}$ as per-stage intermediate target.  
> 3. **Exponent inversion trap** — When solving for $Q/A$, students write $Q^{n}$ as the HLR directly. The correct path is: find $Q^{n}$, then raise to power $1/n$.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Things You MUST Remember Before the Exam**

- ✅ Trickling filter = **attached-growth biofilm reactor**; rock/plastic media; gravity-driven.
- ✅ Five components: **Distributor, Media, Biofilm, Underdrain, Ventilation.**
- ✅ BOD removal mechanism: **Adsorption → Diffusion → Aerobic Oxidation → Sloughing → Humus Separation.**
- ✅ Microbial strata: **Aerobic outer → Facultative middle → Anaerobic inner.**
- ✅ HLR units: **m³/m²·d**; OLR units: **kg BOD/m³·d**. Never confuse them.
- ✅ Standard rate: OLR 0.08–0.16; High rate: OLR 0.24–0.96; Roughing: OLR 0.96–4.8.
- ✅ Velz equation: $\frac{S_e}{S_0} = \exp\left(-\frac{kD}{Q^{n}}\right)$ with $n \approx 0.5$–$0.67$.
- ✅ Eckenfelder: $\frac{S_e - S_R}{S_0 - S_R} = \exp\left(-\frac{kD}{Q^{n}}\right)$ for recirculated systems.
- ✅ Recirculation ratio $R = Q_R/Q$. Use weighted average for $S_{in}$ in design.
- ✅ Distributor rotates at **0.5–2 rpm**; hydraulic reaction drives rotation.
- ✅ DO in filter bed ≥ **2 mg/L** maintained by **natural draft ventilation**.
- ✅ Effluent contains humus sludge → removed in secondary clarifier.
- ✅ Filter depth typical: **1.8–3.0 m**; Plastic media surface area: **100–220 m²/m³**.
- ✅ Comparison with ASP: TF saves energy; ASP gives better effluent quality; TF has lower sludge yield.
- ✅ Sloughing is **self-regulatory** — biofilm thickness auto-balanced by hydraulic shear and predation.
- ✅ For two-stage design, target per-stage intermediate BOD = $\sqrt{S_e \times S_{in}}$ (log-mean split).
- ✅ Convert BOD mg/L → kg/m³ by multiplying by **10⁻³**.

> [!TIP]  
> **Last-Minute Formula Mnemonic — "VELD":**  
> **V**elz **E**ckenfelder **L**ogarithmic **D**ecay — all four share the same exponential skeleton $\exp(-k \cdot \text{something})$. Just remember what goes inside the bracket: depth, time, or $D/Q^n$.

---

> **End of Module 2 — Trickling Filters | KTU 2024 Scheme | OEBBT725**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: UASB and RBC Anaerobic Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** UASB and RBC Anaerobic Filters # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB) Reactor ### Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **UASB Reactor Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** > The **Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB)** reactor is a high-rate, suspended-growth anaerobic treatment system in which wastewater is introduced at the bottom of the reactor and flows upward through a dense, granular, biologically active sludge blanket. The process couples **biological conversion** (anaerobic digestion) with **internal physical separation** (gas-solids-liquid separation via a three-phase separator) in a single vessel, eliminating the need for an external clarifier. The UASB reactor was developed by **Dr. Gatze Lettinga** and colleagues at Wageningen University, Netherlands, in the late 1970s, and is now considered one of the most widely applied high-rate anaerobic technologies worldwide for high-strength industrial and municipal wastewater. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of the UASB reactor as a "biological volcano in reverse":** > Imagine a tall vertical cylindrical vessel filled with sand-like pellets (these are the **anaerobic granules** — dense microbial communities bound together). When dirty, smelly wastewater is pumped in from the **bottom**, it slowly percolates **upward** through this dense bed, just like water rising through wet sand. The microbes (invisible workers) attached to these granules "eat" the organic pollutants (food) and release **biogas** (methane + CO₂) as a metabolic by-product. The biogas bubbles rise, stirring the bed gently (like boiling water), and at the **top**, a clever internal hood catches the bubbles and the floating sludge, returning the solids back down. Clean water leaves from the top. Thus, a single tank acts as a digester, settler, and gas collector simultaneously. ### Key Design Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard UASB Design Metrics (Bolded for High Recall):** > - **Upflow Velocity (V_up):** **0.5 to 1.0 m/h** (typical) — up to **3.0 m/h** for granular sludge. > - **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** **4 to 12 hours** (for high-strength wastewater) — can be as low as **2 hours**. > - **Organic Loading Rate (OLR):** **5 to 15 kg COD/m³·day** (can reach **30–50 kg COD/m³·day** with granular sludge). > - **COD Removal Efficiency:** **60% to 95%** depending on wastewater characteristics. > - **Operating Temperature:** > - Mesophilic: **30 °C to 38 °C** (optimum **35 °C**) > - Thermophilic: **50 °C to 55 °C** > - **Biogas Yield:** **0.30 to 0.50 m³ CH₄ per kg COD removed** (theoretical maximum ≈ **0.35 Nm³ CH₄/kg COD** at STP). > - **Reactor Height-to-Diameter Ratio (H/D):** Generally between **0.2 and 0.5** for industrial scale. --- ## 1.2 Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC) Anaerobic Filter ### Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **RBC Anaerobic Filter Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** > The **Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC)** is an **attached-growth (biofilm)** biological treatment system in which a series of closely spaced, circular discs mounted on a horizontal shaft rotate slowly through the wastewater. A biofilm develops on the disc surfaces, and the rotation alternately exposes the biomass to wastewater (submerged) and to air (atmospheric oxygen for aerobic RBCs) or maintains contact under submerged/anaerobic conditions (anaerobic variant). The **Anaerobic Filter (AF)** is a related upflow packed-bed or biofilm reactor filled with support media (stones, plastic rings) on which anaerobic biomass attaches, retaining biomass without reliance on granulation. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **RBC Analogy — "The Washing Line of Microbes":** > Picture a clothesline with many circular "rotating plates" attached, half-immersed in a tank of dirty water. Slowly, a moss-like layer (the **biofilm**) grows on these plates. As the plates rotate, every revolution: > 1. **Dips** the moss into the wastewater → microbes absorb organic food (BOD/COD). > 2. **Lifts** the moss into the air → for aerobic systems, oxygen is absorbed; for anaerobic variants, gas exchange and moisture draining occur. > This is essentially a **slow conveyor belt of microbes** that continuously alternates between feeding and aeration/dewatering. The slow rotation (**1–2 rpm**) is crucial — too fast would shear off the biofilm. > > **Anaerobic Filter Analogy — "The Clogged Stone Fountain":** > An anaerobic filter is like a vertical column packed with porous stones or plastic media. As wastewater flows upward (or downward) through the stones, anaerobic microbes coat every stone surface. The stones act as both **microbial home** and **mechanical filter**, trapping suspended solids. Because the microbes are stuck in place (attached), the reactor can operate at much higher loading rates than suspended-growth systems. ### Key Design Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard RBC Design Metrics (Bolded):** > - **Disc Rotational Speed:** **1 to 2 rpm** (typical); up to **5 rpm** for high-strength waste. > - **Disc Diameter:** Typically **1.0 m to 3.6 m**. > - **Disc Spacing:** **20 mm to 40 mm**. > - **Submergence:** **30% to 40%** of disc area submerged. > - **Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR):** **0.04 to 0.16 m³/m²·day**. > - **Organic Loading Rate (OLR):** **4 to 12 g BOD/m²·day** (aerobic RBC). > - **Number of Stages:** Typically **3 to 5** in series for staged BOD removal (carbonaceous → nitrification). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Anaerobic Filter Design Metrics (Bolded):** > - **HRT:** **24 to 96 hours** (often **1 to 5 days**). > - **OLR:** **3 to 16 kg COD/m³·day**. > - **COD Removal Efficiency:** **60% to 90%**. > - **Upflow Velocity:** **< 2 m/h** to prevent media washout and biomass loss. > - **Support Media:** Raschig rings, Pall rings, plastic modular media (specific surface area **100–200 m²/m³**). ### Comparative Snapshot (Quick Reference Table) > [!NOTE] > | Feature | UASB Reactor | RBC Anaerobic Filter | > | --- | --- | --- | > | Biomass form | Suspended granules | Attached biofilm | > | Flow direction | Upflow | Rotation through batch contact | > | HRT | 4–12 h | Hours to days (filter) | > | Energy input | Low (gravity-driven) | Moderate (motor rotation) | > | Footprint | Compact vertical | Larger horizontal area | > | Sludge yield | Very low | Low | > | Biogas recovery | Yes (internal) | Limited | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 UASB Reactor — Detailed Operating Principle The UASB reactor integrates **four physical-chemical phenomena** in a single vessel: 1. **Even Distribution of Influent:** Through a specialized influent distribution system at the base, ensuring uniform upflow across the entire reactor cross-section. 2. **Sludge-Wastewater Contact:** Wastewater ascends through a dense bed of **granular sludge** (mean diameter **1–3 mm**, settling velocity **50–100 m/h**) where anaerobic microorganisms degrade organic matter. 3. **Biogas Collection:** Methane (CH₄), carbon dioxide (CO₂), and trace H₂S rise and are captured. 4. **Three-Phase Separation:** A bell-shaped **Gas-Solids Separator (GSS)** at the top deflects rising gas bubbles; gas is collected in a dome; solids slide down internal walls back to the sludge bed; clarified effluent overflows via weirs. ### Three Functional Zones Inside a UASB Reactor > [!NOTE] > **Zone Architecture of a UASB Reactor (Top to Bottom):** > - **Zone 1 — Effluent Zone (Clear Supernatant):** Above the GSS; low SS concentration (< **200 mg/L**). > - **Zone 2 — Sludge Blanket Zone:** Suspended/flocculent sludge layer (SS = **5,000–15,000 mg/L**). > - **Zone 3 — Sludge Bed Zone:** Dense, granular bed (SS = **50,000–100,000 mg/L**); this is where the **bulk of biological activity** occurs. ### The 'Why' Behind Each Step - **Why distribute influent at the bottom?** To prevent channeling and dead zones; ensures every granule contacts wastewater. - **Why use granular sludge?** Granules have excellent settling properties and high biomass concentration → smaller reactor volume. - **Why internal GSS?** Avoids washout of biomass → no external settler needed → simpler operation. - **Why methanogenic archaea thrive here?** The upflow pattern and dense sludge create ideal **anaerobic microenvironment** with low redox potential (Eh < **−200 mV**). ### Microbiology Cascade (KTU-Favored Concept) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Anaerobic Digestion in Four Microbial Trophs:** > 1. **Hydrolysis** — Complex polymers (proteins, carbohydrates, lipids) → monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids). > 2. **Acidogenesis** — Monomers → Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs) + H₂ + CO₂. > 3. **Acetogenesis** — VFAs → Acetate + H₂ + CO₂. > 4. **Methanogenesis** — Acetate → CH₄ (≈ **70%** of methane); H₂ + CO₂ → CH₄ (≈ **30%** of methane). --- ## 2.2 Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC) — Detailed Operating Principle ### Mechanism in Four Steps - **Biofilm Attachment:** Microbes colonize the disc surfaces, forming a **biofilm** (thickness **0.1–3 mm**). - **Rotation Cycle:** At **1–2 rpm**, each disc completes a revolution every **30–60 seconds**. - **Mass Transfer:** During submersion, organic substrates diffuse into the biofilm; during air exposure, oxygen (for aerobic RBCs) or gas exchange occurs. - **Sloughing:** Excess biofilm detaches naturally due to shear stress and is carried out with effluent. ### Why Staging Matters > [!NOTE] > **KTU Concept:** RBCs are typically divided into **stages** because: > - **Stage 1** handles the highest organic load (carbonaceous BOD removal; heterotrophic bacteria dominate). > - **Middle Stages** transition to lower substrate levels. > - **Final Stages** support nitrification (autotrophic *Nitrosomonas* and *Nitrobacter* genera). > - Staging prevents **bulking** and provides ecological niches for different microbial communities. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet** > > | Formula Description | Equation | Units | Notes | > | --- | --- | --- | --- | > | Hydraulic Retention Time | $HRT = V \, / \, Q$ | hours (h) | $V$ = reactor volume, $Q$ = flow rate | > | Organic Loading Rate (UASB) | $OLR = (Q \cdot S_0) \, / \, V$ | kg COD/m³·day | $S_0$ = influent COD | > | Upflow Velocity | $V_{up} = Q \, / \, A$ | m/h | $A$ = cross-sectional area | > | COD Removal Efficiency | $\eta = (S_0 - S_e) \, / \, S_0 \times 100$ | % | $S_e$ = effluent COD | > | Methane Yield (Stoichiometric) | $Y_{CH_4} = 0.35 \cdot (S_0 - S_e) \cdot Q$ | Nm³ CH₄/day | At STP (0 °C, 1 atm) | > | Surface Loading Rate (RBC) | $SLR = Q \, / \, A_{disc}$ | m³/m²·day | $A_{disc}$ = total disc area | > | Organic Loading Rate (RBC) | $OLR_{RBC} = (Q \cdot S_0) \, / \, A_{disc}$ | g BOD/m²·day | Per unit disc area | > | Specific Surface Area (Media) | $SSA = A_{media} \, / \, V_{reactor}$ | m²/m³ | For anaerobic filters | > | Biomass Volumetric Productivity | $P_{vol} = (Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e)) \, / \, V$ | kg COD/m³·day | Space-time yield | > | F/M Ratio (Analogous for Attached) | $F/M = (Q \cdot S_0) \, / \, (M \cdot V)$ | day⁻¹ | $M$ = biomass concentration | > | Biogas Production Rate | $Q_{gas} = Y_{gas} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e)$ | m³/day | $Y_{gas}$ ≈ 0.4–0.5 m³/kg COD | > | Reynolds Number (for biofilm shear) | $Re = \rho v L \, / \, \mu$ | dimensionless | For biofilm sloughing analysis | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields > [!NOTE] > **Why These Reactors Matter (Industry Perspective):** > - **UASB** dominates **brewery, dairy, distillery, pulp & paper, sugar, food-processing, and slaughterhouse** wastewater treatment globally (especially in tropical countries like India, Brazil, and Colombia). Pre-treatment for municipal sewage in warm climates. > - **RBC** is widely used for **small communities, decentralized systems, package plants, landfill leachate, and pre-denitrification** in municipal STP/ETP setups. Highly favored where low energy input and operator skill are key constraints. > - **Anaerobic Filters** are particularly suitable for **dissolved/colloidal organic waste** where granulation is poor; common in **chemical, pharmaceutical, and textile** effluents. > - Both produce **biogas** (renewable energy) and **minimize sludge disposal costs** — aligned with **Net-Zero** and **circular economy** objectives under India's National Mission on Advanced Wastewater Treatment. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — UASB Reactor Volume from Organic Loading Rate **Problem Setup:** Design a UASB reactor for a dairy industry wastewater stream with the following given data: - Influent flow rate, $Q$ = **1,000 m³/day** - Influent COD concentration, $S_0$ = **6,000 mg/L** - Target effluent COD, $S_e$ = **600 mg/L** (90% removal) - Design OLR = **12 kg COD/m³·day** **Find:** Required reactor volume $V$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Express the Organic Loading Rate formula: $$ OLR = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V} $$ **Step 2:** Convert influent COD from mg/L to kg/m³ (since 1 mg/L = 0.001 kg/m³): $$ S_0 = 6{,}000 \, \text{mg/L} = 6{,}000 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kg/L} = 6.0 \, \text{kg/m}^3 $$ **Step 3:** Substitute into the OLR formula and solve for $V$: $$ V = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{OLR} $$ $$ V = \frac{1{,}000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day} \times 6.0 \, \text{kg/m}^3}{12 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \cdot \text{day}} $$ **Step 4:** Carry out the arithmetic: $$ V = \frac{6{,}000 \, \text{kg/day}}{12 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \cdot \text{day}} $$ $$ V = 500 \, \text{m}^3 $$ **Step 5:** State the result with units and a sanity check: > The required UASB reactor volume is **500 m³**. For verification: HRT = V/Q = 500/1,000 = **0.5 day = 12 hours**, which falls within the typical UASB design range of **4–12 hours**. ✓ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Methane Gas Yield Estimation **Problem Setup:** Continuing from Derivation 1, estimate the daily methane gas production. **Given:** - COD removed per day = $Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) = 1{,}000 \times (6.0 - 0.6) = 5{,}400$ kg COD/day - Methane yield factor, $Y_{CH_4}$ = **0.35 Nm³ CH₄/kg COD removed** (stoichiometric at STP) **Find:** Daily methane volume $V_{CH_4}$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Compute COD removed per day: $$ \Delta S = S_0 - S_e = 6.0 - 0.6 = 5.4 \, \text{kg/m}^3 $$ $$ \text{COD removed/day} = Q \cdot \Delta S = 1{,}000 \times 5.4 = 5{,}400 \, \text{kg COD/day} $$ **Step 2:** Apply the methane yield formula: $$ V_{CH_4} = Y_{CH_4} \cdot (Q \cdot \Delta S) $$ $$ V_{CH_4} = 0.35 \, \frac{\text{Nm}^3}{\text{kg}} \times 5{,}400 \, \text{kg/day} $$ **Step 3:** Multiply: $$ V_{CH_4} = 1{,}890 \, \text{Nm}^3/\text{day} $$ **Step 4:** Convert to energy equivalent (Methane LHV ≈ 35.8 MJ/Nm³): $$ \text{Energy} = 1{,}890 \times 35.8 = 67{,}662 \, \text{MJ/day} \approx 67.66 \, \text{GJ/day} $$ $$ \text{Equivalent power} = \frac{67{,}662}{86{,}400 \, \text{s}} \approx 0.78 \, \text{MW}_{th} $$ > **Conclusion:** The UASB reactor at this dairy plant recovers approximately **1,890 Nm³ CH₄/day** — enough to displace natural gas imports and offset a portion of plant thermal energy demand. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — RBC Disc Area Calculation **Problem Setup:** Design an RBC for the following scenario: - Influent flow $Q$ = **500 m³/day** - Influent BOD, $S_0$ = **300 mg/L** - Target OLR = **8 g BOD/m²·day** **Find:** Required total disc surface area $A_{disc}$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Convert units of influent BOD: $$ S_0 = 300 \, \text{mg/L} = 0.300 \, \text{g/L} = 0.300 \, \text{kg/m}^3 = 300 \, \text{g/m}^3 $$ **Step 2:** Convert daily BOD load: $$ \text{BOD load} = Q \cdot S_0 = 500 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day} \times 300 \, \text{g/m}^3 = 150{,}000 \, \text{g BOD/day} $$ **Step 3:** Apply OLR formula for RBC: $$ OLR_{RBC} = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{A_{disc}} $$ $$ A_{disc} = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{OLR_{RBC}} = \frac{150{,}000 \, \text{g/day}}{8 \, \text{g/m}^2 \cdot \text{day}} $$ **Step 4:** Compute: $$ A_{disc} = 18{,}750 \, \text{m}^2 $$ **Step 5:** Convert to standard disc modules. Each disc of **3 m diameter** has area ≈ **28.3 m²** (both faces). Each shaft typically holds **30–100 discs**. $$ \text{Number of discs} = \frac{18{,}750}{28.3} \approx 663 \, \text{discs} $$ > **Conclusion:** Approximately **663 discs** of **3 m diameter** are required, organized into roughly **7 to 10 shafts** with **80–100 discs each** arranged in **3–4 stages**. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance > [!NOTE] > **Performance Comparison Matrix (UASB vs RBC vs Anaerobic Filter)** > > | Parameter | UASB | RBC | Anaerobic Filter | > | --- | --- | --- | --- | > | OLR range | 5–30 kg COD/m³·day | 4–12 g BOD/m²·day | 3–16 kg COD/m³·day | > | HRT range | 4–12 h | n/a (kinetic) | 24–96 h | > | COD removal | 60–95 % | 70–95 % | 60–90 % | > | Biomass retention | Granular | Biofilm on discs | Biofilm on media | > | Energy requirement | Very low | Moderate | Low | > | Biogas recovery | High (internal) | Negligible | Moderate | > | Footprint | Vertical, compact | Horizontal, large | Vertical, compact | > | Best suited waste | Soluble, high-strength | Variable, low-medium | Soluble + colloidal | > | Sensitivity to toxicants | High (granule disruption) | Moderate | Moderate | > | Capital cost | Medium-High | High | Medium | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 UASB Reactor — Internal Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD A[Influent Wastewater
Q in m3 per day] --> B[Inlet Distribution
System] B --> C[Sludge Bed Zone
Dense Granular Sludge
SS 50000 to 100000 mg per L] C --> D[Sludge Blanket Zone
Flocculent Sludge
SS 5000 to 15000 mg per L] D --> E[Three Phase Separator
GSS Internal Hood] E -->|Gas| F[Biogas Collection Dome
CH4 plus CO2] E -->|Liquid| G[Effluent Weir System] E -->|Solids| C F --> H[Gas Metering
and Utilization] G --> I[Treated Effluent
SS less than 200 mg per L] C --> J[Excess Sludge
Withdrawal] ``` ## 4.2 RBC Anaerobic System — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid graph LR A[Influent
Wastewater] --> B[Primary
Sedimentation] B --> C[RBC Stage 1
Carbonaceous BOD Removal
High OLR Zone] C --> D[RBC Stage 2
Intermediate Treatment
BOD Reduction] D --> E[RBC Stage 3
Polishing Stage

Clarifier] F --> G[Disinfected
Effluent] F --> H[Return Sludge
or Wasted] C -.Biofilm Growth.-> I[Disc 1 Biofilm
Heterotrophs] D -.Biofilm Growth.-> J[Disc 2 Biofilm

1 to 2 rpm] -.Drives Shaft.-> C L -.Drives Shaft.-> D L -.Drives Shaft.-> E ``` ## 4.3 Anaerobic Filter — Upflow Packed-Bed Configuration ```mermaid graph TD A[Influent
Wastewater
Q m3 per day] --> B[Flow Distributor
at Reactor Base] B --> C[Packed Media Zone
Plastic Rings or Stones

Attached Anaerobic Biomass] D --> E[Biogas Collection
at Reactor Top] E --> F[Gas Holder
and Utilization] E --> G[Effluent Outlet

Periodic Withdrawal] ``` ## 4.4 Integrated Process Flow — Dairy Wastewater Treatment Plant ```mermaid graph TD A[Raw Dairy
Wastewater] --> B[Equalization Tank
pH and Flow Buffer] B --> C[Screening and
Grit Removal] C --> D[Oil and Grease
Removal via DAF] D --> E[UASB Reactor
Primary Treatment
COD 6000 to 600 mg per L] E -->|Biogas| F[Biogas Holder
and Boiler] E --> G[Activated Sludge
or Trickling Filter
Secondary Treatment] G --> H[Clarifier] H --> I[Tertiary Treatment
Sand Filtration plus UV] I --> J[Disinfected Effluent
Reuse for Landscaping] H --> K[Sludge Thickener
and Digester] K --> L[Sludge Drying Beds] ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: UASB Granular Sludge Properties **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper Q1.2]** > "Explain the characteristics and significance of granular sludge in a UASB reactor." **Model Answer (Concise, KTU-Examiner Style):** Granular sludge is a dense, well-settled agglomerate of anaerobic microorganisms (mean diameter **1–3 mm**) with high methanogenic activity. Its **excellent settling velocity** (**50–100 m/h**) prevents washout and allows very high biomass concentrations (**50,000–100,000 mg/L SS**). The layered granule structure protects methanogens from adverse conditions and enables high organic loading rates (**10–30 kg COD/m³·day**). Granule formation is a critical selection criterion for stable UASB operation. [3 marks] --- ### Question 2: RBC vs Trickling Filter **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1.4]** > "Differentiate between a Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC) and a Trickling Filter with respect to energy input and biomass control." **Model Answer (Concise):** | Feature | RBC | Trickling Filter | | --- | --- | --- | | Energy | Mechanical rotation (**1–2 rpm**) | Natural draft + distributor motor | | Biomass | Controlled sloughing via rotation | Passive sloughing by hydraulic wash | | Operator control | High (rotation speed adjustable) | Limited | RBCs offer **better process control** and lower footprint, while trickling filters are simpler but more prone to clogging. [3 marks] --- ## Part B Question A (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A — UASB Reactor Design for Industrial Effluent **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, End Semester Model Q2.1]** A distillery industry generates wastewater with the following characteristics: - Flow rate, $Q$ = **2,500 m³/day** - Influent COD, $S_0$ = **8,500 mg/L** - Target COD removal efficiency = **90%** - Design OLR = **15 kg COD/m³·day** - Methane yield factor = **0.35 Nm³ CH₄/kg COD removed** ### Part (a) — Design the UASB Reactor Volume and Check HRT [7 Marks] **Step 1:** Convert COD to kg/m³: $$ S_0 = 8{,}500 \, \text{mg/L} = 8.5 \, \text{kg/m}^3 $$ **Step 2:** Apply OLR formula: $$ V = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{OLR} = \frac{2{,}500 \times 8.5}{15} = \frac{21{,}250}{15} = 1{,}416.7 \, \text{m}^3 $$ **Step 3:** Compute HRT: $$ HRT = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{1{,}416.7}{2{,}500} = 0.567 \, \text{days} \approx 13.6 \, \text{hours} $$ **Step 4:** Verify against typical UASB HRT range (**4–12 h**). HRT is slightly above; recommend HRT adjustment with **V = 1,250 m³** → HRT = 12 h. > **Result:** Recommended reactor volume = **1,250 m³**; HRT = **12 hours**. ✓ ### Part (b) — Estimate Daily Methane Production and Effluent Quality [7 Marks] **Step 1:** Effluent COD: $$ S_e = S_0 \cdot (1 - \eta) = 8.5 \times 0.10 = 0.85 \, \text{kg/m}^3 = 850 \, \text{mg/L} $$ **Step 2:** COD removed per day: $$ \Delta S \cdot Q = (8.5 - 0.85) \times 2{,}500 = 7.65 \times 2{,}500 = 19{,}125 \, \text{kg COD/day} $$ **Step 3:** Methane volume: $$ V_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times 19{,}125 = 6{,}693.75 \, \text{Nm}^3/\text{day} $$ **Step 4:** Energy equivalent (LHV = 35.8 MJ/Nm³): $$ \text{Energy} = 6{,}693.75 \times 35.8 = 239{,}636 \, \text{MJ/day} \approx 67 \, \text{MWh}_{th}/\text{day} $$ > **Final Answer:** **6,693.75 Nm³ CH₄/day** with effluent COD = **850 mg/L**. Energy recovery ≈ **67 MWh thermal/day**. --- ## Part B Question B (14 Marks Alternative Choice) ### Question B — RBC Design for Municipal STP **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, End Semester Model Q2.2 Alternative]** A municipal STP receives: - Flow $Q$ = **2,000 m³/day** - Influent BOD, $S_0$ = **250 mg/L** - Required BOD removal = **85%** - Design OLR for RBC = **10 g BOD/m²·day** - Standard disc diameter = **3 m** ### Part (a) — Calculate Total Disc Area Required [7 Marks] **Step 1:** Convert BOD: $$ S_0 = 250 \, \text{mg/L} = 250 \, \text{g/m}^3 $$ **Step 2:** Daily BOD load: $$ \text{BOD load} = Q \cdot S_0 = 2{,}000 \times 250 = 500{,}000 \, \text{g BOD/day} $$ **Step 3:** Disc area required: $$ A_{disc} = \frac{500{,}000}{10} = 50{,}000 \, \text{m}^2 $$ ### Part (b) — Determine Number of Discs and Stages [7 Marks] **Step 1:** Area of one 3-m disc (both faces): $$ A_{one} = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot (1.5)^2 = 14.14 \, \text{m}^2 $$ **Step 2:** Total discs: $$ N = \frac{50{,}000}{14.14} \approx 3{,}536 \, \text{discs} $$ **Step 3:** Recommend 4 shafts × 900 discs each. Provide 3 stages: - Stage 1: 1,200 discs (40% area) - Stage 2: 1,200 discs (40% area) - Stage 3: 1,136 discs (20% area, for nitrification) > **Final Answer:** **50,000 m²** total disc area; **3,536 discs** in **3 stages**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Loss Traps:** > - **Unit conversions (mg/L to kg/m³):** Forgetting to multiply by **10⁻³** when working with kg units — costs **1 to 2 marks** consistently. > - **OLR formula misuse:** Mixing up **volumetric OLR** (kg/m³·day) used for UASB and **areal OLR** (g/m²·day) used for RBC — wrong formula = **2 marks lost**. > - **HRT vs SRT confusion:** UASB HRT is short, but SRT is very long (granule retention); examiners want you to distinguish these. > - **Skipping unit steps:** Always state the final value with units (m³, m², Nm³/day, etc.) — missing units = **½ to 1 mark deducted**. > - **Forgetting stage design justification for RBC:** Without explaining *why* multiple stages, full 7 marks for design part is rarely awarded. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — UASB & RBC Anaerobic Filters** > > - **UASB = Upflow + Granular Sludge Blanket + Internal 3-Phase Separator** — three functions in one vessel. > - **Key UASB Design Metrics:** OLR = **5–15 kg COD/m³·day** (granular); HRT = **4–12 h**; V_up = **0.5–1.0 m/h**. > - **Three Zones (Top → Bottom):** Effluent zone → Sludge blanket → Sludge bed. > - **Anaerobic Digestion Cascade:** Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis. > - **Methane Yield:** Theoretical max = **0.35 Nm³ CH₄/kg COD**; real = **0.30–0.35**. > - **UASB Best For:** Soluble, high-strength, warm wastewater (dairy, brewery, distillery, sugar). > - **RBC = Rotating Discs + Biofilm + 1–2 rpm rotation** — alternating air/water contact. > - **RBC Design:** OLR = **4–12 g BOD/m²·day**; submergence = **30–40%**; multi-staged for nitrification. > - **Anaerobic Filter = Packed Bed + Attached Biofilm + Upflow/Downflow** — no granulation required. > - **Anaerobic Filter Metrics:** HRT = **24–96 h**; OLR = **3–16 kg COD/m³·day**; SSA = **100–200 m²/m³**. > - **Common Pitfall:** UASB = suspended-growth; RBC & AF = attached-growth — never confuse. > - **Sludge Yield:** Anaerobic systems produce **~5–10%** of aerobic sludge yield — major advantage. > - **Energy Balance:** UASB produces energy (biogas); RBC consumes energy (rotation motor). > - **Always include units** in KTU numerical answers; examiners deduct marks for unit omission. > - **Mention GSS** explicitly when describing UASB — it is the key novelty over conventional anaerobic digesters. > - **Biogas composition (typical):** **CH₄ ≈ 60–70%**, **CO₂ ≈ 30–40%**, **H₂S < 1%**.

topics.title: Low-Cost Wastewater Treatment: Aerated Lagoons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Low-Cost Wastewater Treatment: Aerated Lagoons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

An **Aerated Lagoon** is a suspended-growth biological wastewater treatment system in which oxygen is artificially introduced into the lagoon contents by means of mechanical surface aerators or diffused aeration systems. Functionally, it occupies the design space between a conventional activated sludge process and a stabilization pond, combining the high-rate biological oxidation capability of activated sludge with the simplicity, low operating cost, and minimal sludge handling requirements of a pond system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examinable Form):**
> An aerated lagoon is an earthen or lined basin, typically **2 m to 5 m deep**, in which wastewater is treated biologically by a mixed microbial population maintained in suspension through continuous mechanical aeration, with detention times ranging from **2 to 30 days** depending on the mode of operation (aerobic, facultative, or anaerobic).

## 1.2 Classification of Aerated Lagoons (KTU Syllabus Standard)

| Type | Oxygen Regime | Depth (m) | Detention Time | Typical BOD Removal |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Aerobic (Aero-accelator type)** | Fully aerobic | 2 – 3 | 2 – 7 d | 80 – 95% |
| **Facultative Aerated Lagoon** | Aerobic surface / anaerobic bottom | 2.5 – 5 | 5 – 30 d | 60 – 85% |
| **Anaerobic Aerated Lagoon** | Anaerobic (no O₂, only mixing) | 3 – 5 | 20 – 30 d | 50 – 70% |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mechanical Pond"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:**
> Imagine a large, shallow swimming pool filled with wastewater. If you leave it still, only the top surface touches air, so bacteria work slowly. Now install a giant underwater fan (a **mechanical aerator**). The fan does two jobs at once:
> 1. **Pumps air** into the water, so oxygen dissolves everywhere.
> 2. **Mixes** the entire pool, so every microbe gets food and oxygen.
> 
> The result is a "pond that works like a factory" — you get the cheap construction of a pond and the speed of an activated sludge tank. The only running cost is electricity for the aerator.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Design Metrics (Bolded for KTU Recall)

- **Standard oxygen transfer efficiency (SOTE) of surface aerator:** 1.0 – 2.5 kg O₂/kWh
- **Specific power input (P/V):** 5 – 30 W/m³ for aerobic lagoons
- **Mixing power requirement (minimum):** 2 – 5 W/m³
- **Operating temperature range:** 5 – 35 °C
- **pH range (aerobic):** 6.5 – 8.5
- **Freeboard:** 0.5 – 1.0 m above maximum liquid level
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Treatment — Step-by-Step Logic

The biological removal pathway in an aerated lagoon follows this sequence:

1. **Step 1 — Oxygen Transfer:** Mechanical aerator shears water into droplets, increasing air-water interfacial area. Oxygen diffuses into the bulk liquid following **Two-Film Theory**.
2. **Step 2 — Contact & Adsorption:** Suspended microorganisms (floc) come into contact with dissolved organic matter (substrate) through mixing.
3. **Step 3 — Substrate Assimilation (Aerobic Oxidation):** Bacteria oxidize BOD to CO₂ and H₂O using the simplified Monod-type kinetics:
   
   $$\text{Organic matter} + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Bacteria}} CO_2 + H_2O + \text{New cells}$$

4. **Step 4 — Endogenous Decay & Sludge Accumulation:** Old cells self-oxidize, producing inert residues that settle to the bottom.
5. **Step 5 — Clarification (in facultative mode):** Quiescent zones allow partial settling of suspended solids before effluent discharge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the lagoon is "low cost":**
> No concrete tanks, no return activated sludge (RAS) pumps, no sludge digesters. Earthwork + liner + aerator = complete plant.

## 2.2 Why Aerated Lagoons are Used (Engineering Utility)

- **Utility 1:** Ideal for small communities (population 500 – 50,000) where skilled operators are scarce.
- **Utility 2:** Industrial wastewater from food processing, dairies, distilleries, pulp & paper.
- **Utility 3:** Acts as a **roughing treatment** ahead of polishing ponds or constructed wetlands.
- **Utility 4:** Handles **shock loads** better than activated sludge because of long detention time and large buffer volume.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **directly examinable** in KTU ESE and internal assessments. Memorize units!

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Volume of lagoon** | $V = Q \cdot \theta$ | $V$ in m³, $Q$ in m³/d, $\theta$ in days |
| 2 | **Surface area** | $A = V \cdot H^{-1}$ | $A$ in m², $H$ = depth in m |
| 3 | **Surface overflow rate** | $SOR = Q \cdot A^{-1}$ | m/d |
| 4 | **BOD removal (CSTR, first-order)** | $\dfrac{S}{S_0} = \dfrac{1}{1 + k \cdot \theta}$ | $k$ = 1st order rate constant (d⁻¹) |
| 5 | **BOD removal (with biomass)** | $S = \dfrac{S_0}{1 + k \cdot X \cdot \theta \cdot Y^{-1}}$ | $X$ = MLSS (mg/L), $Y$ = yield coeff. |
| 6 | **Volumetric BOD loading** | $L_v = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | kg BOD/m³·d |
| 7 | **Areal BOD loading** | $L_s = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{A}$ | kg BOD/ha·d |
| 8 | **Oxygen demand (synthesis + endogenous)** | $O_2 = a \cdot L_r + b \cdot X_v$ | $L_r$ = BOD removed (kg/d), $a$ ≈ 0.5, $b$ ≈ 0.06 |
| 9 | **Power requirement** | $P = \dfrac{O_2 \, (\text{kg/d})}{24 \cdot OE \cdot 1.0}$ | $P$ in kW, $OE$ = OTE (kg O₂/kWh) |
| 10 | **Specific power** | $P \cdot V^{-1}$ | W/m³ |
| 11 | **Solids retention time** | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$ | days (rarely controlled in lagoons → ∞) |
| 12 | **Detention time check** | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | days |

> **Note on units in tables:** All vertical bars in formulas (e.g., $\vert V \vert$) are rendered as $\vert \cdot \vert$ via inline LaTeX to prevent table-breaking issues.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Significance

In practice, aerated lagoons are the **default choice for Indian municipalities under AMRUT and SBM schemes** for towns below 1 lakh population. The National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG) also deploys aerated lagoons as a primary treatment step for drains discharging into the river. Their **low energy demand (~0.4–0.6 kWh per m³ treated)** compared to activated sludge (~0.8–1.2 kWh/m³) is a major sustainability advantage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: BOD Removal in a Completely Mixed Aerated Lagoon (First-Order Kinetics)

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

We assume:
- Steady-state, continuous flow
- Complete mixing (CSTR assumption — valid because aerator provides turbulence)
- Microbial concentration $X$ remains approximately constant (excess sludge not removed)
- Substrate (BOD) follows first-order decay

### 3.1.2 Mass Balance Around the Lagoon

**Steady-state mass balance:** (Accumulation = 0) ⇒ (Inflow + Generation = Outflow)

$$
\text{Inflow of BOD} - \text{Outflow of BOD} - \text{BOD consumed} = 0
$$

Substituting in symbols:

$$
Q \cdot S_0 - Q \cdot S - r \cdot V = 0
$$

where the volumetric rate of BOD consumption follows first-order kinetics:

$$
r = k \cdot S
$$

### 3.1.3 Algebraic Manipulation

Substituting $r$ into the balance:

$$
Q \cdot S_0 - Q \cdot S - k \cdot S \cdot V = 0
$$

Factor out $S$:

$$
Q \cdot S_0 = S \, (Q + k \cdot V)
$$

Divide both sides by $(Q + k \cdot V)$:

$$
S = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{Q + k \cdot V}
$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $Q$:

$$
S = \frac{S_0}{1 + k \cdot \dfrac{V}{Q}}
$$

Since $\theta = V / Q$:

$$
\boxed{\,S = \dfrac{S_0}{1 + k \cdot \theta}\,}
$$

Or equivalently, the **fractional removal efficiency**:

$$
\eta = \frac{S_0 - S}{S_0} = \frac{k \cdot \theta}{1 + k \cdot \theta}
$$

### 3.1.4 Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:** $Q = 5000$ m³/d, $S_0 = 300$ mg/L, $k = 0.35$ d⁻¹ (at 25 °C), $\theta = 7$ d.

**Find:** Effluent BOD $S$ and removal efficiency $\eta$.

**Step 1:** Apply the formula.

$$
S = \frac{300}{1 + (0.35)(7)} = \frac{300}{1 + 2.45} = \frac{300}{3.45}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute.

$$
S = 86.96 \, \text{mg/L} \approx 87 \, \text{mg/L}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute removal efficiency.

$$
\eta = \frac{300 - 87}{300} \times 100 = 71.0 \, \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** KTU often gives you $k$ at 20 °C and asks for corrected $k$ at a different temperature using the Arrhenius-van't Hoff relation: $k_{T_2} = k_{T_1} \cdot \theta^{(T_2 - T_1)}$, where $\theta \approx 1.056$ for biological systems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Requirement for Aerator Sizing

### 3.2.1 Oxygen Demand from BOD Removal

Total oxygen demand is the sum of:
- Oxygen for **synthesis** (new cell growth)
- Oxygen for **endogenous respiration** (cell maintenance)

$$
O_2^{\text{total}} = a \cdot L_r + b \cdot X_v \cdot V
$$

where:
- $L_r$ = mass of BOD removed per day = $Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$ in kg/d
- $a$ = oxygen coefficient for synthesis, typically **0.4 – 0.5 kg O₂/kg BOD**
- $b$ = endogenous decay coefficient, typically **0.05 – 0.08 d⁻¹**
- $X_v$ = volatile suspended solids concentration (kg/m³)
- $V$ = lagoon volume (m³)

### 3.2.2 Power Equation

Aerator power is determined from the Oxygen Transfer Efficiency (OTE) of the device:

$$
P = \frac{O_2^{\text{total}}}{24 \cdot \eta_{OTE}}
$$

where $\eta_{OTE}$ is the standard oxygen transfer efficiency in **kg O₂ per kWh** (typical value: **1.2 kg O₂/kWh** for surface aerators).

### 3.2.3 Numerical Illustration

**Given:** $L_r = 1000$ kg/d, $X_v = 0.2$ kg/m³, $V = 30000$ m³, $a = 0.45$, $b = 0.06$ d⁻¹, $\eta_{OTE} = 1.2$ kg O₂/kWh.

**Step 1:** Compute oxygen for synthesis.

$$
O_{2,1} = 0.45 \times 1000 = 450 \, \text{kg O}_2/\text{d}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute oxygen for endogenous respiration.

$$
O_{2,2} = 0.06 \times 0.2 \times 30000 = 360 \, \text{kg O}_2/\text{d}
$$

**Step 3:** Total oxygen demand.

$$
O_2^{\text{total}} = 450 + 360 = 810 \, \text{kg O}_2/\text{d}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to power required.

$$
P = \frac{810}{24 \times 1.2} = \frac{810}{28.8} = 28.13 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 5:** Check specific power.

$$
P/V = 28130 \, \text{W} / 30000 \, \text{m}^3 = 0.94 \, \text{W/m}^3
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Below 5 W/m³ = poor mixing. The lagoon is under-aerated!** The above value (0.94 W/m³) is too low for proper suspension of biomass. KTU expects students to **comment on mixing adequacy** in design problems.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Temperature Correction of Rate Constant

The biological rate constant is highly temperature-dependent. The standard relationship is:

$$
k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T - 20)}
$$

where $\theta = 1.056$ for aerobic systems (commonly used KTU value).

**Derivation of expected removal at any temperature:**

$$
\eta_T = \frac{k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)} \cdot \theta_{\text{det}}}{1 + k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)} \cdot \theta_{\text{det}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategic Hint:** Kerala's ambient temperature is 25 – 32 °C most of the year. So $k$ at 30 °C is nearly **1.3×** the value at 20 °C. This is why aerated lagoons perform *better* in tropical climates — a favorite KTU viva question!
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Aerated Lagoon Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Raw Wastewater Inlet] --> node2[Screening and Grit Removal]
    node2 --> node3[Influent Pumping Station]
    node3 --> node4[Aerated Lagoon Cell 1]
    node4 --> node5[Aerated Lagoon Cell 2]
    node5 --> node6[Aerated Lagoon Cell 3]
    node6 --> node7[Quiescent Settling Zone]
    node7 --> node8[Treated Effluent Outlet]
    node4 --> node9[Surface Mechanical Aerator 1]
    node5 --> node10[Surface Mechanical Aerator 2]
    node6 --> node11[Surface Mechanical Aerator 3]
    node9 --> node12[Atmospheric Oxygen Source]
    node10 --> node12
    node11 --> node12
    node4 --> node13[Sludge Accumulation Zone Bottom]
    node5 --> node13
    node6 --> node13
    node13 --> node14[Periodic Sludge Removal Once in 5 to 10 Years]
    node9 --> node15[Electric Power Supply]
    node10 --> node15
    node11 --> node15
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Three lagoons in series with progressive BOD reduction. Each cell has its own surface aerator. Sludge accumulates at the bottom for years — a key cost-saving feature.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Aerated Lagoon

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Influent BOD So] --> reactorA[Completely Mixed Aerobic Reactor]
    airA[Atmospheric Air] --> aeratorA[Mechanical Surface Aerator]
    aeratorA --> reactorA
    aeratorA --> mixA[Mixing and Suspension of Biomass]
    mixA --> reactorA
    reactorA --> bioA[Biomass X MLSS]
    bioA --> reactorA
    reactorA --> outA[Effluent BOD S]
    bioA --> sludgeA[Sludge Storage Lagoon Bottom]
    outA --> clarA[Optional Polishing Pond or Wetland]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description (For Examiner's Mental Picture)

A **typical aerated lagoon layout** consists of:

- **Earthen basin** with clay or HDPE liner (to prevent groundwater contamination)
- **Dikes** of 1:2 to 1:3 side slopes
- **Aerators** mounted on floats or fixed piers
- **Inlet pipe** discharging near the aerator for rapid mixing
- **Baffle curtains** (optional) to prevent short-circuiting
- **Outlet weir** at the far end, downstream of a quiescent zone
- **Freeboard** of ~0.5 m
- **Sludge storage** at bottom (cleaned once in 5–10 years)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why baffles?** Without baffles, water takes a "shortcut" across the lagoon. With **three or more cells in series**, BOD removal is closer to plug-flow behavior and is much more efficient. This is a common KTU short-note question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] 
**Define an aerated lagoon. Mention any two advantages over a conventional activated sludge process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **aerated lagoon** is a suspended-growth biological treatment system in which oxygen is supplied by mechanical surface aerators or diffused aeration, treating wastewater in an earthen/lined basin of 2–5 m depth with detention times of 2–30 days.
>
> **Two advantages over activated sludge:**
> 1. **Lower capital and operating cost** — no concrete tanks, no RAS pumping, no separate clarifier.
> 2. **Handles shock loads better** — large buffer volume dilutes toxic/inhibitory inflows.
> 3. *(Any other valid point: simpler operation, no skilled manpower needed.)*

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] 
**Compare aerobic and facultative aerated lagoons in terms of detention time, depth, and BOD removal efficiency.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Aerobic Aerated Lagoon | Facultative Aerated Lagoon |
|---------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| Detention time | 2 – 7 days | 5 – 30 days |
| Depth | 2 – 3 m | 2.5 – 5 m |
| BOD removal | 80 – 95% | 60 – 85% |
| Dissolved oxygen | Present throughout | Only in upper aerobic layer |
| Power input (P/V) | 5 – 30 W/m³ | 2 – 10 W/m³ |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

> **(a)** [7 marks] With a neat sketch, describe the working principle and components of an **aerobic aerated lagoon**. List the design parameters you would adopt.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] A municipal wastewater flow of **4000 m³/d** with an influent BOD of **250 mg/L** is to be treated in a completely mixed aerobic aerated lagoon. The first-order BOD removal rate constant at 25 °C is **k = 0.30 d⁻¹**. If a detention time of **6 days** is provided, determine:
> (i) Effluent BOD
> (ii) BOD removal efficiency
> (iii) Total volume and surface area of the lagoon (assume depth = 3 m)

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

- **Working principle (2 marks):** Wastewater enters an earthen basin where mechanical surface aerators continuously supply dissolved oxygen and keep microbial floc in suspension. Bacteria oxidize organic matter (BOD) to CO₂ and H₂O under aerobic conditions.
- **Components (2 marks):** Earthen basin (with liner), inlet works, mechanical aerators (floating or fixed), outlet weir, baffles, sludge storage zone, freeboard.
- **Design parameters (3 marks):**
  - Detention time: 2 – 7 d
  - Depth: 2 – 3 m
  - BOD loading: 0.2 – 0.5 kg BOD/m³·d
  - Power input: 5 – 30 W/m³
  - Temperature correction factor
  - Multiple cells in series (≥3)

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Effluent BOD:**

$$
S = \frac{S_0}{1 + k \cdot \theta} = \frac{250}{1 + (0.30)(6)} = \frac{250}{1 + 1.8} = \frac{250}{2.8}
$$

$$
S = 89.29 \approx 89.3 \, \text{mg/L}
$$

**(ii) BOD removal efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{250 - 89.3}{250} \times 100 = 64.3 \, \%
$$

**(iii) Volume and area:**

$$
V = Q \cdot \theta = 4000 \times 6 = 24000 \, \text{m}^3
$$

$$
A = \frac{V}{H} = \frac{24000}{3} = 8000 \, \text{m}^2
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

> **(a)** [7 marks] Discuss the **types of aerated lagoons** with diagrams and their typical operating conditions. State any **four advantages** and **three limitations** of aerated lagoons.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] An industry discharges **2000 m³/d** of wastewater with BOD **500 mg/L** to a facultative aerated lagoon. The lagoon volume is **20000 m³** and the operating temperature is **30 °C**. The first-order rate constant at 20 °C is **0.20 d⁻¹**. Using the temperature correction factor $\theta = 1.056$, calculate:
> (i) The corrected rate constant at 30 °C
> (ii) The effluent BOD
> (iii) The daily BOD removed in kg/d

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

- **Types (4 marks):** Aerobic, Facultative, Anaerobic — describe each with characteristics (depth, detention, BOD removal, oxygen regime).
- **Advantages (2 marks):** Low cost, simple operation, handles shock loads, no sludge recirculation.
- **Limitations (1 mark):** Large land area, mosquito nuisance, possible odor (in facultative), affected by temperature.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Corrected rate constant at 30 °C:**

$$
k_{30} = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(30-20)} = 0.20 \times (1.056)^{10}
$$

Compute $(1.056)^{10}$:

$$
(1.056)^{10} \approx 1.724
$$

$$
k_{30} = 0.20 \times 1.724 = 0.345 \, \text{d}^{-1}
$$

**(ii) Detention time and effluent BOD:**

$$
\theta = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{20000}{2000} = 10 \, \text{d}
$$

$$
S = \frac{S_0}{1 + k_{30} \cdot \theta} = \frac{500}{1 + (0.345)(10)} = \frac{500}{1 + 3.45} = \frac{500}{4.45}
$$

$$
S = 112.36 \approx 112.4 \, \text{mg/L}
$$

**(iii) Daily BOD removed:**

$$
L_r = Q \cdot (S_0 - S) = 2000 \times (500 - 112.4) \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
L_r = 2000 \times 0.3876 = 775.2 \, \text{kg BOD/d}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting temperature correction** of $k$ — a guaranteed 2-mark deduction if working at 30 °C with $k_{20}$.
> 2. **Mixing up $V = Q\theta$ and $A = Q/H$** — volume uses detention time, area uses volume and depth.
> 3. **Not stating units** in intermediate steps — KTU penalizes unit-less answers heavily.
> 4. **Omitting the "complete mixing" assumption** in CSTR derivation — without it, the formula $S = S_0/(1 + k\theta)$ is invalid.
> 5. **Confusing SRT and HRT** — for aerated lagoons, sludge is not wasted daily, so $\theta_c \to \infty$, but HRT = $\theta$ is finite.
> 6. **Reporting surface area in m² when KTU asks for hectares** — conversion: 1 ha = 10,000 m².

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullets for Last-Minute Revision:**
> 
> ✅ Aerated lagoon = **earthen basin + mechanical aerator + suspended biomass**
> 
> ✅ Three types: **Aerobic (2–7 d), Facultative (5–30 d), Anaerobic (20–30 d)**
> 
> ✅ Key formula: $S = S_0 / (1 + k\theta)$ — assume **complete mixing**
> 
> ✅ Temperature correction: $k_T = k_{20} \cdot (1.056)^{(T-20)}$
> 
> ✅ Design depth: **2 – 5 m**; Freeboard: **0.5 – 1.0 m**
> 
> ✅ Specific power for mixing: minimum **2 – 5 W/m³**; for aerobic: **5 – 30 W/m³**
> 
> ✅ **No RAS, no separate clarifier, no skilled operator** — main cost savers
> 
> ✅ Volume = $Q \cdot \theta$; Area = $V / H$
> 
> ✅ Oxygen demand = $a L_r + b X_v V$ (synthesis + endogenous)
> 
> ✅ Aerator power = $O_2^{\text{total}} / (24 \cdot \eta_{OTE})$
> 
> ✅ **Use 3 cells in series** to approximate plug-flow and avoid short-circuiting
> 
> ✅ Sludge is **stored in situ** and removed only every 5–10 years
> 
> ✅ **Disadvantages:** Large land area, mosquito breeding, odor in facultative mode
> 
> ✅ Tropical climates (Kerala: 25–32 °C) **enhance** biological kinetics → better performance
> 
> ✅ Most suited for **small communities (< 1 lakh population)** and **industrial wastewater** (dairy, distillery, food processing)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stabilization Ponds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stabilization Ponds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Stabilization Pond** (also called a **Waste Stabilization Pond**, WSP) is a shallow, earthen impoundment designed to treat raw or partially treated wastewater through the synergistic biological action of **bacteria** (which oxidize organic matter) and **algae** (which produce oxygen via photosynthesis). According to the **Manual on Sewerage and Sewage Treatment (CPHEEO, 2013)** and standard KTU 2024 syllabus classification, stabilization ponds are large, shallow basins (typically **1.0 to 2.5 m deep**) in which wastewater is retained for extended periods (**20 to 180 days**) to allow natural biological processes to stabilize organic pollutants.

The three principal classifications recognized by KTU syllabus and CPHEEO are:

| Pond Type | Dominant Zone Biology | Typical Depth (m) | Detention Time (days) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aerobic Pond | Fully aerobic, light penetrates entire depth | 0.3 to 0.5 | 3 to 7 |
| Facultative Pond | Aerobic surface, anaerobic bottom | 1.0 to 2.0 | 7 to 30 |
| Anaerobic Pond | Fully anaerobic | 2.5 to 5.0 | 20 to 50 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Stabilization ponds are assessed as a *low-cost, energy-free, decentralized biological treatment unit*. The examiner expects students to identify pond type, justify design parameters (depth, BOD loading, detention time), and apply the **Marais–Shaw kinetic model** for facultative pond design.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (The "Pond-as-Ecosystem" Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a giant, shallow aquarium sitting in the sun. Sunlight enters the water, microscopic algae floating in the pond behave like underwater solar panels — they use the sun's energy + CO₂ from wastewater to release **oxygen**. Meanwhile, sewage-eating bacteria floating in the same water use this oxygen to "digest" organic dirt in the wastewater, just like aquarium filters work — but using **natural sunlight and wind** instead of electricity. After several weeks, the murky sewage becomes clear, oxygen-rich water. The pond has acted as a *miniature engineered lake ecosystem*.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Surface BOD Loading Rate (L₀):** **100 to 400 kg BOD/ha/day** (facultative ponds)
- **Solar Energy Input:** **16.7 to 25.1 MJ/m²/day** (tropical Kerala conditions)
- **Oxygen Production by Algae:** **0.25 kg O₂/kg algae** (photosynthetic yield)
- **Temperature correction constant (θ):** **1.036 to 1.06** (typically **θ = 1.06** for BOD removal)
- **Standard Design Temperature:** **20 °C** (reference for rate constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Stabilization Ponds Matter for KTU Kerala Context:** Kerala receives **3,000+ hours of sunlight/year** and has an average temperature of **27 °C**, making stabilization ponds one of the most efficient, sustainable, and low-cost treatment options for small towns, hospitals, and rural clusters — directly aligning with the **Sustainable Development Goal (SDG 6)** for clean water.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stepwise Operational Logic of a Facultative Pond

A facultative pond operates in **three stratified layers**, each performing a different biochemical function simultaneously:

1. **Surface Aerobic Zone (top 0.4 to 0.6 m):**
   - Sunlight penetrates completely; algae perform photosynthesis.
   - Bacteria degrade soluble organic matter using dissolved oxygen (DO).
   - Aeration occurs via **wind action, surface re-aeration, and algal O₂ release**.
   - DO concentration: **>2 mg/L** sustained for **>16 hours/day** (CPHEEO criterion).

2. **Intermediate Facultative Zone (middle 0.5 to 0.8 m):**
   - DO fluctuates diurnally; both aerobic and anaerobic decomposition occur.
   - BOD removal is **highest in this zone** (50 to 70 % of total removal).
   - Transition zone for bacterial metabolism.

3. **Bottom Anaerobic Zone (lower 0.3 to 0.5 m):**
   - Sunlight does not penetrate; **settled solids** are anaerobically digested.
   - Produces **CH₄, CO₂, H₂S, and NH₃** (typical anaerobic biogas composition).
   - Accumulates **sludge** that requires removal every **5 to 10 years**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Algal–Bacterial Symbiosis:** Bacteria consume organic BOD and release **CO₂ + nutrients (N, P)**. Algae absorb this CO₂ and nutrients to grow, releasing **O₂** that the bacteria use. This is a **closed-loop ecosystem** requiring **zero external energy** — the foundational engineering principle of all WSPs.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) Module 2. Memorize the constants, units, and applicability conditions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Constants | Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_e = \dfrac{L_i}{1 + k \cdot t}$ | **Marais–Shaw first-order BOD removal equation** | $L_e, L_i$ in mg/L; $t$ in days; $k$ in day⁻¹ | Facultative pond effluent BOD |
| 2 | $k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$ | **Temperature correction for rate constant** | $k_{20}$ at 20 °C; $\theta = 1.056$ (typical) | Adjusts $k$ for field temperature |
| 3 | $L_s = \dfrac{10 \cdot Q \cdot L_i}{A}$ | **Areal BOD loading** | $L_s$ in kg BOD/ha/day; $Q$ in m³/day; $L_i$ in mg/L; $A$ in ha | Surface loading check |
| 4 | $V_s = \dfrac{Q \cdot L_i}{V}$ | **Volumetric BOD loading** | $V_s$ in kg BOD/m³/day; $V$ in m³ | Anaerobic pond design |
| 5 | $t = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{A \cdot d}{Q}$ | **Theoretical detention time (hydraulic)** | $t$ in days; $A$ in m²; $d$ in m; $Q$ in m³/day | All pond types |
| 6 | $A = \dfrac{Q \cdot (L_i - L_e)}{L_s \cdot \eta}$ | **Required pond surface area** | $\eta$ = removal efficiency (decimal); $L_s$ in kg/ha/day | Facultative pond design |
| 7 | $f_{b} = \dfrac{\text{Algal O}_2 \text{ produced}}{\text{Bacterial O}_2 \text{ demand}} \geq 1.0$ | **Oxygen balance ratio (must be $\geq 1$ for healthy pond)** | Dimensionless | Aerobic pond design check |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE:** In KTU answer scripts, always define every variable immediately after writing the equation. Examiners award partial marks for **correctly defined symbols**.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Low-Cost Sanitation for Tier 2/3 Towns:** India has **>7,000 operational WSPs** as per MoUD reports; ideal for Kerala's panchayats.
- **Polishing Treatment:** Used as **tertiary polishing** after activated-sludge plants to remove residual BOD, nutrients, and pathogens.
- **Land-Based Treatment Continuum:** WSPs integrate with **constructed wetlands** and **duckweed ponds** for nutrient recovery.
- **Carbon-Negative Operation:** Unlike activated sludge, ponds produce **biogas (60–70 % CH₄)** in anaerobic cells — recoverable for cooking or power.

### 2.4 The Four Design Parameters a KTU Examiner Expects

1. **BOD Loading (kg BOD/ha/day):** Dictates the **type of pond** (aerobic vs. facultative vs. anaerobic).
2. **Detention Time (t, days):** Dictates the **degree of treatment** and **pond volume**.
3. **Depth (d, m):** Dictates **light penetration** vs. **anaerobic digestion capacity**.
4. **Temperature (T, °C):** Drives **reaction rate constant k**; critical for Kerala's tropical climate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (CPHEEO):** For facultative ponds, surface BOD loading should be $\leq \mathbf{300}$ **kg BOD/ha/day** (at 20 °C). For warm climates (>25 °C), it can be increased to **400 kg/ha/day**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Marais–Shaw BOD Removal Equation (Most Important for KTU 14-Mark Questions)

The Marais–Shaw (1961) model treats the facultative pond as a **completely mixed, single-cell reactor** with first-order reaction kinetics for BOD removal. The derivation proceeds in **four rigorous steps**.

#### Step 1: Mass Balance Setup

Consider a facultative pond of volume $V$ receiving influent BOD concentration $L_i$ (mg/L) and discharging effluent BOD concentration $L_e$ (mg/L). Let the flow rate be $Q$ (m³/day). Apply the **law of conservation of mass** for BOD over a small time interval:

$$
\text{Accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output} - \text{Decay by reaction}
$$

In mathematical form (steady-state condition, so Accumulation = 0):

$$
0 = Q \cdot L_i - Q \cdot L_e - r \cdot V
$$

where $r$ is the rate of BOD removal per unit volume (mg/L/day).

#### Step 2: Apply First-Order Reaction Kinetics

The rate of BOD disappearance is **proportional to the remaining BOD concentration** (typical of biological reactions with substrate as the limiting factor):

$$
r = k \cdot L_e
$$

where $k$ is the **first-order rate constant** (day⁻¹) and $L_e$ is the effluent BOD (the BOD actually present in the reactor).

#### Step 3: Substitute and Simplify

Substituting the kinetic expression into the mass balance:

$$
0 = Q \cdot L_i - Q \cdot L_e - k \cdot L_e \cdot V
$$

Rearranging to isolate $L_e$:

$$
Q \cdot L_i = Q \cdot L_e + k \cdot L_e \cdot V
$$

$$
Q \cdot L_i = L_e \cdot (Q + k \cdot V)
$$

Dividing both sides by $Q$:

$$
L_i = L_e \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{k \cdot V}{Q}\right)
$$

#### Step 4: Recognize Hydraulic Detention Time

The hydraulic detention time is defined as $t = \dfrac{V}{Q}$. Substituting:

$$
L_i = L_e \cdot (1 + k \cdot t)
$$

Solving for the effluent BOD:

$$
\boxed{L_e = \dfrac{L_i}{1 + k \cdot t}}
$$

This is the **Marais–Shaw equation** — the single most examinable formula in Module 2.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style — 14 Mark)

**Problem:** A facultative pond treats domestic wastewater with the following design data:

- Influent flow rate, $Q = 5000$ m³/day
- Influent BOD, $L_i = 250$ mg/L
- Required effluent BOD, $L_e = 30$ mg/L
- Pond temperature, $T = 27$ °C
- Depth, $d = 1.5$ m
- Rate constant at 20 °C, $k_{20} = 0.30$ day⁻¹
- Temperature coefficient, $\theta = 1.056$

**Required:** (a) Detention time and pond volume. (b) Surface area. (c) Check areal BOD loading.

#### Part (a): Detention Time and Pond Volume

Apply the Marais–Shaw equation:

$$
L_e = \dfrac{L_i}{1 + k \cdot t}
$$

Rearranging to solve for $t$:

$$
1 + k \cdot t = \dfrac{L_i}{L_e}
$$

$$
k \cdot t = \dfrac{L_i}{L_e} - 1
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{L_i}{L_e}\right) - 1}{k}
$$

First, **correct the rate constant for temperature**:

$$
k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)} = 0.30 \cdot (1.056)^{(27-20)} = 0.30 \cdot (1.056)^{7}
$$

Compute $(1.056)^7$ step-by-step:

$$
(1.056)^2 = 1.115136
$$

$$
(1.056)^4 = (1.115136)^2 = 1.243528
$$

$$
(1.056)^7 = (1.056)^4 \cdot (1.056)^2 \cdot (1.056)^1 = 1.243528 \cdot 1.115136 \cdot 1.056
$$

$$
1.243528 \cdot 1.115136 = 1.3867
$$

$$
1.3867 \cdot 1.056 = 1.4644
$$

Therefore:

$$
k_{27} = 0.30 \cdot 1.4644 = 0.4393 \; \text{day}^{-1}
$$

Now compute detention time:

$$
t = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{250}{30}\right) - 1}{0.4393} = \dfrac{8.333 - 1}{0.4393} = \dfrac{7.333}{0.4393}
$$

$$
t = 16.69 \; \text{days} \approx 16.7 \; \text{days}
$$

Compute pond volume:

$$
V = Q \cdot t = 5000 \cdot 16.69 = 83{,}450 \; \text{m}^3
$$

#### Part (b): Surface Area

$$
A = \dfrac{V}{d} = \dfrac{83{,}450}{1.5} = 55{,}633.3 \; \text{m}^2 = 5.56 \; \text{ha}
$$

#### Part (c): Areal BOD Loading Check

$$
L_s = \dfrac{10 \cdot Q \cdot L_i}{A}
$$

$$
L_s = \dfrac{10 \cdot 5000 \cdot 250}{55{,}633.3} = \dfrac{12{,}500{,}000}{55{,}633.3}
$$

$$
L_s = 224.7 \; \text{kg BOD/ha/day}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Verification:** Since $L_s = 224.7 < 300$ kg BOD/ha/day (CPHEEO limit for facultative pond at 20 °C), the design is **safe and acceptable**. The pond operates in the correct facultative regime.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Highly Examinable)

| Parameter | Aerobic Pond | Facultative Pond | Anaerobic Pond |
|---|---|---|---|
| Depth (m) | 0.3 to 0.5 | 1.0 to 2.0 | 2.5 to 5.0 |
| Detention Time (days) | 3 to 7 | 7 to 30 | 20 to 50 |
| BOD Loading (kg/ha/day) | 80 to 160 | 100 to 400 | 2000 to 4000 |
| Effluent BOD (mg/L) | 20 to 40 | 30 to 100 | 100 to 300 |
| Algae Present? | Yes (abundant) | Yes (surface) | Negligible |
| Light Penetration | Full depth | Top layer only | Negligible |
| Dominant Organism | Aerobic bacteria + algae | Aerobic + facultative + anaerobic bacteria | Anaerobic bacteria |
| Typical Application | Tertiary polishing | Secondary treatment | Strong industrial waste, pre-treatment |
| BOD Removal Efficiency | 80 to 95 % | 70 to 90 % | 50 to 70 % |
| Sludge Accumulation | Negligible | Moderate (every 5–10 yrs) | High (every 1–3 yrs) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic (for exam recall):** **"AFA"** → **A**erobic (sunlight fully penetrates), **F**acultative (stratified zones), **A**naerobic (sunlight blocked, biogas produced).

### 3.4 Critical Design Checks Students Often Miss

1. **Temperature correction** for $k$: Students often use $k_{20}$ directly at field temperature — KTU examiner **deducts up to 2 marks** for this.
2. **Unit consistency**: $Q$ in m³/day, $L_i$ in mg/L, $A$ in ha → the factor **10** in the areal loading equation converts m³·mg → ha·kg. **Memorize this factor.**
3. **Dimensional analysis check**: Always write units in every line of numerical solution.
