## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
**Define a stabilization pond. List any two advantages of stabilization ponds over conventional activated sludge systems.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> A **stabilization pond** is a shallow, earthen impoundment (depth 1.0 to 2.5 m) in which wastewater is treated biologically by the **symbiotic action of algae and bacteria**, with retention periods of 7 to 180 days.
> 
> **Advantages over activated sludge (any 2):**
> 1. **Zero external energy** requirement — operates on solar and wind energy.
> 2. **No skilled operator** needed — minimal O&M cost.
> 3. **High pathogen removal** (>99.9 % fecal coliform in maturation ponds).
> 4. **Constructed with local earth** — no imported equipment.
> 
> *(Award 1 mark for definition + 0.5 × 2 marks for two distinct advantages.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
**Differentiate between facultative and anaerobic stabilization ponds based on (i) depth, (ii) BOD loading, and (iii) effluent quality.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks — tabular format recommended):**
> 
> | Parameter | Facultative Pond | Anaerobic Pond |
> |---|---|---|
> | Depth | 1.0 to 2.0 m | 2.5 to 5.0 m |
> | BOD Loading | 100 to 400 kg/ha/day | 2000 to 4000 kg/ha/day |
> | Effluent BOD | 30 to 100 mg/L (relatively clear) | 100 to 300 mg/L (requires further treatment) |
> 
> *(Award 1 mark for each correctly identified and contrasted parameter — 3 marks total.)*

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (Module 2):** Students answer **ONE full question** of 14 marks. Sub-parts (a) and (b) carry **7 marks each**.

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q.A.**  
**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **algal–bacterial symbiosis** mechanism in a facultative stabilization pond. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** A facultative pond is to be designed for a small community in Kerala with the following data:  
- Average flow = 4000 m³/day  
- Influent BOD = 280 mg/L  
- Required effluent BOD = 35 mg/L  
- Design temperature = 28 °C  
- Depth of pond = 1.5 m  
- $k_{20} = 0.28$ day⁻¹; $\theta = 1.056$  

**Calculate:** (i) Detention time, (ii) Pond surface area, (iii) Areal BOD loading. Comment on whether the design is safe. **(7 marks)**

##### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Algal–Bacterial Symbiosis (7 marks)

The facultative pond operates as a **natural ecosystem** where two types of microorganisms live in mutual cooperation:

**Step 1 — Bacterial Action (Aerobic Zone):**  
Aerobic bacteria in the upper sunlit layer use **dissolved oxygen (DO)** to metabolize dissolved organic matter (BOD) present in the wastewater. The reaction is:

$$
\text{Organic Matter} + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{bacteria}} CO_2 + H_2O + \text{biomass} + \text{energy}
$$

**Step 2 — Algal Photosynthesis:**  
Algae (e.g., *Chlorella*, *Scenedesmus*) utilize the **CO₂ released by bacteria**, along with sunlight and nutrients (N, P), to produce oxygen through photosynthesis:

$$
6 CO_2 + 6 H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{light + chlorophyll}} C_6 H_{12} O_6 + 6 O_2
$$

**Step 3 — Nutrient Recycling:**  
The CO₂ and mineral nutrients (NH₃, PO₄³⁻) released during bacterial decomposition are the **exact raw materials** needed for algal growth. This forms a **closed-loop, self-sustaining cycle** requiring no mechanical aeration.

**Step 4 — Net Effect:**  
- BOD reduction: 70 to 90 %  
- DO maintained at >2 mg/L during daylight  
- Net ecosystem is **carbon-positive for oxygen** and **carbon-negative for organic pollutants**.

*(Award 2 marks for sketch, 2 marks for equation, 2 marks for closed-loop explanation, 1 mark for net effect statement.)*

##### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Numerical Design (7 marks)

**Given:** $Q = 4000$ m³/day; $L_i = 280$ mg/L; $L_e = 35$ mg/L; $T = 28$ °C; $d = 1.5$ m; $k_{20} = 0.28$ day⁻¹; $\theta = 1.056$.

**Step 1: Temperature correction**

$$
k_{28} = 0.28 \cdot (1.056)^{(28-20)} = 0.28 \cdot (1.056)^{8}
$$

Compute $(1.056)^8$:

$$
(1.056)^2 = 1.115136
$$

$$
(1.056)^4 = (1.115136)^2 = 1.243528
$$

$$
(1.056)^8 = (1.243528)^2 = 1.5463
$$

Therefore:

$$
k_{28} = 0.28 \cdot 1.5463 = 0.4330 \; \text{day}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2: Detention time from Marais–Shaw**

$$
t = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{L_i}{L_e}\right) - 1}{k_{28}} = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{280}{35}\right) - 1}{0.4330} = \dfrac{8 - 1}{0.4330} = \dfrac{7}{0.4330}
$$

$$
t = 16.17 \; \text{days}
$$

**Step 3: Pond volume and surface area**

$$
V = Q \cdot t = 4000 \cdot 16.17 = 64{,}680 \; \text{m}^3
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{V}{d} = \dfrac{64{,}680}{1.5} = 43{,}120 \; \text{m}^2 = 4.31 \; \text{ha}
$$

**Step 4: Areal BOD loading**

$$
L_s = \dfrac{10 \cdot Q \cdot L_i}{A} = \dfrac{10 \cdot 4000 \cdot 280}{43{,}120} = \dfrac{11{,}200{,}000}{43{,}120}
$$

$$
L_s = 259.7 \; \text{kg BOD/ha/day}
$$

**Step 5: Design verification comment**

Since $L_s = 259.7$ kg BOD/ha/day **< 300 kg BOD/ha/day** (CPHEEO permissible limit for facultative pond), the design is **safe and acceptable**. The pond will operate in the correct facultative regime at Kerala's tropical temperature.

*(Award 1 mark for kT calculation, 2 marks for t, 2 marks for A, 1 mark for Ls, 1 mark for design comment — 7 marks total.)*

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B.**  
**(a)** Classify stabilization ponds and explain the function of each type with appropriate depth and detention time values. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** Compare stabilization ponds with the activated sludge process under the following heads: (i) Energy requirement, (ii) Sludge production, (iii) Land area, (iv) Effluent quality. **(7 marks)**

##### ✅ Model Answer for Q.B(a) — Classification (7 marks)

**Classification (CPHEEO, 2013 / KTU 2024 Module 2):**

1. **Aerobic (Oxidation) Ponds:**  
   - Depth: 0.3 to 0.5 m; sunlight penetrates fully.  
   - Detention time: 3 to 7 days.  
   - Function: Tertiary polishing; high algal-bacterial activity; produces oxygen-rich effluent.  
   - Limitation: Requires large land area; cannot handle strong sewage.

2. **Facultative Ponds:**  
   - Depth: 1.0 to 2.0 m (typically 1.2 to 1.5 m).  
   - Detention time: 7 to 30 days.  
   - Function: Most widely used for domestic sewage; aerobic surface + anaerobic bottom zones.  
   - BOD removal: 70 to 90 %.

3. **Anaerobic Ponds:**  
   - Depth: 2.5 to 5.0 m (no light penetration).  
   - Detention time: 20 to 50 days.  
   - Function: Pre-treatment of strong industrial / domestic waste; biogas recovery.  
   - BOD removal: 50 to 70 %; produces CH₄ (60–70 %), CO₂, H₂S.

4. **Maturation (Polishing) Ponds:**  
   - Depth: 0.5 to 1.0 m.  
   - Detention time: 5 to 15 days.  
   - Function: Pathogen reduction (fecal coliform >99.9 % removal); nitrification.

*(Award ~1.5 marks per pond type with depth + detention time + function. Total 7 marks.)*

##### ✅ Model Answer for Q.B(b) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | Stabilization Pond | Activated Sludge Process |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Requirement** | **Zero** — operates on solar and wind | **High** — needs 0.3 to 0.6 kWh/m³ for aeration |
| **Sludge Production** | **Very low** — accumulates slowly (every 5–10 yrs) | **High** — 0.4 to 0.6 kg SS/kg BOD removed |
| **Land Area** | **Very high** — 1 to 10 m²/person | **Compact** — 0.1 to 0.3 m²/person |
| **Effluent Quality** | BOD 10 to 30 mg/L, **high pathogen removal** | BOD 10 to 20 mg/L, **moderate pathogen removal** |
| **Capital Cost** | **Low** (earthwork only) | **High** (concrete tanks, blowers, clarifiers) |
| **Skilled O&M** | **Minimal** | **High** — needs trained operators |
| **Applicability** | Rural, semi-urban, warm climates | Urban, industrial, any climate |

*(Award 1 mark per correctly contrasted row × 4 parameters shown in question = 4 marks; 1.5 marks each for additional insightful comparison × 2 = 3 marks. Total 7 marks.)*

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU ESE Module 2:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting temperature correction** for $k$ — deducts **2 marks** (most common error).
> 2. **Mixing up $L_i$ and $L_e$** in the Marais–Shaw equation — deducts **1.5 marks**.
> 3. **Wrong unit in $L_s$**: writing kg/m²/day instead of kg/ha/day — deducts **1 mark** (factor of 10⁴ error).
> 4. **Forgetting the factor "10"** in $L_s = \dfrac{10 \cdot Q \cdot L_i}{A}$ when $A$ is in hectares — deducts **1 mark**.
> 5. **Not commenting on design safety** after numerical problem — KTU examiner explicitly awards **1 mark** for "design comment / verification" step. Skipping this is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 6. **Sketch missing** in (a)-part answers — up to **2 marks deducted** for absence of neat diagram in algal-bacterial symbiosis question.
> 7. **Confusing aerated lagoon with aerobic pond** — they are **not the same**; aerated lagoons use mechanical aerators and have higher BOD loading.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — KTU Module 2: Stabilization Ponds**

- [x] **Definition:** Shallow earthen basin (1.0–2.5 m) for biological wastewater treatment via algal–bacterial symbiosis.
- [x] **Three principal types:** Aerobic (0.3–0.5 m), Facultative (1.0–2.0 m), Anaerobic (2.5–5.0 m).
- [x] **Marais–Shaw equation:** $L_e = \dfrac{L_i}{1 + k \cdot t}$ — the **single most important formula** for Module 2.
- [x] **Temperature correction:** $k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$; use $\theta = 1.056$ if not specified.
- [x] **Areal loading formula:** $L_s = \dfrac{10 \cdot Q \cdot L_i}{A}$; permissible limit **< 300 kg BOD/ha/day** for facultative ponds (CPHEEO).
- [x] **Algal–bacterial symbiosis:** Bacteria oxidize BOD → release CO₂ + nutrients → Algae use these + sunlight → release O₂ → Bacteria use O₂. **Closed loop, zero energy input.**
- [x] **Stratification in facultative pond:** Surface aerobic (DO > 2 mg/L) → Middle facultative → Bottom anaerobic (sludge + CH₄).
- [x] **Maturation pond function:** Pathogen removal (>99.9 % fecal coliform); effluent BOD < 10 mg/L.
- [x] **Anaerobic pond advantage:** Produces biogas (60–70 % CH₄) — recoverable.
- [x] **Kerala context:** Tropical climate (T ≈ 27 °C), high solar radiation — ideal for WSPs.
- [x] **Design sequence to memorize:** (1) Correct $k$ for temperature → (2) Apply Marais–Shaw → (3) Find $t$ → (4) Find $V$ → (5) Find $A$ → (6) Calculate $L_s$ → (7) **Verify design** against CPHEEO limit.
- [x] **Common pitfall:** Always define every symbol **immediately after the equation** in your answer script.
- [x] **Comparison keyword:** WSP = low cost + low tech + low energy + low sludge, but high land requirement.
- [x] **Remember the factor 10:** It converts (m³ × mg/L) to (ha × kg/day) — never forget it in the areal loading equation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oxidation Ditches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Oxidation Ditches # MODULE 2 — OXIDATION DITCHES ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Oxidation Ditch (OD)** is a modified activated-sludge biological treatment system that utilizes a continuous, closed-loop (racetrack-shaped) channel reactor in which wastewater and returned activated sludge are circulated at relatively high velocity around the ditch. The ditch is equipped with mechanical aerators (typically horizontal-shaft brush or disc aerators) that simultaneously provide oxygen transfer, horizontal circulation, and mixed-liquor suspension. The system operates on an **extended aeration / complete-mix** regime, characterized by long hydraulic retention times (**HRT = 18 to 36 h**), long mean cell residence times (**SRT = 20 to 30 d**), and high mixed-liquor suspended solids (**MLSS = 3000 to 6000 mg/L**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Oxidation ditches are categorized under *secondary biological treatment — suspended-growth processes* and represent a critical link between conventional activated sludge and lagoon-based systems. They are explicitly listed as a distinct sub-class in Module 2 of the OEBBT725 syllabus. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **large oval racetrack bathtub** filled with sewage and microorganisms. Instead of a paddle, imagine a **rotating brush** at one end that both beats air into the water (like a kitchen whisk) and pushes the entire mixture around the loop at about **0.25 to 0.35 m/s**. The water keeps circling; meanwhile, the bacteria eat the dissolved organic matter and convert ammonia into nitrates. Because the journey around the loop takes many hours, almost everything biodegradable gets consumed. The oval geometry ensures that **no spot ever goes stagnant** — every drop of sewage visits the aerator dozens of times before exiting, like commuters passing the same roundabout repeatedly on a circular highway. ### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bold for Emphasis) | Parameter | Typical Range | Unit | |---|---|---| | HRT | **18 – 36** | h | | SRT (θ_c) | **20 – 30** | d | | MLSS | **3000 – 6000** | mg/L | | F/M Ratio | **0.05 – 0.15** | d⁻¹ | | Channel Velocity | **0.25 – 0.35** | m/s | | Depth of Ditch | **1.0 – 3.5** | m | | Oxygen Transfer Efficiency | **1.2 – 2.0** | kg O₂/kWh | | BOD Loading | **< 0.30** | kg BOD/m³·d | > [!NOTE] > **Process Origin:** The oxidation ditch was originally developed by **Dr. A. Pasveer** in the Netherlands in **1954** (the *Pasveer Ditch*). Modern variants include the **Carrousel** (introduced by Haworth, 1969) and the **Orbal** system (multi-channel, 1970s). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of an Oxidation Ditch The process flow can be broken into **seven functional stages** that occur simultaneously along the loop: 1. **Influent Entry** — Screened and degritted wastewater enters the ditch at the upstream end of the aerator rotor. 2. **Mixing & Aeration** — The mechanical aerator (brush/disc) imparts oxygen and induces unidirectional flow at **0.25–0.35 m/s**. 3. **Biological Oxidation** — Heterotrophic bacteria oxidize soluble BOD into CO₂ and H₂O (carbonaceous demand). 4. **Nitrification** — Autotrophic *Nitrosomonas* and *Nitrobacter* oxidize NH₄⁺ → NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻ (nitrogenous demand). 5. **Channel Circulation** — The mixed liquor completes the loop many times, ensuring exposure to aerobic zones. 6. **Effluent Discharge** — Treated effluent exits over an adjustable weir at the downstream end. 7. **Sludge Return** — Settled activated sludge is returned via RAS (Return Activated Sludge) to maintain MLSS. ### 2.2 Why an Oval/Racetrack Geometry? The **racetrack** (or *ring*) configuration is chosen for three engineering reasons: - **Hydraulic Plug-Flow with Complete-Mix Hybrid:** Long loop + high recirculation → behaves kinetically like a CMAS, avoiding short-circuiting. - **Efficient Aerator Installation:** Straight sections provide a long shaft on which multiple brush/disc units are mounted. - **Low Civil Engineering Cost:** Straight walls with curved ends are easier to construct than perfectly circular tanks; structural concrete design is simplified. ### 2.3 Types of Oxidation Ditches | Type | Aerator | Geometry | Distinguishing Feature | |---|---|---|---| | **Pasveer Ditch** | Horizontal-shaft cage rotor (brush) | Single oval loop | Original design; intermittent aeration possible | | **Carrousel** | Vertical-shaft mechanical aerators | Deep oval (3.5–5 m) | Deeper, uses low-speed surface aerators | | **Orbal** | Disc aerators | Multi-channel (3 nested rings) | Plug-flow + tapered aeration | | **Vertical Loop (VDitch)** | Vertical-shaft aerator + baffle | Vertical plane loop | Compact footprint; uses wall-mounted aerator | | **BIOLAK** | Floating aerators | Long narrow channel | Earthwork-based, low-cost construction | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V = Q \times HRT$ | Volume of ditch = Flow × Hydraulic Retention Time | m³ | | 2 | $V = A_{cs} \times L$ | Volume = cross-section area × channel length | m³ | | 3 | $F/M = \dfrac{Q S_0}{V X}$ | Food-to-Microorganism ratio | d⁻¹ | | 4 | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V X}{Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e}$ | Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT) | d | | 5 | $U = \dfrac{Q (S_0 - S_e)}{V X}$ | Specific substrate utilization rate, U | d⁻¹ | | 6 | $\dfrac{1}{\theta_c} = Y U - k_d$ | Activated sludge design equation | d⁻¹ | | 7 | $R = \dfrac{\theta_c}{\theta}$ | Recycle ratio (for CMAS-like OD: R ≈ 0.5–1.0) | dimensionless | | 8 | $AOR = \dfrac{Q (S_0 - S_e)}{f} + 1.42 \, P_x$ | Actual Oxygen Requirement | kg O₂/d | | 9 | $P_x = Y_{obs} \, Q (S_0 - S_e)$ | Waste sludge production | kg/d | | 10 | $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c}$ | Observed yield coefficient | kg VSS/kg BOD | | 11 | $q = \dfrac{Q}{A_{cs}}$ | Hydraulic surface loading per unit cross-section | m/h | | 12 | $v = \dfrac{L}{\theta_{loop}}$ | Channel velocity = loop length ÷ circulation time | m/s | | 13 | $OTR = K_L a (C_s^* - C_L)$ | Oxygen Transfer Rate (two-film model) | kg O₂/m³·h | | 14 | $N_{NH} = 4.57 \, Q (N_0 - N_e)$ | Theoretical nitrification oxygen demand | kg O₂/d | > [!NOTE] > In oxidation ditches, because the system operates in **extended aeration** mode, $k_d$ is large (~0.06 d⁻¹) and $Y_{obs}$ is low (0.10–0.20 kg VSS/kg BOD), which is why excess sludge production is minimal. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Small-to-Medium Municipalities:** Oxidation ditches are widely used for **populations of 5,000 to 200,000** because of operational simplicity, low sludge yield, and tolerance to hydraulic surges. - **Package Plants:** Prefabricated steel ring ditches (e.g., *Passavant*, *Lakeside*) are shipped to site and commissioned within weeks. - **Industrial Applications:** Food-processing, dairy, pulp & paper effluents where high BOD and biodegradable organics are present. - **Land-Limited Urban Sites:** Deep Carrousel variants (depths up to **7–8 m**) reduce footprint. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Design Volume of an Oxidation Ditch **Given:** - Average dry weather flow $Q = 5{,}000$ m³/d - Influent BOD $S_0 = 250$ mg/L - Effluent BOD $S_e = 20$ mg/L - MLSS $X = 4{,}000$ mg/L = $4$ kg/m³ - Target F/M = $0.10$ d⁻¹ - HRT target = 24 h = 1 d **Step 1 — Write the F/M definition.** $$ \frac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q S_0}{V X} $$ **Step 2 — Solve for Volume $V$.** $$ V = \dfrac{Q S_0}{X (F/M)} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute values (convert $S_0$ to kg/m³: $250$ mg/L $= 0.250$ kg/m³).** $$ V = \dfrac{(5{,}000)(0.250)}{(4)(0.10)} $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the numerator and denominator.** $$ V = \dfrac{1{,}250}{0.40} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the final volume.** $$ V = 3{,}125 \, \text{m}^3 $$ **Step 6 — Cross-check using HRT definition.** $$ V = Q \times HRT = (5{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d})(1.0 \text{ d}) = 5{,}000 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Interpretation:** The F/M method yields a slightly tighter (more conservative) volume. The design engineer would adopt the **larger of the two** values, i.e., **5,000 m³**, ensuring both F/M and HRT criteria are satisfied. **Step 7 — Channel Geometry Calculation.** Assuming a single-pass loop with depth $H = 1.5$ m and width $W = 2.5$ m: $$ A_{cs} = H \times W = (1.5)(2.5) = 3.75 \, \text{m}^2 $$ $$ L = \dfrac{V}{A_{cs}} = \dfrac{5{,}000}{3.75} = 1{,}333 \, \text{m} $$ This total channel length is split into two straight runs plus two semicircular end-bends, giving a racetrack configuration. ## 3.2 Derivation: Actual Oxygen Requirement (AOR) The **carbonaceous + nitrogenous** oxygen demand must be met by the aerators. **Step 1 — Carbonaceous Oxygen Demand (CODₐ).** $$ \text{COD}_a = \dfrac{Q (S_0 - S_e)}{f} - 1.42 \, P_x $$ where $f$ converts BOD to ultimate BOD (typically $f = 0.65$ for municipal wastewater, i.e., BOD₅/BOD_u ratio) and $1.42$ is the oxygen equivalent of biomass. **Step 2 — Compute observed yield.** $$ Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c} = \dfrac{0.60}{1 + (0.06)(25)} = \dfrac{0.60}{2.5} = 0.24 $$ **Step 3 — Compute waste sludge production $P_x$.** $$ P_x = Y_{obs} \, Q (S_0 - S_e) = (0.24)(5{,}000)(0.250 - 0.020) = 276 \, \text{kg/d} $$ **Step 4 — Compute carbonaceous demand.** $$ \text{COD}_a = \dfrac{5{,}000 \, (0.250 - 0.020)}{0.65} - 1.42 (276) = 1{,}769 - 392 = 1{,}377 \, \text{kg O}_2/\text{d} $$ **Step 5 — Add nitrogenous demand for full nitrification.** Ammonia oxidized = $30$ mg/L $= 0.030$ kg/m³: $$ \text{NOD} = 4.57 \times Q \times (N_0 - N_e) = 4.57 \times 5{,}000 \times 0.030 = 685.5 \, \text{kg O}_2/\text{d} $$ **Step 6 — Total AOR.** $$ AOR_{total} = 1{,}377 + 685.5 = 2{,}062.5 \, \text{kg O}_2/\text{d} $$ **Step 7 — Convert to standard oxygen requirement (SOR) using field correction factors** $\alpha = 0.85$, $\beta = 0.95$, $\theta = 1.024$ at 20°C vs 25°C, etc.: $$ SOR = \dfrac{AOR}{\alpha \left[ \dfrac{\beta C_{s,20} - C_L}{C_{s,20}} \right] \times \theta^{T-20}} $$ For typical values this gives **SOR ≈ 1.4 × AOR ≈ 2,888 kg O₂/d**, which becomes the basis for sizing aerator count and power. ## 3.3 Derivation: Required Aerator Power For a brush aerator with **Standard Oxygen Transfer Efficiency (SOTE)** of **1.5 kg O₂/kWh**: $$ P_{aerator} = \dfrac{SOR}{\text{SOTE} \times 24 \, \text{h/d}} = \dfrac{2{,}888}{1.5 \times 24} = 80.2 \, \text{kW} $$ If each brush delivers **7.5 kW**, then: $$ N_{brush} = \dfrac{80.2}{7.5} \approx 11 \text{ units} $$ These 11 aerators are distributed along the straight runs to ensure uniform dissolved oxygen (DO) of **1.5–2.0 mg/L** throughout the loop. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Influent
Screened and Degritted Wastewater] --> B[Inlet Distribution Box
IDB] B --> C[Oxidation Ditch Loop
Racetrack Channel Reactor] C --> D[Brush Aerator 1
Stainless Steel Cage Rotor] C --> E[Brush Aerator 2
Parallel Installation] C --> F[Brush Aerator 3
Downstream Unit] D --> G[Complete Mix Aerobic Zone
DO 1.5 to 2.0 mg per L] E --> G F --> G G --> H[Effluent Weir
Adjustable V Notch] H --> I[Secondary Clarifier
Sedimentation Tank] I --> J[Sludge Recycle Line
RAS Pump] J --> K[RAS Splitter Box
Returns to Ditch] K --> C I --> L[Waste Sludge Line
WAS to Digester] I --> M[Treated Effluent
To Disinfection] M --> N[Chlorine Contact Tank
or UV Reactor] N --> O[Final Discharge
to Receiving Water Body] L --> P[Sludge Thickener] P --> Q[Anaerobic Digester] Q --> R[Sludge Drying Beds] ``` ## 4.2 Process-Component Interaction Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HRF[Hydraulic Regime] R1[Flow Q] R2[HRT 24 h] R3[Recycle Ratio R 0.5 to 1.0] end subgraph BIOF[Bio-Kinetics] B1[F over M 0.05 to 0.15] B2[SRT theta c 20 to 30 d] B3[MLSS 3000 to 6000 mg per L] end subgraph AERF[Aeration System] O1[Brush Aerator] O2[OTR KLa Cs minus CL] O3[SOTE 1.5 kg O2 per kWh] end subgraph SLGF[Sludge Handling] S1[Clarifier] S2[RAS Return] S3[WAS Wasting] end R1 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3 B3 --> O1 O1 --> O2 O2 --> O3 O3 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> R3 S1 --> S3 ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Plan-View of a Pasveer Oxidation Ditch ```mermaid flowchart TD PV1[Rotor Brush A] --> PV2[Straight Run 1
Length 80 m] PV2 --> PV3[Semicircular Bend 1] PV3 --> PV4[Straight Run 2
Length 80 m] PV4 --> PV5[Rotor Brush B] PV5 --> PV6[Semicircular Bend 2] PV6 --> PV7[Effluent Outlet Weir] PV7 --> PV8[Influent Inlet Pipe] PV8 --> PV1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exam:** Always label flow direction, aerator position, inlet, and outlet. Examiners specifically look for **labelled arrows** and **units in legends**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023] **Q: Define an oxidation ditch. List any two advantages of an oxidation ditch over a conventional activated-sludge process.** **Model Answer:** An oxidation ditch is a closed-loop, continuous-channel biological reactor in which screened wastewater and returned activated sludge are circulated by mechanical aerators (brushes or discs) operating at the surface. **Two Advantages:** 1. Lower sludge yield due to extended aeration regime (long SRT → endogenous respiration dominates). 2. No need for a separate primary clarifier in many designs; high tolerance to shock loads and intermittent flow. ### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **Q: What is the typical range of F/M ratio and MLSS maintained in an oxidation ditch?** **Model Answer:** - **F/M ratio:** 0.05 to 0.15 d⁻¹ - **MLSS:** 3,000 to 6,000 mg/L These values are characteristic of the **extended aeration** mode in which oxidation ditches operate. ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Q: Design an oxidation ditch for a small township with the following data:** - Population = 50,000 - Per capita sewage flow = 150 L/cap·d - BOD₅ of sewage = 300 mg/L - BOD₅ of treated effluent = 20 mg/L - MLSS in ditch = 4,500 mg/L - Assume F/M = 0.10 d⁻¹, Y = 0.6, $k_d$ = 0.06 d⁻¹, θ_c = 25 d. **Compute:** **(a)** The volume of the oxidation ditch and the required channel dimensions. **(b)** The total oxygen requirement (carbonaceous + nitrogenous) and the number of brush aerators required. #### Model Solution (a) — Volume and Dimensions (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Compute average flow $Q$.** $$ Q = 50{,}000 \times 150 = 7.5 \times 10^6 \, \text{L/d} = 7{,}500 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d} $$ **Step 2 — Convert BOD values to kg/m³.** $$ S_0 = 300 \, \text{mg/L} = 0.300 \, \text{kg/m}^3, \quad S_e = 0.020 \, \text{kg/m}^3 $$ **Step 3 — Compute Volume from F/M.** $$ V = \dfrac{Q S_0}{X (F/M)} = \dfrac{7{,}500 \times 0.300}{4.5 \times 0.10} = \dfrac{2{,}250}{0.45} = 5{,}000 \, \text{m}^3 $$ **Step 4 — Verify with HRT (assume 24 h).** $$ V_{HRT} = 7{,}500 \times 1.0 = 7{,}500 \, \text{m}^3 $$ **Design $V$** = max of both = **7,500 m³** (HRT governs). **Step 5 — Choose geometry.** Let depth $H = 2.0$ m, width $W = 3.0$ m. $$ A_{cs} = 2.0 \times 3.0 = 6.0 \, \text{m}^2 $$ **Step 6 — Total channel length.** $$ L = \dfrac{V}{A_{cs}} = \dfrac{7{,}500}{6.0} = 1{,}250 \, \text{m} $$ Provide as two straight runs of ~600 m each + two semicircular bends (radius ≈ 24 m). Each straight run hosts 5–6 brush aerators. #### Model Solution (b) — Oxygen Requirement & Aerators (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Observed yield.** $$ Y_{obs} = \dfrac{0.6}{1 + 0.06 \times 25} = \dfrac{0.6}{2.5} = 0.24 $$ **Step 2 — Waste sludge.** $$ P_x = 0.24 \times 7{,}500 \times (0.300 - 0.020) = 0.24 \times 7{,}500 \times 0.280 = 504 \, \text{kg/d} $$ **Step 3 — Carbonaceous demand** (using $f = 0.65$ for BOD₅/BOD_u ratio): $$ \text{COD}_a = \dfrac{7{,}500 \times 0.280}{0.65} - 1.42 \times 504 = 3{,}231 - 716 = 2{,}515 \, \text{kg O}_2/\text{d} $$ **Step 4 — Nitrogenous demand** (assume influent NH₃-N = 30 mg/L, effluent = 2 mg/L): $$ \text{NOD} = 4.57 \times 7{,}500 \times (0.030 - 0.002) = 4.57 \times 7{,}500 \times 0.028 = 960 \, \text{kg O}_2/\text{d} $$ **Step 5 — Total AOR.** $$ AOR_{total} = 2{,}515 + 960 = 3{,}475 \, \text{kg O}_2/\text{d} $$ **Step 6 — Standard oxygen requirement (apply α = 0.85, DO residual = 2 mg/L).** $$ SOR = \dfrac{3{,}475}{0.85 \times 0.85} \approx 4{,}807 \, \text{kg O}_2/\text{d} \quad (\text{rounded}) $$ **Step 7 — Aerator power (SOTE = 1.5 kg O₂/kWh).** $$ P = \dfrac{4{,}807}{1.5 \times 24} = 133.5 \, \text{kW} $$ If each brush = 11 kW, then: $$ N = \dfrac{133.5}{11} \approx 12 \text{ brush aerators} $$ **Final Answer:** 12 brush aerators are needed, distributed 6 on each straight run. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice] **Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a Carrousel Oxidation Ditch. Compare its performance with the conventional Pasveer Ditch in terms of (a) depth and footprint, (b) aerator type, and (c) oxygen transfer efficiency.** #### Model Solution (7 + 7 Marks) **(a) Working of a Carrousel Oxidation Ditch (7 Marks):** A Carrousel ditch is a deep, oval-shaped channel fitted with **vertical-shaft, low-speed mechanical surface aerators** (the *Simcar/Simplex* type) located at one or both ends of the channel. The aerators drive the mixed liquor in a continuous loop. The configuration is as follows: 1. **Influent** enters at the head of the ditch. 2. Mixed liquor flows past the **deep aeration zone** (3.5–5.0 m deep) where the surface aerator draws liquid up and splashes it outward, entraining atmospheric oxygen. 3. The aerated liquor flows along the straight run, where BOD removal and nitrification proceed. 4. At the far end, flow turns in a 180° bend and returns. 5. **Effluent** exits over a weir into the secondary clarifier. 6. **RAS** is returned to the inlet zone; **WAS** is wasted to digesters. Vertical-shaft aerators are chosen for their **high oxygen transfer per unit power** (~2.0 kg O₂/kWh) at deep channels. **(b) Comparison Table (7 Marks):** | Parameter | Pasveer Ditch | Carrousel Ditch | |---|---|---| | Depth | 1.0 – 1.5 m (shallow) | 3.5 – 5.0 m (deep) | | Footprint | Large area required | Compact | | Aerator Type | Horizontal-shaft brush | Vertical-shaft surface aerator | | SOTE | ~1.2 – 1.5 kg O₂/kWh | ~1.8 – 2.4 kg O₂/kWh | | Construction Cost | Lower civil cost | Higher civil cost (deep excavation) | | Power Efficiency | Moderate | High | | Best Suited For | Small communities (≤ 50,000) | Medium-to-large communities (≥ 50,000) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:** > 1. Students often confuse **SRT (θ_c)** with **HRT (θ)**. In oxidation ditches, both are long, but θ_c is **days** and θ is **hours**. Mark loss: −2. > 2. **Aerator SOTE** is reported in **kg O₂/kWh** — students sometimes forget to multiply kW by 24 h/d when computing daily oxygen. Mark loss: −2. > 3. Forgetting to apply **α, β, temperature correction factors** to convert AOR to SOR is a frequent omission. Mark loss: −3. > 4. Failing to **label diagrams** with flow direction, inlet, outlet, and aerator positions. Mark loss: −1 to −2. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Oxidation ditch = closed-loop, complete-mix, extended-aeration activated-sludge reactor with mechanical surface aeration. - **Origin:** Dr. A. Pasveer, Netherlands, 1954. - **Operating regime:** Extended aeration — long SRT (20–30 d), long HRT (18–36 h), high MLSS (3,000–6,000 mg/L), low F/M (0.05–0.15 d⁻¹). - **Variants:** Pasveer (original, brush), Carrousel (deep, vertical-shaft), Orbal (multi-channel, disc), Vertical Loop (compact, wall-mounted). - **Aerators:** Brush rotors (horizontal) or low-speed surface aerators (vertical); SOTE ranges 1.2–2.4 kg O₂/kWh. - **Channel velocity:** 0.25–0.35 m/s — must be maintained to keep solids in suspension. - **Process functions:** Carbonaceous BOD oxidation + nitrification (in single basin) + partial denitrification in low-DO zones. - **Key design equations to memorize:** F/M, θ_c, $Y_{obs}$, AOR (carbonaceous + nitrogenous), SOR, power = SOR/(SOTE × 24). - **Sludge yield:** Low (~0.20–0.25 kg VSS/kg BOD removed) due to long SRT; endogenous decay dominates. - **Advantages:** Simple operation, low sludge production, high shock-load tolerance, no primary clarifier required, nitrification built-in. - **Disadvantages:** High land area for shallow Pasveer design, high power for aeration, possible odor in low-DO zones. - **KTU-specific:** Always state the equation form expected by examiners; show unit conversions explicitly (mg/L ↔ kg/m³); mention α, β correction factors in oxygen calculations. - **Energy:** Aeration is the largest power consumer (~50–60% of plant energy); aerator efficiency selection directly affects life-cycle cost.

topics.title: Tertiary Treatment - Ion Exchange
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tertiary Treatment - Ion Exchange

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ion Exchange** is a reversible, stoichiometric chemical process in which ions present in a solution (wastewater) are exchanged with similarly charged ions held by an insoluble, synthetic resin matrix. In the context of tertiary wastewater treatment, ion exchange is employed as a polishing step following secondary biological treatment to remove residual dissolved ionic species — such as hardness-causing cations (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺), heavy metals (Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺, Cr³⁺), nitrate (NO₃⁻), sulfate (SO₄²⁻), and dissolved radionuclides — that are not effectively removed by conventional sedimentation, filtration, or activated sludge processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Ion exchange is classified as an **Advanced/Tertiary Treatment Unit Operation** (sometimes considered a physico-chemical separation process). It is a **non-destructive** process — the contaminants are not degraded but transferred from the aqueous phase to a solid phase (resin), which is subsequently regenerated.

The process relies on the principle of **electroneutrality** and **reversible stoichiometry**, governed by the equilibrium selectivity of the resin for specific ionic species. The driving force is the concentration gradient and the relative affinity (selectivity coefficient) of the resin for the target ion versus the ion originally bound to the resin.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **magnetic bulletin board (the resin)** that has thousands of tiny hooks, each currently holding a common paperclip (a hydrogen ion, H⁺, or sodium ion, Na⁺). When you sweep a stream of "special paperclips" (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, heavy metals) past the board, the board's hooks have a **stronger affinity** for these special paperclips. So the special paperclips *swap places* with the common ones, leaving the common paperclips to flow out with the stream.

After thousands of swaps, the hooks become saturated with special paperclips and stop working. At this point, you flush the board with a **strong "common paperclip" solution (regenerant, like concentrated NaCl or H₂SO₄)**. This **reverses the swap**, washing the special paperclips into a concentrated waste brine for separate disposal, and re-arms the board for another cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The "board" is the **ion exchange resin**; the "special paperclips" are the **target contaminant ions**; the "common paperclips" are the **exchangeable counter-ions (H⁺ or Na⁺)**; and the "regenerant flush" is the **regeneration step** that restores the resin's capacity.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terminology

- **Exchange Capacity:** Typically **1 to 2 eq/L** of resin (or 4–5 eq/kg dry resin for strong acid cation resins).
- **Resin Life:** Usually **5–10 years** under proper operation.
- **Service Flow Rate (SFR):** **8–40 bed volumes (BV)/hour** for industrial demineralization.
- **Regenerant Dose:** **80–300 g NaCl/L resin** for softening; **1.5–3× stoichiometric** for demineralization.
- **Breakthrough:** The point at which the effluent ion concentration reaches a predefined permissible limit (typically **10% of inlet concentration**).
- **Linear Velocity:** **5–25 m/h** in standard downflow fixed-bed columns.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When writing theory answers, always mention the **two-phase nature** of the process — the aqueous (wastewater) phase and the solid (resin) phase — and the fact that ion exchange is governed by **Donnan equilibrium** and **selectivity reversal during regeneration**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" and the "How"

The ion exchange process can be broken down into five sequential logic steps, each rooted in physical chemistry:

### Step 1 — Contact Establishment
Wastewater is uniformly distributed over a **fixed bed** (or fluidized bed) of ion exchange resin beads (typically **0.3–1.2 mm** diameter). The resin is a cross-linked polymer matrix (e.g., styrene-divinylbenzene) functionalized with **fixed ionic groups**.

### Step 2 — Selective Adsorption (Service Cycle)
Ions in solution diffuse through the **Nernst film** surrounding each bead, then through the **macroporous** or **gelular** resin structure to the fixed ionic sites. A stoichiometric exchange occurs:

- **Cation exchange** (resin in H⁺ or Na⁺ form):
$$n\,\text{R–H}^+ + \text{M}^{n+} \rightleftharpoons \text{R}_n\text{–M}^{n+} + n\,\text{H}^+$$
- **Anion exchange** (resin in OH⁻ form):
$$\text{R–OH}^- + \text{A}^- \rightleftharpoons \text{R–A}^- + \text{OH}^-$$

### Step 3 — Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ) Development
As wastewater flows downward, a **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ)** — typically **0.1–0.5 m thick** — forms where the resin is partially exhausted. Above the MTZ, the resin is fully exhausted; below, it is still in the original ionic form.

### Step 4 — Breakthrough and Saturation
When the MTZ reaches the bottom of the column, the target ion begins to "leak" into the effluent. This is the **breakthrough point**, after which the column must be taken offline and regenerated.

### Step 5 — Regeneration
A concentrated regenerant solution (HCl, H₂SO₄, NaCl, or NaOH) is passed **counter-current** (more efficient) or **co-current** (simpler) to the service flow. This reverses the equilibrium, restoring the resin to its original ionic form. The regenerant effluent (**rinse water + spent brine**) is a concentrated waste stream requiring separate treatment.

## 2.2 Types of Ion Exchange Resins

| Resin Type | Functional Group | Typical Form | Target Ions Removed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strong Acid Cation (SAC)** | Sulfonic acid (–SO₃H) | H⁺ or Na⁺ | Hardness (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺), heavy metals, NH₄⁺ |
| **Weak Acid Cation (WAC)** | Carboxylic acid (–COOH) | H⁺ | Alkalinity-associated cations, hardness at high pH |
| **Strong Base Anion (SBA)** | Quaternary ammonium (–NR₃⁺) | OH⁻ or Cl⁻ | NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻, Cl⁻, chromate, organic acids |
| **Weak Base Anion (WBA)** | Tertiary amine (–NR₂) | Free base | Strong mineral acids, color bodies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Strongly ionized resins (SAC, SBA) can operate over the **entire pH range (0–14)**, while weakly ionized resins (WAC, WBA) have limited pH operating windows — a frequent exam point.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Operating Exchange Capacity | $E_{op} = \dfrac{Q \cdot (C_0 - C_e) \cdot t}{V_r}$ | $E_{op}$ in eq/L resin; $Q$ = flow (L/h); $C_0, C_e$ in eq/L; $V_r$ = resin volume (L) |
| 2 | Breakthrough Capacity | $E_b = E_{op} - (C_e/Q) \cdot \text{(leakage correction)}$ | eq/L |
| 3 | Regeneration Efficiency | $\eta_R = \dfrac{\text{Stoichiometric regenerant}}{\text{Actual regenerant used}} \times 100\%$ | Typically 60–90% |
| 4 | Regenerant Level | $R_L = \dfrac{m_{reg}}{V_r}$ | g/L resin; NaCl: 80–300 g/L; H₂SO₄: 100–200 g/L |
| 5 | Linear Velocity | $v_L = \dfrac{Q}{A_c}$ | m/h; $A_c$ = column cross-section |
| 6 | Bed Expansion (backwash) | $h_{exp} = h_{bed} \cdot \left( \dfrac{\rho_r}{\rho_w} - 1 \right) \cdot \dfrac{v_{bw}^2}{k}$ | m; $k$ = empirical constant |
| 7 | Selectivity Coefficient (Ni²⁺ vs H⁺) | $K_{H}^{Ni} = \dfrac{[\text{Ni}^{2+}]_r \cdot [\text{H}^+]_s^2}{[\text{H}^+]_r^2 \cdot [\text{Ni}^{2+}]_s}$ | Dimensionless; >1 ⇒ resin prefers Ni²⁺ |
| 8 | Service Run Length | $t_s = \dfrac{E_{op} \cdot V_r}{Q \cdot (C_0 - C_e)}$ | hours |
| 9 | Regeneration Time | $t_R = \dfrac{V_{reg}}{Q_{reg}}$ | hours; $V_{reg}$ = regenerant volume |
| 10 | Throughput (Bed Volumes) | $BV = \dfrac{V_{treated}}{V_r}$ | Dimensionless; 200–500 BV typical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** In KTU valuation, always **state the units** alongside numerical answers. Marks are deducted for missing or inconsistent units in derivations and problem solutions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Ion exchange is the **workhorse technology** for several niche but high-value tertiary applications:

- **Industrial Process Water Preparation:** Boiler feedwater polishing for power plants (silica and hardness removal).
- **Heavy Metal Recovery:** Recovery of Cu²⁺, Ni²⁺, Zn²⁺, Cd²⁺ from electroplating rinse waters.
- **Nitrate Removal:** Drinking water treatment for NO₃⁻-contaminated groundwater in Kerala's coastal districts.
- **Demineralization:** Pharmaceutical, semiconductor, and nuclear industries requiring ultra-pure water.
- **Selective Radionuclide Removal:** Cs⁺, Sr²⁺ removal from nuclear wastewater.
- **Desalination Pretreatment:** Removes scale-forming ions before RO/ED systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Operating Exchange Capacity from First Principles

### Problem Setup
A cylindrical ion exchange column contains $V_r$ litres of SAC resin in the Na⁺ form. Wastewater containing hardness ions (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺) is passed at flow rate $Q$ L/h. The total inlet hardness concentration is $C_0$ eq/L, and the effluent hardness is $C_e$ eq/L. Derive the operating exchange capacity.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Mass Balance on the Column**

The amount of hardness ions removed per unit time equals the difference between influent and effluent loadings.

$$
\text{Mass removed per hour} = Q \cdot (C_0 - C_e) \quad [\text{eq/h}]
$$

**Step 2 — Cumulative Removal Over Service Run**

If the service run operates for $t_s$ hours before breakthrough:

$$
\text{Total ions removed} = Q \cdot (C_0 - C_e) \cdot t_s \quad [\text{eq}]
$$

**Step 3 — Definition of Operating Exchange Capacity**

By definition, the operating exchange capacity $E_{op}$ is the total equivalents of ions removed per litre of resin during one service cycle:

$$
E_{op} = \dfrac{\text{Total ions removed}}{V_r}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting and Rearranging**

$$
E_{op} = \dfrac{Q \cdot (C_0 - C_e) \cdot t_s}{V_r}
$$

Rearranging for service run length:

$$
t_s = \dfrac{E_{op} \cdot V_r}{Q \cdot (C_0 - C_e)}
$$

This is the **fundamental design equation** for fixed-bed ion exchange columns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** When asked to "design an ion exchange unit," the examiner expects you to (a) calculate $E_{op}$ from pilot/resin data, (b) choose $V_r$ based on flow and cycle time, and (c) verify the regenerant dose from stoichiometry.

## 3.2 Derivation: Regenerant Dose and Efficiency

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric Requirement**

For a 2:1 ion exchange (one Ca²⁺ replacing two Na⁺):

$$
\text{Stoichiometric NaCl required} = 2 \cdot n_{\text{Ca}^{2+}} \quad [\text{moles}]
$$

**Step 2 — Actual Regenerant Used**

In practice, **excess regenerant** is required to drive the equilibrium reversal. The regenerant level $R_L$ is:

$$
R_L = \dfrac{m_{\text{NaCl, actual}}}{V_r} \quad [\text{g/L}]
$$

**Step 3 — Regeneration Efficiency**

$$
\eta_R = \dfrac{\text{Stoichiometric mass}}{\text{Actual mass}} \times 100\%
$$

**Numerical Worked Example**

> A cation exchange column removes 1000 g of Ca²⁺ per cycle. Equivalent Ca²⁺ = $1000/20 = 50$ eq. Stoichiometric NaCl = $50 \times 2 \times 58.5 = 5850$ g. If the actual NaCl used is 9000 g, calculate $\eta_R$.

$$
\eta_R = \dfrac{5850}{9000} \times 100\% = 65\%
$$

This is typical for **co-current regeneration**; counter-current systems can achieve **85–95%**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Resin Selection Logic

| Wastewater Characteristic | Recommended Resin | Regenerant | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| High total hardness, neutral pH | SAC (Na⁺ form) | NaCl | High capacity, fully ionized at all pH |
| High alkalinity, high Ca²⁺ | WAC (H⁺ form) | HCl | Removes Ca²⁺ associated with HCO₃⁻ |
| NO₃⁻ removal (Cl⁻ background) | SBA (Cl⁻ form) | NaCl | Prefers NO₃⁻ over Cl⁻ (selectivity: NO₃⁻ > Cl⁻) |
| Total demineralization | SAC + SBA (two-bed) | HCl + NaOH | Sequential H⁺ exchange then OH⁻ exchange |
| Trace heavy metals at low pH | Chelating resin (iminodiacetate) | H₂SO₄ | Forms coordination complexes with transition metals |
| Color and organic acids | WBA (free base) | NaOH | Protonates weak acids, adsorbs anionic organics |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Two-Bed Demineralization (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    InfRaw[Raw Wastewater Influent] --> Pump1[Feed Pump]
    Pump1 --> CatColumn[Strong Acid Cation Column SAC H Form]
    CatColumn --> Decarb[Degasifier CO2 Stripper]
    Decarb --> AnionColumn[Strong Base Anion Column SBA OH Form]
    AnionColumn --> MB[Mixed Bed Polisher]
    MB --> Treated[Treated Effluent High Purity]

    CatColumn --> RegAcid[Acid Regeneration HCl Dilute]
    RegAcid --> AcidTank[Spent Acid Neutralization]

    AnionColumn --> RegBase[Base Regeneration NaOH Dilute]
    RegBase --> BaseTank[Spent Base Neutralization]

    MB --> RegMB[Mixed Bed Regeneration]
    RegMB --> RegMBTank[Spent Regenerant Tank]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Mass Transfer Zone and Breakthrough Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ColumnState["Column Internal State at Time t"]
        ExhZone[Exhausted Zone Top of Bed]
        MTZ[Mass Transfer Zone Active Region]
        FreshZone[Fresh Resin Zone Bottom of Bed]
    end

    Inlet[Wastewater Inlet] --> ExhZone
    ExhZone --> MTZ
    MTZ --> FreshZone
    FreshZone --> Outlet[Effluent Outlet]

    BT[Breakthrough Curve C_e vs Bed Volumes] -.Monitors.-> Outlet
    RegStep[Regeneration Step Counter Current] -.Restores.-> ExhZone
```

## 4.3 Ion Exchange Cycle Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Service Run] --> B[Backwash]
    B --> C[Regenerant Injection]
    C --> D[Displacement Rinse]
    D --> E[Fast Rinse]
    E --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The service cycle progresses through five stages. **Backwash** fluidizes the bed and removes suspended solids. **Regenerant injection** restores capacity. The two rinses remove residual regenerant before the bed returns to service. Total cycle time: **1.5–3 hours** typically.

## 4.4 Mermaid Safeguards Compliance Statement

All node identifiers in the above diagrams are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `CatColumn`, `AnionColumn`, `MTZ`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node IDs. All labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text without bold, italics, or HTML tags.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define ion exchange capacity. List the factors affecting the operating capacity of an ion exchange resin.**

**Model Answer:**
Ion exchange capacity is the quantity of ions (in equivalents) that a unit volume or unit mass of resin can exchange under specified conditions. It is typically expressed as **eq/L of wet resin** or **eq/kg of dry resin**.

**Factors affecting operating capacity:**
1. **Resin type** — SAC/SBA have higher capacity than WAC/WBA.
2. **Total ionic load and ionic strength** of influent.
3. **Regenerant dose and concentration** — higher dose ⇒ higher capacity utilization.
4. **Contact time / linear velocity** — lower velocity ⇒ higher capacity (more complete equilibrium).
5. **Temperature** — higher temperature improves kinetics and capacity.
6. **Selectivity of resin** for target ions over competing ions.
7. **Regeneration mode** — counter-current yields higher operating capacity than co-current.
8. **Bed depth** — deeper beds allow more complete utilization before breakthrough.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between strong acid cation and weak acid cation exchange resins.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Strong Acid Cation (SAC) | Weak Acid Cation (WAC) |
|---|---|---|
| Functional group | Sulfonic acid (–SO₃H) | Carboxylic acid (–COOH) |
| Operating pH range | 0–14 (entire range) | 6–14 only (limited) |
| Capacity | 1.8–2.2 eq/L | 3–4 eq/L (theoretical, higher) |
| Regenerant | HCl, H₂SO₄, NaCl | HCl only (must be H⁺ form) |
| Salt-splitting ability | Yes (splits neutral salts like NaCl) | No (only removes cations of bicarbonate salts) |
| Regeneration efficiency | 30–50% | 80–95% |
| Typical use | Total hardness, demineralization | Alkalinity reduction, partial softening |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle, construction, and working of a fixed-bed cation exchange unit for water softening**. Include a neat sketch and describe the five stages of the operational cycle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A softening plant uses a strong acid cation resin in the Na⁺ form. The column contains 1.5 m³ of resin with an operating capacity of 50 eq/L. The raw water has total hardness 250 mg/L as CaCO₃. The plant operates 8 hours per day at 20 m³/h. Calculate:
- (i) The service run length in days.
- (ii) The mass of NaCl regenerant required per cycle if 200 g NaCl per litre of resin is used.
- (iii) The regeneration efficiency if 100% regeneration requires 60 g NaCl/L resin (stoichiometric).

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
- **Principle:** The Na⁺ ions on the resin are exchanged for Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ in the water. The reaction is reversible.
- **Construction:** Cylindrical pressure vessel (rubber-lined steel or FRP), internal distributor, supporting gravel layer, resin bed, backwash collector, control valves, and a brine tank.
- **Working — Five Stages:**
  1. **Service Run:** Hard water flows downflow through the resin bed; hardness ions exchange with Na⁺.
  2. **Backwash:** Upflow water expands the bed 50–75% to remove suspended solids and reclassify resin.
  3. **Brining (Regeneration):** 8–12% NaCl solution flows downflow (co-current) or upflow (counter-current) for 30–45 min.
  4. **Displacement Rinse:** Slow rinse with raw water to push remaining brine through the bed.
  5. **Fast Rinse:** High-rate rinse to remove residual NaCl; effluent tested for hardness before returning to service.

**Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $V_r = 1.5\,\text{m}^3 = 1500\,\text{L}$
- $E_{op} = 50\,\text{eq/L}$
- $C_0 = 250\,\text{mg/L}$ as CaCO₃ = $250/50 = 5\,\text{meq/L} = 0.005\,\text{eq/L}$
- $Q = 20\,\text{m}^3/\text{h} = 20{,}000\,\text{L/h}$
- $C_e \approx 0$ (assumed for softening)
- $R_L = 200\,\text{g NaCl/L resin}$

**(i) Service run length:**

$$
t_s = \dfrac{E_{op} \cdot V_r}{Q \cdot (C_0 - C_e)} = \dfrac{50 \times 1500}{20{,}000 \times 0.005}
$$

$$
t_s = \dfrac{75{,}000}{100} = 750\,\text{h}
$$

Since the plant operates 8 h/day:

$$
\text{Service days} = \dfrac{750}{8} = 93.75 \approx 94\,\text{days}
$$

**(ii) Mass of NaCl per cycle:**

$$
m_{\text{NaCl}} = R_L \cdot V_r = 200 \times 1500 = 300{,}000\,\text{g} = 300\,\text{kg}
$$

**(iii) Regeneration efficiency:**

$$
\eta_R = \dfrac{60}{200} \times 100\% = 30\%
$$

This is a typical co-current regeneration efficiency. **Counter-current systems** would achieve 80–90% efficiency.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** $C_0$ must be converted from mg/L as CaCO₃ to eq/L by dividing by 50 (equivalent weight of CaCO₃). Marks deducted for using 100 (molecular weight) instead of 50.
> 2. **Missing operating hours:** Students often forget to convert the 750 h service run into days by dividing by 8 h/day operation.
> 3. **Stoichiometric vs. actual regenerant confusion:** Regeneration efficiency is calculated using **mass of NaCl per litre of resin**, not moles of Na⁺.
> 4. **Forgetting to state regeneration mode:** Always state whether co-current or counter-current regeneration is assumed — it directly affects the efficiency result.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **anion exchange process for nitrate removal from wastewater**. Discuss the choice of resin and regenerant, and explain why chloride-form resin is preferred for nitrate-selective exchange.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wastewater stream contains 100 mg/L NO₃⁻-N. The flow is 50 m³/day. The plant uses a strong base anion resin in Cl⁻ form with an operating capacity of 35 eq/L for NO₃⁻. Calculate:
- (i) The volume of resin required for a 24-hour service cycle.
- (ii) The mass of NaCl regenerant required per cycle, assuming a regenerant level of 150 g NaCl per litre of resin and an exchange stoichiometry of 1:1 (Cl⁻ for NO₃⁻).
- (iii) The volume of waste brine produced if the regenerant is applied as 10% NaCl solution.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
- **Process description:** Nitrate-selective anion exchange uses a strong base anion (SBA) resin in the Cl⁻ form. NO₃⁻ ions in the water exchange with Cl⁻ on the resin: $\text{R–Cl} + \text{NO}_3^- \rightleftharpoons \text{R–NO}_3 + \text{Cl}^-$.
- **Resin choice:** SBA resin with quaternary ammonium functional groups is preferred because it has a **higher selectivity for NO₃⁻ over Cl⁻** (selectivity sequence: $\text{NO}_3^- > \text{SO}_4^{2-} > \text{Cl}^- > \text{HCO}_3^-$).
- **Regenerant:** Concentrated NaCl (brine) is used. High chloride concentration and frequent regeneration keep the resin in working order.
- **Why Cl⁻ form?** The Cl⁻ form avoids the introduction of OH⁻ or HCO₃⁻ into the treated water (which would raise pH) and allows simple regeneration with cheap, food-grade NaCl.

**Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $C_0 = 100\,\text{mg/L}$ NO₃⁻-N
- Equivalent weight of N as NO₃⁻ = 14 g/eq (1 equivalent of N = 14 g of N)
- $C_0$ in eq/L = $100/14 = 7.143\,\text{meq/L} = 0.007143\,\text{eq/L}$
- $Q = 50\,\text{m}^3/\text{day} = 50{,}000\,\text{L/day}$
- $E_{op} = 35\,\text{eq/L}$
- $R_L = 150\,\text{g NaCl/L resin}$
- Stoichiometry: 1:1

**(i) Volume of resin required:**

$$
V_r = \dfrac{Q \cdot (C_0 - C_e) \cdot t}{E_{op}} = \dfrac{50{,}000 \times 0.007143 \times 1}{35}
$$

$$
V_r = \dfrac{357.15}{35} = 10.2\,\text{L}
$$

**(ii) Mass of NaCl per cycle:**

$$
m_{\text{NaCl}} = R_L \cdot V_r = 150 \times 10.2 = 1530\,\text{g} = 1.53\,\text{kg}
$$

**(iii) Volume of 10% NaCl solution:**

10% NaCl = 100 g NaCl per litre of solution.

$$
V_{\text{brine}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{NaCl}}}{\text{Concentration}} = \dfrac{1530\,\text{g}}{100\,\text{g/L}} = 15.3\,\text{L}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Equivalent weight of nitrogen:** For NO₃⁻-N, the equivalent weight is **14 g/eq (for N)** and **62 g/eq (for NO₃⁻ ion)**. If the concentration is reported "as N," divide by 14; if "as NO₃⁻," divide by 62. Marks lost when students use 62 for "NO₃⁻-N" data.
> 2. **Selectivity order:** The selectivity sequence is essential to justify why Cl⁻ form is used despite Cl⁻ having *lower* affinity than NO₃⁻ — the high Cl⁻ concentration during regeneration reverses the equilibrium.
> 3. **Brine disposal:** A complete answer must mention that the spent brine is a concentrated waste stream requiring separate treatment (e.g., biological denitrification or evaporation). Loss of 1 mark for omitting waste handling.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

- ☐ **Definition:** Ion exchange is a **reversible stoichiometric** exchange of ions between a solid (resin) and a liquid (wastewater) phase.
- ☐ **Resin classes:** **SAC, WAC, SBA, WBA** — know the functional groups and operating pH windows.
- ☐ **Cycle stages:** Service → Backwash → Brining → Displacement Rinse → Fast Rinse.
- ☐ **Key design equation:** $t_s = E_{op} \cdot V_r / [Q \cdot (C_0 - C_e)]$.
- ☐ **Selectivity sequence (cations):** $\text{Al}^{3+} > \text{Ca}^{2+} > \text{Mg}^{2+} > \text{Na}^+ > \text{H}^+$.
- ☐ **Selectivity sequence (anions):** $\text{SO}_4^{2-} > \text{NO}_3^- > \text{Cl}^- > \text{HCO}_3^- > \text{OH}^-$.
- ☐ **Higher valence ⇒ higher selectivity.** This is the **electrostatic valence rule** (Glueckauf).
- ☐ **Counter-current regeneration > co-current regeneration** in efficiency and treated water quality.
- ☐ **Operating capacity** is always **less than total capacity** due to incomplete regeneration and leakage.
- ☐ **MTZ** = Mass Transfer Zone; breakthrough occurs when MTZ reaches the column outlet.
- ☐ **Equivalent weight of CaCO₃ = 50 g/eq**; this is the universal reference for hardness calculations.
- ☐ **Major limitations:** Resin fouling by organics, suspended solids, and oxidants (Cl₂); high brine waste; high capital cost.
- ☐ **Applications to remember:** Boiler feed polishing, heavy metal recovery, NO₃⁻ removal, nuclear wastewater treatment, semiconductor rinse water.
- ☐ **Comparison trick:** Ion exchange ≠ adsorption. Ion exchange is **stoichiometric and reversible**; adsorption is **non-stoichiometric and based on surface forces**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy Tip:**
> When a question asks "explain ion exchange in tertiary treatment," structure your answer in **three blocks**:
> 1. **Principle + Resin chemistry** (3–4 lines).
> 2. **Cycle operation** (5 stages with brief purpose).
> 3. **Application + limitation** (1 real example + 1 drawback).
> This structure consistently scores **7+ out of 10** on KTU theory valuation grids.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Membrane Separation Techniques: Brief Description of MF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Membrane Separation Techniques: Brief Description of MF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Microfiltration (MF)** is a pressure-driven membrane separation process that employs a microporous membrane with a nominal pore size typically in the range of **0.1 µm to 10 µm** to physically separate suspended solids, bacteria, and large colloidal particles from a liquid stream. The separation mechanism is predominantly **size exclusion (sieving)**, where particles larger than the membrane pore diameter are retained on the feed side while the solvent and dissolved species smaller than the pores pass through as permeate.

In the context of advanced wastewater treatment under the KTU 2024 syllabus, MF occupies a critical pre-treatment position upstream of Ultrafiltration (UF), Nanofiltration (NF), and Reverse Osmosis (RO) units, where it protects the downstream finer membranes from fouling by removing turbidity-causing matter, oil emulsions, and microbial load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** MF is the membrane process with the largest pore size and operates at the lowest transmembrane pressure (typically **0.1 – 2.0 bar**). It is *not* capable of removing dissolved salts, viruses (<0.1 µm), or low-molecular-weight organics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pouring a mixture of **sand, gravel, and water** through a **kitchen strainer**. The strainer (analogous to the MF membrane) has holes just small enough to catch the gravel and sand (analogous to bacteria and suspended solids) while letting the water (analogous to the clarified permeate) drip through freely.

A more precise engineering analogy is the **coffee filter in a drip coffee maker**. The filter paper retains coffee grounds (particulates) but allows the dissolved coffee flavor compounds and water to pass. Similarly, the MF membrane retains suspended particles, pathogens, and turbidity-causing colloids but allows water, monovalent ions, and small dissolved molecules to permeate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** Unlike UF and RO, MF operates purely on a **physical sieving mechanism** — there is no diffusion through the membrane matrix in the classical solution-diffusion sense. The driving force is purely hydraulic pressure.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics for MF

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Pore Size | **0.1 µm – 10 µm** |
| Transmembrane Pressure (TMP) | **0.1 – 2.0 bar** |
| Operating Flux | **500 – 1500 L/m²·h** (LMH) |
| Removal Target | Suspended solids, bacteria, turbidity |
| Log Removal (Bacteria) | **~ 6 log** |
| Log Removal (Viruses) | **~ 0 – 1 log** (insignificant) |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, memorize the **pore size hierarchy** of membrane processes: MF (0.1–10 µm) > UF (0.001–0.1 µm) > NF (~0.001 µm) > RO (non-porous). This is the single most-tested concept in membrane separation modules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The MF process is governed by the following sequential logic:

1. **Feed Introduction:** Raw wastewater (or pre-screened effluent) is pumped into the membrane module at a controlled flow rate.
2. **Pressure Application:** A transmembrane pressure (TMP) is applied across the membrane. This pressure difference is the thermodynamic driving force for permeation.
3. **Size-Based Separation:** Particles with diameter greater than the membrane's nominal pore size are physically intercepted and retained on the feed (retentate) side.
4. **Permeate Collection:** Water and sub-micron solutes pass through the membrane pores and are collected as the clarified permeate stream.
5. **Retentate Recycle/Discharge:** The concentrated retentate is either recycled back to the feed tank or bled off as a waste concentrate (sludge).
6. **Fouling & Cleaning:** Over time, accumulated particles form a **cake layer** on the membrane surface, increasing resistance and reducing flux. Periodic **backwash** or **chemical cleaning** (Clean-in-Place, CIP) restores performance.

### 2.2 Membrane Configurations

MF membranes are fabricated in two principal geometries:

- **Hollow Fiber (HF):** Bundles of small-diameter tubes (inner diameter ~ 0.5 – 2 mm). Highest packing density (up to **~ 30,000 m²/m³**).
- **Flat Sheet (Plate & Frame or Spiral Wound):** Membrane cast as flat sheets and assembled into cassettes. Lower packing density but easier to clean.

### 2.3 Operating Modes

- **Dead-End Filtration:** Feed flow is perpendicular to the membrane; all feed passes through (100 % recovery per pass). Used for low-solids streams.
- **Cross-Flow Filtration:** Feed flows tangentially across the membrane surface, scouring the cake layer and reducing fouling. Recovery per pass is typically **50 – 90 %**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **high-yield** and frequently tested in KTU ESE examinations.

$$
J = \frac{V}{A \cdot t} = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_{\text{total}}}
$$

$$
R_{\text{total}} = R_{m} + R_{c} + R_{f}
$$

$$
\text{TMP} = \frac{P_{\text{in}} + P_{\text{out}}}{2} - P_{\text{permeate}}
$$

$$
\text{Rejection}\ (\%)\, R = \left(1 - \frac{C_{p}}{C_{f}}\right) \times 100
$$

| Symbol | Definition | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $J$ | Permeate flux | L/m²·h (LMH) or m³/m²·s |
| $V$ | Volume of permeate collected | L or m³ |
| $A$ | Effective membrane area | m² |
| $\Delta P$ | Transmembrane pressure (TMP) | Pa or bar |
| $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity of permeate | Pa·s |
| $R_{m}$ | Intrinsic membrane resistance | m⁻¹ |
| $R_{c}$ | Cake layer resistance | m⁻¹ |
| $R_{f}$ | Fouling resistance | m⁻¹ |
| $R_{\text{total}}$ | Total hydraulic resistance | m⁻¹ |
| $C_{f}, C_{p}$ | Feed and permeate solute concentrations | mg/L |
| $P_{\text{in}}, P_{\text{out}}$ | Inlet and outlet feed pressures | bar |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, the rejection formula uses plain parentheses around the percentage. Do **not** confuse rejection with **recovery**, which is defined as:
>
>$$
>\text{Recovery}\ (\%)\, = \left(\frac{Q_{p}}{Q_{f}}\right) \times 100
>$$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

MF is deployed in the following municipal and industrial wastewater applications:

- **Pretreatment for RO** in water reclamation plants (sizing reduction of fouling load).
- **Tertiary treatment** of secondary effluents to meet bathing water or industrial cooling reuse standards.
- **Oil-water emulsion separation** in petroleum refineries and metal-finishing industries.
- **Biomass separation** in Membrane Bioreactors (MBR), where MF replaces the secondary clarifier.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Permeate Flux Using Darcy's Law

The governing transport equation for MF is derived from **Darcy's Law** for flow through a porous medium. The volumetric flow rate $Q$ through a porous medium is proportional to the pressure gradient and inversely proportional to the fluid viscosity.

**Step 1:** State Darcy's Law for volumetric flow rate through a porous membrane of area $A$ and thickness $L$:

$$
Q = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_{\text{total}}}
$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that flux $J$ is defined as volumetric flow rate per unit membrane area:

$$
J = \frac{Q}{A}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $Q$ from Step 1 into the flux definition of Step 2 and cancel $A$:

$$
J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_{\text{total}}}
$$

**Step 4:** For a *clean* (fouling-free) membrane, the only resistance is the intrinsic membrane resistance $R_{m}$, defined for a porous medium by the Kozeny-Carman relation as:

$$
R_{m} = \frac{k \cdot L}{\varepsilon^{3} \cdot d_{p}^{2}}
$$

where $k$ is the Kozeny-Carman constant (typically $\sim 5$ for porous membranes), $L$ is membrane thickness, $\varepsilon$ is porosity, and $d_{p}$ is the effective pore diameter.

**Step 5:** Substitute $R_{m}$ into the clean-water flux equation:

$$
J_{\text{clean}} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot \varepsilon^{3} \cdot d_{p}^{2}}{\mu \cdot k \cdot L}
$$

**Step 6:** During real wastewater filtration, the total resistance is the **resistance-in-series** sum:

$$
R_{\text{total}} = R_{m} + R_{c} + R_{f}
$$

**Step 7:** Therefore, the operating flux during filtration becomes:

$$
J_{\text{operational}} = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot (R_{m} + R_{c} + R_{f})}
$$

This is the foundational equation upon which all KTU numerical problems on membrane flux are based.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A hollow-fiber MF module with effective area $A = 25$ m² is used to filter secondary effluent. The applied TMP is **0.8 bar**, the permeate viscosity $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, and the total resistance $R_{\text{total}} = 2.5 \times 10^{11}$ m⁻¹. Calculate:
(a) The permeate flux in LMH.
(b) The daily permeate volume in m³/day.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert TMP from bar to Pascals:
$$
\Delta P = 0.8 \times 10^{5} = 8 \times 10^{4} \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the flux equation:
$$
J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{8 \times 10^{4}}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3})(2.5 \times 10^{11})}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the numerator and denominator separately:
$$
\text{Numerator} = 8 \times 10^{4}
$$
$$
\text{Denominator} = 2.5 \times 10^{8}
$$

**Step 4:** Divide:
$$
J = \frac{8 \times 10^{4}}{2.5 \times 10^{8}} = 3.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 / \text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to LMH (multiply by 1000 to go from m³ to L, and by 3600 to go from seconds to hours):
$$
J = 3.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000 \times 3600 = 1152 \text{ LMH}
$$

**Step 6:** Calculate daily permeate volume:
$$
V_{\text{daily}} = J \times A \times t = (3.2 \times 10^{-4}) \times 25 \times (24 \times 3600)
$$

**Step 7:** Evaluate the time factor:
$$
t = 86400 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 8:** Final calculation:
$$
V_{\text{daily}} = 3.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 25 \times 86400 = 691.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Answers:** (a) $J \approx 1152$ LMH; (b) $V \approx 691.2$ m³/day.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison with Other Membrane Processes

| Property | MF | UF | NF | RO |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pore size (µm) | 0.1 – 10 | 0.001 – 0.1 | ~0.001 | Non-porous |
| TMP (bar) | 0.1 – 2 | 1 – 5 | 5 – 20 | 10 – 80 |
| Separation mechanism | Sieving | Sieving | Sieving + charge | Solution-diffusion |
| Removes | Bacteria, TSS | Viruses, proteins | Divalent salts | Monovalent salts |
| Virus removal | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of MF

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the functional architecture of an MF unit integrated into an advanced wastewater treatment train. Each node uses an alphanumeric identifier prefixed with letters and contains clean uppercase text only, per the compilation safeguard rules.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["RAW WASTEWATER INLET"]
    nodeB["COARSE SCREEN 5 MM"]
    nodeC["EQUALIZATION TANK"]
    nodeD["MF FEED PUMP"]
    nodeE["MF MEMBRANE MODULE"]
    nodeF["PERMEATE TO UF OR RO"]
    nodeG["RETENTATE RECYCLE"]
    nodeH["BACKWASH TANK"]
    nodeI["CIP CLEANING UNIT"]
    nodeJ["WASTE CONCENTRATE"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeC
    nodeH --> nodeE
    nodeI --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeJ
```

### 4.2 Dead-End vs Cross-Flow Filtration Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DE["DEAD END MODE"]
        nodeDA["FEED INLET"]
        nodeDB["MEMBRANE SURFACE"]
        nodeDC["CAKE LAYER BUILDS UP"]
        nodeDD["PERMEATE OUTLET"]
        nodeDA --> nodeDB
        nodeDB --> nodeDC
        nodeDB --> nodeDD
    end

    subgraph CF["CROSS FLOW MODE"]
        nodeCA["FEED INLET"]
        nodeCB["TANGENTIAL FLOW VELOCITY"]
        nodeCC["MEMBRANE SURFACE"]
        nodeCD["THIN CAKE LAYER"]
        nodeCE["PERMEATE OUTLET"]
        nodeCF["RETENTATE OUTLET"]
        nodeCA --> nodeCB
        nodeCB --> nodeCC
        nodeCC --> nodeCD
        nodeCC --> nodeCE
        nodeCC --> nodeCF
    end
```

### 4.3 Resistance-in-Series Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR1["FEED WATER TMP DRIVING FORCE"]
    nodeR2["RM MEMBRANE RESISTANCE"]
    nodeR3["RC CAKE LAYER RESISTANCE"]
    nodeR4["RF FOULING RESISTANCE"]
    nodeR5["PERMEATE FLUX J"]

    nodeR1 --> nodeR2
    nodeR2 --> nodeR3
    nodeR3 --> nodeR4
    nodeR4 --> nodeR5
```

> [!NOTE]
> In **dead-end** filtration, $R_{c}$ grows continuously until backwash is required. In **cross-flow** filtration, the tangential velocity creates a shear-induced diffusion that keeps $R_{c}$ at a quasi-steady value, allowing longer operational cycles between cleanings.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*  
Define Microfiltration. State its nominal pore size range and give **two** examples of contaminants removed by MF.

**Model Answer:**

Microfiltration (MF) is a pressure-driven membrane separation process that uses a porous membrane with a nominal pore size in the range of **0.1 µm to 10 µm** to separate suspended solids, bacteria, and large colloidal particles from a liquid stream.

**Two contaminants removed:**
1. Suspended solids and turbidity-causing colloids.
2. Bacteria and protozoan cysts (e.g., *E. coli*, *Giardia*).

*(3 marks – 1 for definition, 1 for pore size, 1 for examples)*

---

**Q2.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*  
Distinguish between **dead-end** and **cross-flow** filtration modes in MF.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Dead-End | Cross-Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Flow direction | Perpendicular to membrane | Tangential to membrane |
| Cake layer | Builds up continuously | Limited by shear |
| Recovery per pass | ~100 % | 50 – 90 % |
| Fouling rate | High | Low |
| Typical application | Low-solids streams | High-solids streams |

*(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### Question A (14 Marks) *[KTU University Exam – July 2024]*

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of Microfiltration (MF). List any **four** applications of MF in wastewater treatment. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An MF membrane with area $A = 40$ m² operates at TMP of **1.2 bar**. The clean-water resistance is $R_{m} = 1.0 \times 10^{11}$ m⁻¹, the cake resistance after 1 hour of operation is $R_{c} = 4.0 \times 10^{11}$ m⁻¹, and the fouling resistance is $R_{f} = 0.5 \times 10^{11}$ m⁻¹. The permeate viscosity is $1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Calculate:  
(i) The clean-water flux in LMH.  
(ii) The operating flux after 1 hour in LMH.  
(iii) The percentage decline in flux. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle:**

Feed water is pressurized and passed through a microporous membrane. Particles larger than the pore size (typically > 0.1 µm) are retained on the feed side, while water and dissolved species smaller than the pores pass through as permeate. The separation is purely physical (size exclusion/sieving).

A labelled block diagram should show: Feed tank → Pump → MF module (with inlet, outlet, permeate) → Permeate collection tank → Retentate return/discharge.

**Four applications:**
1. Pretreatment for RO/UF in water reclamation.
2. Tertiary polishing of secondary effluents.
3. Biomass retention in Membrane Bioreactors (MBR).
4. Oil-water emulsion separation in industrial wastewater.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert TMP:
$$
\Delta P = 1.2 \times 10^{5} = 1.2 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}
$$

**(i) Clean-water flux:**
$$
J_{\text{clean}} = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_{m}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{5}}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3})(1.0 \times 10^{11})} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}
$$

Convert to LMH:
$$
J_{\text{clean}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 \times 1000 = 4320 \text{ LMH}
$$

**(ii) Operating flux:**
$$
R_{\text{total}} = (1.0 + 4.0 + 0.5) \times 10^{11} = 5.5 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}^{-1}
$$

$$
J_{\text{op}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{5}}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3})(5.5 \times 10^{11})} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{5}}{5.5 \times 10^{8}} = 2.18 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}
$$

Convert to LMH:
$$
J_{\text{op}} = 2.18 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.6 \times 10^{6} = 785.4 \text{ LMH}
$$

**(iii) Percentage decline:**
$$
\% \text{Decline} = \frac{J_{\text{clean}} - J_{\text{op}}}{J_{\text{clean}}} \times 100 = \frac{4320 - 785.4}{4320} \times 100 \approx 81.8 \ \%
$$

**Final Answers:** (i) 4320 LMH; (ii) 785.4 LMH; (iii) ~81.8 % decline.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]*

**(a)** Explain the **resistance-in-series model** for MF. Describe the contribution of each resistance component to the total resistance. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A wastewater treatment plant uses an MF unit to filter a feed with TSS = **200 mg/L**. The permeate TSS is measured at **5 mg/L**. The permeate flow rate is **1500 m³/day** and the feed flow rate is **1800 m³/day**. Calculate the rejection percentage and the recovery percentage. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Resistance-in-Series Model:**

According to Darcy's Law applied to membrane filtration, the permeate flux is inversely proportional to the total resistance, which is the sum of three components in series:

$$
R_{\text{total}} = R_{m} + R_{c} + R_{f}
$$

- **$R_{m}$ (Membrane Resistance):** Intrinsic resistance of the clean, unused membrane due to its pore structure. Determined by clean-water flux test.
- **$R_{c}$ (Cake Layer Resistance):** Resistance offered by the deposited layer of rejected particles on the membrane surface. Increases with time and is a function of feed solids concentration and cross-flow velocity.
- **$R_{f}$ (Fouling Resistance):** Resistance due to irreversible adsorption of dissolved species, colloidal fouling, and pore plugging. Cannot be removed by simple backwash; requires chemical CIP.

The operating flux is then given by:
$$
J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, (R_{m} + R_{c} + R_{f})}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate Rejection:
$$
R = \left(1 - \frac{C_{p}}{C_{f}}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{5}{200}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - 0.025\right) \times 100 = 97.5 \ \%
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate Recovery:
$$
\text{Recovery} = \left(\frac{Q_{p}}{Q_{f}}\right) \times 100 = \left(\frac{1500}{1800}\right) \times 100 = 83.33 \ \%
$$

**Final Answers:** Rejection = **97.5 %**; Recovery = **83.33 %**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Forgetting to convert bar → Pa (1 bar = $10^{5}$ Pa) before using in SI flux equations. This single error can wipe out the full 7 marks in Part B numericals.
> 2. **Confusing Rejection with Recovery:** Rejection is concentration-based; recovery is flow-based. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for swapping these definitions.
> 3. **Forgetting the Permeate Viscosity:** When the problem gives $\mu$, it is a hint that the *flux equation* (not just Darcy's raw form) must be used. Skipping $\mu$ loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Sketch Quality:** In Part B (a), a **labelled sketch** of the MF module (with arrows for feed, permeate, and retentate) carries 2 of the 7 marks. Omitting it is a frequent mark-loss trigger.
> 5. **LMH vs m³/m²·s:** Always state the final unit explicitly. Mixing m/s and LMH without conversion is treated as an incomplete answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- MF operates by **physical sieving** with pore sizes **0.1 – 10 µm**.
- Driving force is **transmembrane pressure** (typically **0.1 – 2.0 bar**).
- MF removes **suspended solids, bacteria, turbidity** — but **not dissolved salts or viruses**.
- Governing equation: $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_{\text{total}}}$, where $R_{\text{total}} = R_{m} + R_{c} + R_{f}$.
- **Cross-flow** mode reduces fouling relative to dead-end mode via tangential shear.
- MF is widely used as **pretreatment for RO**, in **MBRs**, and for **oil-water separation**.
- Rejection $\neq$ Recovery — always identify which is asked before substituting values.
- **1 bar = $10^{5}$ Pa** — keep this conversion at your fingertips for numerical problems.
- Cake layer resistance $R_{c}$ grows with time; it is the dominant long-term resistance in dead-end operation.
- Fouling resistance $R_{f}$ is **irreversible** — removed only by chemical Clean-in-Place (CIP).
- Typical clean-water flux of an MF module: **~1000 – 5000 LMH**; operating flux drops to **~500 – 1500 LMH** during real wastewater filtration.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NF Membranes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** NF Membranes ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Nanofiltration (NF)** is a **pressure-driven membrane separation process** that occupies the operational window between **Ultrafiltration (UF)** and **Reverse Osmosis (RO)**.
