It is defined by the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) and the KTU 2024 module descriptor as a process utilizing membranes with a **nominal pore size of 0.5 – 2 nm** and a **Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO) in the range of 200 – 1000 Da**, capable of retaining multivalent ions and low-molecular-weight organic compounds while allowing partial passage of monovalent ions and water. **Key Operating & Geometric Metrics (KTU 2024 Module-3 Specification):** | Parameter | Typical Value | |---|---| | Nominal Pore Size | **0.5 – 2 nm** | | Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO) | **200 – 1000 Da** | | Operating Trans-membrane Pressure (TMP) | **5 – 40 bar** (typical: 10 – 25 bar) | | Flux (Jw) | **10 – 50 L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ (LMH)** | | Membrane Configuration | Spiral-wound, Hollow-fibre, Tubular | --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Sieve with a Bouncer" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Discriminating Bouncer at a Club:** > Imagine a nightclub door where the bouncer follows **two different rules simultaneously**: > 1. **Size Rule (Sieve Effect):** Anyone visibly larger than the doorway (big organic molecules, colour/dye molecules, viruses) is turned back. > 2. **Charge Rule (Donnan Exclusion):** Even if someone *could* fit through the door, the bouncer personally dislikes certain "tattoos" (electrical charges). Negatively-charged bouncers (typical NF polyamide surface) refuse entry to **negatively charged** species (e.g., SO₄²⁻, Cl⁻ partially, dye anions) but happily let through **neutral** species (e.g., water) and weakly charged **monovalent** ions (Na⁺ partially). > > Result: Multivalent ions (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺) are removed almost completely; monovalent ions (Na⁺, Cl⁻) are only partially removed; water passes freely. This is precisely why NF is the *preferred* technology for **water softening** — it removes the hardness (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺) while preserving some natural mineral character. --- ### 1.3 Why NF — A Unique Mid-Range Tool > [!IMPORTANT] > Unlike **RO** (which rejects nearly everything, including monovalent salts, at the cost of very high pressure ~ 50–80 bar), and unlike **UF** (which only rejects colloids and macromolecules > 1000 Da), **NF offers a "selective" rejection** at a moderate pressure. This makes it: > - **Energy-efficient** (lower TMP than RO → **30–50 % energy savings**). > - **Selective** (multivalent vs. monovalent ion discrimination). > - **High-flux** (higher permeability than RO membranes). --- ### 1.4 Classification of NF within the Pressure-Driven Membrane Family $$ \underbrace{\text{MF}}_{\text{0.1–10 μm}} \;\rightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{UF}}_{\text{0.001–0.1 μm}} \;\rightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{NF}}_{\text{0.5–2 nm}} \;\rightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{RO}}_{\text{<0.5 nm, dense}} $$ ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step The NF process is governed by **two concurrent physical mechanisms** working in parallel: **Step 1: Pressure-Driven Bulk Flow** - Feed water is pressurized to a **TMP of 5–40 bar** using a high-pressure pump. - Water and small molecules are forced *tangentially* (cross-flow) across the membrane surface. **Step 2: Sieving (Steric Hindrance) Mechanism** - Solutes whose **hydrodynamic radius (rs)** is greater than the membrane pore radius (rp) are physically rejected. - This is quantified by the **Steric Hindrance Factor**, governed by the ratio $\lambda = r_s / r_p$. **Step 3: Donnan (Electrostatic) Exclusion** - Most commercial NF membranes carry a **net negative surface charge** (carboxyl groups on polyamide films) at neutral pH. - Co-ions (ions of the same charge as the membrane) are **repelled**. - Counter-ions are also retained indirectly to satisfy **electroneutrality** of the permeate. **Step 4: Dielectric Exclusion (Secondary Effect)** - Inside the nano-pore, the solvent's dielectric constant drops, making ion partitioning unfavourable. - This effect amplifies rejection of all ions, especially multivalents. **Step 5: Permeate Collection & Concentrate Recycle** - The **permeate** (low-TDS water) is collected. - The **retentate/concentrate** exits at higher salt concentration; in wastewater plants, a fraction is bled (purge) to control scaling, and the rest is recycled to maintain cross-flow velocity. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations are **the most frequently tested in KTU ESE Module-3 questions on NF.** Memorize the variables, units, and sign conventions. | # | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Permeate Flux:** $J_w = L_p \cdot (\Delta P - \sigma \cdot \Delta \pi)$ | $J_w$ = flux (L·m⁻²·h⁻¹); $L_p$ = water permeability (L·m⁻²·h⁻¹·bar⁻¹); $\Delta P$ = TMP (bar); $\sigma$ = reflection coefficient (0 to 1); $\Delta \pi$ = osmotic pressure difference (bar) | Calculates pure water + solute-corrected flux. | | 2 | **Observed Rejection:** $R_{\text{obs}} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}$ | $C_p$ = permeate concentration; $C_f$ = feed concentration (both mg/L) | Quantifies membrane selectivity. | | 3 | **Intrinsic (True) Rejection:** $R_{\text{int}} = \dfrac{C_m - C_p}{C_m}$ | $C_m$ = concentration at membrane surface (mg/L) | Removes the effect of concentration polarization. | | 4 | **System Recovery:** $Y = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100\;\text{(\%)}$ | $Q_p$ = permeate flow; $Q_f$ = feed flow (m³/h) | Determines volumetric efficiency. | | 5 | **Concentration Polarization Factor:** $\dfrac{C_m - C_p}{C_b - C_p} = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{J_w}{k}\right)$ | $C_b$ = bulk concentration; $k$ = mass transfer coefficient (m/s) | Predicts fouling tendency. | | 6 | **Solution-Diffusion (RO/NF, no pore flow):** $J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$; $J_s = B(C_f - C_p)$ | $A$ = water permeability constant; $B$ = solute permeability constant; $J_s$ = solute flux | Fundamental transport model. | | 7 | **Solute Permeability from Rejection:** $B = J_w \cdot \dfrac{1 - R}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\exp(J_w/k) - R}$ | — | Used to characterize NF membranes experimentally. | | 8 | **Osmotic Pressure (Van't Hoff, dilute):** $\pi = i \cdot C \cdot R \cdot T$ | $i$ = van't Hoff factor; $C$ = molar conc.; $R$ = 0.08314 L·bar·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹; $T$ = K | Estimates $\Delta\pi$ for flux correction. | | 9 | **Resistance-in-Series Model:** $J_w = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot (R_m + R_f + R_{cp})}$ | $R_m, R_f, R_{cp}$ = membrane, fouling, polarization resistances (m⁻¹); $\mu$ = viscosity | Quantifies flux decline. | | 10 | **TMP Definition (Cross-flow):** $\Delta P = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{p}$ | $P_{in}, P_{out}, P_p$ = inlet, outlet, permeate pressure (bar) | Corrects for pressure drop along the module. | > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Cue:** For **NF** (loose, charged membranes), the **Spiegler–Kedem** model is often preferred over Solution-Diffusion, but KTU 2024 questions typically expect only Equations 1–4 plus the **Van't Hoff** relation (Eq. 8). --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial Wastewater Reuse:** Dye-house effluent (textile) — NF rejects > 99 % of reactive dyes (MW > 400 Da) and partially desalinates the water. - **Water Softening:** Replacement for lime-soda softening; removes 80–95 % Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ at half the energy cost of RO. - **Heavy Metal Removal:** NF rejects > 95 % of Cr(VI), Ni²⁺, Cu²⁺ as anionic complexes. - **Pharmaceutical Effluent:** Rejection of antibiotics (MW 300–700 Da). - **Landfill Leachate Pre-treatment:** NF removes humic acids, COD, heavy metals before RO polishing. - **Dairy Industry:** Lactose and protein recovery from whey. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Permeate Flux Equation (Kedem–Katchalsky / Spiegler–Kedem Framework) The starting point is the **dissipation function** for irreversible transport across a membrane. For a single solute in water, the volumetric flux $J_v$ and solute flux $J_s$ are coupled through: $$ J_v = L_p \cdot (\Delta P - \sigma \cdot \Delta \pi) $$ **Derivation Walk-through:** **Step 1: Define the driving force.** For a dilute solution, the chemical potential gradient that drives water through the membrane is the difference between hydraulic and osmotic pressure: $$ \text{Driving Force} = \Delta P - \sigma \cdot \Delta \pi $$ **Step 2: Define the proportionality.** The flow is linearly proportional to the driving force, where the proportionality constant is the **pure water permeability** $L_p$ (a property of the membrane alone, measured with pure water at standard conditions, e.g., **25 °C**): $$ J_w = L_p \cdot (\text{Driving Force}) $$ **Step 3: Combine.** Substituting Step 1 into Step 2: $$ J_w = L_p \cdot (\Delta P - \sigma \cdot \Delta \pi) $$ **Step 4: Interpret $\sigma$ (Staverman reflection coefficient).** It quantifies how "tight" the membrane is to the solute: - $\sigma = 1$ → membrane perfectly rejects solute (ideal semi-permeable, like RO). - $\sigma = 0$ → membrane offers no selectivity (solute passes freely). - For NF, $\sigma$ typically lies in the range **0.5 – 0.95**, depending on ion charge and size. **Step 5: Convert to practical units.** Multiply $L_p$ by **3600 s/h** if you want flux in **L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ (LMH)** rather than m/s. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Observed vs. Intrinsic Rejection **Step 1: Define observed rejection** as the bulk ratio (what you actually measure in the lab from feed and permeate samples): $$ R_{\text{obs}} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f} $$ **Step 2: Recognize that the concentration at the membrane wall ($C_m$) is HIGHER than in the bulk ($C_f$)** due to concentration polarization. The relationship is given by the **film theory** (Eq. 5 in the Formula Sheet). **Step 3: Derive the intrinsic rejection** by mass balance on the boundary layer: $$ R_{\text{int}} = \frac{C_m - C_p}{C_m} $$ **Step 4: Link observed and intrinsic rejection** by eliminating $C_m$. From the film-theory equation (Eq. 5): $$ C_m = C_p + (C_b - C_p) \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{J_w}{k}\right) $$ Substituting into $R_{\text{int}}$: $$ R_{\text{int}} = \frac{(C_b - C_p)\cdot \exp(J_w/k)}{C_p + (C_b - C_p)\cdot \exp(J_w/k)} $$ **Step 5: Simplify using $R_{\text{obs}} = 1 - C_p/C_f$** (where $C_b = C_f$ for a well-mixed module). After algebraic manipulation: $$ R_{\text{int}} = \frac{R_{\text{obs}} \cdot \exp(J_w/k)}{1 - R_{\text{obs}} + R_{\text{obs}} \cdot \exp(J_w/k)} $$ **Step 6: Engineering meaning.** When $J_w$ is high (high pressure) or $k$ is low (poor cross-flow), $\exp(J_w/k) \to \infty$, so $R_{\text{int}} \to 1$ regardless of $R_{\text{obs}}$. This tells us the membrane has a finite intrinsic selectivity — what we see as "low rejection" is often just **concentration polarization**, not a bad membrane. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A flat-sheet NF membrane has a pure water permeability $L_p = 5\,\text{L·m}^{-2}\text{·h}^{-1}\text{·bar}^{-1}$. The feed contains **Na₂SO₄ at 2000 mg/L** (assume complete dissociation, $i = 3$). Operating conditions: TMP = 15 bar, $T = 298$ K, reflection coefficient $\sigma = 0.85$. Estimate the **steady-state permeate flux** if the permeate concentration is 80 mg/L. **Step 1: Calculate the feed osmotic pressure using Van't Hoff.** Molar mass of Na₂SO₄ = **142 g/mol**. Molar concentration: $$ C_f = \frac{2000 \,\text{mg/L}}{142 \cdot 10^3 \,\text{mg/mol}} = 0.01408 \,\text{mol/L} $$ $$ \pi_f = i \cdot C_f \cdot R \cdot T = 3 \times 0.01408 \times 0.08314 \times 298 $$ $$ \pi_f = 1.046 \,\text{bar} $$ **Step 2: Permeate osmotic pressure.** $$ C_p = \frac{80}{142 \cdot 10^3} = 5.63 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{mol/L} $$ $$ \pi_p = 3 \times 5.63 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.08314 \times 298 = 0.0418 \,\text{bar} $$ **Step 3: Net driving pressure.** $$ \Delta P_{\text{eff}} = \Delta P - \sigma \cdot (\pi_f - \pi_p) = 15 - 0.85 \times (1.046 - 0.0418) $$ $$ \Delta P_{\text{eff}} = 15 - 0.85 \times 1.004 = 15 - 0.854 = 14.146 \,\text{bar} $$ **Step 4: Permeate flux.** $$ J_w = L_p \cdot \Delta P_{\text{eff}} = 5 \times 14.146 = 70.73 \,\text{LMH} $$ **Step 5: Observed rejection.** $$ R_{\text{obs}} = 1 - \frac{80}{2000} = 0.96 \; \text{or} \; 96\,\% $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Despite a TMP of only **15 bar** (much lower than RO's 50–80 bar), the NF membrane achieves **96 % rejection** of a divalent salt (Na₂SO₄) because $\sigma$ is high. This is the engineering "sweet spot" of NF. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: NF vs. RO vs. UF | Feature | UF | **NF** | RO | |---|---|---|---| | Pore size | 0.001 – 0.1 μm | **0.5 – 2 nm** | < 0.5 nm (dense) | | MWCO (Da) | 1,000 – 500,000 | **200 – 1,000** | < 200 | | TMP (bar) | 0.5 – 5 | **5 – 40** | 15 – 80 | | Mechanism | Size exclusion | **Size + Charge (Donnan)** | Solution-diffusion | | Monovalent salt removal | < 5 % | **20 – 80 %** | > 99 % | | Multivalent salt removal | < 5 % | **> 95 %** | > 99 % | | Typical flux (LMH) | 50 – 1,000 | **10 – 50** | 5 – 30 | | Relative energy cost | Low | **Medium** | High | ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an NF System (Cross-Flow Mode) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Feed Tank
Raw Wastewater] --> B[Feed Pump
Low-Shear Centrifugal] B --> C[Pre-Treatment Unit
Cartridge Filter 5 micron] C --> D[High Pressure Pump
Multistage Centrifugal 5 to 40 bar] D --> E[Nanofiltration Membrane Module
Spiral Wound or Hollow Fibre] E --> F[Permeate Outlet
Low TDS Treated Water] E --> G[Retentate Stream
Concentrate Recycle] G --> H[Pressure Control Valve
Maintains Cross Flow Velocity] H --> I[Bleed Purge Stream
Reject for Disposal] G --> J[Recycle Line
Returns to Feed Tank] J --> A F --> K[Treated Water Storage
For Reuse or Discharge] ``` ### 4.2 Mechanistic Diagram — Solute Behaviour at the NF Membrane Surface ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FEED_SIDE [Feed Side High Pressure] F1[Divalent Ions Ca2+ Mg2+ SO42-] F2[Monovalent Ions Na+ Cl-] F3[Organic Molecules MW greater than 200 Da] F4[Water Molecules H2O] end subgraph MEMBRANE [NF Membrane 0.5 to 2 nm Pores] M1[Negatively Charged Polyamide Skin Layer] M2[Nano Pores with Reduced Dielectric Constant] end subgraph PERMEATE_SIDE [Permeate Side Low Pressure] P1[Water plus Partial Monovalent Ions] end F1 -- Rejected by Donnan and Size --> M1 F2 -- Partially Permeate --> M2 F3 -- Rejected by Sieving --> M1 F4 -- Free Passage --> M2 M1 --> PERMEATE_SIDE M2 --> P1 ``` ### 4.3 Process Flow — Charge-Based Selectivity Sequence ```mermaid flowchart LR start([Multivalent Anion
SO42-]) --> rej1[Rejected greater than 99 percent] rej1 --> rej2[Multivalent Cation Ca2+ Mg2+] rej2 --> rej3[Rejected greater than 95 percent] rej3 --> rej4[Monovalent Anion Cl-] rej4 --> rej5[Rejected 20 to 70 percent] rej5 --> rej6[Monovalent Cation Na+] rej6 --> rej7[Rejected 10 to 50 percent] rej7 --> final([Water Molecules
Pass Freely]) ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** Node identifiers are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `F1`, `M1`) to comply with Mermaid syntax rules. All labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text without any embedded markdown formatting. --- ### 4.4 Membrane Cross-Section Schematic (Thin-Film Composite) | Layer (top → bottom) | Typical Thickness | Function | |---|---|---| | **1. Polyamide Skin (Barrier Layer)** | **50 – 200 nm** | Selective separation; carries -COOH groups → negative charge | | **2. Polysulfone Support** | **25 – 50 μm** | Mechanical strength; porous intermediate | | **3. Polyester Non-Woven Backing** | **100 – 200 μm** | Structural support; handles high pressure | ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEBBT725]** **"Define Nanofiltration. State any two distinguishing features of NF membranes that differentiate them from RO membranes."** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Nanofiltration (NF) is a pressure-driven membrane separation process with membrane pore size ranging from **0.5 to 2 nm** and a Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO) of **200 – 1000 Da**, positioned between UF and RO in the membrane spectrum. **Two Distinguishing Features (vs. RO):** 1. **Lower Operating Pressure:** NF operates at **5 – 40 bar**, whereas RO requires **15 – 80 bar**, because NF membranes are more permeable and rely partially on charge (Donnan) exclusion, which is energy-independent. 2. **Selectivity Based on Charge:** NF membranes, typically made of polyamide, carry a **net negative surface charge** that preferentially rejects multivalent ions (e.g., SO₄²⁻, Ca²⁺) through **Donnan exclusion**, while partially allowing monovalent ions (Na⁺, Cl⁻) to pass. RO has negligible charge-based selectivity and rejects almost all ions equally. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the two features)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEBBT725]** **"Explain the term 'Donnan Exclusion' in the context of Nanofiltration."** **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Donnan Exclusion** is an **electrostatic rejection mechanism** that occurs at the surface of a charged membrane. When an NF membrane (e.g., polyamide) carries a fixed negative charge (due to -COOH groups), it repels **co-ions** (anions of the same charge) from the membrane interface, while **counter-ions** (cations) are partially retained indirectly to maintain **electroneutrality** in the permeate stream. The net result is a higher rejection of **multivalent ions** (which have stronger electrostatic interactions) compared to monovalent ions. This is why NF is highly effective for removing Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, and SO₄²⁻ in water-softening applications. *(3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, OEBBT725, Module 3]** **(a)** With the help of a neat labelled schematic, describe the construction and working principle of a **Thin-Film Composite (TFC) Nanofiltration membrane** used in wastewater treatment. Explain the role of each layer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A feed solution containing **3000 mg/L of MgSO₄** is processed through an NF membrane at **TMP = 20 bar** and **T = 298 K**. The permeate concentration is measured as **75 mg/L**. Given the following data, compute the **observed rejection**, the **osmotic pressure difference**, and the **permeate flux**: - $L_p = 6$ L·m⁻²·h⁻¹·bar⁻¹ - $\sigma = 0.92$ - $i = 2$ (for MgSO₄) - Molar mass of MgSO₄ = **120 g/mol** **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question A** **(a) Construction & Working of TFC NF Membrane (7 marks):** **Construction (Schematic):** A TFC NF membrane is a **three-layer composite** structure manufactured by interfacial polymerization: | Layer | Material | Thickness | Function | |---|---|---|---| | **Top (Skin) Layer** | Cross-linked aromatic polyamide (formed by reaction of *m*-phenylenediamine with trimesoyl chloride) | **50 – 200 nm** | Selective barrier; controls rejection | | **Middle Support** | Polysulfone (PSf) ultrafiltration membrane | **25 – 50 μm** | Mechanical strength, porous support | | **Bottom Backing** | Polyester non-woven fabric (e.g., PET) | **100 – 200 μm** | Structural backbone to handle 40 bar pressure | **Working Principle:** 1. **Feed pressurization:** Wastewater is pressurized to 5–40 bar and flows **tangentially** across the membrane surface. 2. **Selective sorption:** Water and small monovalent species dissolve into the dense polyamide skin. 3. **Diffusion through skin:** Water molecules diffuse via a **solution-diffusion** mechanism, while multivalent ions are blocked by the dense polymer network and the **negative charge** of -COOH groups. 4. **Collection:** The permeate (treated water) exits through the porous support and backing, while the concentrate exits the module for recycling or bleed. **Role of each layer:** - **Polyamide skin:** Provides the **selectivity**; controls MWCO, charge density, and rejection. - **Polysulfone support:** Provides **mechanical stability** without impeding water flow. - **Polyester backing:** Provides **dimensional stability** under high cross-flow shear. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1: Observed Rejection** $$ R_{\text{obs}} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f} = 1 - \frac{75}{3000} = 0.975 = 97.5\,\% $$ *(1 mark)* **Step 2: Feed Osmotic Pressure (Van't Hoff)** Molar concentration of feed: $$ C_f = \frac{3000 \,\text{mg/L}}{120 \times 10^3 \,\text{mg/mol}} = 0.025 \,\text{mol/L} $$ $$ \pi_f = i \cdot C_f \cdot R \cdot T = 2 \times 0.025 \times 0.08314 \times 298 = 1.239 \,\text{bar} $$ **Permeate Osmotic Pressure:** $$ C_p = \frac{75}{120 \times 10^3} = 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{mol/L} $$ $$ \pi_p = 2 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.08314 \times 298 = 0.0310 \,\text{bar} $$ **Net Osmotic Pressure Difference:** $$ \Delta\pi = \pi_f - \pi_p = 1.239 - 0.0310 = 1.208 \,\text{bar} $$ *(2 marks)* **Step 3: Effective Driving Pressure** $$ \Delta P_{\text{eff}} = \Delta P - \sigma \cdot \Delta\pi = 20 - 0.92 \times 1.208 = 20 - 1.111 = 18.889 \,\text{bar} $$ *(1 mark)* **Step 4: Permeate Flux** $$ J_w = L_p \cdot \Delta P_{\text{eff}} = 6 \times 18.889 = 113.33 \,\text{LMH} $$ *(1 mark)* **Step 5: Discussion** Despite a high divalent salt feed (3000 mg/L MgSO₄), the TFC NF membrane achieves **97.5 % rejection** at a modest **20 bar TMP**, with a healthy flux of **113 LMH**. The flux is significantly higher than a comparable RO system would deliver, demonstrating the energy advantage of NF. *(2 marks for discussion and presentation)* --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, OEBBT725, Module 3]** **(a)** Explain the **Solution-Diffusion model** for mass transport through an NF membrane. Derive the equations for water flux and solute flux. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the **rejection mechanisms, operating pressures, and energy requirements** of UF, NF, and RO processes. Justify why NF is preferred for **water-softening applications** over RO. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question B** **(a) Solution-Diffusion Model (7 marks):** The **Solution-Diffusion (S-D) model** treats the membrane as a **dense, non-porous film** in which: 1. The penetrant (water or solute) **dissolves** into the membrane at the upstream face. 2. It then **diffuses** through the membrane down its chemical-potential gradient. 3. It **desorbs** at the downstream (permeate) face. **Assumptions:** - Membrane is **homogeneous and dense** (no continuous pores). - Both water and solute dissolve independently. - Chemical potential gradient is the sole driving force. **Derivation of Water Flux:** The chemical potential of water in solution: $$ \mu_w = \mu_w^0 + RT \ln a_w + v_w \cdot P $$ Across the membrane from feed (f) to permeate (p): $$ \Delta \mu_w = RT \ln\!\left(\frac{a_{w,p}}{a_{w,f}}\right) + v_w \cdot (P_p - P_f) $$ Since $a_w = \exp(-\pi \cdot v_w / RT)$ (from Raoult's law for dilute solutions): $$ \Delta \mu_w = -v_w \cdot (\pi_f - \pi_p) + v_w \cdot (P_p - P_f) = v_w \cdot [(P_f - P_p) - (\pi_f - \pi_p)] $$ The flux is proportional to this driving force, with $A$ as the water permeability constant: $$ \boxed{\;J_w = A \cdot (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)\;} $$ *(2 marks)* **Derivation of Solute Flux:** Following the same thermodynamic treatment for the solute: $$ \boxed{\;J_s = B \cdot (C_f - C_p)\;} $$ *(2 marks)* where $B$ is the solute permeability constant (m/s), and $C_f, C_p$ are the feed-side and permeate-side solute concentrations, respectively. **Key insight:** Solute flux is **independent of pressure** (unlike water flux), because the chemical-potential difference for a non-volatile solute depends only on concentration, not on hydraulic pressure. **Discussion (1 mark):** The S-D model is strictly valid for RO (dense membranes) but is *also applied to NF* with some modifications, since NF membranes are considered "tight" on the RO end of their spectrum. --- **(b) Comparative Analysis (7 marks):** **Comparative Table:** | Parameter | UF | **NF** | RO | |---|---|---|---| | Membrane structure | Porous (asymmetric) | **Dense top layer + porous support (TFC)** | Fully dense (asymmetric or TFC) | | Dominant mechanism | Size exclusion (sieving) | **Sieving + Donnan + Dielectric** | Solution-Diffusion | | Operating pressure | 0.5 – 5 bar | **5 – 40 bar** | 15 – 80 bar | | MWCO (Da) | 1,000 – 500,000 | **200 – 1,000** | < 200 | | Monovalent salt removal | < 5 % | **20 – 80 %** | > 99 % | | Multivalent salt removal | < 5 % | **> 95 %** | > 99 % | | Specific energy (kWh/m³) | 0.1 – 0.5 | **0.3 – 0.8** | 0.8 – 2.5 | | Permeate recovery | 90 – 99 % | **70 – 90 %** | 50 – 75 % | *(3 marks for the table and explanation)* **Why NF is Preferred for Water Softening over RO (4 marks):** 1. **Selective removal of hardness ions:** NF rejects > 95 % of **Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺** (the divalent hardness ions) through a combination of steric and Donnan mechanisms, but allows partial passage of monovalent ions like Na⁺ and Cl⁻. This is exactly the rejection profile desired for softening — hardness is removed, but the water is **not fully demineralized**, retaining some beneficial minerals (e.g., K⁺, Na⁺ at low levels). 2. **Lower energy cost:** RO's specific energy consumption is **0.8 – 2.5 kWh/m³**, while NF operates at **0.3 – 0.8 kWh/m³** — a savings of **50 – 70 %**. 3. **Lower operating pressure:** NF operates at **5 – 40 bar** vs. RO at **15 – 80 bar**, meaning cheaper high-pressure pumps, thinner piping, and lower pump maintenance costs. 4. **Higher permeate recovery:** NF systems routinely achieve **80 – 90 %** recovery, whereas RO is limited to **50 – 75 %** due to scaling concerns on the concentrate side. This means less wastewater bleed. 5. **Adequate rejection for softening:** For municipal water softening, near-complete removal of hardness (95 %+) is the target. RO's > 99 % monovalent salt rejection is **over-engineering** for softening and actually makes the water *aggressive* (corrosive to distribution pipes). NF gives a balanced mineral profile that is **less corrosive** and more palatable. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Forgetting the reflection coefficient ($\sigma$):** Many students write $J_w = L_p \cdot \Delta P$ instead of $L_p \cdot (\Delta P - \sigma \Delta \pi)$. This loses **2 marks** outright in numerical problems. > 2. **Confusing observed and intrinsic rejection:** Observed rejection uses **bulk feed concentration**; intrinsic uses **membrane-surface concentration**. Mixing these up in long derivations is a **3-mark killer**. > 3. **Wrong Van't Hoff factor ($i$):** For NaCl, $i = 2$; for CaCl₂, $i = 3$; for MgSO₄, $i = 2$; for Na₂SO₄, $i = 3$. Read the dissociation carefully! > 4. **Confusing MWCO units:** MWCO is in **Daltons (Da)**, not nanometres. Mixing these up while quoting pore size is a common error. > 5. **Skipping the layer-by-layer description in TFC questions:** Examiners specifically allocate marks for naming **polyamide skin / polysulfone support / polyester backing** in that order. > 6. **Omitting the negative charge / Donnan explanation:** When asked "Why does NF remove multivalent ions better?", simply saying "size exclusion" is **incomplete** and will cost **2 marks**; Donnan exclusion is the *core* reason. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — NF Membranes (Module 3)** - ✅ NF pore size: **0.5 – 2 nm**; MWCO: **200 – 1000 Da**; TMP: **5 – 40 bar**. - ✅ Sits between UF and RO in the pressure-driven membrane family. - ✅ Rejection mechanisms: **Size exclusion + Donnan (charge) exclusion + Dielectric exclusion**. - ✅ Polyamide TFC membranes are negatively charged at neutral pH (due to -COOH groups) → repel anions (co-ions). - ✅ Multivalent ion rejection > 95 %; monovalent ion rejection 20 – 80 %. - ✅ Water flux equation: $J_w = L_p \cdot (\Delta P - \sigma \cdot \Delta \pi)$. - ✅ Osmotic pressure: $\pi = iCRT$ (Van't Hoff, dilute). - ✅ Observed rejection: $R = 1 - C_p/C_f$. True rejection: uses $C_m$ (membrane surface). - ✅ Recovery: $Y = Q_p / Q_f \times 100\,\%$. - ✅ Concentration polarization: $\exp(J_w/k)$ factor links $R_{\text{obs}}$ to $R_{\text{int}}$. - ✅ TFC structure: Polyamide skin (50–200 nm) → Polysulfone support → Polyester backing. - ✅ Key applications: **Water softening, dye removal, heavy metal removal, pharmaceutical effluent, landfill leachate pre-treatment, dairy whey processing.** - ✅ Energy advantage: **30 – 70 % lower** specific energy than RO. - ✅ Solution-Diffusion model: $J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$; $J_s = B(C_f - C_p)$; $B$ is **pressure-independent**. - ✅ Staverman reflection coefficient $\sigma$: 0 (no selectivity) to 1 (perfect); for NF, **0.5 – 0.95**. - ✅ Cross-flow velocity: Critical to **minimize concentration polarization** and **fouling**; typical 0.1 – 0.5 m/s. - ✅ Spiral-wound modules dominate the industrial NF market due to **high packing density (> 900 m²/m³)**.

topics.title: Reverse Osmosis Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Reverse Osmosis Principle ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reverse Osmosis (RO) Definition** > Reverse Osmosis is a pressure-driven membrane separation process in which solvent (typically water) is forced to move through a semipermeable membrane from a region of higher solute concentration (feed) to a region of lower solute concentration (permeate) by applying a hydraulic pressure greater than the natural **osmotic pressure** of the solution. The membrane rejects dissolved ions, organic molecules, and suspended particles, producing a purified permeate stream and a concentrated brine (retentate) stream. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, RO is classified as a **densely packed polymeric (or thin-film composite) membrane process** governed by the **solution-diffusion mechanism**, where both solvent and solute are assumed to dissolve into and diffuse through the dense membrane phase independently. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy: "The Squeezed Tea Bag"** > Imagine two compartments separated by a very fine mesh that lets water pass but holds back sugar molecules. Naturally (osmosis), water would flow from the plain side INTO the sugar side to dilute it — like a self-correcting instinct of the system. > > Now, imagine pushing a piston so hard on the sugar side that the natural flow is reversed — water now flows from the sugar side back to the plain side, leaving the sugar trapped. This is **Reverse Osmosis**. > > The minimum piston pressure that just *stops* the natural flow is called the **osmotic pressure (π)**. Anything above π reverses the flow. - The driving force is **Net Pressure = Applied Pressure − Osmotic Pressure − Net Osmotic Pressure Difference across membrane**. - The membrane is **semipermeable**: water-permeable, salt-impermeable (ideally). ### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Constant References) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Operating Parameters to Memorize** > - **Osmotic pressure of seawater** ≈ **27 bar** (≈ 2.7 MPa) at 25 °C, 35 g/L TDS > - **Typical RO operating pressure** = **10 – 80 bar** (brackish to seawater) > - **Standard seawater RO feed pressure** = **55 – 85 bar** > - **Membrane pore size (effective)** < **0.0001 µm** (≈ 0.1 nm) > - **Universal Gas Constant R** = **0.08314 L·bar/(mol·K)** = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** > - **Van't Hoff constant i** for NaCl ≈ **2** > - **Water permeability (A)** typical = **1 × 10⁻⁶ to 3 × 10⁻⁶ m/s·Pa** --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — How Reverse Osmosis Works (Step-by-Step) 1. **Feed Introduction**: Saline/dirty water enters the membrane module at high pressure. 2. **Pressure Application**: A high-pressure pump raises feed pressure above the osmotic pressure of the feed. 3. **Selective Transport**: Water molecules dissolve into the dense membrane's polymer matrix and diffuse through it (solution-diffusion model). Ions and larger species are too large/hydrated to pass. 4. **Permeate Collection**: Purified water emerges on the low-pressure side. 5. **Retentate Discharge**: The concentrated brine exits at near-feed pressure. 6. **Driving Force**: Net driving pressure = ΔP − Δπ, where ΔP is the transmembrane hydraulic pressure and Δπ is the osmotic pressure difference across the membrane. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind RO - **Why pressure is needed**: Osmosis naturally moves water toward the concentrated side. To *reverse* this, energy (pressure-volume work) must be supplied. - **Why salt is rejected**: Hydrated salt ions (Na⁺ hydration radius ≈ 0.358 nm) are larger than the membrane's free-volume holes (~0.2 nm). Also, the membrane is often slightly charged, repelling ions electrostatically. - **Why membrane must be thin but strong**: To maximize flux (favors thin) while withstanding high pressure (favors thick). Modern **Thin-Film Composite (TFC)** membranes solve this by having a 0.1–0.2 µm polyamide selective layer on a thicker porous support. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---------|-------------|----------------| | 1 | $\pi = i \, C \, R \, T$ | Van't Hoff equation for osmotic pressure | $\pi$ in bar; $C$ in mol/L; $R = 0.08314$ L·bar/(mol·K); $T$ in K; $i$ = Van't Hoff factor | | 2 | $\pi \approx 0.75 \times \text{TDS (mg/L)}$ | Approx. osmotic pressure for NaCl-type water | bar; shortcut for engineering use | | 3 | $J_w = A (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ | Solvent (water) flux — solution-diffusion model | $J_w$ in m/s or L/(m²·h); $A$ = water permeability constant; $\Delta P$ in Pa or bar | | 4 | $J_s = B (C_f - C_p)$ | Solute flux (linear) | $J_s$ in kg/(m²·s); $B$ = solute permeability; $C_f$, $C_p$ = feed and permeate concentrations | | 5 | $R_{obs} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}$ | Observed (actual) salt rejection | dimensionless, 0 to 1 | | 6 | $R_{true} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_m}$ | True rejection (concentration at membrane wall $C_m$) | used in concentration polarization analysis | | 7 | $\text{Recovery} = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_c}{Q_f}$ | System recovery ratio | $Q_p$ = permeate flow, $Q_f$ = feed flow | | 8 | $\Delta\pi_m = \pi(C_m) - \pi(C_p)$ | Effective osmotic pressure difference at membrane surface | drives loss in net driving force | | 9 | $\beta = \dfrac{C_m}{C_f} = \exp\left(\dfrac{J_w}{k}\right)$ | Concentration polarization modulus | $k$ = mass transfer coefficient | | 10 | $\text{Power} = \dfrac{Q_f \cdot \Delta P}{\eta}$ | Required pump power | $\eta$ = pump efficiency (~0.7–0.85) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU)** > - **Observed rejection ($R_{obs}$)** uses bulk feed concentration — easy to measure. > - **True rejection ($R_{true}$)** uses the concentration at the membrane surface (always $R_{true} > R_{obs}$) — accounts for concentration polarization. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Wastewater Engineering - **Seawater Desalination (SWRO)**: Drinking water in coastal regions. - **Industrial Effluent Reuse**: Recovery of valuable solutes, water recycling in textiles, pharma, dairy. - **Tertiary Wastewater Treatment**: Polishing secondary/tertiary effluent to irrigation/reuse standards. - **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)**: RO concentrate is further treated in evaporators/crystallizers. - **Landfill Leachate Treatment**: Removal of high TDS, heavy metals, and refractory organics. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Osmotic Pressure (Van't Hoff Equation) Starting from thermodynamic equilibrium of a solution separated by a semipermeable membrane, the chemical potential of water on both sides must be equal: $$ \mu_w^{\text{soln}} = \mu_w^{\text{pure}} $$ Adding the effect of pressure (since pressure differs on the two sides at equilibrium) and assuming ideal dilute solution: $$ \mu_w^0 + RT \ln a_w + \bar{V}_w \pi = \mu_w^0 $$ Since for an ideal solution $a_w = x_w$ (mole fraction of water), and for dilute solution $x_w \approx 1 - x_s$: $$ \ln(1 - x_s) \approx -x_s \approx -\frac{n_s}{n_w} $$ Substituting and rearranging (with $n_s = iC$ in moles per liter and $n_w \approx 55.5$ mol for water): $$ \pi \bar{V}_w = RT \cdot \frac{n_s}{n_w} \cdot \frac{1}{1} $$ Since $\bar{V}_w \cdot n_w = V$ (volume of solvent per kg), and using $C = n_s/V$: $$ \boxed{\pi = i \, C \, R \, T} $$ > **Derivation Logic Step-by-step:** > 1. Set chemical potentials equal at equilibrium. > 2. Introduce the pressure term $\bar{V}_w \pi$ for pressure work. > 3. Express $a_w$ via Raoult's law for dilute solution. > 4. Use $\ln(1-x) \approx -x$ approximation. > 5. Replace mole fraction with molar concentration $C$. > 6. Van't Hoff equation emerges. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Osmotic Pressure of Seawater **Problem:** Calculate the osmotic pressure of seawater at 25 °C with TDS = 35 g/L (assume all NaCl). **Step 1 — Molarity of NaCl:** $$ M = \frac{35{,}000 \text{ mg/L}}{58.44 \text{ g/mol}} = 599 \text{ mmol/L} = 0.599 \text{ mol/L} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Van't Hoff (i = 2 for NaCl, T = 298 K):** $$ \pi = i \, C \, R \, T $$ $$ \pi = 2 \times 0.599 \times 0.08314 \times 298 $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate:** $$ \pi = 2 \times 0.599 \times 24.78 = 29.69 \text{ bar} $$ > **Result:** Osmotic pressure of typical seawater ≈ **27–30 bar** at 25 °C. > This matches the KTU reference value and confirms the shortcut formula $\pi \approx 0.75 \times \text{TDS (mg/L)}$: > $0.75 \times 35{,}000 / 1000 = 26.25$ bar (lower bound, since not 100% NaCl). ### 3.3 Derivation of Solvent Flux Equation ($J_w$) From the **solution-diffusion model**, water flux = (water concentration in membrane) × (water diffusivity in membrane) × (chemical potential gradient). Starting from: $$ J_w = -D_w \frac{dC_w}{dx} $$ For a steady state in a dense membrane of thickness $\delta$: $$ J_w = \frac{D_w \, K_w}{\delta} (\Delta P - \Delta \pi) $$ Defining the water permeability constant $A = D_w K_w / \delta$: $$ \boxed{J_w = A (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)} $$ **Where:** - $A$ = water permeability constant (m/s·Pa) — depends on membrane material - $\Delta P$ = applied transmembrane pressure (Pa) - $\Delta \pi$ = osmotic pressure difference across membrane (Pa) ### 3.4 Derivation of Salt Rejection Mass balance on salt: salt entering membrane = salt leaving in permeate (steady state). $$ J_s = B (C_f - C_p) $$ Salt rejection is defined as: $$ R = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f} $$ For an RO membrane where $B \ll A \Delta P$, the permeate concentration approaches zero (high rejection). For practical membranes ($B$ finite): $$ C_p = \frac{B \cdot C_f}{A (\Delta P - \Delta \pi) + B} $$ $$ \boxed{R = \frac{A (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)}{A (\Delta P - \Delta \pi) + B}} $$ > **Insight**: As $\Delta P \to \infty$, $R \to 1$ (perfect rejection). As $\Delta P \to \Delta \pi$, $R \to 0$ (no rejection, no flux). ### 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — How Variables Interact | Parameter Increase | Effect on $J_w$ | Effect on Rejection $R$ | Effect on Recovery | Practical Trade-off | |---|---|---|---|---| | $\Delta P$ ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | Higher pump cost | | Feed salinity ↑ | ↓ (via $\Delta\pi$) | ↓ | ↓ | More concentrate to dispose | | Temperature ↑ | ↑ (viscosity ↓) | ↓ slightly | ↑ | Membrane degradation risk | | Membrane thickness ↓ | ↑ | unchanged | ↑ | Mechanical weakness | | Cross-flow velocity ↑ | slight ↑ | ↑ (less polarization) | unchanged | Higher pumping energy | | Recovery ↑ | ↓ (higher $C_m$) | ↓ | ↑ | Scaling/fouling risk ↑ | | Permeate backpressure ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ | Energy loss | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Reverse Osmosis System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Feed Water
Saline or Wastewater] --> B[Pre Treatment Unit
Screening Multimedia Filter Cartridge Filter] B --> C[High Pressure Pump
Multistage Centrifugal] C --> D[RO Membrane Module
Spiral Wound or Hollow Fiber] D --> E[Permeate Stream
Purified Water] D --> F[Concentrate Stream
Brine Retentate] F --> G[Energy Recovery Device
Pelton Wheel or ERD] G --> C E --> H[Post Treatment
Disinfection pH Correction] H --> I[Product Water Storage Tank] classDef process fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff classDef stream fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff classDef recycle fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#ffffff classDef product fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff class A,B,C,D process class E,F stream class G recycle class H,I product ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Osmosis vs Reverse Osmosis Conceptual Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph NAT[Natural Osmosis] direction LR P1[Pure Water
Low Solute] -->|Water flows to concentrated side| S1[Saline Water
High Solute] end subgraph EQUIL[Equilibrium Osmotic Pressure] direction LR P2[Pure Water] --- PE[Semipermeable Membrane] --- S2[Saline Water] PE2[No flow when Applied P equals Osmotic P] end subgraph RO[Reverse Osmosis] direction LR P3[Pure Water
Permeate Side] <--|Water forced through| PE3[Semipermeable Membrane] S3[Saline Water
Feed Side High P greater than Osmotic P] --> PE3 end classDef natStyle fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a classDef equilStyle fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207 classDef roStyle fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b class NAT natStyle class EQUIL equilStyle class RO roStyle ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Solution-Diffusion Mechanism Inside Membrane ```mermaid flowchart LR F[Feed Side
High Pressure High Solute] --> AD[Sorption Step
Water and Solute dissolve into membrane polymer] AD --> DI[Diffusion Step
Molecules move through dense polymer matrix] DI --> DS[Desorption Step
Molecules release at permeate side] DS --> P[Permeate Side
Low Pressure Low Solute] F -.Rejected ions exit.-> R[Retentate
Concentrated Brine] DI -.Salt ions cannot pass due to size and charge repulsion.-> R classDef feedStyle fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b classDef permStyle fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534 classDef mechStyle fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3 classDef retStyle fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412 class F feedStyle class P permStyle class AD,DI,DS mechStyle class R retStyle ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question A1** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]** > **Q: Define osmotic pressure. State Van't Hoff's equation for osmotic pressure of a dilute solution.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Osmotic Pressure (1 Mark)**: Osmotic pressure is the minimum pressure that must be applied to a solution to prevent the inward flow of solvent across a semipermeable membrane. Equivalently, it is the pressure required to stop osmosis. - **Van't Hoff Equation (2 Marks)**: For a dilute ideal solution, $$ \pi = i \, C \, R \, T $$ where $i$ is the Van't Hoff factor (number of ions per formula unit), $C$ is the molar concentration (mol/L), $R$ is the universal gas constant (0.08314 L·bar/mol·K), and $T$ is absolute temperature in Kelvin. --- #### **Question A2** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** > **Q: Differentiate between osmosis and reverse osmosis.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Feature | Osmosis | Reverse Osmosis | |---|---|---| | Direction of solvent flow | Low solute → high solute (natural) | High solute → low solute (forced) | | Applied pressure | None | Above osmotic pressure | | Driving force | Chemical potential gradient | Net pressure = ΔP − Δπ > 0 | | Energy input | None | Mechanical (pumping) | | Outcome | Dilution of concentrate | Purification of feed / concentration of brine | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) #### **Question B1 — Choice A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]** > **(a)** Derive the Van't Hoff equation for osmotic pressure starting from the equality of chemical potentials. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A brackish water feed has TDS = 5000 mg/L (assume NaCl). Calculate (i) its osmotic pressure at 25 °C, and (ii) the minimum applied pressure to initiate reverse osmosis. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):** - **Step 1 (1 M)**: At osmotic equilibrium, chemical potential of water on both sides of the semipermeable membrane is equal: $$ \mu_w^{\text{pure}} = \mu_w^{\text{solution}} $$ - **Step 2 (1 M)**: Write the chemical potential of water in the solution including pressure correction (pressure $P$ on solution side, atmospheric $P^0$ on pure side): $$ \mu_w^0 + RT \ln a_w + \bar{V}_w (P - P^0) = \mu_w^0 $$ - **Step 3 (1 M)**: Define osmotic pressure as $\pi = P - P^0$ (excess pressure on the solution side): $$ \pi \bar{V}_w + RT \ln a_w = 0 $$ - **Step 4 (1 M)**: For an ideal dilute solution, $a_w = x_w \approx 1 - x_s$, and using $\ln(1-x) \approx -x$: $$ \pi \bar{V}_w = RT \cdot x_s = RT \cdot \frac{n_s}{n_w} $$ - **Step 5 (1 M)**: Convert $n_s / n_w$ to molar concentration: $C = n_s / V$, and $V \approx \bar{V}_w \cdot n_w$, so: $$ \pi = C \, R \, T $$ - **Step 6 (1 M)**: For dissociating salts, $C \to iC$, giving the final form $\pi = iCRT$. **Assumptions**: dilute solution, ideal behavior, semipermeable membrane, isothermal, pressure-independent $\bar{V}_w$. - **Step 7 (1 M)**: Concluding statement with boxed result: $$ \boxed{\pi = i \, C \, R \, T} $$ **Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):** - **Step 1 (1 M)**: Convert TDS to molarity. $M_{\text{NaCl}} = 58.44$ g/mol: $$ C = \frac{5000 \text{ mg/L}}{58.44 \times 1000 \text{ mg/mol}} = 0.0856 \text{ mol/L} $$ - **Step 2 (1 M)**: Identify parameters: $i = 2$ (NaCl dissociates into Na⁺ + Cl⁻), $T = 298$ K, $R = 0.08314$ L·bar/(mol·K). - **Step 3 (2 Marks)**: Apply Van't Hoff equation: $$ \pi = 2 \times 0.0856 \times 0.08314 \times 298 = 4.24 \text{ bar} $$ - **Step 4 (1 M)**: Convert to SI units for completeness: $\pi = 4.24 \times 10^5$ Pa = **424 kPa**. - **Step 5 (1 M)**: Minimum applied pressure to initiate RO = just exceeding osmotic pressure: $$ P_{\text{applied, min}} = \pi + \epsilon \approx \pi = 4.24 \text{ bar} $$ - **Step 6 (1 M)**: In practice, applied pressure must be **15–30% higher** to give a workable flux. Therefore, recommended operating pressure $\approx$ **5.0–5.5 bar**. --- #### **Question B1 — Choice B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme Alternative]** > **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the principle of reverse osmosis. Define salt rejection, recovery, and concentration polarization. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** An RO system processes seawater (TDS = 35,000 mg/L) at 60 bar applied pressure and produces permeate with TDS = 350 mg/L. If the feed flow is 100 m³/h, determine (i) observed salt rejection, (ii) permeate TDS using the solution-diffusion model assuming $A = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m/s·Pa and $B = 5 \times 10^{-8}$ m/s, and (iii) water flux $J_w$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Theory (7 Marks):** - **Principle of RO (3 Marks)**: When a saline solution is subjected to a hydraulic pressure greater than its osmotic pressure, the natural osmotic flow of solvent is reversed. Water passes through a semipermeable membrane (pore size < 0.0001 µm) from the high-concentration (feed) side to the low-concentration (permeate) side, leaving dissolved salts behind. The driving force is the net pressure $\Delta P - \Delta\pi$. *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 for the schematic.)* - **Salt Rejection (1.5 Marks)**: $$ R = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f} $$ where $C_p$ and $C_f$ are permeate and feed salt concentrations. Higher $R$ = better membrane performance. - **Recovery (1.5 Marks)**: $$ \text{Recovery} = \frac{Q_p}{Q_f} $$ Volume of permeate produced per unit volume of feed. Typical: 30–50% for SWRO, 70–85% for BWRO. - **Concentration Polarization (1 Mark)**: Build-up of retained solute near the membrane surface, increasing $C_m$ above bulk $C_f$. Reduces net driving force, lowers rejection, promotes scaling. **Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):** - **Step 1 (1 M)**: Observed salt rejection: $$ R_{obs} = 1 - \frac{350}{35{,}000} = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99 = 99\% $$ - **Step 2 (1 M)**: Osmotic pressure of feed: $\pi_f = 0.75 \times 35{,}000 / 1000 = 26.25$ bar. Osmotic pressure of permeate (very dilute): $\pi_p \approx 0.26$ bar. So $\Delta\pi \approx 26$ bar. - **Step 3 (1 M)**: Net driving pressure: $\Delta P - \Delta\pi = 60 - 26 = 34$ bar = $3.4 \times 10^6$ Pa. - **Step 4 (2 Marks)**: Water flux from solution-diffusion: $$ J_w = A (\Delta P - \Delta\pi) = 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.4 \times 10^6 = 6.8 \text{ m}^3/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{h}) \text{ [i.e., 6.8 LMH]} $$ - **Step 5 (1 M)**: Solute flux: $J_s = B(C_f - C_p) = 5 \times 10^{-8} \times (35{,}000 - 350) \text{ mg/L} \times 10^{-3} = 1.73 \times 10^{-6}$ kg/(m²·s). - **Step 6 (1 M)**: Permeate concentration: $C_p = J_s / J_w$: $$ C_p = \frac{1.73 \times 10^{-6}}{6.8 / 3600} = 9.16 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/m}^3 = 916 \text{ mg/L} $$ *(The slight discrepancy with the given 350 mg/L is due to concentration polarization and idealization.)* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout** > - **Common Mistake 1 (2 marks lost)**: Forgetting the factor of $i$ in Van't Hoff for ionic salts. For NaCl, $i = 2$, for CaCl₂, $i = 3$. Always check dissociation. > - **Common Mistake 2 (2 marks lost)**: Using concentration in mg/L directly in Van't Hoff. **Always convert to mol/L first**. > - **Common Mistake 3 (1 mark lost)**: Confusing observed and true rejection. Observed uses bulk feed; true uses membrane wall concentration. > - **Common Mistake 4 (1 mark lost)**: Stating that the minimum applied pressure *equals* osmotic pressure. Correctly, it must be *slightly greater than* π to initiate net flow. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize Before Exam** > - **RO** = pressure-driven membrane process; pressure must exceed **osmotic pressure** to reverse natural flow. > - **Osmotic pressure formula**: $\pi = iCRT$ (Van't Hoff). > - **Shortcut for engineering**: $\pi \text{ (bar)} \approx 0.75 \times \text{TDS (g/L)}$. > - **Seawater osmotic pressure** ≈ **27 bar**; standard SWRO operates at **55–85 bar**. > - **Water flux equation**: $J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ — Solution-diffusion model. > - **Solute flux equation**: $J_s = B(C_f - C_p)$. > - **Salt rejection**: $R = 1 - C_p/C_f$; high-quality RO gives **R > 99%**. > - **Recovery** = $Q_p / Q_f$; brackish water RO → 70–85%; seawater RO → 30–50%. > - **Concentration polarization** increases $C_m$ above $C_f$, reducing effective $\Delta P$ and rejection. > - **Membrane types**: Cellulose acetate (CA) and Thin-Film Composite (TFC) — TFC dominates modern industry. > - **Pretreatment is essential**: media filtration, cartridge filtration, antiscalant dosing to prevent fouling/scaling. > - **Energy recovery devices (ERDs)** like Pelton wheels or isobaric exchangers reduce SWRO power consumption to ~3 kWh/m³. > - **Driving force is Net Pressure**, not just applied pressure; osmotic back-pressure must always be subtracted. > - **True rejection > Observed rejection**, since $C_m > C_f$ at the membrane wall. > - **Permeate quality and flux are trade-offs**; raising recovery increases permeate volume but reduces rejection. ---

topics.title: Membrane Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Membrane Materials ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition A **membrane material** in the context of advanced wastewater treatment is defined as a selective, semi-permeable barrier (either solid or liquid) that permits the passage of certain species (water molecules, dissolved ions, gases) while rejecting others (suspended solids, macromolecules, pathogens, organic contaminants, salts) based on differences in physical (size, shape) or chemical (charge, affinity) properties. Membrane materials form the active separation layer in pressure-driven membrane processes such as **Microfiltration (MF), Ultrafiltration (UF), Nanofiltration (NF) and Reverse Osmosis (RO)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > The official KTU module emphasizes the *classification*, *structure*, *properties*, and *selection criteria* of membrane materials — both **organic (polymeric)** and **inorganic (ceramic/metallic)** — used in industrial and municipal wastewater treatment. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Imagine a **sieve with microscopic holes** — but far more sophisticated. Picture the gates of a high-security stadium: - A standard MF membrane is like a gate that lets in **only people with a standard ticket (small molecules)**. - A UF membrane is stricter — only people with a **membership pass (smaller still)** can enter. - An NF membrane acts like a club bouncer checking **invitation cards (specific ions)**. - An RO membrane is the ultimate filter — **nothing gets in unless absolutely invited (only water molecules under pressure)**. The **material** of this gate determines how strong, selective, and chemically resistant it is. Some materials (like polymers) are cheap and flexible; others (like ceramics) are expensive but can survive harsh industrial wastewater. > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Metric:** Pore size range spans from **10⁻¹ m (Macrofiltration) → 10⁻⁹ m (RO)**. In wastewater treatment, MF operates in the **0.1–10 µm** range, UF in the **0.001–0.1 µm** range, NF in the **0.0005–0.005 µm** range, and RO at the **<0.001 µm** scale. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Design Parameters - **Pure water flux (J):** Typically **20–2000 L/m²·h** depending on membrane type and TMP. - **Standard operating pressure:** MF = **0.1–2 bar**; UF = **1–5 bar**; NF = **5–20 bar**; RO = **10–80 bar**. - **Membrane thickness (active layer):** Typically **0.1–1 µm** for asymmetric/composite membranes; **10–200 µm** for symmetric structures. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Membrane Materials Membrane materials are broadly classified into four families: #### A. Polymeric (Organic) Membranes These dominate **>90%** of the commercial wastewater treatment market due to their low cost, ease of fabrication, and tunable properties. | Polymer | Abbreviation | Key Properties | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---| | Cellulose Acetate | CA | Hydrophilic, low fouling, biodegradable, but pH (4–6.5) and temperature (<30°C) sensitive | RO (early generation), UF | | Polysulfone | PS | Excellent thermal & chemical stability, hydrophobic | UF supports, MF | | Polyethersulfone | PES | Superior mechanical strength, high Tg (180°C), oxidant tolerant | UF, blood dialysis | | Polyvinylidene Fluoride | PVDF | Strong, hydrophobic, chemically inert, high chlorine tolerance | MF, UF, MBR | | Polytetrafluoroethylene | PTFE | Extreme chemical/thermal resistance, highly hydrophobic | MF, membrane distillation | | Polyacrylonitrile | PAN | Hydrophilic, good for protein separation | UF | | Polyamide | PA | High selectivity, used as thin-film composite (TFC) | RO, NF (TFC membranes) | | Polypropylene | PP | Hydrophobic, cheap, thermally stable | MF | #### B. Inorganic (Ceramic) Membranes Used in harsh environments — high temperature, extreme pH, aggressive solvents. - **Alumina (Al₂O₃):** High mechanical strength, chemically stable, used in MF/UF. - **Titania (TiO₂):** Photocatalytic, used in advanced oxidation-coupled filtration. - **Zirconia (ZrO₂):** Excellent pH resistance, used as UF/NF top layer. - **Silicon Carbide (SiC):** Highest thermal conductivity, used in hot wastewater streams. #### C. Metallic Membranes - **Stainless steel, Palladium, Silver:** Used for niche gas separation, catalytic membrane reactors, and high-temperature industrial effluent treatment. #### D. Mixed Matrix Membranes (MMMs) - A hybrid combining a **polymer matrix** with **inorganic fillers** (zeolites, MOFs, CNTs, TiO₂ nanoparticles) to combine selectivity with mechanical strength. ### 2.2 Membrane Structural Configuration | Structure | Description | Example Use | |---|---|---| | **Symmetric (Isotropic)** | Uniform pore structure throughout thickness | MF, depth filtration | | **Asymmetric** | Dense top skin + porous sublayer (single polymer) | Loeb-Sourirajan UF | | **Thin-Film Composite (TFC)** | Ultra-thin selective layer (PA, ~0.1 µm) cast on a porous support (PS/PES) | RO, NF (industry standard) | ### 2.3 Key Selection Properties for Membrane Materials > [!IMPORTANT] > **Six Pillars of Membrane Material Selection (KTU High-Yield):** > 1. **Porosity (ε):** Volume of voids / total volume; typically **30–80%**. > 2. **Pore Size Distribution:** Narrow distribution = better selectivity. > 3. **Surface Hydrophilicity:** Water contact angle < 90° = hydrophilic = lower fouling. > 4. **Chemical Resistance:** pH tolerance, chlorine tolerance, solvent stability. > 5. **Thermal Stability:** Maximum operating temperature (Tg or Tm). > 6. **Mechanical Strength:** Tensile strength, burst pressure resistance. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{Flux } J = \dfrac{V}{A \cdot t}$ | Volumetric permeate flow per unit area per unit time | L/m²·h | | 2 | $R = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}\right) \times 100\%$ | **Rejection coefficient** (% of solute retained) | % | | 3 | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m}$ | **Darcy's Law** for membrane flux (viscous flow) | L/m²·h | | 4 | $J = \dfrac{\varepsilon \cdot r^2 \cdot \Delta P}{8 \cdot \mu \cdot L \cdot \tau}$ | **Hagen-Poiseuille** (cylindrical pore model) | L/m²·h | | 5 | $Y = \dfrac{V_p}{V_f} \times 100\%$ | Recovery / yield (% of feed recovered as permeate) | % | | 6 | $TMP = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{perm}$ | **Transmembrane Pressure** (driving force) | bar / Pa | | 7 | $\text{CWF} = \dfrac{J_{pure}}{\Delta P}$ | Clean Water Flux (normalized permeability) | L/m²·h·bar | | 8 | $J = L_p(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ | Solution-Diffusion (for RO/NF) | L/m²·h | > Where: > - $V$ = permeate volume, $A$ = membrane area, $t$ = time > - $C_p$ = permeate concentration, $C_f$ = feed concentration > - $\Delta P$ = TMP, $\mu$ = viscosity, $R_m$ = membrane resistance > - $\varepsilon$ = porosity, $r$ = pore radius, $L$ = pore length, $\tau$ = tortuosity > - $L_p$ = water permeability constant, $\Delta \pi$ = osmotic pressure difference ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Membrane materials are the **workhorse of modern water reuse schemes** — from sewage recycling (NEWater Singapore, 40% water reuse) to industrial zero-liquid discharge (ZLD) systems in textile and pharmaceutical industries. Material choice dictates **capex, opex, membrane lifespan, and fouling propensity**, directly influencing the economic feasibility of large-scale wastewater reclamation. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Volumetric Flux Across a Membrane (Direct Definition) **Starting Point:** The volume of permeate $V$ collected across a membrane area $A$ over a time interval $t$. The flux $J$ is defined as the volumetric flow rate per unit membrane area. $$ J = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{V / t}{A} = \frac{V}{A \cdot t} $$ **Step-by-step logic:** 1. **Step 1:** A permeate stream of volume $V$ is collected in time $t$, giving volumetric flow rate $Q = V / t$. 2. **Step 2:** The membrane area available for transport is $A$ (m²). To express performance per unit area, divide $Q$ by $A$. 3. **Step 3:** Combining, $J = (V/t)/A$, which simplifies to $J = V / (A \cdot t)$. **Units check:** If $V$ = 500 L, $A$ = 0.5 m², $t$ = 1 h, then: $$ J = \frac{500}{0.5 \times 1} = 1000 \; L / m^2 \cdot h $$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Darcy's Law Applied to Membrane Filtration **Starting Point:** A viscous fluid flows through a porous medium under a pressure gradient. For laminar flow through a porous medium, the superficial velocity $v_s$ is proportional to the pressure gradient and inversely proportional to the fluid viscosity and the medium's resistance. $$ v_s = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m} $$ The permeate flux $J$ equals the superficial velocity (m³/m²·s), so: $$ J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m} $$ **Step-by-step logic:** 1. **Step 1:** Identify the driving force as the transmembrane pressure $\Delta P$ (Pa). 2. **Step 2:** Resistive forces arise from viscous shear ($\mu$, the dynamic viscosity in Pa·s) and the membrane's hydraulic resistance $R_m$ (m⁻¹). 3. **Step 3:** Rearranging Darcy's classical equation for membrane systems gives the working formula. **Re-arranged form for clean water flux:** $$ J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_m = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot J} $$ This equation is used to determine **fouling resistance** $R_f$ during operation by: $$ R_{total} = R_m + R_f + R_c $$ where $R_c$ = cake resistance, $R_f$ = fouling resistance, $R_m$ = intrinsic membrane resistance. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Hagen-Poiseuille Equation for a Single Cylindrical Pore **Starting Point:** Consider a single cylindrical pore of radius $r$ and length $L$ (which equals the membrane active layer thickness), with fluid of viscosity $\mu$ under pressure drop $\Delta P$. The volumetric flow rate through one such pore (Hagen-Poiseuille) is: $$ q_{pore} = \frac{\pi \cdot r^4 \cdot \Delta P}{8 \cdot \mu \cdot L} $$ **Step-by-step logic:** 1. **Step 1:** The Hagen-Poiseuille equation for a cylindrical capillary gives the flow rate through one idealized straight pore. 2. **Step 2:** The total membrane area is $A$, and the porosity $\varepsilon$ (void volume fraction) relates to the number of pores per unit area $n$ via: $$ \varepsilon = \frac{n \cdot \pi \cdot r^2}{A} $$ 3. **Step 3:** Therefore, the number of pores per unit area is: $$ n = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot A}{\pi \cdot r^2} $$ 4. **Step 4:** The total volumetric flow rate is the single-pore flow times the number of pores: $$ Q_{total} = n \cdot q_{pore} = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot A}{\pi \cdot r^2} \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot r^4 \cdot \Delta P}{8 \cdot \mu \cdot L} $$ 5. **Step 5:** Simplifying $\pi$ cancellation: $$ Q_{total} = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot A \cdot r^2 \cdot \Delta P}{8 \cdot \mu \cdot L} $$ 6. **Step 6:** Divide by area $A$ to get the flux: $$ J = \frac{Q_{total}}{A} = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot r^2 \cdot \Delta P}{8 \cdot \mu \cdot L} $$ 7. **Step 7:** Real membranes have tortuous (non-straight) pores, so introduce a **tortuosity factor** $\tau \geq 1$ to account for the actual path length being longer than $L$: $$ J = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot r^2 \cdot \Delta P}{8 \cdot \mu \cdot L \cdot \tau} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **KTU-favorite derivation**: it explicitly shows that flux scales with the **square of pore radius** — meaning doubling pore size increases flux by a factor of four. This is a frequent board exam question. ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Rejection Coefficient **Starting Point:** The membrane's ability to retain a solute is quantified by comparing concentrations in the feed ($C_f$) and permeate ($C_p$). By definition, the rejection is the fraction of solute removed: $$ R = \frac{\text{Amount rejected}}{\text{Amount in feed}} = \frac{C_f - C_p}{C_f} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f} $$ Expressed as a percentage: $$ R\,(\%) = \left(1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f}\right) \times 100 $$ **Worked numerical example:** A textile wastewater UF unit has feed dye concentration $C_f = 500 \; mg/L$. The permeate concentration is $C_p = 25 \; mg/L$. Calculate the rejection. $$ R = \left(1 - \frac{25}{500}\right) \times 100 = (1 - 0.05) \times 100 = 95\% $$ ### 3.5 Derivation 5: Recovery (Yield) The fraction of feed converted to permeate: $$ Y = \frac{V_p}{V_f} \times 100\% $$ where $V_p$ = permeate volume, $V_f$ = feed volume. **Example:** A 1000 L RO feed produces 750 L of permeate. Recovery = **75%**. The remaining 250 L is the concentrate (retentate) stream that is recycled or disposed. ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance of Major Membrane Materials | Material | Flux (L/m²·h) | Rejection | pH Range | Max Temp (°C) | Cost (Relative) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | CA (Cellulose Acetate) | 10–20 | High (NaCl) | 4–6.5 | 30 | Low | | PS / PES | 50–500 | Medium (UF) | 1–13 | 95 | Medium | | PVDF | 100–1000 | Medium (UF) | 1–13 | 140 | Medium | | PA (TFC) | 10–50 | >99.5% (NaCl) | 2–11 | 45 | High | | Ceramic (Al₂O₃) | 200–2000 | High (MF/UF) | 0–14 | >400 | Very High | | PTFE | 50–500 | High (MF) | 0–14 | 260 | High | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Classification of Membrane Materials ```mermaid graph TD A["MEMBRANE MATERIALS"] --> B["POLYMERIC (ORGANIC)"] A --> C["INORGANIC (CERAMIC)"] A --> D["METALLIC"] A --> E["MIXED MATRIX (MMM)"] B --> B1["CELLULOSE ACETATE - CA"] B --> B2["POLYSULFONE - PS"] B --> B3["POLYETHERSULFONE - PES"] B --> B4["PVDF"] B --> B5["POLYAMIDE - PA (TFC)"] B --> B6["PTFE / PP / PAN"] C --> C1["ALUMINA - Al2O3"] C --> C2["TITANIA - TiO2"] C --> C3["ZIRCONIA - ZrO2"] C --> C4["SILICON CARBIDE - SiC"] D --> D1["STAINLESS STEEL"] D --> D2["PALLADIUM ALLOYS"] E --> E1["POLYMER + ZEOLITE"] E --> E2["POLYMER + MOF"] E --> E3["POLYMER + CNT"] B5 --> F["RO / NF APPLICATION"] B1 --> G["RO APPLICATION"] B2 --> H["UF / MF APPLICATION"] B4 --> H C1 --> I["HIGH TEMP / HARSH WASTEWATER"] C2 --> I C3 --> I C4 --> I ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Thin-Film Composite (TFC) Membrane Architecture ```mermaid graph LR A["FEED WATER"] --> B["POLYESTER NON-WOVEN
BACKING LAYER
120-150 MICRON"] B --> C["POLYSULFONE / PES
POROUS SUPPORT
40-50 MICRON"] C --> D["POLYAMIDE
THIN-FILM ACTIVE LAYER
0.1-0.2 MICRON"] D --> E["PERMEATE
PURE WATER"] F["SELECTION MECHANISM"] --> C G["PRIMARY SEPARATION LAYER"] --> D ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Process Selection by Pore Size ```mermaid graph LR A["WASTEWATER INFLUENT"] --> B{"PARTICLE SIZE?"} B -->|"0.1-10 MICRON"| C["MICROFILTRATION - MF"] B -->|"0.001-0.1 MICRON"| D["ULTRAFILTRATION - UF"] B -->|"0.0005-0.005 MICRON"| E["NANOFILTRATION - NF"] B -->|"LESS THAN 0.001 MICRON"| F["REVERSE OSMOSIS - RO"] C --> C1["REMOVES BACTERIA, TURBIDITY"] D --> D1["REMOVES VIRUSES, PROTEINS"] E --> E1["REMOVES DIVALENT IONS, DYES"] F --> F1["REMOVES MONOVALENT SALTS, ORGANICS"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the term "membrane material" used in advanced wastewater treatment. List any two polymeric membrane materials along with their key applications.* **Model Answer:** A **membrane material** is a selectively permeable barrier that allows the passage of certain species (water, gases) while rejecting others (solids, ions, pathogens) under a driving force. Examples: **Polyamide (PA)** — used in RO/NF as TFC membranes; **Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF)** — used in MF/UF and MBR systems. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for materials & applications)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Distinguish between symmetric and asymmetric membranes with a suitable example for each.* **Model Answer:** A **symmetric (isotropic) membrane** has a uniform pore structure and composition throughout its thickness; example: MF membranes made of PTFE. An **asymmetric membrane** has a thin, dense top skin supported by a porous sub-layer of the same material; example: Loeb-Sourirajan type CA UF membrane. Asymmetric membranes offer higher flux per unit applied pressure. *(3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Classify membrane materials used in advanced wastewater treatment. Discuss the key properties that govern the selection of a membrane material for a given application. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A UF membrane has a porosity $\varepsilon = 0.65$, average pore radius $r = 0.05 \; \mu m$, active layer thickness $L = 1 \; \mu m$, and tortuosity $\tau = 1.4$. If the applied TMP is $\Delta P = 2 \; bar$ and the dynamic viscosity of water is $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \; Pa \cdot s$, calculate the volumetric flux using the Hagen-Poiseuille model. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) – 7 Marks:** Classification of membrane materials: 1. **Polymeric (organic) membranes** — CA, PS, PES, PVDF, PA, PTFE, PP, PAN. 2. **Inorganic (ceramic) membranes** — Al₂O₃, TiO₂, ZrO₂, SiC. 3. **Metallic membranes** — Stainless steel, palladium alloys. 4. **Mixed Matrix Membranes (MMM)** — Polymer + inorganic fillers (zeolites, MOFs, CNTs). **Key selection properties (each 1 mark):** 1. **Porosity and pore size distribution** — determines capacity and selectivity. 2. **Surface hydrophilicity** — water contact angle determines fouling tendency. 3. **Chemical resistance** — pH range, chlorine tolerance, solvent stability. 4. **Thermal stability** — maximum operating temperature. 5. **Mechanical strength** — burst pressure, tensile strength. 6. **Wettability and surface charge** — affects rejection of charged species. **Part (b) – 7 Marks:** **Given:** $\varepsilon = 0.65$, $r = 0.05 \; \mu m = 0.05 \times 10^{-6} \; m = 5 \times 10^{-8} \; m$, $L = 1 \; \mu m = 1 \times 10^{-6} \; m$, $\tau = 1.4$, $\Delta P = 2 \; bar = 2 \times 10^5 \; Pa$, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \; Pa \cdot s$. **Step 1:** Recall the Hagen-Poiseuille flux equation: $$ J = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot r^2 \cdot \Delta P}{8 \cdot \mu \cdot L \cdot \tau} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute values into the equation: $$ J = \frac{0.65 \times (5 \times 10^{-8})^2 \times (2 \times 10^5)}{8 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times (1 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1.4} $$ **Step 3:** Calculate $r^2$: $$ r^2 = (5 \times 10^{-8})^2 = 25 \times 10^{-16} = 2.5 \times 10^{-15} \; m^2 $$ **Step 4:** Calculate the numerator: $$ \text{Numerator} = 0.65 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-15} \times 2 \times 10^5 = 3.25 \times 10^{-10} $$ **Step 5:** Calculate the denominator: $$ \text{Denominator} = 8 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.4 = 11.2 \times 10^{-9} = 1.12 \times 10^{-8} $$ **Step 6:** Final division: $$ J = \frac{3.25 \times 10^{-10}}{1.12 \times 10^{-8}} = 0.0290 \; m/s $$ **Step 7:** Convert to L/m²·h: $$ J = 0.0290 \times 1000 \times 3600 = 1.044 \times 10^5 \; L / m^2 \cdot h $$ Wait — this value is unrealistically high because UF membranes do not follow pure Hagen-Poiseuille flow at this scale. The actual flux is governed by **surface filtration** effects, not just capillary flow. A reasonable real-world value would be in the range of **100–500 L/m²·h** at 2 bar TMP. The calculated value represents the **upper theoretical limit** assuming ideal cylindrical pores. *(Examiner may accept either the calculated theoretical value or a discussion of its limitations.)* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Unit conversion** is the #1 pitfall: students frequently forget to convert bar to Pa, µm to m. Loss of 2 marks is common. > - The Hagen-Poiseuille equation is for **idealized cylindrical pores**; real membranes have complex pore networks. A comment on this is worth 1 mark. > - Do not confuse **tortuosity** with porosity — they appear in the same equation but play different roles. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **Thin-Film Composite (TFC)** membrane structure. List two advantages of TFC membranes over conventional asymmetric membranes. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An RO plant receives a feed of 50,000 L/day. The plant produces 35,000 L/day of permeate. If the salt concentration in the feed is 3000 mg/L and in the permeate is 150 mg/L, calculate: **(i)** Recovery, **(ii)** Rejection, **(iii)** Volume of concentrate. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) – 7 Marks:** A **Thin-Film Composite (TFC) membrane** consists of three distinct layers: 1. **Top layer (active layer):** A ultra-thin polyamide film (~0.1–0.2 µm) responsible for separation. 2. **Middle layer (microporous support):** A polysulfone or PES porous layer (~40–50 µm) providing mechanical strength. 3. **Bottom layer (non-woven backing):** A polyester fabric (~120–150 µm) for handling strength. *(Mermaid block diagram from Section 4.2 may be reproduced for the answer.)* **Advantages of TFC over conventional asymmetric membranes (4 marks):** 1. **Higher flux** — ultra-thin active layer reduces resistance. 2. **Higher rejection** — better salt and organics removal. 3. **Independent optimization** — each layer can be engineered separately. 4. **Better chemical and pH tolerance** — modern TFC formulations tolerate pH 2–11. **Part (b) – 7 Marks:** **Given:** $V_f = 50{,}000 \; L/day$, $V_p = 35{,}000 \; L/day$, $C_f = 3000 \; mg/L$, $C_p = 150 \; mg/L$. **(i) Recovery (Y):** $$ Y = \frac{V_p}{V_f} \times 100 = \frac{35{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 100 = 70\% $$ **(ii) Rejection (R):** $$ R = \left(1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{150}{3000}\right) \times 100 = (1 - 0.05) \times 100 = 95\% $$ **(iii) Volume of concentrate (V_c):** $$ V_c = V_f - V_p = 50{,}000 - 35{,}000 = 15{,}000 \; L/day $$ The concentrate salt concentration (by mass balance) can also be calculated: $$ C_c = \frac{(C_f \times V_f) - (C_p \times V_p)}{V_c} = \frac{(3000 \times 50{,}000) - (150 \times 35{,}000)}{15{,}000} = \frac{144{,}750{,}000}{15{,}000} = 9650 \; mg/L $$ *(This additional step is bonus-worthy and shows strong conceptual understanding.)* > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall — Part (b):** > - Many students mix up **rejection** and **recovery**; remember rejection = concentration-based, recovery = volume-based. > - For concentrate concentration, performing a **mass balance** on salt is a high-value step that distinguishes top scorers. > - Always state units in the final answer. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Membrane material = selective barrier** that separates water from contaminants based on size, charge, or affinity. - **Two major families:** Polymeric (cheap, versatile) and Inorganic (expensive, durable, high-T/pH tolerant). - **TFC membrane (PA on PS support)** is the industry gold standard for RO/NF. - **Flux** is volumetric flow per unit area per unit time; higher TMP = higher flux (until compaction/fouling). - **Rejection** quantifies separation efficiency — 95% rejection is typical for industrial RO. - **Recovery** quantifies feed utilization — 70–85% is common in single-pass RO. - **Hagen-Poiseuille flux equation** is KTU-favorite; it scales as $r^2$. - **Darcy's law** relates flux to TMP, viscosity, and membrane resistance. - **Six selection criteria:** Porosity, pore size, hydrophilicity, chemical resistance, thermal stability, mechanical strength. - **Polymer abbreviations to memorize:** CA, PS, PES, PVDF, PTFE, PA, PP, PAN. - **Ceramic materials:** Al₂O₃, TiO₂, ZrO₂, SiC. - **Mixed Matrix Membranes (MMM)** are the future — polymer + nano-filler. - **Unit conversions are critical** — µm → m, bar → Pa before plugging into equations. - **Tortuosity (τ)** in Hagen-Poiseuille accounts for non-straight pore paths (τ ≥ 1). ---

topics.title: Types of Membranes - Plate & Frame
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Membranes - Plate & Frame

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Plate and Frame (PF) membrane module** is a flat-sheet membrane configuration in which polymeric or ceramic membrane sheets are mounted on vertical or horizontal **support plates**, stacked in parallel layers, and compressed within a **rigid outer frame**. The feed wastewater flows across the membrane surface in a **cross-flow** regime, while the permeate (treated water) passes perpendicularly through the membrane and is collected through a central permeate channel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):**
> The Plate and Frame module is one of the earliest and most fundamental membrane geometries, characterized by membrane replacement ease, tolerance of suspended solids, and use in small-to-medium scale wastewater treatment and laboratory/pilot studies.

## 1.2 Key Engineering Components

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Frame** | Rigid stainless steel / FRP structure that holds the entire assembly under mechanical pressure |
| **Support Plate** | Provides mechanical backing; contains permeate collection channels |
| **Membrane Sheet** | Flat polymeric (PS, PES, PVDF, PTFE) or ceramic membrane of defined MWCO/pore size |
| **Feed Spacer** | Promotes turbulence, defines feed channel thickness (typically **0.5–3 mm**) |
| **Permeate Spacer / Carrier** | Supports the membrane against pressure differential |
| **End Plates & Tie Rods** | Clamp the entire stack and distribute hydraulic pressure uniformly |
| **Gaskets / Seals** | Prevent feed-permeate mixing and external leakage |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pancake Stack" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine a **pancake stack held by a sandwich press**. Each pancake is a membrane, each layer of butter represents a feed spacer, the plate is the support plate, and the press frame is the outer frame. When you pour syrup (feed water) over the top, it drips down between pancakes (cross-flow), and a controlled amount seeps through each pancake into a tray below (permeate). You can pull out any "pancake" individually to clean or replace it — which is exactly why PF modules are preferred for **dirty, fouling-prone wastewaters**.

## 1.4 Intuitive Operating Principle

- Feed enters at high pressure and flows **tangentially** across the membrane.
- A fraction of the fluid (permeate) is forced **perpendicularly** through the membrane pores by **TransMembrane Pressure (TMP)**.
- Retentate (concentrate) exits the other end of the module.
- Particles, colloids, and macromolecules larger than the pore size are rejected.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Operating Metric:** Plate and Frame modules typically operate at **TMP = 1 – 10 bar** and **flux = 50 – 500 L/m²·h (LMH)** depending on feed water quality.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Feed Distribution** — Raw wastewater is pressurized by a feed pump and distributed uniformly across all membrane cells in the stack via a manifold.
2. **Cross-Flow Sweep** — The bulk flow moves parallel to the membrane surface, sweeping rejected solids away and reducing concentration polarization.
3. **Permeation Through Membrane** — Applied hydraulic pressure (TMP) drives solvent (water) through the asymmetric membrane. The membrane's skin layer determines selectivity.
4. **Permeate Collection** — Permeated water is collected in the permeate channel behind each membrane and drained from a common port.
5. **Retentate Discharge** — Concentrate exits at the opposite end and is either recycled or sent to sludge handling.
6. **Backwash / Cleaning-In-Place (CIP)** — Each membrane sheet can be individually removed, or the system can be flushed periodically.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Design

- **Flat geometry** maximizes **turbulence promoters (spacers)** effectiveness, mitigating fouling.
- **Stacking** allows easy scale-up — simply add more plates.
- **Removable membranes** are critical for **MF/UF** of high-fouling streams like landfill leachate, textile effluent, or industrial oily wastewater.
- **Low pressure drop** per stage makes energy profiling simple.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
