| 1 | $J = \dfrac{Q_p}{A_m}$ | **Permeate Flux** — Volumetric permeate flow per unit membrane area | L/m²·h (LMH) |
| 2 | $TMP = \dfrac{P_{f,in} + P_{f,out}}{2} - P_p$ | **TransMembrane Pressure** — Driving force for permeation | bar or kPa |
| 3 | $J = \dfrac{TMP}{\mu R_t}$ | **Darcy's Law for Membranes** — Resistance-in-Series model | LMH |
| 4 | $R_t = R_m + R_f + R_{cp}$ | **Total Resistance** — Membrane + Fouling + Concentration Polarization | m⁻¹ |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu J}$ | **Specific Resistance** — Resistance per unit flux | m⁻¹ |
| 6 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v d_h}{\mu}$ | **Reynolds Number** in feed channel | dimensionless |
| 7 | $d_h = \dfrac{4 \cdot \text{cross-section}}{\text{wetted perimeter}}$ | **Hydraulic Diameter** of spacer-filled channel | m |
| 8 | $RR = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100\%$ | **Recovery Ratio** — % of feed converted to permeate | % |
| 9 | $VCF = \dfrac{Q_f}{Q_r}$ | **Volumetric Concentration Factor** | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\beta = \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}$ | **Sieve / Rejection Coefficient** | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention:** In the Darcy equation $J = \dfrac{TMP}{\mu R_t}$, **TMP must be in Pa**, **$\mu$ in Pa·s**, and **$R_t$ in m⁻¹** for $J$ to come out in m/s. Convert to LMH by multiplying by **3.6 × 10⁶**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Landfill leachate treatment** (high TDS, refractory organics)
- **Textile/dye wastewater** decolorization (UF + NF/RO hybrid)
- **Dairy and food industry** — protein fractionation
- **Pharmaceutical** — antibiotic recovery, fermentation broth clarification
- **Metalworking fluids** — oil-water emulsion separation
- **Pre-treatment** to RO in industrial ZLD (Zero Liquid Discharge) plants

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Permeate Flux from Darcy's Law

### Starting Premise
For a clean, symmetric, isothermal membrane, the volumetric flux $J$ through the membrane is linearly proportional to the net applied pressure (TMP) and inversely proportional to the fluid's dynamic viscosity $\mu$ and the membrane's hydraulic resistance $R_m$.

### Step 1 — State Darcy's Law (Hagen-Poiseuille analog)

$$
J = \frac{TMP}{\mu \cdot R_m}
$$

*Text:* This is the foundational equation. TMP (in Pa) is the driving force; viscosity $\mu$ and resistance $R_m$ oppose flow.

### Step 2 — Expand to Resistance-in-Series Model

As fouling and concentration polarization develop during operation, additional resistance terms appear:

$$
R_t = R_m + R_f + R_{cp}
$$

*Text:* $R_m$ is intrinsic membrane resistance, $R_f$ is the fouling resistance from deposited solids, and $R_{cp}$ is the resistance from the concentrated boundary layer at the membrane surface.

### Step 3 — Substitute into the Flux Equation

$$
J = \frac{TMP}{\mu \left(R_m + R_f + R_{cp}\right)}
$$

*Text:* This is the **practical flux equation** used in all KTU design problems.

### Step 4 — Express TMP in Operating Variables

$$
TMP = \frac{P_{f,in} + P_{f,out}}{2} - P_p
$$

*Text:* The driving pressure is the difference between the average feed-side pressure (inlet and outlet) and the permeate-side pressure. For PF modules, $P_p$ is usually atmospheric.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Given:** A Plate and Frame UF module processes textile wastewater.
- $P_{f,in} = 4.0$ bar, $P_{f,out} = 3.6$ bar, $P_p = 0$ bar (atmospheric)
- $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s (water at 25°C)
- $R_m = 5.0 \times 10^{11}$ m⁻¹
- $R_f = 2.0 \times 10^{11}$ m⁻¹
- Membrane area $A_m = 10$ m²

**Step 5a — Compute TMP:**

$$
TMP = \frac{4.0 + 3.6}{2} - 0 = 3.8 \text{ bar}
$$

Convert to Pa:

$$
TMP = 3.8 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 5b — Total Resistance:**

$$
R_t = (5.0 + 2.0) \times 10^{11} = 7.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5c — Flux in SI (m/s):**

$$
J = \frac{3.8 \times 10^{5}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.0 \times 10^{11}} = 5.43 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5d — Convert to LMH:**

$$
J = 5.43 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.6 \times 10^{6} = 1954 \text{ LMH}
$$

**Step 5e — Permeate Flow Rate:**

$$
Q_p = J \times A_m = 1954 \times 10 = 19540 \text{ L/h} = 19.54 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Concentration Polarization Modulus

For cross-flow PF modules, the **Gel Polarization Model** (valid for UF/MF) gives:

$$
J = k \cdot \ln\left(\frac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p}\right)
$$

*Text:* Where $C_w$ is the wall (membrane surface) concentration, $C_b$ is the bulk concentration, and $C_p$ is the permeate concentration. The mass transfer coefficient $k$ is correlated via Sherwood correlations for spacer-filled channels.

For spacers, a common empirical correlation (Da Costa et al.):

$$
Sh = 0.46 \cdot Re^{0.36} \cdot Sc^{0.33}
$$

Where $Sh = k \cdot d_h / D$ and $Sc = \nu / D$.

## 3.3 Recovery Ratio Linkage

$$
RR = \frac{Q_p}{Q_f} = 1 - \frac{Q_r}{Q_f} = 1 - \frac{1}{VCF}
$$

*Text:* This is critical for designing a multi-pass PF stack — each pass increases recovery but also $C_b$, raising $C_w$ and hence lowering $J$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Plate and Frame Module Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FRAME[OUTER FRAME ASSEMBLY]
        A1[FEED INLET MANIFOLD] --> B1[FEED DISTRIBUTION CHANNEL]
        B1 --> C1[STACKED MEMBRANE CELLS LAYER 1]
        B1 --> C2[STACKED MEMBRANE CELLS LAYER 2]
        B1 --> C3[STACKED MEMBRANE CELLS LAYER N]
        C1 --> D1[PERMEATE COLLECTION CHANNEL 1]
        C2 --> D2[PERMEATE COLLECTION CHANNEL 2]
        C3 --> D3[PERMEATE COLLECTION CHANNEL N]
        D1 --> E1[PERMEATE MANIFOLD]
        D2 --> E1
        D3 --> E1
        C1 --> F1[RETENTATE EXIT 1]
        C2 --> F2[RETENTATE EXIT 2]
        C3 --> F3[RETENTATE EXIT 3]
        F1 --> G1[CONCENTRATE MANIFOLD]
        F2 --> G1
        F3 --> G1
    end
    E1 --> H1[TREATED WATER TO TANK]
    G1 --> H2[CONCENTRATE TO SLUDGE HANDLING]
    I1[FEED PUMP] --> A1

```


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three advantages of Plate and Frame membrane modules over Spiral Wound modules.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Ease of membrane replacement** — Individual flat sheets can be removed and replaced without discarding the entire module, reducing operating cost.
2. **High tolerance to suspended solids and fouling** — Open feed channels and spacer-induced turbulence allow processing of high-TSS wastewaters.
3. **Flexible scalability** — Membrane area can be increased simply by adding more plates to the stack, ideal for pilot and small-scale plants.

*(1 mark per correct point with brief technical justification)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Transmembrane Pressure (TMP) and state its typical operating range for Plate and Frame UF modules.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**TMP** is the net hydraulic pressure difference across the membrane, defined as the average feed-side pressure minus the permeate-side pressure:

$$
TMP = \frac{P_{f,in} + P_{f,out}}{2} - P_p
$$

For Plate and Frame **UF** modules processing wastewater, TMP typically lies in the range of **1 – 5 bar**. For MF it is **0.2 – 2 bar**, and for tight UF/NF it can reach **5 – 10 bar**.

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark range with unit)*

---

## **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)**

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **construction and working principle of a Plate and Frame membrane module**. Discuss the role of feed and permeate spacers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Plate and Frame UF unit treats **20 m³/h** of dairy wastewater. The feed inlet pressure is **3.5 bar**, outlet is **3.0 bar**, and permeate is at atmospheric pressure. Membrane resistance $R_m = 4.5 \times 10^{11}$ m⁻¹ and fouling resistance $R_f = 1.5 \times 10^{11}$ m⁻¹. Viscosity $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. If the installed membrane area is **25 m²**, determine:
   (i) TMP in bar
   (ii) Permeate flux in LMH
   (iii) Recovery ratio if concentrate flow = 4 m³/h **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- Rigid **frame** (SS-304 / SS-316 / FRP) housing the entire stack.
- Multiple **support plates** with grooved permeate channels.
- **Flat-sheet membranes** (PS, PES, PVDF) sealed by gaskets on both sides of each plate.
- **Feed spacers** define the cross-flow channel; **permeate carriers** support the membrane and collect permeate.
- **Tie rods and end plates** apply uniform compression.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
- Feed enters via distribution manifold and flows **tangentially** (cross-flow) across the membrane surface.
- Applied pressure forces water **permeating perpendicularly** through the membrane; rejected solutes exit as retentate.
- Permeate from all cells joins the common permeate manifold.

**Role of Spacers (1 mark):**
- **Feed spacers** create turbulence → reduce concentration polarization and fouling.
- **Permeate carriers** provide mechanical support and uniform permeate collection.

---

#### **Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) TMP (1 mark):**

$$
TMP = \frac{3.5 + 3.0}{2} - 0 = 3.25 \text{ bar}
$$

Convert to Pa: $TMP = 3.25 \times 10^5$ Pa.

**(ii) Flux (3 marks):**

Total resistance:

$$
R_t = (4.5 + 1.5) \times 10^{11} = 6.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}^{-1}
$$

Flux in m/s:

$$
J = \frac{3.25 \times 10^5}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.0 \times 10^{11}} = 5.417 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}
$$

Flux in LMH:

$$
J = 5.417 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.6 \times 10^{6} = 1950 \text{ LMH}
$$

**(iii) Recovery Ratio (3 marks):**

Permeate flow:

$$
Q_p = J \times A_m = 1950 \times 25 = 48{,}750 \text{ L/h} = 48.75 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

Recovery:

$$
RR = \frac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100\% = \frac{48.75}{20} \times 100\% = 243.75\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> The calculated $RR > 100\%$ is **physically impossible** and signals that the **assumed membrane area is too small** for the given feed. In real valuation, the examiner expects students to (a) report the calculated value, (b) **comment on the impossibility**, and (c) **solve for the minimum required area** to achieve a sensible $RR$ of 70–80%. Many students lose **2–3 marks** here by blindly writing the absurd number. **Always sanity-check your final answer against physical realism.**

Sanity-corrected answer — for $RR = 75\%$, $Q_p = 15$ m³/h, so required area:

$$
A_m = \frac{15 \times 10^3}{1950} = 7.69 \text{ m}^2
$$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Question]**

**(a)** Compare **Plate and Frame** with **Spiral Wound** and **Hollow Fiber** membrane modules in terms of packing density, fouling tolerance, ease of cleaning, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Plate and Frame MF module operates at $TMP = 0.8$ bar with $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. The clean membrane resistance is $R_m = 2.0 \times 10^{12}$ m⁻¹. After 30 days, flux drops to **60%** of the initial value. Assuming the drop is entirely due to fouling, calculate the **fouling resistance $R_f$** developed. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) — 7 marks**

| Parameter | Plate and Frame | Spiral Wound | Hollow Fiber |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|--------------|
| Packing Density (m²/m³) | **100 – 400** | **600 – 1000** | **1000 – 10000** |
| Fouling Tolerance | **High** (open channels) | Moderate (narrow spacers) | High (backwashable) |
| Cleaning | **Easy** (sheets replaceable) | Difficult (replace whole cartridge) | Easy (backwash + CIP) |
| Cost per m² | High | Low | Lowest |
| Typical Use | Leachate, dye, dairy, pharma | Brackish water RO, large WWTP | UF drinking water, MBR |

*(Allocation: 1 mark per correct cell × 6 cells, plus 1 mark for the concluding statement of "best suited to" high-fouling wastewaters)*

---

#### **Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Initial flux (1 mark):**

$$
J_0 = \frac{TMP}{\mu R_m} = \frac{0.8 \times 10^5}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.0 \times 10^{12}} = 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s} = 144 \text{ LMH}
$$

**Fouled flux (1 mark):**

$$
J_f = 0.60 \times 144 = 86.4 \text{ LMH} = 2.4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}
$$

**Total resistance after fouling (2 marks):**

$$
R_t = \frac{TMP}{\mu J_f} = \frac{0.8 \times 10^5}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.4 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.33 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}
$$

**Fouling resistance (3 marks):**

$$
R_f = R_t - R_m = (3.33 - 2.00) \times 10^{12} = 1.33 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B (b):**
> 1. Students often forget to convert **bar → Pa** (loses 1 mark).
> 2. Confusing **TMP as a gauge or absolute** pressure (loses 1 mark).
> 3. Forgetting the **60% drop means 60% of original**, not 60% reduction (loses 2 marks). Remember: $J_f = 0.6 J_0$, **not** $J_f = J_0 - 0.6$.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ⭐ **Plate and Frame = oldest, simplest, flat-sheet configuration** with replaceable membranes.
- ⭐ Feed flow is **cross-flow (tangential)**; permeate is **perpendicular**.
- ⭐ **TMP formula:** $\dfrac{P_{f,in} + P_{f,out}}{2} - P_p$ — average feed minus permeate.
- ⭐ **Darcy's Law:** $J = \dfrac{TMP}{\mu R_t}$ where $R_t = R_m + R_f + R_{cp}$.
- ⭐ **Unit conversion:** $1$ m/s $= 3.6 \times 10^6$ LMH.
- ⭐ Typical PF operating ranges: **MF 0.2–2 bar**, **UF 1–5 bar**, **NF 5–10 bar**.
- ⭐ **Flux range:** 50–500 LMH for wastewater (varies with foulant load).
- ⭐ **Key advantage:** individual membrane replacement and **highest fouling tolerance** among modules.
- ⭐ **Key disadvantage:** lowest packing density (100–400 m²/m³) → high footprint.
- ⭐ **Recovery Ratio:** $RR = Q_p / Q_f \times 100\%$. If $RR > 100\%$, re-check your arithmetic.
- ⭐ **Spacer role:** feed spacer = turbulence/fouling control; permeate carrier = support/permeate collection.
- ⭐ **Rejection coefficient** $\beta = C_p / C_f$ — must be **low** for treated wastewater discharge.
- ⭐ **Concentration polarization** increases wall concentration $C_w$, reducing flux — mitigated by spacers and high cross-flow velocity.
- ⭐ **Applications to remember for KTU:** landfill leachate, textile/dye effluent, dairy, oily wastewater, ZLD pre-treatment, MBR.
- ⭐ **CIP (Cleaning-In-Place)** is essential every 1–4 weeks depending on feed; uses NaOH, citric acid, or hypochlorite depending on foulant.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tubular
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tubular

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tubular Membrane Module**: A membrane separation device in which the selective membrane is cast on the inside (or outside) of a rigid porous support tube, typically with an internal diameter of **5 mm to 25 mm**, through which the feed wastewater flows under pressure. The permeate is collected radially through the membrane wall, while the concentrate (retentate) exits from the other end of the tube.

Tubular membrane modules represent one of the four principal geometric configurations used in pressure-driven membrane processes, alongside **Hollow Fiber**, **Spiral Wound**, and **Plate-and-Frame (Flat Sheet)** modules. They are extensively employed in **Microfiltration (MF)**, **Ultrafiltration (UF)**, **Nanofiltration (NF)**, and to a limited extent in **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** for wastewater streams carrying high concentrations of suspended solids, oils, greases, and biological flocs.

The classification hierarchy is as follows:

> [!NOTE]
> **Membrane Module Geometry Classification**
> 1. **Tubular** (diameter > 5 mm) – Subject of this module
> 2. **Hollow Fiber** (diameter < 2.5 mm)
> 3. **Spiral Wound** (flat sheet envelope wrapped around permeate tube)
> 4. **Plate-and-Frame** (flat sheets stacked in series)

The defining geometric feature of the tubular configuration is the **large lumen diameter**, which classifies it as a "flow-through" module rather than a "flow-along-surface" module.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Garden Hose with a Sieve**
> Imagine cutting a standard garden hose lengthwise and lining it with a fine kitchen sieve. Now imagine pushing muddy water through the inside of the hose. Clean water squeezes out radially through the sieve wall, while the mud and grit continue along the hose and exit at the far end. The garden hose is the **porous tubular support**, the sieve is the **membrane skin**, the clean water is the **permeate**, and the exiting mud is the **concentrate (retentate)**. Because the hose has a wide opening, it never chokes on the mud — this is precisely why tubular modules tolerate dirty, solids-laden wastewaters better than any other configuration.

**Why is this important in wastewater engineering?** Municipal and industrial effluents contain rags, hair, fibrous material, oil globules, and biological flocs that would instantly plug the narrow lumens of hollow-fiber modules. Tubular modules, with their generous bore, act as the **"workhorses" of pre-treatment and high-strength industrial wastewater polishing**, accepting feeds with TSS values up to several thousand mg/L.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following benchmark values are standard in KTU reference materials for tubular membrane design:

- **Tube internal diameter**: **5 mm – 25 mm** (most common: 12.5 mm and 25 mm)
- **Operating pressure (MF/UF)**: **1 – 10 bar**
- **Operating pressure (RO)**: **10 – 70 bar** (rare in tubular)
- **Crossflow velocity**: **1 – 6 m/s**
- **Membrane area per module**: **0.2 – 10 m²** (single tube), up to **30–50 m²** in multi-tube housings
- **Reynolds number**: typically **10,000 – 100,000** (turbulent flow is normal and *desirable*)
- **Reported flux range (UF)**: **40 – 120 L/m²·h** for secondary effluent
- **Backwash frequency**: every 15 – 60 minutes (CIP every 1 – 6 months)

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Philosophy Trade-off**: Tubular membranes sacrifice packing density (m²/m³ of module volume is low) in exchange for superior hydrodynamic fouling control and mechanical cleanability. Hollow fibers achieve 5–10× higher packing density but require extensive pre-screening.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The tubular membrane operates on the **crossflow filtration** principle, fundamentally different from dead-end filtration. The mechanistic logic is structured as follows:

1. **Feed Introduction**: Wastewater is pressurized by a feed pump and introduced into the lumen (bore) of the tubular membrane through an inlet header.
2. **Crossflow Generation**: The fluid flows axially along the tube length with a deliberately high velocity (1–6 m/s). This **axial crossflow** shears the membrane surface continuously, scouring deposited particles back into the bulk flow.
3. **Transmembrane Pressure (TMP) Differential**: The pressure inside the tube (P_in) is maintained higher than the pressure on the permeate side (P_permeate). This hydrostatic differential acts as the **driving force** pushing solvent and small solutes radially outward through the membrane pores.
4. **Permeate Collection**: The clean permeate passes through the membrane wall and is collected in the module's outer shell, then routed to a permeate manifold.
5. **Concentrate Discharge**: The retained stream (retentate), now concentrated in suspended/colloidal matter, exits the tube outlet at a lower pressure (P_out) and is either recycled or sent to further treatment/disposal.
6. **Fouling Mitigation**: Because shear is continuously applied, the **concentration polarization** and **cake layer** buildup are physically limited, sustaining flux over operational cycles.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Turbulent Flow**: Reynolds number > 4000 ensures turbulent eddies, which disrupt the boundary layer and prevent the rapid flux decline that would occur in laminar flow regimes. This is why tubular modules are deliberately operated in the **turbulent regime**, unlike spiral-wound modules which operate in laminar flow (Re < 1000) due to spacer-induced flow geometry.

## 2.2 Construction and Materials

- **Support Tube (Substrate)**: Porous ceramic (Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, TiO₂) for harsh chemical/thermal duties, or porous carbon/PE for polymeric systems.
- **Membrane Layer**: Cast directly on the inside (most common) or outside of the support.
- **Membrane Materials**:
  - Polymeric: **Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF)**, **Polysulfone (PSf)**, **Polyethersulfone (PES)**, **Polyacrylonitrile (PAN)**
  - Ceramic: **Alumina (α-Al₂O₃)**, **Zirconia (ZrO₂)**, **Titania (TiO₂)** — used in MBRs for oily wastewater and high-temperature streams.
- **Housing**: Stainless steel shell with permeate collection ports; end-caps with feed and retentate headers.
- **Sealing**: O-rings or compression fittings at tube ends to isolate feed/permeate streams.

## 2.3 Process Configurations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Standard Configurations for Tubular Modules**
>
> | Configuration | Description | Typical Use |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Single-pass (once-through)** | Feed passes through once; concentrate discharged | High-recovery industrial applications |
> | **Recirculation (feed-and-bleed)** | Retentate partially recycled to feed tank | Maintaining high crossflow velocity for fouling control |
> | **Multi-stage cascade** | Multiple modules in series with decreasing pressure | RO systems approaching thermodynamic limit |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> All formulae below are **directly testable** in KTU Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

$$
J = \frac{Q_p}{A_m} = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_m}
$$

This is the foundational flux equation. $J$ is the **permeate flux** [L/m²·h], $Q_p$ is volumetric permeate flow, $A_m$ is membrane area, $\Delta P$ is the **Transmembrane Pressure (TMP)**, $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, and $R_m$ is the intrinsic membrane resistance.

$$
\text{TMP} = \frac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{permeate}
$$

The TMP is the **time-averaged pressure differential** driving filtration.

$$
R_{total} = R_m + R_c + R_p
$$

Where $R_m$ is membrane resistance, $R_c$ is cake resistance, $R_p$ is pore-blocking resistance.

$$
\text{Re} = \frac{\rho \, v \, d_i}{\mu}
$$

Reynolds number for flow inside the tube. Turbulent: Re > 4000.

$$
v = \frac{Q_f}{A_{cs}} = \frac{4 \, Q_f}{\pi \, d_i^2}
$$

Crossflow velocity from volumetric feed flow.

$$
\text{Recovery} = \frac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100\,\%
$$

$$
\text{Rejection (\%)} = \left(1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f}\right) \times 100\,\%
$$

$$
A_{m,\text{tube}} = \pi \, d_i \, L
$$

Membrane area of a single tube.

$$
\text{Power}_{\text{pump}} = Q_f \times \text{TMP} \times \eta^{-1}
$$

> **Summary Table — Critical Tubular Membrane Design Variables**

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tube inner diameter | $d_i$ | **5 – 25** | mm |
| Crossflow velocity | $v$ | **1 – 6** | m/s |
| Reynolds number | Re | **10,000 – 100,000** | dimensionless |
| Transmembrane pressure | TMP | **1 – 10** (UF) | bar |
| Permeate flux (UF) | $J$ | **40 – 120** | L/m²·h |
| Recovery | $R$ | **50 – 95** | % |
| Rejection | $R_{ej}$ | **90 – 99.9** | % |
| Membrane area per tube | $A_m$ | $\pi d_i L$ | m² |
| Specific energy | $E$ | **0.5 – 5** | kWh/m³ permeate |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Tubular membranes dominate the following application niches in advanced wastewater treatment:

- **Membrane Bioreactor (MBR) — Sidestream Configuration**: Ceramic tubular modules operate with biomass concentrations of 15,000–30,000 mg/L MLSS, far exceeding immersed hollow-fiber MBRs.
- **Oily wastewater (refineries, metal working)**: Oil droplets coalesce and pass through hollow fibers as fouling; tubular geometry tolerates free oil up to several hundred ppm.
- **Landfill leachate**: High TDS, humic acids, and ammonia — tubular NF/RO modules handle aggressive chemistries.
- **Dairy and food processing**: CIP-cleanable ceramic tubes withstand 80°C, pH 1–14, and oxidizing agents.
- **Pre-treatment for SWRO**: Tubular UF is increasingly used as pre-treatment for seawater desalination, replacing conventional dual-media filters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Operating Flux from Darcy's Law

The flux through a porous membrane is governed by an analogy to **Darcy's Law** for flow through a porous medium.

**Step 1 — Start with Darcy's empirical law for porous media**

$$
J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_{total}}
$$

The volumetric flux per unit area is directly proportional to the pressure drop across the membrane and inversely proportional to the viscosity of the permeate and the total resistance to flow.

**Step 2 — Decompose the total resistance into additive components**

$$
R_{total} = R_m + R_c + R_p
$$

The membrane offers intrinsic hydraulic resistance $R_m$. As filtration proceeds, particles accumulate on the surface as a **cake** (resistance $R_c$), and some smaller particles lodge within the pores (resistance $R_p$). These are additive because they act in series — the fluid encounters each layer sequentially.

**Step 3 — Express cake resistance using the Carman–Kozeny relation**

$$
R_c = \frac{\alpha \, m_c}{A_m}
$$

Where $\alpha$ is the specific cake resistance [m/kg] and $m_c$ is the mass of cake per unit membrane area [kg/m²]. For compressible biological cakes, $\alpha$ depends on TMP.

**Step 4 — Substitute into the flux equation**

$$
J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \left( R_m + \dfrac{\alpha \, m_c}{A_m} \right)}
$$

**Step 5 — In steady-state crossflow operation, the cake thickness reaches an equilibrium**

At equilibrium, the **convective deposition** of particles is balanced by the **shear-induced back-transport**. This gives a steady $m_c$, and thus steady $J$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: This is precisely why increasing crossflow velocity (and hence shear rate) increases steady-state flux — it reduces the equilibrium cake thickness $m_c$, thereby lowering $R_c$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Flux and Power Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem** *(KTU-style calculation)*: A tubular UF module contains 50 tubes, each with inner diameter 12.5 mm and length 3 m. The feed flow rate is 8 m³/h, viscosity is 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s, density 1000 kg/m³, and TMP is 2.5 bar. Membrane resistance $R_m$ is 1.5 × 10¹² m⁻¹.
> **Find**: (a) Crossflow velocity, (b) Reynolds number, (c) Membrane area, (d) Permeate flux (ignoring cake), (e) Required pump power assuming η = 0.7.

**Part (a) — Crossflow velocity**

The cross-sectional area of one tube is:

$$
A_{cs} = \frac{\pi \, d_i^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.0125)^2}{4} = 1.227 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2
$$

The total flow area for 50 tubes is:

$$
A_{cs,\text{total}} = 50 \times 1.227 \times 10^{-4} = 6.136 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2
$$

The crossflow velocity is:

$$
v = \frac{Q_f}{A_{cs,\text{total}}} = \frac{8/3600}{6.136 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2.222 \times 10^{-3}}{6.136 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.362 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Part (b) — Reynolds number**

$$
\text{Re} = \frac{\rho \, v \, d_i}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 0.362 \times 0.0125}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 4526
$$

This is at the lower boundary of turbulent flow — design engineers would typically increase Q_f or use a recirculation pump to push Re > 10,000.

**Part (c) — Membrane area per tube and total**

$$
A_{m,\text{tube}} = \pi \, d_i \, L = \pi \times 0.0125 \times 3 = 0.1178 \, \text{m}^2
$$

$$
A_{m,\text{total}} = 50 \times 0.1178 = 5.89 \, \text{m}^2
$$

**Part (d) — Permeate flux (clean water)**

$$
J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_m} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^5}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5 \times 10^{12}} = 1.667 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m/s}
$$

Converting to L/m²·h:

$$
J = 1.667 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 \times 1000 = 600 \, \text{L/m}^2 \cdot \text{h}
$$

**Part (e) — Pump power**

$$
P_{\text{pump}} = \frac{Q_f \times \text{TMP}}{\eta} = \frac{(8/3600) \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} \times 2.5 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}}{0.7} = 793.7 \, \text{W} \approx 0.79 \, \text{kW}
$$

> **Verification**: Specific energy = 0.79 kW / (8 m³/h ÷ 3600 × 8) h = reasonable for UF.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Tubular vs. Other Module Geometries

| Parameter | **Tubular** | **Hollow Fiber** | **Spiral Wound** | **Plate & Frame** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Packing density [m²/m³] | **Low (30–300)** | Very High (5000–15000) | High (500–1000) | Low-Medium (200–500) |
| Feed channel size | **5–25 mm** | 0.5–2.5 mm | ~0.7–1.0 mm (with spacer) | ~0.5–2.0 mm |
| Hydraulic tolerance to TSS | **Excellent (up to several g/L)** | Poor (pre-filtered <50 mg/L) | Poor (pre-filtered <30 mg/L) | Moderate |
| Crossflow velocity | **1–6 m/s** | 0.1–0.5 m/s | 0.1–0.3 m/s | 0.2–0.8 m/s |
| Operating pressure | **1–10 bar (UF), 10–70 (RO)** | 0.5–3 bar | 5–70 bar | 5–50 bar |
| Energy consumption | **High** | Low | Moderate | High |
| Cleaning ease | **Excellent (CIP, backflush)** | Moderate (backflush only) | Poor (chemical) | Moderate |
| Capital cost per m² | **High** | Very Low | Low | High |
| Typical applications | Oily wastewater, leachate, MBR sidestream | Drinking water, immersed MBR | Seawater RO, brackish RO | Dairy, pharma, food |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for Examiners**: Tubular membranes win on **fouling tolerance and cleanability**; they lose on **capital cost and footprint**. Choose tubular when the feed is dirty, variable, or chemically aggressive.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Tubular Membrane Operating Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed Tank] --> B[Feed Pump]
    B --> C[Inlet Header]
    C --> D[Tubular Membrane Module]
    D --> E[Permeate Collection Shell]
    D --> F[Retentate Outlet]
    E --> G[Permeate Storage]
    F --> H{Recirculation Mode?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Recirculation Pump]
    I --> A
    H -->|No| J[Discharge / Further Treatment]
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style B fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    style D fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    style E fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000
    style F fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    style G fill:#e2d6f0,stroke:#5b2c6f,color:#000
    style H fill:#fef5e7,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000
    style I fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
    style J fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#78281f,color:#000
```

**Interpretation of the diagram**:
- The **feed pump** pressurizes the wastewater to operating TMP.
- The **inlet header** distributes flow evenly across the parallel tubes inside the module.
- The **tubular membrane module** is the heart of the system, where separation occurs.
- The **permeate** is collected in the outer shell (radial flow).
- The **retentate** exits axially; depending on operating mode, it is either recirculated (closed loop for high crossflow velocity) or sent to discharge.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Tubular Membrane System

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    FeedIntake["FEED INTAKE AND SCREENING"]:1
    FeedPump["HIGH PRESSURE FEED PUMP"]:1
    CyclonePreFilter["CYCLONIC PRE-FILTER 200 UM"]:1
    TubularBank["TUBULAR MEMBRANE BANK MULTIPLE TUBES IN PARALLEL"]:3
    PermeateManifold["PERMEATE MANIFOLD AND FLOW METER"]:1
    CIPSystem["CLEAN IN PLACE CIP SYSTEM"]:1
    RetentateValve["RETENTATE CONTROL VALVE FOR PRESSURE REGULATION"]:1
    RecircPump["RECIRCULATION PUMP FOR CROSSFLOW"]:1
    BackflushTank["BACKFLUSH WATER TANK PERIODIC REVERSE FLOW"]:1
    PLC["PLC AND SCADA CONTROL UNIT"]:3
    style FeedIntake fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style FeedPump fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style CyclonePreFilter fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    style TubularBank fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    style PermeateManifold fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000
    style CIPSystem fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
    style RetentateValve fill:#e2d6f0,stroke:#5b2c6f,color:#000
    style RecircPump fill:#fef5e7,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000
    style BackflushTank fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    style PLC fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#78281f,color:#000
```

**Functional Architecture Logic**:
- The **PLC** orchestrates the entire system: feed pump speed, retentate valve position, backflush timing, and CIP initiation.
- The **recirculation pump** maintains the high crossflow velocity critical for fouling control.
- The **CIP system** allows in-place chemical cleaning using acids, bases, or oxidizers — feasible only because tubular membranes are mechanically robust.
- The **backflush tank** periodically reverses flow to dislodge reversible cake fouling.

## 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — Crossflow Filtration Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Feed Qf enters tube inlet under pressure]
    P2[Axial flow along tube with velocity v]
    P3[Permeate Qp passes radially through membrane]
    P4[Retentate Qc exits with higher concentration]
    P5[Concentration polarization layer forms at surface]
    P6[Shear from crossflow limits polarization thickness]
    P1 --> P2 --> P3
    P2 --> P4
    P2 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    style P1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style P3 fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000
    style P4 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    style P5 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    style P6 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
```

**Interpretation**: This diagram visualizes the central physical insight of tubular membrane operation — the **competition** between permeate drag (which deposits particles on the membrane) and crossflow shear (which sweeps them back into the bulk flow). The **steady-state cake thickness** is the equilibrium outcome of this balance.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define a tubular membrane module. State any two advantages and one limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

> **Definition (1 mark)**: A tubular membrane module is a pressure-driven membrane device in which the selective skin is cast on the inside (or outside) of a porous cylindrical support tube, typically with internal diameter **5–25 mm**, operated in crossflow mode.

> **Advantages (1 mark)**:
> 1. Excellent tolerance to suspended solids and oily feed streams.
> 2. Easy mechanical and chemical cleaning (CIP) due to rigid support structure.

> **Limitation (1 mark)**: Low packing density (30–300 m²/m³) and high specific energy consumption (0.5–5 kWh/m³) lead to high capital and operating costs per unit permeate.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List the four main membrane module configurations. Why is turbulent flow preferred in tubular modules?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

> **Four configurations (2 marks)**: Tubular, Hollow Fiber, Spiral Wound, and Plate-and-Frame.

> **Reason for turbulent flow (1 mark)**: Turbulent flow (Re > 4000) generates high shear at the membrane surface, suppressing concentration polarization and cake formation, thereby sustaining higher and more stable permeate flux over time.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Total)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
Explain with a neat sketch the construction, working principle, and process configurations of a tubular membrane module used in advanced wastewater treatment. Discuss the role of crossflow velocity in fouling control.

**Model Answer**:

**Construction (2 marks)**: A tubular module consists of a porous support tube (ceramic or polymeric) with the selective membrane layer cast on the inner wall. Tubes are typically 5–25 mm in diameter and 1–3 m long, bundled inside a stainless-steel shell fitted with end-caps, inlet and retentate headers, and a permeate collection port. The membrane material may be PVDF, PES, alumina, zirconia, or titania, selected based on chemical compatibility and operating temperature.

**Working Principle (2 marks)**: Pressurized feed enters the tube lumen and flows axially with a crossflow velocity of 1–6 m/s. The TMP drives solvent and small solutes radially outward through the membrane wall, where they are collected as permeate in the outer shell. Larger particles, oils, and rejected solutes continue axially and exit as the retentate. The high crossflow velocity continuously shears the membrane surface, preventing rapid cake accumulation.

**Process Configurations (2 marks)**: 
- **Single-pass (once-through)**: Used when the feed has low fouling potential and high recovery is feasible.
- **Feed-and-bleed (recirculation)**: Retentate is partially recycled through a recirculation pump to sustain crossflow velocity; preferred for high-solids feed.
- **Multi-stage cascade**: Modules in series with progressively decreasing pressure; used in RO for maximum water recovery.

**Role of Crossflow Velocity (1 mark)**: Higher crossflow velocity increases wall shear stress, thins the concentration polarization boundary layer, and reduces the steady-state cake thickness. This raises sustainable flux and lengthens the interval between chemical cleanings. The trade-off is increased pumping energy.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**
A tubular UF module has 100 tubes of inner diameter 14 mm and length 2.5 m. Feed flow rate is 12 m³/h at TMP 3 bar. The clean membrane resistance is 1.2 × 10¹² m⁻¹, viscosity 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s, density 1000 kg/m³, and pump efficiency 75%. Calculate: (i) crossflow velocity, (ii) Reynolds number, (iii) membrane area, (iv) clean water flux in L/m²·h, and (v) required pump power.

**Model Solution**:

**(i) Crossflow velocity** (2 marks):

$$
A_{cs,\text{tube}} = \frac{\pi \times (0.014)^2}{4} = 1.539 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2
$$

$$
A_{cs,\text{total}} = 100 \times 1.539 \times 10^{-4} = 0.01539 \, \text{m}^2
$$

$$
v = \frac{12/3600}{0.01539} = 0.217 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**(ii) Reynolds number** (1 mark):

$$
\text{Re} = \frac{1000 \times 0.217 \times 0.014}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 3033
$$

This is **laminar** (Re < 4000). Recirculation is recommended to increase Re.

**(iii) Membrane area** (1 mark):

$$
A_m = 100 \times \pi \times 0.014 \times 2.5 = 10.996 \approx 11.0 \, \text{m}^2
$$

**(iv) Clean water flux** (2 marks):

$$
J = \frac{3 \times 10^5}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.2 \times 10^{12}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m/s}
$$

$$
J = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 \times 1000 = 900 \, \text{L/m}^2\cdot\text{h}
$$

**(v) Pump power** (1 mark):

$$
P = \frac{(12/3600) \times 3 \times 10^5}{0.75} = 1333 \, \text{W} = 1.33 \, \text{kW}
$$

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
Compare tubular, hollow fiber, and spiral wound membrane modules with respect to packing density, fouling tolerance, cleaning ease, and typical applications. Under what circumstances is the tubular configuration clearly preferred?

**Model Answer**:

**Comparison Table (4 marks)**:

| Parameter | Tubular | Hollow Fiber | Spiral Wound |
|---|---|---|---|
| Packing density | Low (30–300 m²/m³) | Very High (5000–15000) | High (500–1000) |
| Feed channel diameter | 5–25 mm | 0.5–2.5 mm | ~0.7–1.0 mm |
| TSS tolerance | Excellent (>1000 mg/L) | Poor (<50 mg/L) | Poor (<30 mg/L) |
| Cleaning | CIP, backflush possible | Backflush only | Chemical clean only |
| Energy demand | High | Low | Moderate |
| Typical applications | Oily WW, leachate, sidestream MBR | Drinking water, immersed MBR | Seawater RO |

**Tubular Preference Scenarios (3 marks)**:
1. **High suspended solids or oil content**: The wide lumen avoids plugging by rags, fibers, hair, or oil droplets.
2. **Variable or aggressive feed chemistry**: Ceramic tubular modules tolerate pH 0–14, chlorine, ozone, and temperatures up to 80–100°C.
3. **Frequent backflushing or CIP required**: Robust support structure allows mechanical cleaning, sponge-ball cleaning (for tubular ceramic), and harsh chemical regimens.
4. **Strict permeate quality with dirty feed**: Tubular NF/RO can replace conventional pre-treatment in difficult matrices such as landfill leachate and produced water.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**
Derive the steady-state permeate flux expression for a tubular membrane operating in crossflow mode, considering the resistance-in-series model. Explain how increasing the crossflow velocity affects the cake resistance and the sustainable flux.

**Model Solution**:

**Resistance-in-Series Model (3 marks)**:

Starting from Darcy's law:

$$
J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_{total}}
$$

The total resistance comprises three additive terms:

$$
R_{total} = R_m + R_c + R_p
$$

- $R_m$ = intrinsic membrane resistance (constant for a given membrane).
- $R_c$ = cake resistance (function of deposited mass per area and specific cake resistance).
- $R_p$ = pore-blocking resistance (from internal fouling).

Substituting:

$$
J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \left( R_m + R_c + R_p \right)}
$$

**Cake Resistance Expression (2 marks)**:

$$
R_c = \alpha \, m_c = \alpha \, \rho_s \, \delta_c
$$

Where $\alpha$ is the specific cake resistance [m/kg], $m_c$ is the mass of cake per unit membrane area, $\rho_s$ is the cake solids density, and $\delta_c$ is the cake thickness.

**Effect of Crossflow Velocity (2 marks)**:

At steady state, the convective deposition of particles is balanced by the shear-induced back-transport. The equilibrium cake thickness follows an empirical relation (often a power-law in shear rate $\gamma$):

$$
\delta_c \propto \gamma^{-n} \propto v^{-n}
$$

Where $n$ is typically between 0.3 and 0.7 for colloidal/biological foulants. Therefore, **increasing crossflow velocity decreases $\delta_c$, reduces $R_c$, and hence increases sustainable flux $J$**. The trade-off is the cubic-in-velocity increase in pumping power (for turbulent pipe flow, $\Delta P_{friction} \propto v^3$), establishing a clear **energy-flux optimization problem** in tubular membrane design.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Tubular Membrane Questions**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors**: Forgetting to convert m³/h to m³/s in power calculations, or mm to m in diameter — **always show conversions explicitly**.
> 2. **Re boundary confusion**: Students often claim tubular flow is always turbulent. In reality, low feed rates can produce laminar flow even in large tubes — **verify Re > 4000 before assuming turbulence**.
> 3. **Confusing TMP with feed pressure**: TMP is the **average** of inlet and outlet pressures minus permeate pressure, **not** the pump discharge pressure.
> 4. **Omitting cake resistance**: For *clean water* flux problems, $R_{total} = R_m$ is correct; for *wastewater* flux, the examiner expects $R_{total} = R_m + R_c + R_p$ — **state this assumption clearly**.
> 5. **Mixing module and membrane area**: A 50-tube module has 50 times the area of one tube, **not** the sum of inner + outer surface area. The membrane area is the **inner cylindrical surface** $\pi d_i L$ per tube.
> 6. **Spelling "permeate" as "permate" or "permate"** — examiners deduct ½ mark for repeated spelling errors in technical terms.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Tubular Membranes**
>
> ☐ **Definition**: Membrane cast on porous tube (5–25 mm ID), crossflow operation, permeate radial, retentate axial.
>
> ☐ **Operating regime**: Turbulent flow (Re = 10,000–100,000) preferred; high crossflow velocity (1–6 m/s) for fouling control.
>
> ☐ **Key formulas to memorize**:
>  - $J = \Delta P / (\mu \, R_{total})$
>  - $\text{TMP} = (P_{in} + P_{out})/2 - P_{permeate}$
>  - $\text{Re} = \rho v d_i / \mu$
>  - $A_m = \pi d_i L$ per tube
>  - $P_{\text{pump}} = Q_f \times \text{TMP} / \eta$
>  - Recovery $= Q_p / Q_f$
>
> ☐ **Resistance model**: $R_{total} = R_m + R_c + R_p$ — additive in series.
>
> ☐ **Material options**: Polymeric (PVDF, PES, PSf) for moderate duties; Ceramic (Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, TiO₂) for aggressive chemical/thermal/abrasive feeds.
>
> ☐ **Configurations**: Single-pass, feed-and-bleed (recirculation), multi-stage cascade.
>
> ☐ **Applications**: Oily wastewater, landfill leachate, dairy/food processing, sidestream MBR, RO pre-treatment.
>
> ☐ **Trade-offs**: Excellent fouling tolerance and cleanability vs. low packing density and high specific energy consumption.
>
> ☐ **Comparison trap**: Tubular wins on **fouling and cleanability**; hollow fiber wins on **packing density and cost**; spiral wound wins on **RO/compactness**.
>
> ☐ **Energy-flux optimization**: Higher crossflow velocity → higher flux BUT $\propto v^3$ pumping power penalty.
>
> ☐ **Examiner focus areas**: Numerical problems on flux, Re, power, and area; descriptive questions on construction, working, configurations, and comparison with other module types.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hollow Fibre (HF)** refers to a specific membrane module configuration in which the membrane material is manufactured as a self-supporting tubular structure possessing a hollow central lumen (bore). The fibre is characterized by an outer diameter typically ranging from **0.5 mm to 2.0 mm** and an inner lumen diameter from **0.2 mm to 1.0 mm**, with wall thicknesses between **0.1 mm and 0.5 mm**. The membrane wall is inherently porous (for MF/UF) or dense (for RO/NF/FO), and the geometry allows separation to occur either from the **inside of the lumen to the outside (Inside-Out)** or from the **outside of the fibre to the inside (Outside-In)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, **Hollow Fibre Membranes (HFM)** are classified under advanced membrane module geometries and represent the **highest packing density** configuration (often exceeding **10,000 m²/m³**), making them indispensable in modern **Membrane Bioreactors (MBR)**, **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** desalination, and **Ultrafiltration (UF)** potable reuse systems.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **bundle of drinking straws**, but instead of being made of plastic, each straw is made of a microscopic, sponge-like material that acts as a hyper-fine sieve. Now imagine thousands of these straws packed tightly inside a single pressure vessel (a white tube-like housing used to contain the bundle).

- When **dirty wastewater** is forced through the straws, the **clean water** seeps out through the straw walls (membrane), while the **impurities, bacteria, and suspended solids** are trapped inside the straw and flushed away as concentrate.
- Alternatively, if the dirty water is on the *outside* of the straws, the clean water is sucked *into* the hollow bore (lumen) and collected separately.

This is exactly how a **Hollow Fibre Membrane** operates — millions of tiny, semi-permeable tubes working in parallel to produce pristine treated water from contaminated wastewater.

## 1.3 Standard Physical & Performance Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)**
> - **Outer Diameter (OD):** 0.5 – 2.0 mm
> - **Inner Diameter (ID) / Lumen:** 0.2 – 1.0 mm
> - **Wall Thickness:** 0.1 – 0.5 mm
> - **Pore Size (for porous HF):** 0.01 µm (UF) – 10 µm (MF)
> - **Packing Density:** **> 10,000 m²/m³** (highest among all module configurations)
> - **Standard Operating TMP (Transmembrane Pressure):** **0.1 – 1.0 bar** (UF/MF), **10 – 30 bar** (RO)
> - **Common Polymers:** **PVDF** (Polyvinylidene Fluoride), **PES** (Polyethersulfone), **PP** (Polypropylene), **PSf** (Polysulfone), **PTFE** (Polytetrafluoroethylene)

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Configurations of Hollow Fibre

Hollow Fibre modules operate in two fundamental flow regimes, and the choice dictates the application, fouling profile, and pre-treatment requirements.

### A. Inside-Out (Closed-End / Suction) Configuration
- Feed flows through the **inner lumen** under pressure.
- Permeate passes radially outward through the membrane wall.
- Collection occurs on the shell side.
- **Advantage:** Defined feed channel; lower fouling for feed waters with low suspended solids.
- **Disadvantage:** Limited by lumen diameter (high pressure drop if feed has high TSS).
- **Typical Use:** Seawater RO, brackish water RO, point-of-use filters.

### B. Outside-In (Submerged / Immersed) Configuration
- Feed contacts the **outer surface** of the fibres.
- Permeate is drawn inward by suction into the lumen.
- **Advantage:** Very high tolerance to suspended solids; fibres can be **backwashed** effectively.
- **Disadvantage:** Risk of fibre bundle clogging (sludging) if aeration is insufficient.
- **Typical Use:** **Submerged MBR systems** in municipal wastewater treatment.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **non-negotiable formulas** that the KTU board examiner expects in any numerical problem on Hollow Fibre membrane design.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Governing Formula | Units (SI) | Remarks |
|---|----------------------|-------------------|------------|---------|
| 1 | **Membrane Flux (J)** | $J = \dfrac{Q_p}{A_m} = \dfrac{V}{A_m \cdot t}$ | $\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$ (LMH) | Permeate flow per unit area |
| 2 | **Darcy's Law for Membranes** | $J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \cdot R_m}$ | m·s⁻¹ | $R_m$ = membrane resistance |
| 3 | **Transmembrane Pressure (TMP)** | $\text{TMP} = \dfrac{P_{\text{in}} + P_{\text{out}}}{2} - P_{\text{permeate}}$ | Pa or bar | Critical for MBR operation |
| 4 | **Recovery Ratio (Y)** | $Y = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100$ | % | % feed recovered as permeate |
| 5 | **Concentration Factor (CF)** | $\text{CF} = \dfrac{C_f}{C_p}$ | dimensionless | Concentration gradient ratio |
| 6 | **Hagen-Poiseuille Flow (Lumen ΔP)** | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi r^4}$ | Pa | Pressure drop inside lumen |
| 7 | **Packing Density (φ)** | $\phi = \dfrac{A_m}{V_{\text{module}}}$ | $\text{m}^2 / \text{m}^3$ | Typically > 10,000 for HF |
| 8 | **Membrane Resistance (R_total)** | $R_t = R_m + R_f + R_c$ | m⁻¹ | $R_f$ = fouling, $R_c$ = cake |
| 9 | **Critical Flux (J_crit)** | $J < J_{\text{crit}}$ for sustainable operation | LMH | Below this, no fouling |
| 10 | **Number of Fibres (N)** | $N = \dfrac{A_m}{\pi \cdot d \cdot L}$ | dimensionless | Total fibres in module |

## 2.3 The "Why & How" Behind Each Step

- **Why TMP matters:** TMP is the **driving force** for permeation. For MBR submerged HF, TMP is usually held below **0.5 bar** to prevent irreversible fouling.
- **Why packing density matters:** Higher packing density ⇒ more membrane area in a smaller footprint ⇒ smaller plant footprint, lower civil costs.
- **Why Hagen-Poiseuille matters:** Inside-out HF systems have a lumen ΔP that limits the maximum fibre length. If $L$ is too long, $\Delta P$ inside the lumen becomes greater than the available TMP, and flux at the **closed end** drops to zero.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hollow Fibre membranes are the workhorse of:
1. **Submerged MBRs** (e.g., Kubota, Zenon/ZeeWeed, Toray) — municipal sewage treatment.
2. **Seawater Desalination (HF SWRO)** — e.g., Toyobo's SP series.
3. **Hemodialysis** (medical-grade HF dialyzers).
4. **Point-of-Use Domestic RO/UF Filters.**
5. **Industrial Effluent Polishing** for pharmaceutical, textile, and food & beverage industries.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Transmembrane Pressure (TMP) for Inside-Out Hollow Fibre

We begin with a single fibre of length $L$. The feed enters at pressure $P_{\text{in}}$ at the open end and exits at pressure $P_{\text{out}}$ at the closed end. The permeate side pressure is $P_p$ (typically atmospheric for submerged MBR, or near-atmospheric for pressurized HF).

Because the lumen pressure drops linearly along the fibre length (Hagen-Poiseuille), the **local TMP** at any axial position $z$ is:

$$\text{TMP}(z) = P(z) - P_p$$

To get the **average TMP** that governs the **average flux** along the fibre, we integrate the local flux over the fibre length and equate it to the integrated form. The axial pressure profile is:

$$P(z) = P_{\text{in}} - \dfrac{8 \mu Q_{\text{permeate}}(z)}{\pi r^4} \cdot z$$

Averaging $P(z)$ from $z = 0$ to $z = L$:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_0^L P(z) \, dz = \dfrac{P_{\text{in}} + P_{\text{out}}}{2}$$

Therefore, the **average TMP** is:

$$\boxed{\text{TMP} = \dfrac{P_{\text{in}} + P_{\text{out}}}{2} - P_p}$$

> **Logic Step:** The arithmetic mean of the inlet and outlet pressures is subtracted from the permeate pressure to yield the effective driving force. This is the **standard board-exam formulation** for inside-out HF.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Membrane Flux via Darcy's Law

For a clean membrane (no fouling), the volumetric flux $J$ is governed by Darcy's law:

$$J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \cdot R_m}$$

where $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity of permeate (≈ viscosity of water, **1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s at 20 °C**) and $R_m$ is the intrinsic membrane hydraulic resistance. As fouling and cake layer build up, the total resistance becomes:

$$R_{\text{total}} = R_m + R_f + R_c$$

The flux equation generalises to:

$$\boxed{J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \cdot (R_m + R_f + R_c)}}$$

For an **operational Hollow Fibre MBR**, typical values are:
- $R_m \approx 1 \times 10^{11} \, \text{m}^{-1}$ (clean)
- $R_c \approx 5 \times 10^{11} \, \text{m}^{-1}$ (after 30 days without cleaning)
- $R_f \approx 2 \times 10^{11} \, \text{m}^{-1}$ (pore-blocking contribution)

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Number of Hollow Fibres in a Module

Given a required total membrane area $A_m$, fibre inner diameter $d_i$, and active fibre length $L$, the number of fibres required is:

$$\boxed{N = \dfrac{A_m}{\pi \cdot d_i \cdot L}}$$

**Worked Example (KTU-style 7-mark):**

> A municipal MBR requires a total membrane area of **1500 m²**. The hollow fibres have an inner diameter of **0.8 mm** and active length of **1.5 m**. Calculate the number of fibres.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Convert units:
$d_i = 0.8 \, \text{mm} = 0.8 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$

Step 2 — Compute area per fibre:
$A_{\text{fibre}} = \pi \cdot d_i \cdot L$
$A_{\text{fibre}} = \pi \times (0.8 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1.5$
$A_{\text{fibre}} = 3.7699 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$

Step 3 — Compute total number of fibres:
$N = \dfrac{1500}{3.7699 \times 10^{-3}} = 397,887 \, \text{fibres}$

> **Answer:** Approximately **3.98 × 10⁵ fibres** are required.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Recovery Ratio and Mass Balance

For a feed flow $Q_f$ producing permeate $Q_p$ and concentrate $Q_c$:

$$Q_f = Q_p + Q_c$$

The **recovery ratio** is:

$$\boxed{Y = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100\%}$$

**Worked Example:**

> A HF RO unit treats **500 m³/day** of secondary effluent and produces **375 m³/day** of permeate. Find the recovery and concentrate flow.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Recovery:
$Y = \dfrac{375}{500} \times 100 = 75\%$

Step 2 — Concentrate flow:
$Q_c = Q_f - Q_p = 500 - 375 = 125 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: HF vs Other Membrane Configurations

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison is **exam-favourite** for KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.

| Parameter | **Hollow Fibre** | Spiral Wound | Plate & Frame | Tubular |
|-----------|------------------|--------------|---------------|---------|
| Packing Density (m²/m³) | **> 10,000** | 500–1,000 | 100–400 | < 100 |
| Fouling Tolerance | Moderate–High | Low | Moderate | Very High |
| Backwash Capability | **Excellent** | Not feasible | Limited | Good |
| Cost per m² | Moderate | Low | High | Very High |
| Suspended Solids Tolerance | High (submerged) | Low | Moderate | Very High |
| Typical Application | MBR, RO | Brackish RO | Specialty chemicals | High-viscosity feeds |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Operational Concept of Hollow Fibre Membrane Module

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        FeedIn["Wastewater Feed Qf, Cf, Pf"]:::feedNode
    end

    subgraph ModuleCore["HOLLOW FIBRE MODULE VESSEL"]

    subgraph AirSystem["AERATION AND CLEANING SUBSYSTEM"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Inside-Out vs Outside-In Flow Configurations

```mermaid

        A1["Feed enters Lumen"] --> A2["Pressure P_in > P_out"]
```

```mermaid
    Step4 -->|Yes| Step5["Trigger Backwash or Relaxation"]
```
