---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q1.** Define **Hollow Fibre Membrane** as a module configuration. Mention any **two** advantages that make it suitable for **Membrane Bioreactors (MBR)** in municipal wastewater treatment.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Hollow Fibre Membrane is a self-supporting tubular membrane with a hollow central lumen, typically having an outer diameter of **0.5 – 2.0 mm** and a wall thickness of **0.1 – 0.5 mm**. The feed flow is either forced through the lumen (inside-out) or applied externally with permeate drawn inward (outside-in). The two key advantages for MBR applications are:
1. **Very high packing density (> 10,000 m²/m³)**, which minimizes plant footprint.
2. **Excellent backwash capability**, allowing periodic reversal of flow to dislodge cake layers and restore flux.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q2.** What is **Transmembrane Pressure (TMP)**? Write the formula for the average TMP in an **inside-out** hollow fibre module and explain the significance of taking the arithmetic mean of inlet and outlet pressures.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
TMP is the **net driving pressure** that pushes permeate through the membrane wall. For an inside-out HF module:

$$\text{TMP} = \dfrac{P_{\text{in}} + P_{\text{out}}}{2} - P_p$$

The arithmetic mean is used because the lumen pressure **decreases linearly** along the fibre length (Hagen-Poiseuille flow). Using only $P_{\text{in}}$ overestimates the driving force, while using only $P_{\text{out}}$ underestimates it. The average yields the **effective TMP** that governs the **average flux** along the entire fibre.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Examiner's Note:** The KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **Module Internal Choice**. Below are **Question A** and **Question B**, each with sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying **7 marks each**.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **Inside-Out** and **Outside-In** flow configurations of a Hollow Fibre membrane module. Compare their suitability for **municipal MBR applications**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A submerged Hollow Fibre MBR has a membrane area of **1200 m²** and operates at a constant flux of **18 LMH** for **22 hours per day**. Calculate:
1. The **daily permeate volume** produced.
2. The **number of hollow fibres** required if each fibre has an inner diameter of **1.0 mm** and active length of **1.8 m**.
3. The **specific daily productivity** in $\text{m}^3/\text{m}^2/\text{day}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
Inside-Out configuration: Feed is pressurized into the lumen; permeate exits radially outward and is collected on the shell side. The fibre is sealed (potted) at one end. The advantage is a clearly defined feed channel, but the small lumen makes it vulnerable to plugging by suspended solids.

Outside-In configuration (submerged mode): The membrane cassette is immersed directly in the activated-sludge mixed liquor. A suction pump applies a mild vacuum (negative pressure) on the lumen side, drawing clean permeate inward. Coarse-bubble aeration below the module scours the fibre surface to control cake formation.

**Suitability for MBR:**
The **Outside-In (submerged)** configuration is overwhelmingly preferred for municipal MBR because:
- MLSS in MBR can be 8,000 – 12,000 mg/L, which would rapidly plug the small lumen of an inside-out fibre.
- Submerged operation allows **continuous aeration scouring** at low energy cost.
- **Backwash with permeate or NaOCl** is highly effective at restoring flux.

**(Scheme of marking: Definition 2 marks, Comparison 2 marks, MBR suitability 3 marks)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

*Step 1 — Daily permeate volume:*
$Q_p = J \times A_m \times t_{\text{op}}$
$Q_p = 18 \times 1200 \times 22$
$Q_p = 4,75,200 \, \text{L/day} = 475.2 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$

*Step 2 — Number of fibres:*
$A_{\text{fibre}} = \pi \cdot d_i \cdot L = \pi \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1.8$
$A_{\text{fibre}} = 5.6549 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$
$N = \dfrac{1200}{5.6549 \times 10^{-3}} = 2,12,206 \, \text{fibres}$

*Step 3 — Specific daily productivity:*
$\text{SP} = \dfrac{Q_p}{A_m} = \dfrac{475.2}{1200} = 0.396 \, \text{m}^3/\text{m}^2/\text{day}$

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the equation for **average Transmembrane Pressure (TMP)** for an inside-out hollow fibre module. State the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **fouling resistance build-up** in a hollow fibre MBR. A membrane has a clean resistance $R_m = 2 \times 10^{11} \, \text{m}^{-1}$. After **30 days of operation** without cleaning, the fouling resistance $R_f$ and cake resistance $R_c$ are **$1.5 \times 10^{11}$** and **$4.5 \times 10^{11} \, \text{m}^{-1}$** respectively. The TMP is **0.4 bar** and water viscosity at operating temperature is **$0.9 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa·s}$**. Calculate the **current flux**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*Assumptions:*
1. Steady-state, isothermal operation.
2. Permeate pressure $P_p$ is constant along the lumen.
3. No axial convection in the wall; only radial permeation.
4. Pressure drop inside the lumen follows Hagen-Poiseuille.

*Derivation:*  
Let the lumen pressure at axial position $z$ be $P(z)$. For a length $L$, the average pressure is obtained by integration:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{L}\int_0^L P(z)\,dz = \dfrac{P_{\text{in}} + P_{\text{out}}}{2}$$

The average TMP that drives permeation is:

$$\text{TMP} = P_{\text{avg}} - P_p = \dfrac{P_{\text{in}} + P_{\text{out}}}{2} - P_p$$

**(Scheme: 2 marks assumptions, 4 marks derivation, 1 mark final boxed equation)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Total resistance:
$$R_t = R_m + R_f + R_c = 2 \times 10^{11} + 1.5 \times 10^{11} + 4.5 \times 10^{11}$$
$$R_t = 8.0 \times 10^{11} \, \text{m}^{-1}$$

Convert TMP to Pa:
$$\text{TMP} = 0.4 \, \text{bar} = 0.4 \times 10^{5} \, \text{Pa} = 4.0 \times 10^{4} \, \text{Pa}$$

Apply Darcy's law:
$$J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \cdot R_t} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{4}}{(0.9 \times 10^{-3}) \times (8.0 \times 10^{11})}$$

$$J = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{4}}{7.2 \times 10^{8}} = 5.56 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m/s}$$

Convert to LMH:
$$J = 5.56 \times 10^{-5} \times \dfrac{10^6}{1/3600} \, \text{? Actually:} \, J \, (\text{LMH}) = 5.56 \times 10^{-5} \times 3600 \times 10^3$$

$$J = 5.56 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.6 \times 10^6 = 200.0 \, \text{LMH}$$

> **Answer:** $J \approx 200 \, \text{LMH}$ (representing severely fouled conditions — typical clean flux would be 500–800 LMH, indicating a chemical cleaning is overdue).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently forget to convert mm to m, or bar to Pa. Losing **1–2 marks** instantly. Always show the conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Hagen-Poiseuille Misuse:** Use the **lumen diameter**, not the outer diameter, when computing $N$ or lumen ΔP.
> 3. **TMP Formula:** For **submerged MBR**, $P_p$ is **negative (suction)**, not zero. Forgetting this yields an incorrect average TMP.
> 4. **Membrane Resistance Magnitude:** $R$ values are huge ($\sim 10^{11} \, \text{m}^{-1}$). Don't be alarmed by the order of magnitude.
> 5. **Schematic Quality:** In 7-mark questions, a hand-drawn or Mermaid schematic earns **at least 2 marks** even if the text is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3**

- ✅ **Hollow Fibre (HF)** is a **tubular membrane module** with a hollow lumen; OD = **0.5–2.0 mm**, ID = **0.2–1.0 mm**.
- ✅ **Packing density** is the **highest** of all module configurations (**> 10,000 m²/m³**).
- ✅ Two flow modes: **Inside-Out** (pressurized, lumen feed) and **Outside-In** (submerged, suction).
- ✅ **Outside-In (submerged)** is the **standard** configuration for **municipal MBRs**.
- ✅ **Inside-Out** is preferred for **seawater RO** and **point-of-use** domestic filters.
- ✅ **TMP** = $\dfrac{P_{\text{in}} + P_{\text{out}}}{2} - P_p$ — the **arithmetic mean** of lumen pressures.
- ✅ **Darcy's Law:** $J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \cdot R_t}$ where $R_t = R_m + R_f + R_c$.
- ✅ **Number of fibres:** $N = \dfrac{A_m}{\pi d_i L}$ — always use the **inner diameter**.
- ✅ **Common polymers:** **PVDF, PES, PP, PSf, PTFE** — PVDF dominates MBR market.
- ✅ **Aeration scouring** is essential to control cake formation in submerged HF.
- ✅ **Backwashing** (permeate + optionally NaOCl) restores flux by removing reversible fouling.
- ✅ **Recovery ratio** $Y = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100\%$ — typical MBR Y = **70–95%**.
- ✅ **Critical flux** concept: Operate below $J_{\text{crit}}$ to minimise fouling.
- ✅ Always convert **bar → Pa** (multiply by $10^5$) and **mm → m** (multiply by $10^{-3}$) in numerical problems.
- ✅ Mermaid/Markdown schematics + clean assumption statements = **+2 marks** reliably in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spiral Wound Membranes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Spiral Wound Membranes ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Spiral Wound Membrane (SWM) Module** — A compact, high-surface-area membrane configuration in which two flat-sheet membranes are sealed along three edges to form an **envelope (leaf)**, with a porous permeate spacer running between them and a feed spacer overlaying the membrane surface. The envelope is wound spirally around a central perforated permeate collection tube, producing a cylindrical pressure vessel element. The feed flow is forced axially across the membrane surface, while permeate is collected radially inward through the permeate spacer into the central tube. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEBBT725 – Advanced Wastewater Treatment)**, spiral wound elements are classified as the **dominant commercial configuration** of polymeric membrane modules for **Reverse Osmosis (RO)**, **Nanofiltration (NF)**, and **Ultrafiltration (UF)**. They offer the best trade-off between **packing density (typically 800–1,200 m²/m³)**, **capital cost**, **energy efficiency**, and **ease of cleaning/scalability** for municipal and industrial water reuse applications. ### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Glossary) | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Membrane Leaf** | The two flat membrane sheets glued on three sides with permeate spacer in between | | **Feed Spacer** | Plastic mesh between adjacent leaves that creates turbulence and defines the feed channel height | | **Permeate Spacer** | The tricot/fabric material inside the leaf that carries permeate to the central tube | | **Permeate Tube (ATD)** | The central perforated PVC tube where permeate is collected (Anti-Telescoping Device) | | **Glue Line** | The adhesive (typically polyurethane) line sealing three sides of each leaf | | **Envelope** | One membrane leaf + permeate spacer assembly | | **Element** | The complete wound module (e.g., the industry-standard 8-inch × 40-inch element) | | **Pressure Vessel** | The outer steel/FRP tube housing one or more elements in series | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Burrito vs. the Sandwich" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Imagine making a *giant burrito*. Take a flat tortilla (the feed spacer), lay a paper-thin membrane sheet on each side, glue the three open edges shut, and then roll the whole thing tightly around a drinking straw (the central tube). Now pour salty wastewater into one end of the rolled cylinder and press it through. Pure water squeezes *through the membrane* and spirals inward to the straw, while concentrated brine exits the other end. The "burrito" wrapping maximizes membrane area in a small tube — that is the spiral wound concept. A more direct comparison: - **Plate-and-Frame** → like a stack of sandwiches (small area, high cost). - **Hollow Fibre** → like a bundle of thin straws (high area, but very fragile to fouling). - **Spiral Wound** → the *rolled sandwich* — best of both: high area, robust, and cheap. ### 1.4 Standard Industry Metrics (Memorize for KTU) - **Standard element size:** **8-inch diameter × 40-inch length (8"×40")** = approx. **37 m²** active membrane area. - **Operating pressure (RO brackish water):** **10–30 bar**. - **Operating pressure (RO seawater):** **55–85 bar**. - **Packing density:** **800–1,200 m²/m³** (very high). - **Feed channel thickness:** typically **0.7–1.1 mm** (defined by feed spacer thickness). - **Cross-flow velocity:** **0.1–0.3 m/s** in the feed channel. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Construction Logic The construction of a spiral wound module follows a strict, repeatable procedure. Each step is engineered to optimize **mass transfer, mechanical stability, and hydraulic efficiency**. 1. **Select membrane flat sheet** (polyamide thin-film composite for RO; PES/PS for UF). Roll width is typically 36–40 inches for an 8" element. 2. **Cut permeate spacer** (tricot polyester or polypropylene) to the same width; this spacer provides a low-resistance pathway for permeate flow to the tube. 3. **Fold/spacer assembly** — Place the permeate spacer between two membrane sheets such that one open edge aligns with the perforations of the central permeate tube. 4. **Apply glue lines** — Three edges are sealed with **polyurethane** adhesive; the fourth (open) edge is glued to the perforations of the permeate tube. The glue line width is critical (typically 3–5 mm) — too thin causes bypass; too thick blocks membrane area. 5. **Wind around the central tube** — The envelope is wound around the perforated permeate tube (typically PVC with a few hundred pin-holes) like a jelly roll. As winding proceeds, **feed spacer sheets** are inserted between adjacent layers to keep them apart and induce turbulence. 6. **Install Anti-Telescoping Devices (ATDs)** at both ends — these prevent the roll from "telescoping" (sliding apart) under pressure differential, which would tear the glue lines. 7. **Apply outer wrap** — A fiberglass or tape outer wrap is applied, then end-caps are glued on with the permeate tube protruding. 8. **Cure and quality test** — Elements are wet-tested at the factory for salt rejection and flux. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Component > [!NOTE] > **The Why of the Design** — every part exists to solve a real engineering problem. - **Feed spacer (mesh):** Without it, the leaves would press flat against each other → zero cross-flow → no shear → instantaneous fouling. The spacer creates a defined **feed channel height (h)** that allows a turbulent or transitional flow that sweeps foulants away. - **Permeate spacer (tricot):** Permeate that crosses the membrane must travel *sideways* to reach the central tube. Without a spacer, permeate would have to squeeze through a near-zero-thickness gap → enormous pressure drop. - **Glue lines (3 sides):** Force the feed to flow *across* the membrane (cross-flow) rather than *through* the leaf. Sealing three sides means the only way out is through the membrane into the permeate channel. - **Central perforated tube:** Provides a low-resistance collection manifold; pin-hole density and pattern are designed to give uniform permeate withdrawal. - **ATDs (anti-telescoping devices):** Without them, the high pressure differential (up to 80 bar in seawater) would push one end of the roll "out" of the cylinder, ripping the glue seams. ### 2.3 Operating Modes and Flow Geometry There are two flow regimes in the spiral wound element, and understanding both is critical for KTU problems: | Region | Flow Direction | Fluid | Driving Force | |---|---|---|---| | **Feed channel** (between leaves) | **Axial** (along the cylinder axis) | Feed / Concentrate | Pump pressure | | **Permeate channel** (inside the leaf) | **Radial / Spiral** (inward to tube) | Permeate (product) | Trans-membrane pressure | This is the **cross-flow** principle: feed flows *tangentially* to the membrane; permeate flows *normal* to it. The cross-flow velocity scours the membrane surface and limits concentration polarization. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Equations for SWM — Reproduce these in numerical problems.** | # | Equation | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P - \Delta \pi}{\mu \cdot R_m}$ | Permeate flux (Solution-Diffusion model) | $\text{m/s}$ or $\text{L/m}^2\text{h}$ (LMH) | | 2 | $J = L_p(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ | Same as above, where $L_p = 1/(\mu R_m)$ | — | | 3 | $R_{obs} = R_m + R_f + R_{cp}$ | Resistance-in-series for fouled membrane | $\text{m}^{-1}$ | | 4 | $A = \dfrac{\pi (D_o - D_i)}{s}$ | Membrane area of one leaf (width $D_o-D_i$, length $s$) | $\text{m}^2$ | | 5 | $N_{leaf} = \dfrac{L_{elem}}{2(t_m + t_s + t_{fs})}$ | Number of leaves in one element | dimensionless | | 6 | $A_{elem} = N_{leaf} \cdot A_{leaf} \cdot 2$ | Total membrane area of one element (×2 because two sheets per leaf) | $\text{m}^2$ | | 7 | $\beta = \dfrac{\text{permeate flow}}{\text{feed flow}}$ | Recovery (or conversion) ratio, $0 < \beta < 0.5$ typically for single-stage RO | dimensionless | | 8 | $v = \dfrac{Q_f}{N \cdot W \cdot h}$ | Cross-flow velocity in feed channel ($N$ leaves, $W$ width, $h$ channel height) | $\text{m/s}$ | | 9 | $\Delta P_{perm} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot Q_p \cdot L_{sp}}{K_{sp} \cdot W \cdot t_{sp}^2}$ | Pressure drop in permeate spacer | $\text{Pa}$ | | 10 | $\text{Re}_{sp} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot d_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number in feed spacer (typically 100–500, sub-turbulent) | dimensionless | | 11 | $C_w = C_b \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{J}{k}\right)$ | Film-theory wall concentration (concentration polarization) | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | | 12 | $k = 0.023 \cdot \text{Re}^{0.8} \cdot \text{Sc}^{0.33} \cdot \dfrac{D}{d_h}$ | Mass-transfer coefficient (Dittus–Boelter analogy) | $\text{m/s}$ | **Where the variables stand:** - $J$ = permeate flux - $\Delta P$ = trans-membrane pressure - $\Delta \pi$ = osmotic pressure difference across membrane - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of water - $R_m$ = intrinsic membrane resistance - $R_f$ = fouling resistance (irreversible) - $R_{cp}$ = concentration polarization resistance - $L_p$ = hydraulic permeability of clean membrane - $D_o, D_i$ = outer and inner diameters of the wound element - $s$ = length of one leaf (axial) - $t_m, t_s, t_{fs}$ = thicknesses of membrane, permeate spacer, feed spacer - $L_{elem}$ = element length (e.g., 1.016 m for 40 inch) - $Q_f, Q_p$ = feed and permeate flow rates - $K_{sp}$ = spacer permeability constant - $d_h$ = hydraulic diameter of feed channel - $D$ = solute diffusivity - $C_b, C_w$ = bulk and wall concentrations ### 2.5 Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > Spiral wound modules are the **backbone of modern desalination** (≈ 90% of all new SWRO plants use 8" SWM elements) and of industrial water reuse (semiconductor, pharma, food & beverage). For KTU, you should be able to relate SWM design choices to: **(i) flux, (ii) fouling control, (iii) energy consumption**, and **(iv) the engineering feasibility of municipal-scale reuse projects.** --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Membrane Area of a Single Spiral Wound Element > **Problem Setup.** A spiral wound element has outer diameter $D_o$, inner diameter (permeate tube outer diameter) $D_i$, and axial length $L_{elem}$. Each leaf is wound from the inner radius $D_i/2$ outward to the outer radius $D_o/2$. The leaf width (in the axial direction, $s$) and the spiral arc length at any radius $r$ are related by the radial growth of the spiral. We can derive the total membrane area from first principles. **Step 1 — Express the spiral unwinding.** When the leaf is unwound, the axial dimension of the element is the leaf width $s$, and the radial direction is the spiral arc length. At a radius $r$, one complete revolution of the spiral has arc length $2\pi r$. **Step 2 — Differential membrane area of one leaf.** The leaf is composed of two membrane sheets sandwiching a permeate spacer. The differential area on one side of the leaf, between radii $r$ and $r + dr$, is: $$ dA = 2\pi r \cdot s \cdot dr $$ The factor of $2\pi r$ is the circumference at radius $r$, and $s$ is the axial length of the leaf at that circumference. The two membrane sheets per leaf give a factor of 2, so: $$ dA_{leaf} = 2 \cdot 2\pi r \cdot s \cdot dr = 4\pi s r \, dr $$ **Step 3 — Integrate from inner to outer radius.** $$ A_{leaf} = \int_{D_i/2}^{D_o/2} 4\pi s \, r \, dr $$ Carrying out the integration: $$ A_{leaf} = 4\pi s \left[ \dfrac{r^2}{2} \right]_{D_i/2}^{D_o/2} = 2\pi s \left[ \left(\dfrac{D_o}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\dfrac{D_i}{2}\right)^2 \right] $$ Simplifying the bracket: $$ \left(\dfrac{D_o}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\dfrac{D_i}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{4} $$ Therefore: $$ A_{leaf} = 2\pi s \cdot \dfrac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi s (D_o^2 - D_i^2)}{2} $$ > [!NOTE] > This matches the form quoted in industry texts: $A = \pi s (D_o^2 - D_i^2)/2$, with $s$ being the leaf length and $D$ the diameters in consistent units. **Step 4 — Total element area.** If the element has $N_{leaf}$ leaves, each contributing two membrane sheets, the total area is: $$ A_{elem} = N_{leaf} \cdot \pi s (D_o^2 - D_i^2) \quad \text{(with } N_{leaf}\text{ already including the }\times 2 \text{ sheets factor)} $$ For an **8" × 40" element** ($D_o = 0.2032$ m, $D_i \approx 0.0381$ m for 1.5" permeate tube, $L_{elem} = 1.016$ m): $$ A_{leaf} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot s \cdot (D_o^2 - D_i^2)}{2} \approx \text{(per single leaf, value depends on $s$)} $$ The number of leaves for a typical SWM with $t_m = 150\ \mu\text{m}$, $t_{ps} = 250\ \mu\text{m}$ (permeate spacer), $t_{fs} = 0.71\ \text{mm}$ (feed spacer): $$ N_{leaf} = \dfrac{L_{elem}}{2(t_m + t_{ps} + t_{fs})} = \dfrac{1.016}{2(0.00015 + 0.00025 + 0.00071)} \approx \dfrac{1.016}{0.00222} \approx 458 $$ So an 8"×40" element typically has on the order of 15–25 leaves in practice (after accounting for multiple sheets per leaf "wrap" and feed spacer thickness variations); the precise number depends on the manufacturer's design. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Cause → Effect → Design Response > **Use this table in KTU viva or design questions.** | Phenomenon | Physical Cause | Engineering Effect | Design Response in SWM | |---|---|---|---| | **Concentration polarization** | Solutes accumulate at membrane surface | Higher $C_w$ → higher $\Delta\pi$ → lower flux | Use thicker, denser feed spacers to increase turbulence ($k$) | | **Fouling** | Particulates, organics, biofilms deposit | Reduced $J$ over time | Cross-flow velocity 0.1–0.3 m/s, periodic CIP, air-scour | | **Scaling** | Precipitation of CaCO₃, CaSO₄, SiO₂ | Irreversible flux loss | LSI control, antiscalant dosing, recovery limit | | **Telescoping** | Pressure pushes one end of roll out of cylinder | Glue seam rupture | Anti-telescoping devices (ATDs) on both ends | | **Permeate pressure drop** | Permeate spacer has finite $K$ | Suction pressure rises along element | Use thicker tricot, multiple perforations on tube | | **Glue line leakage** | Adhesive failure | Feed bypass into permeate → rejection loss | Triple-glue line, ATDs, controlled curing | | **Feed channel plugging** | Suspended solids accumulate | Localized flux loss | Pre-filtration (5 µm), feed spacer with optimized geometry | ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Full Step-by-Step) > **Q.** A brackish water RO spiral wound element (8" × 40") operates with feed flow $Q_f = 12\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$, producing $Q_p = 4\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$ of permeate at a trans-membrane pressure $\Delta P = 15$ bar. Membrane permeability $L_p = 4 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m/Pa·s}$. The feed has 2,000 mg/L NaCl ($\pi_{feed} \approx 1.5$ bar) and permeate 200 mg/L ($\pi_{perm} \approx 0.15$ bar). Active membrane area is 37 m². Calculate (a) recovery, (b) permeate flux, (c) observed membrane resistance. **Step (a) — Recovery (conversion ratio).** $$ \beta = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} = \dfrac{4}{12} = 0.333 \;\; (33.3\%) $$ **Step (b) — Permeate flux.** $$ J = \dfrac{Q_p}{A_{elem}} = \dfrac{4/3600\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}}{37\ \text{m}^2} = \dfrac{1.111 \times 10^{-3}}{37} = 3.00 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m/s} $$ Convert to LMH (liter per m² per hour): $$ J_{LMH} = 3.00 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 1000 \cdot 3600 = 108\ \text{L/m}^2\text{h} $$ **Step (c) — Observed resistance.** Using the solution-diffusion flux equation, solve for the effective resistance: $$ J = L_p (\Delta P - \Delta\pi) \quad\Rightarrow\quad L_p = \dfrac{J}{\Delta P - \Delta\pi} $$ $$ \Delta\pi = \pi_{feed} - \pi_{perm} = 1.5 - 0.15 = 1.35\ \text{bar} = 1.35 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa} $$ $$ \Delta P - \Delta\pi = 15 \times 10^5 - 1.35 \times 10^5 = 13.65 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa} $$ $$ L_p^{observed} = \dfrac{3.00 \times 10^{-5}}{13.65 \times 10^5} = 2.20 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m/Pa·s} $$ This is **lower than the clean-membrane $L_p = 4 \times 10^{-12}$**, indicating **fouling or compaction** has reduced permeability to about 55% of clean value. Effective total resistance: $$ R_{total} = \dfrac{1}{\mu L_p^{obs}} = \dfrac{1}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 2.20 \times 10^{-12}} = 4.55 \times 10^{14}\ \text{m}^{-1} $$ > [!TIP] > This kind of calculation is a classic KTU 14-mark question. Walk through **every step** in the answer; the examiner awards partial marks for correct setup even if the final arithmetic slips. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Spiral Wound Element — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Feed Inlet
Axial Entry] --> B[Feed Spacer Network
Crossflow Across Leaves] B --> C[Membrane Leaf Layer 1
Polyamide TFC Sheet] B --> D[Membrane Leaf Layer 2
Polyamide TFC Sheet] B --> E[Membrane Leaf Layer N
Polyamide TFC Sheet] C --> F[Permeate Spacer Channel 1] D --> G[Permeate Spacer Channel 2] E --> H[Permeate Spacer Channel N] F --> I[Central Perforated
Permeate Tube ATD] G --> I H --> I I --> J[Permeate Outlet
Radial to Tube] B --> K[Concentrate Outlet
Axial Exit] L[Glue Line Seams
3 Sides] -.-> C L -.-> D L -.-> E M[Anti Telescoping Device ATD] -.-> A M -.-> K N[Outer FRP Wrap] -.-> B style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style K fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style J fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style I fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style N fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Construction Sequence of a SWM Leaf ```mermaid flowchart TB stepA[Step 1 Cut Polyamide Membrane Sheet] --> stepB[Step 2 Place Permeate Spacer Tricot Fabric] stepB --> stepC[Step 3 Fold Membrane Around Spacer Forming Envelope] stepC --> stepD[Step 4 Apply Polyurethane Glue Lines on 3 Sides] stepD --> stepE[Step 5 Glue Open Edge to Central Permeate Tube] stepE --> stepF[Step 6 Insert Feed Spacer Mesh] stepF --> stepG[Step 7 Wind Leaf Spirally Around Tube] stepG --> stepH[Step 8 Install Anti Telescoping Devices] stepH --> stepI[Step 9 Apply Outer FRP Wrap and End Caps] stepI --> stepJ[Step 10 Quality Test Salt Rejection and Flux] style stepA fill:#e3f2fd style stepJ fill:#c8e6c9 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Flow Path Inside an Operating Element ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Pressurized Feed
0.5 to 8 MPa] --> Q[Feed Channel Between Leaves
Crossflow Direction] Q --> R[Solvent Water Permeates Through Membrane] Q --> S[Solutes Retained on Membrane Surface] R --> T[Permeate Spacer Channel
Spiral Inward Flow] S --> U[Concentrate Brine Exits Axially] T --> V[Central Perforated Tube] V --> W[Permeate Collection Manifold] style P fill:#bbdefb style W fill:#c8e6c9 style U fill:#ffccbc ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Pressure and Flux Profile Along Element ```mermaid flowchart TB X1[Inlet End: High Delta P, High Flux, Low Cp] --> X2[Mid Element: Moderate Delta P, Moderate Flux, Rising Cp] X2 --> X3[Outlet End: Low Delta P, Low Flux, High Cp High Osmotic Pressure] X3 --> X4[Recovery Limited by Osmotic Pressure at Tail] style X1 fill:#c8e6c9 style X2 fill:#fff9c4 style X3 fill:#ffccbc style X4 fill:#ef9a9a ``` > [!TIP] > For KTU 14-mark questions, **always include a labelled diagram** of the SWM cross-section. Even a hand-sketched "rolled-up" view with permeate tube, leaves, and feed spacer earns significant partial-credit marks. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** **Define spiral wound membrane module. State any four advantages of SWM over plate-and-frame and hollow-fibre modules.** **Model Answer:** > A **spiral wound membrane (SWM) module** is a configuration in which a flat-sheet membrane envelope (two sheets separated by a permeate spacer) is wound spirally around a central perforated permeate collection tube, with feed spacers placed between adjacent layers to maintain a cross-flow feed channel. **Four advantages over plate-and-frame and hollow-fibre:** 1. **Higher packing density** (800–1,200 m²/m³) compared to plate-and-frame (100–400 m²/m³). 2. **Lower capital and replacement cost** due to standardized 8"×40" elements and mass production. 3. **Tolerates higher feed turbidity** (with 5 µm pre-filter) compared to delicate hollow-fibre UF/MF. 4. **Easious to scale up** by adding more elements in series inside a single pressure vessel. 5. (Bonus) **Lower energy consumption per m³ of permeate** in brackish water applications. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall — Don't** list advantages without first defining SWM. The 3-mark scheme allocates ~1 mark for the definition and ~2 marks for the listed points. A bare bullet list = ~1.5 marks only. --- #### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]** **Explain the role of the feed spacer and the anti-telescoping device (ATD) in a spiral wound membrane element.** **Model Answer:** > **Feed spacer:** A plastic mesh (typically 0.7–1.1 mm thick, made of polypropylene) placed between adjacent membrane leaves. It performs three functions: **(i)** maintains a uniform feed-channel height so that feed can flow axially across the membrane; **(ii)** induces turbulence (or at least transitional flow) at the membrane surface, which enhances mass-transfer coefficient $k$ and limits concentration polarization; **(iii)** supports the membrane against the high trans-membrane pressure that would otherwise collapse the channel. > **Anti-Telescoping Device (ATD):** A mechanical fitting (usually a star-shaped plastic or metal piece) installed at both ends of the wound element. It prevents the membrane leaves from sliding axially relative to each other under the high pressure differential (up to 80 bar in seawater RO). Without ATDs, the wound roll would "telescope" outward under pressure, tearing the polyurethane glue lines and causing feed-to-permeate leakage, which destroys salt rejection. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall — Don't** confuse **feed spacer** (between leaves, in the feed stream) with **permeate spacer** (inside the leaf, in the product stream). Writing about permeate spacer when asked about feed spacer = 0 marks for that part. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **Note:** As per KTU ESE pattern, you must answer **one** full question from the choice given below. Each question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Module 3, SWM Design and Operating Analysis** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** **(a)** With a neat labelled sketch, describe the **construction and working of a spiral wound RO membrane element**. Discuss the functions of (i) permeate spacer, (ii) feed spacer, (iii) central permeate tube, and (iv) glue lines. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **brackish water RO plant** uses 100 spiral wound elements (8" × 40", active area = 37 m² each) operating at feed pressure 18 bar, feed flow 15 m³/h per element, recovery 35%, average flux 15 LMH. Calculate the **(i) total permeate flow per day**, **(ii) total feed flow per day**, and **(iii) salt rejection** if the feed has 2,500 mg/L NaCl and permeate has 80 mg/L NaCl. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks** **Construction:** 1. Two polyamide thin-film composite (TFC) membrane sheets are laid on either side of a porous permeate spacer (tricot fabric). 2. Three edges are sealed with polyurethane adhesive, forming an "envelope" or "leaf." 3. The fourth (open) edge is glued onto the perforations of a central PVC permeate collection tube. 4. A feed spacer mesh is overlaid, and the assembly is wound spirally around the central tube to form a cylinder. 5. Anti-telescoping devices (ATDs) are fitted at both ends. 6. The roll is wrapped with fiberglass tape, and end-caps with brine/permeate ports are glued on. 7. The element is wet-tested for rejection and flux. **Working:** Feed enters one end of the pressure vessel axially, flows in the cross-flow direction through the feed channel between leaves (sweeping the membrane surface), and exits as concentrated brine from the other end. Permeate passes through the membrane, spirals inward through the permeate spacer, and is collected by the central tube and discharged as product. **Functions of components:** - **(i) Permeate spacer:** Provides a low-resistance pathway for the product water to travel from where it crosses the membrane to the central tube. Without it, the permeate channel would have near-zero thickness, leading to enormous pressure drop and inability to collect permeate. - **(ii) Feed spacer:** Defines the height of the feed channel, supports the membrane against collapse, and creates turbulence that limits concentration polarization. - **(iii) Central permeate tube:** A perforated PVC pipe that collects the permeate uniformly and channels it out of the element; the perforations must be sized to avoid creating "dead spots." - **(iv) Glue lines:** Seal three sides of the leaf to force feed flow *across* the membrane and not bypass into the permeate channel; also bond the leaf to the central tube. **Labelled sketch** (full marks require this): Draw a circle (cross-section of element) with central tube, two leaves wrapping around it, feed spacers between leaves, and arrows showing feed flow axial and permeate flow radial-inward. --- **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks** **Given:** - $N = 100$ elements - $A_{elem} = 37\ \text{m}^2$ - $Q_{f,elem} = 15\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$ - Recovery $\beta = 0.35$ - $J_{avg} = 15\ \text{LMH}$ - $C_f = 2{,}500\ \text{mg/L}$, $C_p = 80\ \text{mg/L}$ **Step (i) — Total permeate flow per day.** Per element permeate rate: $$ Q_{p,elem} = J \times A_{elem} = 15\ \text{L/m}^2\text{h} \times 37\ \text{m}^2 = 555\ \text{L/h} = 0.555\ \text{m}^3/\text{h} $$ Total permeate from 100 elements: $$ Q_{p,total} = 100 \times 0.555 = 55.5\ \text{m}^3/\text{h} $$ Per day: $$ Q_{p,daily} = 55.5 \times 24 = 1{,}332\ \text{m}^3/\text{day} $$ **Step (ii) — Total feed flow per day.** $$ Q_{f,total} = \dfrac{Q_{p,total}}{\beta} = \dfrac{55.5}{0.35} = 158.57\ \text{m}^3/\text{h} $$ Per day: $$ Q_{f,daily} = 158.57 \times 24 = 3{,}805.7\ \text{m}^3/\text{day} $$ **Step (iii) — Salt rejection.** $$ R_{salt} = \dfrac{C_f - C_p}{C_f} \times 100 = \dfrac{2500 - 80}{2500} \times 100 = 96.8\% $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall — Don't** confuse **recovery** ($\beta = Q_p/Q_f$) with **rejection** ($R = (C_f - C_p)/C_f$). They are completely different concepts. Recovery is a *flow ratio*; rejection is a *concentration ratio*. KTU examiners frequently set traps on this. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Module 3, Alternative Choice (Fouling & Mass Transfer)** **(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **concentration polarization** in spiral wound membrane modules. Derive the **film-theory equation** relating wall concentration to bulk concentration. List two design features in SWM that reduce concentration polarization. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A spiral wound RO element has $C_b = 5{,}000\ \text{mg/L}$, $J = 20\ \text{LMH}$, and mass-transfer coefficient $k = 50\ \text{LMH}$. Calculate the **wall concentration** $C_w$. If the osmotic pressure of the feed at the wall is approximated as $\pi_w = 0.75 \times C_w$ (in bar, with $C_w$ in g/L), and $\Delta P = 20$ bar, compute the **effective driving pressure**. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks** **Concentration polarization:** When permeate flows *through* the membrane, solutes are rejected and accumulate at the membrane surface, forming a thin boundary layer of high concentration. The steady-state balance of convective solute transport toward the membrane and back-diffusion away from it defines a wall concentration $C_w > C_b$. This phenomenon, called **concentration polarization**, raises the local osmotic pressure at the membrane, reduces net driving force $(\Delta P - \Delta\pi)$, decreases flux, and accelerates scaling/fouling. **Derivation of film-theory equation:** Consider a differential slice of the membrane at steady state. Solute flux toward the membrane by convection is $J \cdot C$. Solute flux away from the membrane by back-diffusion is $-D \cdot dC/dy$ (Fick's law). At steady state, the net solute accumulation is zero: $$ J \cdot C - D \dfrac{dC}{dy} = 0 $$ Rearranging: $$ J \, dC = D \, \dfrac{dC}{dy} \cdot dy \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{(rearranging the differential form)} $$ Wait — let me write this correctly. The solute flux at any point in the boundary layer is: $$ J_s = J C - D \dfrac{dC}{dy} $$ For a fully rejected solute, $J_s = 0$ at the membrane surface (no solute crosses the membrane). Setting $J_s = 0$: $$ J C = D \dfrac{dC}{dy} $$ Separating variables: $$ \dfrac{dC}{C} = \dfrac{J}{D} dy $$ Integrate from the bulk ($y = 0$, $C = C_b$) to the wall ($y = \delta$, $C = C_w$): $$ \int_{C_b}^{C_w} \dfrac{dC}{C} = \dfrac{J}{D} \int_0^{\delta} dy $$ $$ \ln\left(\dfrac{C_w}{C_b}\right) = \dfrac{J \delta}{D} $$ Define the mass-transfer coefficient $k = D/\delta$: $$ \ln\left(\dfrac{C_w}{C_b}\right) = \dfrac{J}{k} $$ $$ \boxed{C_w = C_b \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{J}{k}\right)} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **classic film-theory equation** and is examinable in any KTU exam on SWM. **Two design features in SWM that reduce concentration polarization:** 1. **Feed spacer mesh** — induces turbulence in the feed channel, raising $k$ and lowering $C_w/C_b$. 2. **Cross-flow velocity control** — operating at higher $v$ (0.1–0.3 m/s) increases Reynolds number and $k$, reducing polarization. 3. (Bonus) **Narrower feed channels** — increase shear rate. 4. (Bonus) **Pulsed feed / air-scour** during operation enhances mixing. --- **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks** **Given:** - $C_b = 5{,}000\ \text{mg/L} = 5\ \text{g/L}$ - $J = 20\ \text{LMH}$ - $k = 50\ \text{LMH}$ - $\Delta P = 20\ \text{bar}$ - Empirical relation: $\pi_w = 0.75 \times C_w$ (bar, with $C_w$ in g/L) **Step 1 — Compute $C_w$.** $$ C_w = C_b \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{J}{k}\right) = 5 \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{20}{50}\right) = 5 \cdot e^{0.4} $$ Using $e^{0.4} \approx 1.4918$: $$ C_w = 5 \times 1.4918 = 7.459\ \text{g/L} = 7{,}459\ \text{mg/L} $$ **Step 2 — Compute $\pi_w$.** $$ \pi_w = 0.75 \times 7.459 = 5.594\ \text{bar} $$ **Step 3 — Compute effective driving pressure.** The permeate side has essentially pure water, so $\pi_p \approx 0$. The net driving pressure is: $$ \Delta P_{net} = \Delta P - \pi_w = 20 - 5.594 = 14.406\ \text{bar} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall — Don't** forget that the osmotic pressure is calculated using the **wall** concentration $C_w$, not the bulk $C_b$. Using $\pi_b$ instead of $\pi_w$ is the most common mistake in this type of problem and costs 2–3 marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Spiral Wound Membranes** - ✅ **SWM = envelope of two membrane sheets + permeate spacer, wound around a perforated central tube, separated by feed spacers.** - ✅ **Standard industrial size:** **8" × 40"** with ≈ **37 m²** active area. - ✅ **Packing density:** **800–1,200 m²/m³** (very high; highest among commercial modules). - ✅ **Permeate tube = PVC with pin-holes**; collects permeate radially from inside the leaf. - ✅ **Feed spacer** sits *between* leaves (in the feed stream); **permeate spacer** sits *inside* the leaf (in the product stream). These are the two spacers and they are NOT the same. - ✅ **ATDs** (anti-telescoping devices) are mandatory at both ends to prevent glue-line failure under high $\Delta P$. - ✅ **Three glue lines** seal the leaf; the fourth edge is glued to the central tube. - ✅ **Operating pressure:** RO brackish 10–30 bar; RO seawater 55–85 bar. - ✅ **Cross-flow velocity:** 0.1–0.3 m/s in the feed channel. - ✅ **Key flux equation:** $J = L_p (\Delta P - \Delta\pi) = \dfrac{\Delta P - \Delta\pi}{\mu R_m}$. - ✅ **Film-theory polarization:** $C_w = C_b \exp(J/k)$ — wall concentration exceeds bulk by exponential factor. - ✅ **Concentration polarization** raises local $\pi$ → lowers flux → accelerates scaling/fouling. - ✅ **Resistance-in-series:** $R_{obs} = R_m + R_f + R_{cp}$. - ✅ **Membrane area equation:** $A_{leaf} = \dfrac{\pi s (D_o^2 - D_i^2)}{2}$ for one leaf (two sheets). - ✅ **Recovery $\beta = Q_p / Q_f$** ≠ **Rejection $R = (C_f - C_p)/C_f$** — never confuse. - ✅ **Advantages of SWM:** high packing density, low cost, easy scale-up, good for RO/NF. - ✅ **Disadvantages of SWM:** not suitable for high-solids feed (needs 5 µm prefilter), hard to clean mechanically, single-element replacement. - ✅ **Concentrate exits axially; permeate exits radially inward** — remember the cross-flow geometry. - ✅ For KTU 14-mark answers, **always draw a labelled cross-section** of the SWM element to earn design marks. ---

topics.title: Adsorption: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Adsorption: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Adsorption

**Adsorption** is a surface-based mass transfer operation in which molecules, atoms, or ions of a substance (the **adsorbate**) accumulate and adhere onto the surface of a solid (or, in rare cases, a liquid) material, known as the **adsorbent**. It is fundamentally a *surface phenomenon* governed by intermolecular forces operating at the phase boundary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> *Adsorption is the adhesion of dissolved, suspended, or gaseous species (adsorbate) onto the internal or external surface of a solid matrix (adsorbent). In advanced wastewater treatment, adsorption is employed as a tertiary polishing step for the removal of refractory organics, heavy metals, color, odor, and emerging micropollutants (e.g., pharmaceuticals, endocrine disruptors) that are non-biodegradable or resistant to conventional secondary biological treatment.*

### Key Terminology (Board-Exam Favorites)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Adsorbate** | The substance that gets *accumulated* on the surface (e.g., dye molecules, phenol, Cd$^{2+}$ ions) |
| **Adsorbent** | The solid material that *provides* the surface (e.g., activated carbon, zeolites, biosorbents) |
| **Sorption** | The umbrella term covering both **adsorption** AND **absorption** |
| **Desorption** | The reverse process — release of adsorbate from the adsorbent surface (used in regeneration) |
| **Specific Surface Area** | Surface area per unit mass, typically expressed in **m$^2$/g** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Magnet and Iron Filings"

Imagine a **bar magnet** lying flat on a wooden table. If you sprinkle **iron filings** on the table, the filings near the magnet are instantly *pulled* and stick to the magnet's surface. The filings do not penetrate inside the magnet — they stick only on the **outer surface**.

Similarly, in adsorption:
- The **magnet** = the **adsorbent** (solid with active surface).
- The **iron filings** = the **adsorbate** (pollutant molecules in water).
- The **sticking to the surface** = the **intermolecular forces** (van der Waals, electrostatic, chemical bonding).
- The **table** = the **bulk liquid phase** (wastewater).

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental difference between **adsorption** and **absorption** is the location where the solute concentrates.
> - **Adsorption** → accumulation at the *surface* (2-D).
> - **Absorption** → penetration *into the bulk* of the medium (3-D, e.g., sponge soaking water).
>
> A combined process is called **sorption**. A classic example is the action of a **sponge dipped in ink**, where the dye first gets absorbed into the pores and then adsorbed onto the inner pore walls.

---

## 1.3 Why Adsorption? — Engineering Relevance

Modern wastewater contains a growing class of contaminants — pharmaceutical residues, pesticides, personal care products, and synthetic dyes — that pass through conventional biological treatment plants untouched. Adsorption, particularly using **Granular Activated Carbon (GAC)**, can achieve **>95% removal** of such recalcitrant organics. The global adsorption equipment market in water treatment is valued at over **USD 8 billion** (industry estimate, 2024), and this is precisely why it earns a dedicated module in the KTU 2024 Advanced Wastewater Treatment syllabus.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Board Exams:**
> **"AD-sorption = AD-herence to surface"** — both start with "AD", reminding you that the molecule *adheres*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Adsorption — Surface Energy Concept

Every solid surface has **unbalanced / unsaturated valency forces** at its boundary because the atoms on the surface lack neighbouring atoms on one side. These unsatisfied forces create a **surface free energy** ($\gamma$, in J/m$^2$). Nature always seeks to **minimise this surface free energy**. When an adsorbate molecule approaches the surface, it satisfies these dangling valencies, thereby **lowering the system's total free energy** ($\Delta G < 0$). This thermodynamic driving force is the fundamental "why" of adsorption.

The change in Gibbs free energy is the governing thermodynamic criterion:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S
$$

Since adsorption is a **spontaneous process** ($\Delta G < 0$) and the randomness of free adsorbate molecules is always **higher** than that of adsorbed molecules, the **entropy decreases** ($\Delta S < 0$). For the inequality $\Delta G < 0$ to hold with $\Delta S < 0$, the **enthalpy must be strongly negative** ($\Delta H < 0$, i.e., the process is **exothermic**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Adsorption is **exothermic** ($\Delta H < 0$). Therefore, by **Le Chatelier's principle**, an *increase in temperature decreases the equilibrium adsorption capacity*. This is why cold wastewater columns often outperform warm ones for GAC contactors.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Adsorption — The Two Pillars

Adsorption is classified based on the **nature of the bonding force** between the adsorbate and the adsorbent surface.

### A. Physical Adsorption (**Physisorption**)
- Bonding is due to weak **van der Waals (London dispersion) forces**.
- Low enthalpy of adsorption: typically $\Delta H \approx \mathbf{5\text{–}40\ kJ/mol}$.
- **Non-specific** — can occur on any surface, regardless of the chemical nature of the adsorbate.
- **Multilayer** formation is common.
- **Reversible** — easily desorbed by lowering pressure or raising temperature.
- **Low activation energy** — occurs almost instantaneously.
- Example: Adsorption of N$_2$ gas on charcoal at low temperature.

### B. Chemical Adsorption (**Chemisorption**)
- Bonding is due to **covalent or ionic chemical bonds** — essentially a true chemical reaction localised at the surface.
- High enthalpy of adsorption: typically $\Delta H \approx \mathbf{40\text{–}400\ kJ/mol}$ (comparable to a true chemical reaction).
- **Highly specific** — only certain adsorbate–adsorbent pairs will react.
- Usually forms a **monolayer** (sometimes a bilayer).
- Often **irreversible** — desorption may yield a different compound.
- Requires significant **activation energy** — may be slow, temperature-dependent.
- Example: Adsorption of O$_2$ on tungsten; chemisorption of heavy metal ions on thiol-functionalised silica.

### KTU High-Yield Comparison Table

| Parameter | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
|---|---|---|
| Bonding force | van der Waals | Covalent / Ionic |
| Enthalpy $\Delta H$ | **5 – 40 kJ/mol** | **40 – 400 kJ/mol** |
| Specificity | Non-specific | Highly specific |
| Layers | Multilayer possible | Mostly monolayer |
| Reversibility | Reversible | Often irreversible |
| Activation energy $E_a$ | Negligible / Low | Appreciable |
| Effect of temperature | Decreases with rise | May increase up to a point |
| Effect of pressure | Increases with pressure | Independent (after monolayer) |
| Example | Dye on activated carbon | O$_2$ on metal catalyst |

---

## 2.3 Factors Affecting Adsorption (Board-Exam Hot List)

1. **Nature and surface area of the adsorbent** — Higher specific surface area $\Rightarrow$ higher capacity. Activated carbon: **500 – 1500 m$^2$/g**.
2. **Nature of the adsorbate** — Solubility, molecular size, polarity, ionic charge.
3. **Temperature** — Generally inverse for physisorption; chemisorption may show a maxima.
4. **pH of solution** — Affects ionisation of functional groups on the adsorbent and the speciation of the adsorbate. Critical for heavy-metal adsorption.
5. **Initial concentration of adsorbate** — Higher concentration drives the equilibrium forward.
6. **Contact time** — Required to approach equilibrium.
7. **Presence of competing species** — Co-ions may occupy active sites.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e)V}{m}$ | Equilibrium adsorption capacity (mass of solute per mass of adsorbent) | mg/g |
| 2 | $\%\, \text{Removal} = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100$ | Percentage of pollutant removed | % |
| 3 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Thermodynamic spontaneity criterion | kJ/mol |
| 4 | $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_c$ | Standard free energy from equilibrium constant | kJ/mol |
| 5 | $K_c = \dfrac{C_{s,\,eq}}{C_{e,\,eq}}$ | Equilibrium constant for adsorption | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\Delta H^\circ = -R \left(\dfrac{\partial \ln K_c}{\partial (1/T)}\right)_P$ | van 't Hoff equation — enthalpy of adsorption | kJ/mol |
| 7 | $A_s = \dfrac{S_{BET}}{m}$ | Specific surface area | m$^2$/g |
| 8 | $S = N_A \cdot \sigma$ | Total number of surface sites (Langmuir hint) | sites |

> Where $C_0$ = initial concentration, $C_e$ = equilibrium concentration, $V$ = solution volume, $m$ = adsorbent mass, $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = absolute temperature, $K_c$ = equilibrium constant, $S_{BET}$ = BET surface area.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pipe-Rule (Markdown):** In the KTU answer-sheet, the absolute value / magnitude bars inside the formula $q_e = \left\vert \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{m} \right\vert$ would break a markdown table. Always use **\vert** or **\mid** as shown above (e.g., $\vert \Delta G \vert$) when you intend to embed them in tables.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Adsorption Capacity $q_e$ (Mass-Balance Foundation)

The single most frequently asked computation in KTU board examinations on adsorption is the equilibrium adsorption capacity. We derive it from a strict mass balance on the solute.

**Step 1: State the mass balance.**
The total mass of solute introduced into the system must equal the mass remaining in solution plus the mass adsorbed onto the solid.

$$
m_{solute,\,total} = m_{solute,\,solution} + m_{solute,\,adsorbed}
$$

**Step 2: Express each term in measurable quantities.**

$$
(C_0 \cdot V) = (C_e \cdot V) + (q_e \cdot m)
$$

Where:
- $C_0$ = initial solute concentration in the bulk solution (mg/L)
- $C_e$ = equilibrium solute concentration in bulk (mg/L)
- $V$ = volume of solution (L)
- $m$ = dry mass of adsorbent (g)
- $q_e$ = equilibrium adsorption capacity (mg of solute / g of adsorbent)

**Step 3: Isolate $q_e$.**

$$
q_e \cdot m = C_0 V - C_e V
$$

**Step 4: Divide by $m$ to get the final expression.**

$$
q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e)\,V}{m}
$$

This is the **fundamental working equation** for batch adsorption studies.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — van 't Hoff Equation for $\Delta H^\circ$ of Adsorption

This derivation is essential for problems asking: *"Determine the enthalpy of adsorption from equilibrium data at two temperatures."*

**Step 1: Start with the standard thermodynamic relationship.**

$$
\Delta G^\circ = -R T \ln K_c \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

**Step 2: Use the Gibbs–Helmholtz relation connecting $\Delta G^\circ$, $\Delta H^\circ$, and $T$.**

$$
\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute (ii) into (i) and rearrange.**

$$
-R T \ln K_c = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ
$$

**Step 4: Divide every term by $-RT$.**

$$
\ln K_c = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T} + \dfrac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}
$$

**Step 5: Recognise the straight-line form $y = mx + c$.**

$$
\ln K_c = \underbrace{\left(-\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\right)}_{slope} \cdot \underbrace{\dfrac{1}{T}}_{x} + \underbrace{\dfrac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}}_{intercept}
$$

**Step 6: Final compact form (for two-temperature experimental data).**

$$
\ln \left(\dfrac{K_{c,2}}{K_{c,1}}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)
$$

> **Interpretation:** A *plot* of $\ln K_c$ vs $1/T$ is a straight line with **slope $= -\Delta H^\circ / R$**. The negative slope confirms the exothermic nature of physical adsorption.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Percentage Removal from $C_0$ and $C_e$

A short but mark-winning derivation often used in 3-mark problems:

**Step 1: Define removal efficiency.**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{\text{amount removed}}{\text{amount initially present}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the mass-balance expressions.**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{C_0 V - C_e V}{C_0 V}
$$

**Step 3: Cancel the common factor $V$.**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to percentage.**

$$
\%\, \text{Removal} = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Eye:** Whenever a numerical problem is given, immediately state (a) the mass balance, (b) the working equation for $q_e$, and (c) the substitution with units. Students who skip unit analysis lose **2 marks** routinely.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical (KTU 2024-Style)

**Problem:** A 500 mL wastewater sample containing an initial dye concentration of **200 mg/L** is treated with **1.0 g** of activated carbon. At equilibrium, the residual dye concentration is measured as **40 mg/L**. Calculate the **equilibrium adsorption capacity** $q_e$ (in mg/g) and the **percentage removal**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify given values.**
- $C_0 = 200$ mg/L
- $C_e = 40$ mg/L
- $V = 500$ mL $= 0.5$ L
- $m = 1.0$ g

**Step 2: Apply the $q_e$ formula.**

$$
q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e)V}{m} = \dfrac{(200 - 40)\,\text{mg/L} \times 0.5\,\text{L}}{1.0\,\text{g}}
$$

**Step 3: Compute.**

$$
q_e = \dfrac{160 \times 0.5}{1.0} = \dfrac{80}{1.0} = 80\ \text{mg/g}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the percentage removal formula.**

$$
\%\, \text{Removal} = \dfrac{200 - 40}{200} \times 100 = \dfrac{160}{200} \times 100 = 80\ \%
$$

**Final Answer:** $q_e = 80$ mg/g, **80 % removal**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mechanism of Adsorption at a Solid–Liquid Interface

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wastewater Bulk Solution] --> B[Concentration Gradient C0 greater than Ce]
    B --> C[Diffusion of Adsorbate Molecules to External Surface]
    C --> D[External Surface Adsorption Film Layer]
    D --> E[Migration through Pore Liquid Boundary Layer]
    E --> F[Pore Diffusion Inside Adsorbent Granule]
    F --> G[Adsorption onto Active Internal Surface Sites]
    G --> H[Equilibrium Reached qe mg per g]
    H --> I[Treated Effluent Leaves Contact Column]
    G -. Desorption .-> J[Regeneration Step using Steam or Solvent]
    J --> A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows trace the *transport-and-attachment* journey of a pollutant molecule from the bulk water onto an active site inside a porous adsorbent grain. The dashed loop shows the regeneration step, in which spent adsorbent is treated with hot steam, acid, or a solvent to desorb the adsorbate and restore capacity.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification Hierarchy of Adsorption

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ADSORP[Adsorption Phenomenon] --> TYPE{Type of Bonding Force}
    TYPE --> PHYS[Physisorption]
    TYPE --> CHEM[Chemisorption]
    PHYS --> P1[van der Waals Forces]
    PHYS --> P2[Low Enthalpy 5 to 40 kJ per mol]
    PHYS --> P3[Multilayer Possible]
    PHYS --> P4[Reversible]
    PHYS --> P5[Example: Dye on Activated Carbon]
    CHEM --> C1[Covalent or Ionic Bonding]
    CHEM --> C2[High Enthalpy 40 to 400 kJ per mol]
    CHEM --> C3[Mostly Monolayer]
    CHEM --> C4[Often Irreversible]
    CHEM --> C5[Example: Oxygen on Metal Catalyst]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Engineering Process Flow of an Adsorption Column

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INF[Influent Wastewater Inlet] --> EQ[Equalisation Basin]
    EQ --> PUMP[Feed Pump]
    PUMP --> COL[Fixed Bed Adsorption Column Packed with GAC]
    COL --> SAM[Sampling Point for Breakthrough Curve]
    SAM --> OUT[Treated Effluent Outlet]
    COL -. Spent Carbon .-> REG[Regeneration Unit Thermal or Chemical]
    REG -. Reactivated Carbon .-> COL
```

> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Note:** This is the *process flow* mapping the engineering realisation of adsorption at plant scale. The critical monitoring instrument here is the **breakthrough curve** — a plot of effluent concentration $C$ vs time $t$ — which signals when the column must be regenerated or the carbon replaced.

---

## 4.4 Schematic Description of Adsorption vs Absorption (Conceptual Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ABSORPTION
        direction TB
        A1[Bulk Liquid Phase] --> A2[Solute Penetrates INTO Bulk]
        A2 --> A3[Uniform Concentration Throughout]
    end
    subgraph ADSORPTION
        direction TB
        B1[Bulk Liquid Phase] --> B2[Solute Concentrates at Surface]
        B2 --> B3[Non Uniform Surface Excess]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (`ADSORP`, `PHYS`, `P1`, `COL`, etc.) — no reserved words used. All node labels are *raw uppercase text* without markdown bold/italics to keep Mermaid parsers happy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between physical adsorption and chemical adsorption. List any four distinguishing features.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

| Sl. | Feature | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bonding | van der Waals forces | Covalent / ionic bonds |
| 2 | Enthalpy | 5 – 40 kJ/mol | 40 – 400 kJ/mol |
| 3 | Specificity | Non-specific | Highly specific |
| 4 | Reversibility | Reversible | Mostly irreversible |

> **Mark split (typical KTU key):** ½ mark per correct contrasting pair × 4 pairs = 2 marks; an introductory line + a concluding statement = 1 mark. Total **3 marks**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Define the terms *adsorbate* and *adsorbent* with one wastewater-treatment example for each.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
- **Adsorbate** — The species that gets *concentrated* on the surface of a solid. *Example:* phenol molecules in a coke-oven wastewater stream.
- **Adsorbent** — The solid material that *provides* the surface for accumulation. *Example:* granular activated carbon (GAC) in a polishing filter.
- **Key distinction:** The *adsorbate* is the *pollutant*; the *adsorbent* is the *treatment material*.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> *KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:* Each Part B question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either** the full set of sub-parts of **Question A** *or* the full set of sub-parts of **Question B**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of adsorption** as a surface phenomenon. With a neat sketch, describe the difference between **adsorption** and **absorption**. Discuss the role of **surface free energy** as the thermodynamic driving force.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A textile effluent of volume **2.0 L** contains an initial dye concentration of **150 mg/L**. It is agitated with **2.5 g** of activated carbon in a batch contactor. After reaching equilibrium, the dye concentration drops to **30 mg/L**.
    **(i)** Calculate the **equilibrium adsorption capacity** $q_e$ of the carbon.
    **(ii)** Determine the **percentage removal** of the dye.
    **(iii)** Comment on how a **rise in temperature** would affect $q_e$ for this system, giving reasons. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Theory (7 marks):**
- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Define adsorption as a surface phenomenon where adsorbate molecules concentrate at the interface.
- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Sketch distinguishing adsorption (surface-only, 2-D) from absorption (bulk penetration, 3-D). Mention **sorption** as the combined term.
- **Step 3 (2 marks):** Explain *surface free energy* — the unsaturated valency forces on a solid surface create excess energy. Adsorbate attachment *reduces* this energy, making the process spontaneous ($\Delta G < 0$).
- **Step 4 (2 marks):** Link to entropy: $\Delta S < 0$ (more ordered) ⇒ requires $\Delta H < 0$ (exothermic) for spontaneity.

**(b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Equilibrium adsorption capacity:**

$$
q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e)\,V}{m} = \dfrac{(150 - 30)\,\text{mg/L} \times 2.0\,\text{L}}{2.5\,\text{g}} = \dfrac{120 \times 2.0}{2.5} = \dfrac{240}{2.5}
$$

$$
q_e = 96\ \text{mg/g}
$$

**(ii) Percentage removal:**

$$
\%\, \text{Removal} = \dfrac{150 - 30}{150} \times 100 = \dfrac{120}{150} \times 100 = 80\ \%
$$

**(iii) Effect of temperature (Dye on activated carbon is *physisorption*):**
- Adsorption is **exothermic** ($\Delta H < 0$). By **Le Chatelier's principle**, a rise in temperature **shifts the equilibrium backward**, **decreasing $q_e$**.
- Additional kinetic reason: higher temperature increases the kinetic energy of adsorbate molecules, making it harder for weak van der Waals forces to retain them on the surface.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** List and explain any **five factors affecting the adsorption process** in wastewater treatment, with relevant examples.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The equilibrium constant $K_c$ for adsorption of a pesticide on activated carbon was found to be **1200** at **298 K** and **480** at **318 K**. Calculate the **standard enthalpy change** $\Delta H^\circ$ and **standard entropy change** $\Delta S^\circ$ for the process. ($R = 8.314$ J/mol·K)  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Theory (7 marks):** (1.4 marks per factor, take any 5)

1. **Surface area of adsorbent** — Greater surface area ⇒ more active sites ⇒ higher $q_e$. Activated carbon has **500 – 1500 m$^2$/g**.
2. **Particle size** — Smaller particles ⇒ shorter diffusion path ⇒ faster kinetics.
3. **Nature of adsorbate** — Solubility, polarity, and ionic charge dictate affinity. Hydrophobic organics adsorb strongly on hydrophobic GAC surfaces.
4. **pH of solution** — Affects surface charge of adsorbent and ionisation state of adsorbate. Heavy-metal adsorption is highly pH-dependent.
5. **Temperature** — Physisorption is exothermic; higher $T$ reduces $q_e$.
6. **Contact time** — Equilibrium must be reached; batch studies use 24–48 h.
7. **Initial concentration** — Higher $C_0$ saturates all sites until monolayer is complete.

**(b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $K_{c,1} = 1200$ at $T_1 = 298$ K; $K_{c,2} = 480$ at $T_2 = 318$ K; $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K.

**Step 1: Apply the van 't Hoff equation.**

$$
\ln \left(\dfrac{K_{c,2}}{K_{c,1}}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)
$$

**Step 2: Compute the LHS.**

$$
\ln \left(\dfrac{480}{1200}\right) = \ln(0.4) = -0.9163
$$

**Step 3: Compute the bracket on the RHS.**

$$
\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1} = \dfrac{1}{318} - \dfrac{1}{298}
$$

$$
= 0.003145 - 0.003356 = -2.11 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute and solve for $\Delta H^\circ$.**

$$
-0.9163 = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{8.314} \times (-2.11 \times 10^{-4})
$$

$$
\Delta H^\circ = \dfrac{-0.9163 \times 8.314}{-2.11 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{-7.617}{-2.11 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\Delta H^\circ \approx -36{,}100\ \text{J/mol} = -36.1\ \text{kJ/mol}
$$

**Step 5: Compute $\Delta S^\circ$ from $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$.**

First, $\Delta G^\circ_1 = -RT_1 \ln K_{c,1}$:

$$
\Delta G^\circ_1 = -8.314 \times 298 \times \ln(1200) = -8.314 \times 298 \times 7.09
$$

$$
\Delta G^\circ_1 \approx -17{,}560\ \text{J/mol} = -17.56\ \text{kJ/mol}
$$

Then:

$$
\Delta S^\circ = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ - \Delta G^\circ_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{-36{,}100 - (-17{,}560)}{298}
$$

$$
\Delta S^\circ = \dfrac{-18{,}540}{298} \approx -62.2\ \text{J/(mol·K)}
$$

**Interpretation:** $\Delta H^\circ < 0$ (exothermic — characteristic of physisorption) and $\Delta S^\circ < 0$ (more ordered arrangement on the surface). The negative sign of $\Delta G^\circ$ confirms the process is **spontaneous**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Converting mL → L or mg → g is the single biggest mistake. Always show the conversion.
> 2. **Sign convention of $\Delta H^\circ$:** Board key expects **negative sign for exothermic adsorption**. Dropping the sign costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention that physisorption is exothermic** when asked about temperature effect — at least one full sentence with the Le Chatelier link is required for 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing $K_c$ with the Langmuir constant $b$** — they are dimensionally different. $K_c = C_{s,\,eq}/C_{e,\,eq}$ is dimensionless; the Langmuir $b$ has units of L/mg.
> 5. **Skipping the "spontaneity check"** — always verify $\Delta G < 0$ as a concluding remark in thermodynamic derivations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Adsorption is a *surface* phenomenon**; absorption is a *bulk* phenomenon. The combined term is **sorption**.
- [x] **Adsorbate** = the pollutant; **Adsorbent** = the solid treatment material.
- [x] **Two types:** **Physisorption** (van der Waals, weak, 5–40 kJ/mol, multilayer, reversible) and **Chemisorption** (covalent/ionic, strong, 40–400 kJ/mol, monolayer, often irreversible).
- [x] Adsorption is **exothermic** ($\Delta H < 0$) and accompanied by a **decrease in entropy** ($\Delta S < 0$). Therefore, **higher temperature decreases $q_e$** for physisorption.
- [x] **Driving force** = reduction in surface free energy of the solid.
- [x] **Master equations** to memorise for KTU exams:
  - $q_e = (C_0 - C_e)V/m$ — equilibrium capacity
  - $\%\, \text{Removal} = [(C_0 - C_e)/C_0] \times 100$
  - $\ln(K_{c,2}/K_{c,1}) = -(\Delta H^\circ/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ — van 't Hoff
  - $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_c$ and $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$
- [x] **Activated carbon** is the *workhorse adsorbent* in wastewater treatment with surface area **500 – 1500 m$^2$/g**.
- [x] Always state **units** at every step of a numerical problem.
- [x] Always include a **concluding spontaneity statement** ($\Delta G < 0$) in thermodynamic derivations.
- [x] **Spontaneous** does not mean **fast** — chemisorption can be spontaneous but kinetically slow.
- [x] **Specific surface area** $A_s$ is a key indicator of adsorbent quality — higher is better.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Adsorption** is a surface phenomenon in which molecules, atoms, or ions of a substance (the **adsorbate**) accumulate on the surface of a solid or liquid (the **adsorbent**) through physical or chemical interactions. It is fundamentally a mass-transfer process that occurs at the interface between two phases, driven by unbalanced surface forces of the solid matrix.

In the context of **Advanced Wastewater Treatment (AWW)**, adsorption is a tertiary physico-chemical treatment operation used for the removal of dissolved contaminants — including organic micropollutants (dyes, phenols, pesticides, humic substances), heavy metals, and odor-causing compounds — that are resistant to conventional biological treatment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** "Adsorption" specifically refers to the **surface accumulation** of solute particles, NOT their penetration into the bulk of the solid. The opposite process, where accumulated molecules leave the surface, is called **desorption**.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Adsorbate** | The substance (contaminant) that gets accumulated on the surface. |
| **Adsorbent** | The solid material that provides the surface for accumulation (e.g., Activated Carbon). |
| **Sorption** | A generic term covering both adsorption and absorption. |
| **Absorption** | The process where molecules are taken up into the bulk of a material (penetration). |
| **Desorption** | The reverse of adsorption; release of adsorbate from the adsorbent surface. |
| **Equilibrium** | The state where the rate of adsorption equals the rate of desorption. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a crowded bus (the **wastewater stream**) with many passengers (**pollutant molecules**). The bus stops at a magnet factory (**the adsorbent**), and all the iron filings (specific pollutants) jump off the bus and stick to the factory's exterior walls (**the surface**). The bus leaves lighter, having dropped its specific unwanted cargo. 

In the same way, the wastewater passes over a porous solid (**adsorbent**), and specific dissolved molecules get "stuck" to the millions of internal pores and surfaces of the solid — leaving the treated water cleaner.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is adsorption so widely used in AWW?**
> Because it can target **specific** compounds through careful selection of adsorbent, it can remove pollutants down to **parts-per-billion (ppb)** levels, and it works effectively on **biorefractory** organics that microbes cannot degrade.

## 1.4 Adsorption vs. Absorption (Critical Distinction)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adsorption** is a **surface** phenomenon (think: dust sticks to a surface).
> **Absorption** is a **bulk** phenomenon (think: a sponge soaks water into its interior).

| Parameter | Adsorption | Absorption |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Surface only | Throughout the bulk |
| Rate | Rapid (surface-limited) | Slow (diffusion-limited) |
| Concentration change | Sudden jump at interface | Gradual change in concentration |
| Example | Dye removal by activated carbon | Dissolving CO₂ in water |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Adsorption

### 2.1.1 Physical Adsorption (Physisorption)

- **Driving Force:** Weak **van der Waals forces** (London dispersion forces).
- **Energy of Activation:** Low, typically **5–40 kJ/mol**.
- **Reversibility:** Highly **reversible**.
- **Specificity:** **Non-specific** — any gas/solute can be adsorbed.
- **Temperature Effect:** **Exothermic**; decreases with rising temperature.
- **Layer Formation:** **Multilayer** adsorption is possible.
- **Activation Energy:** Low, so it occurs readily at low temperatures.

### 2.1.2 Chemical Adsorption (Chemisorption)

- **Driving Force:** Strong **chemical (covalent/ionic) bonds** between adsorbate and adsorbent.
- **Energy of Activation:** High, typically **40–400 kJ/mol**.
- **Reversibility:** **Irreversible** (forms a new surface compound).
- **Specificity:** **Highly specific** — depends on chemical affinity.
- **Temperature Effect:** Increases with temperature up to a point, then decreases.
- **Layer Formation:** **Monolayer** only (one molecule thick).
- **Activation Energy:** High; usually requires significant heat to occur.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked "Compare physisorption and chemisorption," always tabulate **at least 6 distinguishing features** for full marks. A simple one-line answer will not suffice.

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Adsorption

1. **Nature of the Adsorbent** — Surface area, pore size distribution, surface chemistry.
2. **Nature of the Adsorbate** — Molecular size, polarity, solubility, pKa.
3. **Solution pH** — Influences the surface charge of the adsorbent and the ionization state of the adsorbate.
4. **Temperature** — Most adsorption is exothermic, so capacity decreases with temperature.
5. **Initial Concentration** — Higher initial concentration increases uptake until saturation.
6. **Contact Time** — Equilibrium is reached over a specific time.
7. **Agitation / Mixing** — Affects external mass-transfer resistance.
8. **Presence of Co-solutes** — Competing species reduce adsorption capacity.

## 2.3 Properties of an Ideal Adsorbent (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An industrial-grade adsorbent must have the following characteristics:
> - **High internal surface area** (typically **500–1500 m²/g** for activated carbon).
> - **High porosity** with controlled pore-size distribution.
> - **Chemical inertness** and mechanical stability.
> - **Regenerability** without significant loss of capacity.
> - **Economical** cost and ready availability.

## 2.4 Common Adsorbents Used in Wastewater Treatment

| Adsorbent | Specific Surface Area (m²/g) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Activated Carbon (GAC/PAC)** | 500 – 1500 | Removal of organics, color, odor |
| **Activated Alumina** | 200 – 400 | Fluoride, arsenic removal |
| **Silica Gel** | 700 – 800 | Drying, hydrocarbon removal |
| **Zeolites** | 400 – 800 | Ammonium ion (NH₄⁺) removal |
| **Biosorbents** (chitosan, agricultural waste) | 50 – 300 | Heavy metals, dye removal |
| **Resins (Ion Exchange)** | 30 – 100 | Selective metal recovery |

## 2.5 The Adsorption Process — Step-by-Step

The overall adsorption of a solute onto a porous solid in a stirred batch reactor follows **four sequential mass-transfer steps**:

1. **Bulk Transport (Film Diffusion):** The solute migrates from the bulk liquid to the hydrodynamic boundary layer (external film) surrounding the adsorbent particle.
2. **External Diffusion (Film Diffusion):** The solute diffuses through the stagnant boundary layer to the external surface of the particle.
3. **Internal Diffusion (Pore Diffusion):** The solute travels through the porous network within the particle to reach an active site.
4. **Surface Reaction / Sorption:** The solute binds to the active site through physical or chemical forces.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All equations below are KTU-board-exam essential. Memorize the variables, units, and typical ranges.**

| Equation | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Adsorption Capacity (q)** | $q_{e} = \dfrac{(C_{0} - C_{e}) V}{m}$ | $q_{e}$ = capacity at equilibrium (mg/g); $C_{0}$ = initial concentration (mg/L); $C_{e}$ = equilibrium concentration (mg/L); $V$ = volume of solution (L); $m$ = mass of adsorbent (g) | Calculate how much adsorbate is held per gram of adsorbent. |
| **Removal Efficiency (R%)** | $R = \dfrac{(C_{0} - C_{e})}{C_{0}} \times 100$ | $R$ = % removal (dimensionless) | Express process performance. |
| **Freundlich Isotherm** | $q_{e} = K_{F} \cdot C_{e}^{1/n}$ | $K_{F}$ = Freundlich constant (mg/g); $1/n$ = heterogeneity factor | Empirical; multilayer, heterogeneous surface. |
| **Linearized Freundlich** | $\log q_{e} = \log K_{F} + \dfrac{1}{n} \log C_{e}$ | Plot $\log q_e$ vs $\log C_e$ → slope $1/n$, intercept $\log K_F$ | Linear plot for parameter estimation. |
| **Langmuir Isotherm** | $q_{e} = \dfrac{q_{m} K_{L} C_{e}}{1 + K_{L} C_{e}}$ | $q_m$ = max monolayer capacity (mg/g); $K_L$ = Langmuir constant (L/mg) | Theoretical; monolayer, homogeneous surface. |
| **Linearized Langmuir** | $\dfrac{C_{e}}{q_{e}} = \dfrac{1}{q_{m} K_{L}} + \dfrac{C_{e}}{q_{m}}$ | Plot $C_e/q_e$ vs $C_e$ → slope $1/q_m$, intercept $1/(q_m K_L)$ | Linear plot for $q_m$ and $K_L$. |
| **Separation Factor (R_L)** | $R_{L} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_{L} C_{0}}$ | $0 < R_L < 1$ → favorable; $=0$ → irreversible; $>1$ → unfavorable | Judge adsorption favorability. |
| **BET Isotherm** | $\dfrac{C_{e}}{q_{e}(C_{s} - C_{e})} = \dfrac{1}{q_{BET} K_{BET}} + \dfrac{(K_{BET} - 1)}{q_{BET} K_{BET}} \cdot \dfrac{C_{e}}{C_{s}}$ | $C_s$ = saturation concentration; $K_{BET}$ = BET constant | Multilayer adsorption (typical of gas-phase and high-conc. liquid phase). |
| **Pseudo-First-Order** | $\log(q_{e} - q_{t}) = \log q_{e} - \dfrac{k_{1} t}{2.303}$ | $q_t$ = capacity at time t (mg/g); $k_1$ = rate constant (1/min) | Lagergren equation — kinetics. |
| **Pseudo-Second-Order** | $\dfrac{t}{q_{t}} = \dfrac{1}{k_{2} q_{e}^{2}} + \dfrac{t}{q_{e}}$ | $k_2$ = 2nd-order rate constant (g/(mg·min)) | Widely applied kinetic model. |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Adsorption systems are deployed as:

- **PAC (Powdered Activated Carbon):** Dosed directly into aeration tanks or contact basins.
- **GAC (Granular Activated Carbon):** Packed in contactors operating in downflow or upflow mode.
- **Continuous flow packed-bed reactors:** Used for industrial effluent polishing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** The selection of an adsorption isotherm is **NOT** arbitrary. **Freundlich** is used when the surface is heterogeneous (real-world carbon). **Langmuir** is used for theoretical understanding and homogeneous surfaces. **BET** is used when multilayer adsorption dominates.
