
> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid compilation note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `Inf`, `Pump`), and all labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown bold/italic tags, in compliance with the rendering safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Distinguish between **adsorption** and **absorption** with a suitable example each, as applicable to advanced wastewater treatment.

**Model Answer:**
- **Adsorption** is the accumulation of a solute at the *surface* of a solid (e.g., phenol on activated carbon).
- **Absorption** is the penetration of a solute into the *bulk* of a liquid or solid (e.g., ammonia being scrubbed into sulphuric acid in a stripping tower).
- In adsorption, the concentration is highest at the interface; in absorption, the solute is uniformly distributed within the bulk phase.
- Adsorption is a *surface phenomenon*; absorption is a *bulk phenomenon*.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
State the **Freundlich adsorption isotherm** and explain the significance of the constant **$1/n$**.

**Model Answer:**
The Freundlich isotherm is given by:

$$ \frac{x}{m} = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n} $$

The constant $1/n$ is the *adsorption intensity parameter*:
- $0 < 1/n < 1$ → **favourable** adsorption.
- $1/n = 1$ → **linear** (partition coefficient behaviour).
- $1/n > 1$ → **unfavourable** adsorption.
A smaller $1/n$ indicates a more intense, strongly favourable uptake; $K_F$ reflects the *capacity* of the carbon.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled sketch, describe the construction and working of a **Granular Activated Carbon (GAC) contactor** used in tertiary wastewater treatment. Discuss the concept of the **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ)** and **breakthrough curve**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **linear form of the Langmuir isotherm** and explain how the constants $a$ and $b$ are obtained graphically. A GAC sample gave the following data at 25 °C:

| $C_e$ (mg/L) | 2 | 4 | 8 | 12 | 20 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $q_e$ (mg/g) | 1.8 | 3.1 | 4.6 | 5.4 | 6.3 |

Determine $a$ and $b$. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**
- Sketch the contactor: cylindrical pressure vessel, inlet distributor at top, underdrain at bottom, GAC bed supported on graded gravel, effluent collector, backwash system, sampling ports.
- **MTZ**: A finite zone within the bed where active adsorption occurs. Above it, the carbon is saturated; below it, the carbon is virgin.
- **Breakthrough curve**: $C_t$ vs $t$ plot. Three zones: saturation zone, MTZ, fresh zone. The S-shaped curve identifies (i) breakthrough point — when outlet $C$ reaches the regulatory limit; (ii) exhaustion point — when outlet $C \approx$ inlet $C$.
- Design considerations: EBCT (typically 10–30 min), hydraulic loading, bed depth (1.5–3 m), backwash frequency.

**Part (b):**
Linear Langmuir form:

$$ \frac{1}{q_e} = \frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{a \cdot b} \cdot \frac{1}{C_e} $$

Construct the table of $1/C_e$ and $1/q_e$:

| $1/C_e$ (L/mg) | 0.500 | 0.250 | 0.125 | 0.0833 | 0.050 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $1/q_e$ (g/mg) | 0.556 | 0.323 | 0.217 | 0.185 | 0.159 |

Linear regression:
- Slope $\approx 0.985$ / intercept $\approx 0.122$
- Intercept $= 1/a \Rightarrow a = 1/0.122 \approx 8.2$ mg/g
- Slope $= 1/(ab) \Rightarrow b = $ slope$/$intercept $\approx 0.985/0.122 \approx 8.07$ L/mg

$$ \boxed{a \approx 8.2 \text{ mg/g}, \quad b \approx 8.07 \text{ L/mg}} $$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare **Powdered Activated Carbon (PAC)** and **Granular Activated Carbon (GAC)** systems with respect to contact mode, regeneration, and typical applications. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** The following data were obtained in a batch equilibrium study for the adsorption of a textile dye on activated carbon at 30 °C:

| $C_e$ (mg/L) | 5 | 10 | 25 | 50 | 100 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $q_e$ (mg/g) | 12 | 22 | 45 | 70 | 95 |

Fit the **Freundlich isotherm** and estimate the carbon required to treat **5 m³** of dye wastewater from $C_0 = 200$ mg/L to an equilibrium residual of $C_e = 30$ mg/L. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

| Feature | PAC | GAC |
|---|---|---|
| Particle size | < 0.1 mm | 0.6 – 2.5 mm |
| Contact mode | Dosed slurry, mixed | Packed column |
| Regeneration | Generally NOT regenerated (single use) | Thermally regenerated (multiple cycles) |
| Contact time | Hours | Minutes (10 – 30 min EBCT) |
| Application | Seasonal/peak loads, toxicity spikes | Continuous polishing, full-scale plants |
| Capital cost | Low | High |
| Sludge | Increases sludge volume | No sludge increase |

**Part (b):**
Step 1 — Linearise: $\ln q_e = \ln K_F + (1/n) \ln C_e$.

Step 2 — Build the table:

| $\ln C_e$ | 1.609 | 2.303 | 3.219 | 3.912 | 4.605 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\ln q_e$ | 2.485 | 3.091 | 3.807 | 4.248 | 4.554 |

Step 3 — Linear regression yields:
- Slope $(1/n) \approx 0.71$
- Intercept $\ln K_F \approx 1.35 \Rightarrow K_F \approx 3.86$

Step 4 — Compute $q_e$ at $C_e = 30$ mg/L.

$$ q_e = 3.86 \times (30)^{0.71} $$

$$ 30^{0.71}: \ln 30 = 3.401; \quad 0.71 \times 3.401 = 2.415; \quad 30^{0.71} = e^{2.415} \approx 11.19 $$

$$ q_e = 3.86 \times 11.19 \approx 43.2 \text{ mg/g} $$

Step 5 — Mass of dye to be removed.

$$ m_{\text{dye}} = (200 - 30) \text{ mg/L} \times 5000 \text{ L} = 850{,}000 \text{ mg} = 850 \text{ g} $$

Step 6 — Carbon mass.

$$ M_{\text{carbon}} = \frac{850 \text{ g}}{43.2 \text{ mg/g}} = \frac{850{,}000 \text{ mg}}{43.2 \text{ mg/g}} \approx 19{,}676 \text{ g} \approx 19.7 \text{ kg} $$

$$ \boxed{M_{\text{carbon}} \approx 19.7 \text{ kg for 5 m}^3 \text{ (dose} \approx 3.94 \text{ g/L)}} $$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU 2024 valuation:**
> 1. **Mixing up adsorbate/adsorbent** in definitions — KTU examiners deduct 1 full mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to linearise isotherms** before plotting — you must show $\ln q_e$ vs $\ln C_e$ (Freundlich) or $1/q_e$ vs $1/C_e$ (Langmuir).
> 3. **Not stating the assumption** (monolayer for Langmuir, heterogeneous for Freundlich) — 1 mark penalty.
> 4. **Unit mismatch** in $q_e$ (mg/g) vs $C_e$ (mg/L) — always write units in the final answer.
> 5. **Skipping the slope/intercept identification** in the linearised equation — examiners want a clear statement: "Intercept = 1/a, therefore a = 1/intercept".
> 6. **Conflating PAC and GAC** — they have different contact modes, regeneration options, and design equations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Adsorption ≠ Absorption** — surface vs bulk phenomenon.
- Activated carbon has surface area **500 – 1500 m²/g** due to micro/meso/macropores.
- **PAC** = slurry dose, no regeneration; **GAC** = packed column, regenerable.
- **Freundlich isotherm** $q_e = K_F C_e^{1/n}$ is empirical and best for heterogeneous surfaces; $0 < 1/n < 1$ is favourable.
- **Langmuir isotherm** $q_e = a b C_e / (1 + b C_e)$ assumes monolayer adsorption on identical sites.
- **Linearisation** is mandatory in KTU: plot $\ln q_e$ vs $\ln C_e$ (Freundlich) or $1/q_e$ vs $1/C_e$ (Langmuir).
- **EBCT** = $V_{bed}/Q$ — typically **10 – 30 min** for GAC polishing.
- **Breakthrough curve** is the plot of $C_{outlet}$ vs time at constant $C_{inlet}$; the **mass transfer zone (MTZ)** travels down the bed.
- **Carbon use rate (CUR)** = $M_{carbon}/V_{treated}$ — key design parameter (kg/m³).
- **Regeneration**: Thermal (multiple hearth furnace, 800–900 °C, steam-activating); biological (BAC).
- **Selection of isotherm in exam**: Freundlich for *real wastewater / unknown multi-component* systems; Langmuir for *single-solute laboratory data* / *monolayer interpretation*.
- **Common applications**: Phenol removal (coke ovens), dye removal (textile), pesticide removal (drinking water), micropollutants (pharma).
- Always check $1/n$ — if you get $>1$ in a problem, you have a calculation error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Granular Carbon Adsorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Granular Carbon Adsorption # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Granular Activated Carbon (GAC) Adsorption** is a tertiary/advanced physico-chemical wastewater treatment process in which dissolved contaminants are removed from the liquid phase by concentrating (accumulating) them onto the internal and external surfaces of a highly porous, granular carbonaceous adsorbent. The GAC particles typically range in size from **0.5 mm to 2.5 mm**, possess a specific surface area between **500 m²/g and 1500 m²/g**, and function by the combined mechanisms of **physical (van der Waals) adsorption**, **chemisorption**, and **intraparticle pore diffusion**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** GAC adsorption is positioned as the **polishing step** in an Advanced Wastewater Treatment (AWT) train, deployed after secondary biological treatment and often after filtration, to remove **refractory organics, micropollutants, colour, residual COD, synthetic chemicals (Endocrine Disrupting Compounds, pharmaceuticals), and trace toxic substances** that are non-biodegradable. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Kitchen Sponge" Analogy:** > Imagine a kitchen sponge used to soak up a coloured fruit juice spill. The sponge is not a filter; it does not physically strain particles. Instead, the dye molecules *cling* to the thousands of tiny pores and channels inside the sponge because of molecular attraction. Granular Activated Carbon works on exactly this principle, but at an industrial scale. > > One gram of GAC, if unfolded, would cover **an entire football field (~7000 m²/g)**. So when wastewater trickles through a GAC bed, the dissolved impurity molecules literally stick to this enormous internal "molecular velcro." Eventually, the surface gets saturated, and the carbon must be regenerated — just as you would wring out and clean the sponge. --- ## 1.3 Key Physical Characteristics of GAC | Property | Typical Range | Significance | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Particle Size** | 0.5 – 2.5 mm | Controls headloss and contact time | | **Apparent Density** | 0.35 – 0.55 g/cm³ | Affects backwash and bed expansion | | **Specific Surface Area** | 500 – 1500 m²/g | Determines adsorption capacity | | **Iodine Number** | 500 – 1200 mg/g | Indicator of micropore capacity | | **Methylene Blue Number** | 100 – 280 mg/g | Indicator of mesopore capacity | | **Hardness Number** | 75 – 95 | Resistance to attrition | | **Ash Content** | < 8 % | Purity indicator | | **Bed Porosity (ε)** | 0.38 – 0.42 | Fraction of void volume in bed | --- ## 1.4 Why GAC and not PAC? | Parameter | Powdered Activated Carbon (PAC) | Granular Activated Carbon (GAC) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Particle Size | 10 – 100 µm | 0.5 – 2.5 mm | | Mode of Use | Dosed & discarded | Fixed/moving bed, regenerated | | Contact Time | Hours (in basin) | Minutes (in column) | | Regeneration | Not feasible | Standard (thermal) | | Capital Cost | Low | High | | Application | Seasonal/emergency | Continuous polishing | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic — "PAC is Popcorn, GAC is Gravel":** PAC is dust-like and one-time use; GAC is gravel-sized and reusable. KTU exams often ask for this direct comparison. --- ## 1.5 Typical Position in an AWT Train $$ \text{Screening} \rightarrow \text{Primary} \rightarrow \text{Secondary (Biological)} \rightarrow \text{Filtration} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{GAC Adsorption}} \rightarrow \text{Disinfection} $$ GAC is placed **after** biological treatment because biological oxidation removes the bulk of biodegradable organics (~80–90 % BOD), allowing the GAC to focus its enormous capacity on the stubborn **refractory fraction** — maximizing its service life. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Adsorption Mechanism — Stepwise Logic The transfer of a solute molecule from bulk liquid onto the carbon surface is not instantaneous. KTU examiners frequently test the **step-wise transport mechanism**: 1. **Bulk Transport (Film Diffusion Step 1):** The solute molecule migrates from the bulk liquid to the hydrodynamic boundary layer (Nernst film) surrounding the GAC particle — driven by concentration gradient and convection. 2. **Film Diffusion (External Mass Transfer):** The molecule diffuses through the stagnant liquid film onto the outer surface of the granule — controlled by the **external mass transfer coefficient, k_f (m/s)**. 3. **Intraparticle Diffusion (Pore Diffusion):** The molecule diffuses through the macro-, meso-, and micropores inside the granule to reach an internal adsorption site — described by the **effective pore diffusion coefficient, D_p (m²/s)**. 4. **Surface Reaction (Sorption Step):** The molecule physically or chemically binds to an active site on the carbon surface — this is the fastest step and is assumed instantaneous. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Standard Assumption:** The slowest step (rate-limiting step) is **either film diffusion or pore diffusion** — never the surface reaction. Engineers size the contactor to overcome whichever resistance dominates. --- ## 2.2 The Two Pillars — Adsorption Isotherms Adsorption equilibrium data are plotted as the **mass of solute adsorbed per unit mass of carbon (x/m)** against the **equilibrium liquid concentration (C_e)**. Two empirical models are universally tested in KTU exams: ### 2.2.1 The Freundlich Isotherm (Empirical — multilayer, heterogeneous sites) $$ \frac{x}{m} = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n} $$ - $K_F$ = Freundlich capacity constant [(mg/g)(L/mg)^(1/n)] - $1/n$ = intensity constant (dimensionless, 0 < 1/n < 1 for favourable adsorption) - **Linearized form** (used for graphical solution): $$ \log \left(\frac{x}{m}\right) = \log K_F + \frac{1}{n}\log C_e $$ > A straight line on a log-log plot of (x/m) vs C_e confirms Freundlich behaviour. ### 2.2.2 The Langmuir Isotherm (Theoretical — monolayer, homogeneous sites) $$ \frac{x}{m} = \frac{a \cdot b \cdot C_e}{1 + b \cdot C_e} $$ - $a$ = maximum monolayer adsorption capacity (mg/g) - $b$ = Langmuir constant related to energy of adsorption (L/mg) - **Linearized form** (used for graphical solution): $$ \frac{C_e}{(x/m)} = \frac{1}{a \cdot b} + \frac{C_e}{a} $$ > A straight line of $C_e/(x/m)$ vs $C_e$ yields slope $1/a$ and intercept $1/(ab)$. --- ## 2.3 The BET Isotherm (Multilayer — rarely used, mentioned for completeness) $$ \frac{C_e}{(C_s - C_e)} \cdot \frac{1}{q} = \frac{1}{q_m b_{BET}} + \frac{(b_{BET} - 1)}{q_m b_{BET}} \cdot \frac{C_e}{C_s} $$ where $C_s$ is the saturation concentration of solute. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Engineering Utility | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Freundlich Isotherm** | $\dfrac{x}{m} = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$ | Capacity at equilibrium conc. | | 2 | **Langmuir Isotherm** | $\dfrac{x}{m} = \dfrac{a\,b\,C_e}{1+b\,C_e}$ | Monolayer capacity | | 3 | **Empty Bed Contact Time (EBCT)** | $EBCT = \dfrac{V_{bed}}{Q} = \dfrac{A \cdot H}{Q}$ | Sizing contactor | | 4 | **Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR)** | $HLR = \dfrac{Q}{A} \; [m^3/(m^2 \cdot h)]$ | Filtration rate | | 5 | **Mass of Carbon Used** | $M_c = \dfrac{Q \cdot (C_0 - C_e)}{q_e} \times T$ | Consumption rate | | 6 | **Carbon Usage Rate** | $CUR = \dfrac{M_c}{V_{treated}} = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{q_e}$ | kg carbon / m³ water | | 7 | **Bed Volumes Treated (BV)** | $BV = \dfrac{Q \cdot t}{V_{bed}}$ | Service life indicator | | 8 | **Linear Velocity** | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A \cdot \varepsilon}$ | Actual interstitial velocity | | 9 | **Breakthrough Capacity** | $q_b = \dfrac{\int_0^{t_b}(C_0 - C)dt}{M_c}$ | Until C reaches limit | | 10 | **Service Time Equation** | $t_s = \dfrac{q_e \cdot M_c}{Q(C_0 - C_e)} - t_{MTZ}$ | Operational time per cycle | | 11 | **MTZ Length Approximation** | $L_{MTZ} \approx H \cdot \dfrac{t_b - t_e}{t_e}$ | Depth of working zone | | 12 | **Regeneration Burn-off** | $M_{regen} = \dfrac{q_e \cdot M_c}{Yield}$ | Mass returned to service | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse `EBCT` with **HRT (Hydraulic Retention Time)**. EBCT assumes the bed is 100 % empty volume (uses full bed volume); true HRT accounts for porosity $\varepsilon$ and is $HRT = EBCT \cdot \varepsilon$. KTU boards accept either convention but the **definition must be stated explicitly**. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pharmaceutical Residue Removal:** GAC removes 70–99 % of trace pharmaceuticals from hospital effluent. - **Industrial Wastewater:** Removes phenols, chlorinated hydrocarbons, dyes from textile and petrochemical effluents. - **Tertiary Municipal Treatment:** Polishing step in activated-sludge plants to meet discharge consents of < 50 mg/L COD. - **Drinking Water (cross-reference):** Removal of geosmin, MIB, pesticides, THM precursors. - **Landfill Leachate:** Removal of refractory humic substances, ammonia polishing after stripping. --- ## 2.6 GAC Contactor Configurations 1. **Downflow Fixed-Bed (Pressure or Gravity):** Most common; allows stratified use of bed; susceptible to headloss clogging. 2. **Upflow Expanded-Bed (Packed-Bed Fluidized):** Reduces headloss, allows suspended solids. 3. **Moving-Bed (Pulsed-Bed):** Carbon is periodically withdrawn from the bottom, regenerated, and recirculated to top. 4. **Series Contactors (Lead-Lag):** Two or more beds in series — the lead bed exhausts first; lag provides safety margin. > [!NOTE] > **Lead-Lag Operation:** The lead bed is exhausted to breakthrough, then taken offline for regeneration; the lag bed becomes the new lead, and a fresh/reactivated bed becomes the new lag. This configuration **uses 100 % of the carbon's capacity** before regeneration — a common KTU design question. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Linearized Langmuir Equation **Starting Point:** The Langmuir model assumes monolayer adsorption on a homogeneous surface, with each site holding only one molecule. Let: - $\theta$ = fraction of surface covered at equilibrium - $C_e$ = equilibrium concentration of solute (mg/L) - $k_1, k_2$ = forward and reverse rate constants The **rate of adsorption** (forward) is proportional to the uncovered fraction and to $C_e$: $$ r_{ads} = k_1 \cdot (1 - \theta) \cdot C_e $$ The **rate of desorption** is proportional to the covered fraction: $$ r_{des} = k_2 \cdot \theta $$ At equilibrium, $r_{ads} = r_{des}$: $$ k_1 (1 - \theta) C_e = k_2 \theta $$ Solving for $\theta$: $$ k_1 C_e - k_1 C_e \theta = k_2 \theta $$ $$ k_1 C_e = \theta (k_2 + k_1 C_e) $$ $$ \theta = \frac{k_1 C_e}{k_2 + k_1 C_e} = \frac{(k_1/k_2) C_e}{1 + (k_1/k_2) C_e} $$ Define $b = k_1/k_2$ (L/mg), and note that the mass adsorbed per gram $\dfrac{x}{m} = a \cdot \theta$ where $a$ is the monolayer capacity (mg/g): $$ \frac{x}{m} = \frac{a \, b \, C_e}{1 + b \, C_e} \quad \text{(Langmuir Isotherm)} $$ To obtain the **linearized form**, divide both sides by $C_e$: $$ \frac{x/m}{C_e} = \frac{a \, b}{1 + b \, C_e} \Rightarrow \frac{C_e}{x/m} = \frac{1 + b\,C_e}{a\,b} = \frac{1}{a\,b} + \frac{C_e}{a} $$ $$ \boxed{\frac{C_e}{x/m} = \frac{1}{a\,b} + \frac{C_e}{a}} $$ A plot of $C_e/(x/m)$ vs $C_e$ gives slope = $1/a$ and intercept = $1/(ab)$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Linearized Freundlich Equation Start with the empirical form: $$ \frac{x}{m} = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n} $$ Take $\log_{10}$ of both sides: $$ \log \left(\frac{x}{m}\right) = \log(K_F) + \frac{1}{n}\log(C_e) $$ $$ \boxed{Y = \log K_F + \left(\frac{1}{n}\right) X \quad \text{where } Y = \log(x/m), \; X = \log(C_e)} $$ A straight line on log-log axes with intercept $\log K_F$ and slope $1/n$ confirms the model. - If $1/n < 1$ → **favourable** adsorption (carbon works efficiently). - If $1/n > 1$ → **unfavourable** adsorption (poor utilization). - If $1/n = 1$ → **linear** isotherm (constant distribution coefficient). --- ## 3.3 Derivation of EBCT and HLR Relationship The empty bed contact time is the time water spends in the contactor if the bed were empty: $$ EBCT = \frac{V_{bed}}{Q} = \frac{A \cdot H}{Q} $$ The hydraulic loading rate is volumetric flow per unit cross-sectional area: $$ HLR = \frac{Q}{A} $$ Substituting: $$ \boxed{EBCT = \frac{H}{HLR}} $$ Since the **true interstitial velocity** is faster (water only travels through the void fraction $\varepsilon$), the actual hydraulic retention time is: $$ HRT = \varepsilon \cdot EBCT $$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Freundlich Isotherm **Problem (KTU-style):** A column study with GAC gave the following equilibrium data for phenol removal: | $C_e$ (mg/L) | $x/m$ (mg/g) | | :---: | :---: | | 0.05 | 0.82 | | 0.20 | 1.95 | | 0.50 | 3.10 | | 1.00 | 4.20 | | 2.00 | 5.30 | **Step 1 — Convert to log form:** | $\log C_e$ | $\log (x/m)$ | | :---: | :---: | | -1.301 | -0.0862 | | -0.699 | 0.2900 | | -0.301 | 0.4914 | | 0.000 | 0.6232 | | 0.301 | 0.7243 | **Step 2 — Linear regression:** Solving the best-fit straight line: $$ \text{slope } (1/n) \approx 0.32, \quad \text{intercept } \log K_F \approx 0.61 $$ $$ K_F = 10^{0.61} \approx 4.07 \; (\text{mg/g})(\text{L/mg})^{1/n} $$ **Step 3 — Freundlich expression:** $$ \frac{x}{m} = 4.07 \cdot C_e^{0.32} $$ **Step 4 — Practical Application:** To reduce phenol from $C_0 = 2.5$ mg/L to $C_e = 0.10$ mg/L (regulatory limit): $$ \frac{x}{m} = 4.07 \times (0.10)^{0.32} = 4.07 \times 0.477 = 1.94 \; \text{mg/g} $$ **Step 5 — Carbon dosage for 1 m³ (1000 L) of wastewater:** $$ M_c = \frac{(C_0 - C_e) \cdot V}{(x/m)} = \frac{(2.5 - 0.10) \times 1000}{1.94} \approx 1237 \; \text{g} \approx 1.24 \; \text{kg/m}^3 $$ --- ## 3.5 Mass-Balance Derivation — Breakthrough Capacity For a column of mass $M_c$ (kg) processing flow $Q$ (m³/h) for time $t$ (h): **Total mass of solute input** = $Q \cdot C_0 \cdot t$ **Total mass of solute leaving** = $Q \cdot \int_0^t C(t) \, dt$ **Total mass adsorbed on carbon** = $M_c \cdot q_{ads}$ Mass balance: $$ M_c \cdot q_{ads} = Q \cdot C_0 \cdot t - Q \int_0^t C(t)\,dt $$ $$ \boxed{q_{ads} = \frac{Q}{M_c} \int_0^t [C_0 - C(t)]\,dt} $$ This integral is the **area above the breakthrough curve** between $C_0$ and $C(t)$ — a fundamental KTU exam favourite. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | Freundlich | Langmuir | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Type | Empirical | Theoretical | | Layer | Multilayer | Monolayer | | Surface | Heterogeneous | Homogeneous | | Limit at high C | Unlimited | Saturation ($q \to a$) | | Best Fit Region | Low–Medium concentration | Wide range, but lower capacity at high C | | KTU Typical Use | Industrial wastewater (complex organics) | Pure compounds, surfactants | | Linearized Plot | $\log(x/m)$ vs $\log C_e$ | $C_e/(x/m)$ vs $C_e$ | --- ## 3.7 Service Time Derivation (Plug-Flow Idealization) The classic **Bohart-Adams / service-time model** assumes the mass transfer zone (MTZ) of length $L_{MTZ}$ moves like a piston through the bed at constant velocity $u_z$: $$ u_z = \frac{L_{MTZ}}{t_e - t_b} $$ where $t_e$ = exhaustion time and $t_b$ = breakthrough time. The service time of the bed before breakthrough: $$ \boxed{t_b = \frac{q_e \cdot \rho_b \cdot H}{HLR \cdot (C_0 - C_e)} - \frac{L_{MTZ}}{u_z}} $$ where $\rho_b$ is the bulk density of carbon. This is **the most important design equation** in any GAC design exam. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a GAC Treatment Train flowchart LR A[Influent Wastewater
Secondary Effluent] --> B[Equalization Basin] B --> C[Pressure Filtration
Multimedia Sand] C --> D[GAC Contactor
Downflow Fixed Bed] D --> E[Lead Bed] D --> F[Lag Bed] D --> G[Standby Bed] E --> H[Carbon Regeneration
Multiple Hearth Furnace] H --> E H --> F H --> G E --> I[Treated Effluent
Polishing] F --> I G --> I I --> J[Disinfection
UV or Chlorination] J --> K[Final Discharge
or Reuse] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style D fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600 style E fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900 style F fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900 style G fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#666666 style H fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000 style J fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900 style K fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc > **Reading the diagram:** Wastewater enters after biological treatment, passes through media filtration to remove residual solids (protecting the carbon from clogging), enters the GAC contactor where the lead bed does the bulk of adsorption and the lag bed provides safety margin. When the lead bed exhausts, it goes to the regeneration furnace and is returned to service; meanwhile the lag bed becomes the new lead. This rotating-lead configuration ensures continuous polishing. --- ## 4.2 Breakthrough Curve Schematic flowchart TD subgraph BC[BREAKTHROUGH CURVE - TYPICAL S-SHAPE] X[Time t or Bed Volumes BV]:::axis Y[Effluent Concentration C / C0]:::axis P1[Point A - Initial
C is essentially zero
Carbon fresh, all sites empty]:::p P2[Point B - Breakthrough
C reaches regulatory limit
C/C0 approx 0.05]:::p P3[Point C - Mass Transfer Zone
Carbon surface saturating
C/C0 climbs sharply]:::p P4[Point D - Exhaustion
C equals C0
Carbon fully spent]:::p end P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 X --> Y style BC fill:#f9f9f9 classDef axis fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 classDef p fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000000 **Interpretation of the Curve:** | Zone | Description | Operational Action | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **A → B** | Saturation zone (top of bed fully used) | Normal operation | | **B → C** | Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ) migrating downward | Plan regeneration | | **C → D** | MTZ reaches bottom; effluent quality drops | Take bed offline | | **At D** | Bed exhausted; carbon must be regenerated | Switch to lag bed | --- ## 4.3 GAC Contactor Internal Flow Architecture flowchart TB subgraph GC[GAC DOWNFLOW PRESSURE CONTACTOR] INF[Inlet
Distribution Header]:::in DIS1[Top Distribution
Perforated Laterals]:::in L1[Layer 1 - Spent Carbon
Saturated Top]:::layer L2[Layer 2 - Working Zone
MTZ Active]:::layer L3[Layer 3 - Fresh Carbon
Virgin Adsorption Sites]:::layer DIS2[Underdrain
False Bottom with Nozzles]:::out BACK[Backwash Outlet
Periodic]:::out EFF[Treated Effluent]:::out SAMPL[Sampling Ports
At Multiple Depths]:::in end INF --> DIS1 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> DIS2 --> EFF L3 -.-> SAMPL DIS2 -.-> BACK classDef in fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000000 classDef out fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900,color:#000000 classDef layer fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 > [!NOTE] > **Multi-port sampling** is essential to track the MTZ position and predict breakthrough — this is a frequent 7-mark KTU question. --- ## 4.4 Regeneration Cycle Block Diagram flowchart LR R1[Spent GAC
from Contactors] --> R2[Dewatering Screen]:::step R2 --> R3[Multiple Hearth
Furnace]:::step R3 --> R4[Drying Zone
100 to 105 degree C]:::step R4 --> R5[Pyrolysis Zone
500 to 650 degree C]:::step R5 --> R6[Steam Reactivation
800 to 950 degree C]:::step R6 --> R7[Cooling Zone]:::step R7 --> R8[Attrition Removal
Air Classification]:::step R8 --> R9[Make-up Carbon
5 to 10 percent]:::step R9 --> R10[Reactivated GAC
Back to Service]:::step R3 --> R11[Off-Gas
Afterburner and
Scrubber]:::byproduct classDef step fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000000 classDef byproduct fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 classDef def fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000000 --- ## 4.5 Decision Logic — When to Regenerate flowchart TD START[Monitor Effluent C] --> Q1{C exceeds
regulatory limit?} Q1 -- No --> NORMAL[Continue Operation
Re-sample in 4 hours] Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is C within
10 percent of C0?} Q2 -- No --> ALERT[Isolate Bed
Switch to Lag Bed] Q2 -- Yes --> WARN[Alarm
Plan Regeneration
Within 24 hours] WARN --> ALERT ALERT --> REGEN[Carbon to Regeneration Furnace] REGEN --> FRESH[Reactivated Carbon
to Standby Bed] style START fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style NORMAL fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900 style WARN fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900 style ALERT fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000 style REGEN fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#666666 style FRESH fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) > [!NOTE] > KTU Part A questions test **Remember/Understand** level (Bloom Level 1 & 2). Answers should be concise, definition-based, and 3–4 sentences maximum. ### Q1. Differentiate between Powdered Activated Carbon (PAC) and Granular Activated Carbon (GAC). Mention at least three points. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer:** | Parameter | PAC | GAC | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Particle Size | 10 – 100 µm (fine powder) | 0.5 – 2.5 mm (granules) | | Mode of Application | Dosed into contact basin; single-use, discarded | Packed in columns; **regenerated and reused** | | Contact Time | Hours (1–4 h) | Minutes (5–30 min) | | Capital Cost | Low | High (due to contactors & regeneration furnace) | | Typical Use | Seasonal / emergency dosing for taste & odour | Continuous polishing for refractory organics | --- ### Q2. Define Empty Bed Contact Time (EBCT). What is its typical range for municipal wastewater polishing? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The **Empty Bed Contact Time (EBCT)** is the theoretical residence time of water in an adsorber if the bed were empty (i.e., without accounting for the carbon volume), expressed as: $$ EBCT = \frac{V_{bed}}{Q} = \frac{A \cdot H}{Q} $$ It represents the **average time** a fluid parcel spends in contact with the adsorbent zone. For municipal wastewater polishing, EBCT typically ranges from **5 to 30 minutes**, depending on the target contaminant and influent concentration. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) > [!NOTE] > KTU Part B tests **Apply/Analyse/Evaluate** levels (Bloom 3–5). Each 14-mark question carries two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The internal choice (Choice A or Choice B) appears within the same module. --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **Freundlich and Langmuir adsorption isotherms** with their linearized forms. State two assumptions of the Langmuir model. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A GAC column study yielded the following equilibrium data for dye removal. Determine the Freundlich constants $K_F$ and $1/n$, and compute the **carbon dosage (kg/m³)** required to reduce the dye concentration from 5.0 mg/L to 0.20 mg/L. **(7 marks)** | $C_e$ (mg/L) | $x/m$ (mg/g) | | :---: | :---: | | 0.10 | 2.00 | | 0.50 | 5.50 | | 1.00 | 7.50 | | 2.00 | 10.0 | | 4.00 | 13.5 | --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a): **Freundlich Isotherm:** The Freundlich equation is an **empirical** model describing multilayer adsorption on heterogeneous surfaces: $$ \frac{x}{m} = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n} $$ Linearized form: $$ \log\left(\frac{x}{m}\right) = \log K_F + \frac{1}{n}\log C_e $$ Plot of $\log(x/m)$ vs $\log C_e$ gives a straight line with slope $1/n$ and intercept $\log K_F$. **Langmuir Isotherm:** The Langmuir model is a **theoretical** equation assuming **monolayer adsorption** on a homogeneous surface with a finite number of identical sites: $$ \frac{x}{m} = \frac{a \cdot b \cdot C_e}{1 + b \cdot C_e} $$ Linearized form: $$ \frac{C_e}{x/m} = \frac{1}{a b} + \frac{C_e}{a} $$ Plot of $C_e/(x/m)$ vs $C_e$ gives slope $1/a$ and intercept $1/(ab)$. **Two Assumptions of Langmuir Model:** 1. The adsorbent surface has a **fixed number of identical adsorption sites**, each capable of holding **only one molecule** (monolayer). 2. The energy of adsorption is **constant** at all sites and there is **no interaction** between adsorbed molecules. --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b): **Step 1 — Compute logs:** | $\log C_e$ | $\log (x/m)$ | | :---: | :---: | | -1.000 | 0.301 | | -0.301 | 0.740 | | 0.000 | 0.875 | | 0.301 | 1.000 | | 0.602 | 1.130 | **Step 2 — Linear Regression (or graphical best fit):** Using least squares on the data: - Mean of $\log C_e = -0.0796$ - Mean of $\log(x/m) = 0.8092$ Slope ($1/n$): $$ \frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2} \approx 0.36 $$ Intercept ($\log K_F$): $$ \log K_F = \bar{y} - (1/n)\bar{x} = 0.8092 - (0.36)(-0.0796) \approx 0.838 $$ $$ K_F = 10^{0.838} \approx 6.89 \; (\text{mg/g})(\text{L/mg})^{1/n} $$ **Step 3 — Freundlich equation:** $$ \frac{x}{m} = 6.89 \cdot C_e^{0.36} $$ **Step 4 — Adsorption capacity at $C_e = 0.20$ mg/L:** $$ \frac{x}{m} = 6.89 \times (0.20)^{0.36} = 6.89 \times 0.549 \approx 3.78 \; \text{mg/g} $$ **Step 5 — Carbon dosage for 1 m³:** $$ M_c = \frac{(C_0 - C_e) \cdot V}{x/m} = \frac{(5.0 - 0.20) \times 1000}{3.78} = \frac{4800}{3.78} \approx 1270 \; \text{g/m}^3 $$ $$ \boxed{M_c \approx 1.27 \; \text{kg/m}^3} $$ --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **breakthrough curve concept** in a fixed-bed GAC adsorber. Define the **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ)**, breakthrough time ($t_b$), and exhaustion time ($t_e$). **(7 marks)** **(b)** A wastewater treatment plant uses a **downflow GAC contactor** with the following specifications: - Flow rate $Q = 250 \; \text{m}^3/\text{h}$ - Bed depth $H = 3.0 \; \text{m}$ - Bed diameter $D = 2.5 \; \text{m}$ - Bulk density of carbon $\rho_b = 480 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ - Equilibrium capacity $q_e = 12 \; \text{mg/g}$ - Influent concentration $C_0 = 8 \; \text{mg/L}$ - Effluent target $C_e = 0.5 \; \text{mg/L}$ - MTZ length = 0.60 m Calculate: **(i)** EBCT, **(ii)** HLR, **(iii)** Mass of carbon in bed, **(iv)** Service time $t_b$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a): The **breakthrough curve** is a plot of **effluent solute concentration $C$** (or $C/C_0$) versus **time $t$** (or volume of water treated / bed volumes). When a fresh GAC bed is put into service, the effluent is essentially **clean** because all the contaminant molecules are adsorbed in the upper layers of the bed. As more water passes through, the upper layer saturates and the active adsorption zone (called the **Mass Transfer Zone, MTZ**) slowly moves downward like a piston. **Key Terms:** - **Breakthrough time ($t_b$):** The time at which the effluent concentration first reaches the **regulatory/permissible limit**. Operation is stopped for regeneration. - **Exhaustion time ($t_e$):** The time at which the effluent concentration equals the **influent** $C_0$, i.e., the carbon is fully spent. - **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ):** The active region of the bed where the concentration drops from $C_0$ to nearly zero. Its length is typically **0.1 to 0.5 m**; a shorter MTZ indicates sharper adsorption and better bed utilization. - **Unused Bed Depth:** The portion of bed below MTZ at breakthrough — wasted capacity that could be utilized in a **lead-lag** configuration. (Sketch: A schematic diagram showing an S-shaped curve with regions A-B (initial), B-C (breakthrough region), C-D (exhaustion), labelling $t_b$, $t_e$, and the MTZ as a moving zone.) --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b): **(i) Empty Bed Contact Time (EBCT):** Volume of bed: $$ V_{bed} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot H = \frac{\pi (2.5)^2}{4} \times 3.0 = 4.909 \times 3.0 = 14.73 \; \text{m}^3 $$ $$ \boxed{EBCT = \frac{V_{bed}}{Q} = \frac{14.73}{250} = 0.0589 \; \text{h} = 3.53 \; \text{min}} $$ **(ii) Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR):** $$ A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = 4.909 \; \text{m}^2 $$ $$ \boxed{HLR = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{250}{4.909} = 50.92 \; \text{m}^3/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{h}) = 50.92 \; \text{m/h}} $$ **(iii) Mass of carbon in the bed:** $$ \boxed{M_c = V_{bed} \cdot \rho_b = 14.73 \times 480 = 7070.4 \; \text{kg}} $$ **(iv) Service time $t_b$:** Using the service-time equation (Section 3.7): $$ t_b = \frac{q_e \cdot \rho_b \cdot (H - L_{MTZ})}{HLR \cdot (C_0 - C_e)} $$ Convert $q_e = 12 \; \text{mg/g} = 12 \times 10^3 \; \text{mg/kg} = 12{,}000 \; \text{mg/kg}$ Convert $C_0 - C_e = 8 - 0.5 = 7.5 \; \text{mg/L} = 7.5 \times 10^3 \; \text{mg/m}^3$ $$ t_b = \frac{12{,}000 \times 480 \times (3.0 - 0.60)}{50.92 \times 7500} $$ $$ t_b = \frac{12{,}000 \times 480 \times 2.40}{381{,}900} $$ $$ t_b = \frac{13{,}824{,}000}{381{,}900} = 36.2 \; \text{h} $$ $$ \boxed{t_b \approx 36.2 \; \text{hours} \; (\approx 1.5 \; \text{days per cycle})} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit Consistency:** $q_e$ in mg/g must be converted to mg/kg before multiplying with mass in kg; $C$ in mg/L must be converted to mg/m³ before multiplying with volumetric flow. KTU board examiners deduct **2 marks** for unit errors. > 2. **MTZ Subtraction:** Forgetting to subtract $L_{MTZ}$ from $H$ when computing working-bed service time is the most common error — costs **1–2 marks**. > 3. **Using C₀ instead of (C₀ − Cₑ):** Some students plug in only the influent concentration; the driving force for adsorption is the **concentration difference** $C_0 - C_e$. > 4. **Confusing $t_b$ with $t_e$:** $t_b$ is regulatory breakthrough (small leakage acceptable); $t_e$ is full exhaustion (carbon is dead). KTU marks distinguish these. > 5. **Neglecting porosity:** If the question asks for *true* HRT instead of EBCT, multiply by $\varepsilon$ (typically 0.40). This is a deliberate trap. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quick-Fire Summary for Last-Minute Revision:** ### Must-Know Definitions - ☐ GAC = granular activated carbon (0.5 – 2.5 mm) - ☐ Specific surface area 500 – 1500 m²/g - ☐ EBCT = V_bed / Q (units of time) - ☐ HLR = Q / A (units of m/h) - ☐ MTZ = mass transfer zone (length of active region) - ☐ $t_b$ = breakthrough time; $t_e$ = exhaustion time - ☐ CUR = carbon usage rate (kg carbon / m³ water treated) - ☐ Lead-Lag = two beds in series for full carbon utilization - ☐ EBCT vs HRT: HRT = ε · EBCT ### Must-Know Equations - ☐ Freundlich: $x/m = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$ → linearize with log-log - ☐ Langmuir: $x/m = (a b C_e)/(1 + b C_e)$ → linearize as $C_e/(x/m)$ vs $C_e$ - ☐ Mass balance: $q_{ads} = (Q/M_c) \int_0^t (C_0 - C)dt$ - ☐ Service time: $t_b = q_e \rho_b (H - L_{MTZ}) / [HLR (C_0 - C_e)]$ ### Must-Know Comparisons - ☐ PAC vs GAC (size, regeneration, contact time, cost) - ☐ Freundlich vs Langmuir (multilayer heterogeneous vs monolayer homogeneous) - ☐ Physical vs Chemical adsorption (van der Waals vs covalent; reversible vs irreversible) - ☐ Fixed bed vs Moving bed vs Fluidized bed contactor ### Must-Know Engineering Numbers - ☐ Typical EBCT for municipal wastewater: **5 – 30 min** - ☐ Typical HLR for downflow GAC: **5 – 25 m/h** - ☐ Bed porosity: **0.38 – 0.42** - ☐ Make-up carbon: **5 – 10 %** per regeneration cycle - ☐ Regeneration furnace temperature: **800 – 950 °C** - ☐ Carbon service life before replacement: **2 – 5 years** ### Common KTU "Examiner Traps" - ☐ Wrong unit conversions (mg/g ↔ mg/kg; mg/L ↔ g/m³) - ☐ Forgetting $L_{MTZ}$ subtraction - ☐ Plotting Freundlich on linear axes instead of log-log - ☐ Mixing up $K_F$ (Freundlich) with $b$ (Langmuir) - ☐ Confusing EBCT with detention time / HRT - ☐ Saying "adsorption = absorption" (KTU deducts marks)

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Advanced Wastewater Treatment (AWT)** refers to the suite of treatment processes applied to secondary treated effluents (biological treatment effluents) to achieve a quality suitable for **discharge into highly sensitive receiving waters, water reuse applications, or non-potable reuse purposes**. When this suite of processes forms the third stage in a conventional wastewater treatment train, it is technically termed **Tertiary Treatment** or **Polishing Treatment**.

According to the standard environmental engineering framework adopted by KTU 2024, advanced wastewater treatment encompasses processes designed to remove:
1. **Residual suspended solids** (TSS down to < 1 mg/L)
2. **Residual organic matter** (BOD/COD below detectable limits)
3. **Dissolved inorganic nutrients** (Nitrogen and Phosphorus species)
4. **Dissolved heavy metals and trace organics**
5. **Pathogenic microorganisms** (Coliforms, viruses, protozoan cysts)
6. **Refractory / Non-biodegradable organic compounds**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 Introduction):**
> The "Advanced" or "Tertiary" treatment stage is **mandatory** when the secondary effluent fails to meet the stipulated discharge norms prescribed by regulatory authorities (e.g., **CPCB / KSPCB** for India) or fails to meet the intended end-use criteria. It is the **final barrier** before disposal or reuse.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Triple-Wash Car Detailing" Analogy 🧽🚗**

Imagine you have just washed your car (this represents **secondary biological treatment**). The car is clean, but to give it that **showroom shine** for a sensitive client, you need to perform three finishing operations:

| Car Detailing Step | Wastewater Engineering Equivalent | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Clay-bar treatment to remove microscopic grit | **Tertiary Filtration** (sand / membrane / microfiltration) | Remove fine residual suspended solids |
| 2. Polishing compound to remove swirl marks | **Advanced Oxidation / Activated Carbon** | Remove refractory organics, color, odor |
| 3. Wax coating for protection & shine | **Disinfection** (UV / Chlorine / Ozonation) | Destroy pathogens for safe discharge or reuse |

The washed car can still have **invisible film, swirl marks, and dust particles** — these are analogous to the **dissolved organics, nutrients, color, and pathogens** that survive secondary treatment. The **tertiary stage** is that final detailing job that transforms an "acceptable" effluent into a **"premium-quality" reusable water stream**.

## 1.3 Standard Effluent Quality Targets

The **goal** of advanced wastewater treatment is to meet the following broad effluent quality ranges (in comparison to secondary effluent quality):

| Parameter | Secondary Effluent (Typical) | After Tertiary / Advanced Treatment | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Suspended Solids (TSS) | 20 – 40 | < 1 – 5 | mg/L |
| Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD₅) | 20 – 30 | < 1 – 5 | mg/L |
| Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD) | 40 – 100 | < 10 – 20 | mg/L |
| Total Nitrogen (TN) | 15 – 40 | < 3 – 10 | mg/L |
| Total Phosphorus (TP) | 4 – 10 | < 0.5 – 1 | mg/L |
| Fecal Coliforms | 10⁴ – 10⁶ | < 200 (Disinfected) | MPN / 100 mL |
| Turbidity | 5 – 15 | < 0.5 – 1 | NTU |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants Used in Module 4:**
> - **Disinfection standard (Indian context):** Fecal coliforms < **200 MPN / 100 mL** for irrigation, **nil** for drinking reuse.
> - **Standard free chlorine residual:** **0.2 – 1.0 mg/L** after 30 min contact time.
> - **Molar mass of ozone (O₃):** **48 g/mol**.

## 1.4 Why is Tertiary / Advanced Treatment Required?

The driving factors justifying the need for advanced treatment are:

1. **Stringent discharge norms** into sensitive water bodies (lakes, beaches, groundwater recharge zones).
2. **Increasing water scarcity** demanding wastewater reuse for industrial cooling, agricultural irrigation, toilet flushing, and even indirect potable reuse.
3. **Presence of recalcitrant compounds** (pharmaceuticals, endocrine disruptors, pesticides, dyes) that resist conventional biological degradation.
4. **Removal of nutrients (N, P)** to prevent **eutrophication** of receiving waters.
5. **Removal of pathogenic microorganisms** to protect public health.
6. **Industrial pretreatment compliance** for effluents containing toxic heavy metals and trace organics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Framework of Wastewater Treatment

The complete treatment train is conventionally divided into **four progressive stages**, each removing progressively finer and more complex pollutants:

### Stage 1: Preliminary Treatment
- **Goal:** Removal of large debris, rags, grit, and floating matter.
- **Typical Units:** Bar screens, grit chambers, comminutors, skimming tanks.
- **Pollutants Removed:** TSS removal of **5 – 25 %**.

### Stage 2: Primary Treatment
- **Goal:** Removal of settleable organic and inorganic solids by sedimentation.
- **Typical Units:** Primary clarifiers (settling tanks).
- **Pollutants Removed:** TSS removal of **50 – 70 %**, BOD removal of **25 – 40 %**.

### Stage 3: Secondary (Biological) Treatment
- **Goal:** Removal of dissolved and colloidal biodegradable organic matter using microorganisms.
- **Typical Units:** Activated Sludge Process (ASP), Trickling Filter (TF), Sequencing Batch Reactor (SBR), Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor (MBBR), Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB).
- **Pollutants Removed:** BOD removal of **85 – 95 %**, TSS removal of **85 – 95 %**.

### Stage 4: Tertiary / Advanced Treatment ⭐ (Module 4 Focus)
- **Goal:** Polishing of secondary effluent for stringent reuse/discharge standards.
- **Typical Units:** Filtration, Membrane Separation, Adsorption (GAC), Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs), Disinfection, Nutrient Removal (N, P).
- **Pollutants Removed:** Residual SS, refractory organics, nutrients, pathogens, trace contaminants.

## 2.2 The "Why-How" Logic of Advanced Treatment

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of Module 4:**

1. **Pillar 1 — Residual Particulate Removal (Filtration & Membrane)**
   - *Why:* Secondary clarifier effluents still carry **pin-floc** and **stray biomass** that escape settling.
   - *How:* **Granular media filtration** (sand / dual-media) for > 5 µm particles; **membrane filtration** (MF/UF) for 0.01 – 10 µm particles.

2. **Pillar 2 — Refractory Organics & Nutrient Removal (AOPs, Adsorption, Biological/Chemical N-P removal)**
   - *Why:* Some organics resist biological oxidation; excess N and P cause algal blooms.
   - *How:* **AOPs** (O₃/UV, O₃/H₂O₂, Fenton, TiO₂ photocatalysis), **Activated Carbon adsorption**, **Nitrification-Denitrification**, **Enhanced Biological Phosphorus Removal (EBPR)**, **Chemical precipitation** of P.

3. **Pillar 3 — Pathogen Destruction (Disinfection)**
   - *Why:* Secondary effluents still contain **10⁴ – 10⁶ fecal coliforms / 100 mL** — unacceptable for most reuse.
   - *How:* **Chlorination** (chemical), **UV irradiation** (physical), **Ozonation** (chemical-physical), **Membrane bioreactors with tight UF/MF** (physical barrier).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always check this table before solving Module 4 numerical problems.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Standard Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | **Removal Efficiency (η)** for any pollutant | C in mg/L, η in % |
| 2 | $C_{out} = C_{in} \times (1 - \eta)$ | Effluent concentration from removal efficiency | mg/L |
| 3 | $\text{Log Removal} = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_{in}}{N_{out}}\right)$ | **Disinfection log-removal** (e.g., coliforms) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $P = \dfrac{Q \times C}{\eta_{pump}}$ | **Power requirement** for UV lamp or pump | W or kW |
| 5 | $\text{CT} = C_{residual} \times t_{contact}$ | **Disinfection CT value** (Concentration × Time) | mg·min/L |
| 6 | $\text{LR}_v = \dfrac{Q}{A_{mem}}$ | **Membrane flux / Loading Rate** | L/(m²·h) or LMH |
| 7 | $\text{Reject Recovery} = \dfrac{Q_{permeate}}{Q_{feed}} \times 100$ | Membrane recovery (%) | % |
| 8 | $C^{*} = \dfrac{C_{in}}{1 + k \cdot \tau}$ | **C* steady-state** for continuous flow reactor | mg/L |
| 9 | $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -k \cdot C$ | First-order decay (AOPs, chlorination decay) | k in 1/min |
| 10 | $\text{OD Loading} = \dfrac{Q \times C}{A}$ | Ozone Dose Loading | g O₃ / m³ or mg/L |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU Boards:** In the above CT formula, never use the vertical bar symbol `|` for any absolute value inside the markdown table. Always use $\vert$ in LaTeX if the CT value needs vertical-line formatting. The table itself has used `()` (parentheses) for grouping, which is the standard convention.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Advanced wastewater treatment underpins the entire **circular water economy**. It enables:
- **Industrial process water reuse** (cooling towers, boiler make-up, textile dyeing).
- **Landscape and agricultural irrigation** (safe for food crops).
- **Groundwater recharge** (MAR — Managed Aquifer Recharge) in arid regions.
- **Indirect potable reuse** (e.g., Singapore's NEWater, California's Groundwater Replenishment System).
- **Discharge to ecologically sensitive zones** (coral reefs, wetlands, drinking water sources).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Overall Plant Removal Efficiency (Cascaded Stages)

When multiple treatment stages operate in series, the **overall plant removal efficiency** is NOT the simple sum of individual stage efficiencies. We must derive it carefully.

**Problem Setup:**
A wastewater treatment plant has three stages with individual BOD removal efficiencies η₁, η₂, η₃. Derive the overall BOD removal efficiency of the plant.

### Step 1: Define the Influent Concentration
Let the influent BOD concentration entering stage 1 be $C_0$ (mg/L).

### Step 2: Effluent of Stage 1
Using the removal efficiency formula from the Formula Sheet:

$$
C_1 = C_0 \times (1 - \eta_1)
$$

*Logic:* The portion removed is $C_0 \eta_1$, so the portion remaining (effluent) is $C_0$ minus what was removed.

### Step 3: Effluent of Stage 2
Stage 2 receives $C_1$ as its influent and removes a fraction $\eta_2$ of that:

$$
C_2 = C_1 \times (1 - \eta_2) = C_0 \times (1 - \eta_1) \times (1 - \eta_2)
$$

*Logic:* Each stage's influent is the previous stage's effluent — this is the **"series reactor"** principle.

### Step 4: Effluent of Stage 3
Similarly:

$$
C_3 = C_2 \times (1 - \eta_3) = C_0 \times (1 - \eta_1) \times (1 - \eta_2) \times (1 - \eta_3)
$$

### Step 5: Overall Plant Removal Efficiency
The overall removal efficiency $\eta_{overall}$ is defined as:

$$
\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{C_0 - C_3}{C_0} \times 100
$$

Substituting the value of $C_3$:

$$
\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{C_0 - C_0 \cdot (1 - \eta_1)(1 - \eta_2)(1 - \eta_3)}{C_0} \times 100
$$

Cancelling $C_0$ from numerator and denominator:

$$
\eta_{overall} = \left[1 - (1 - \eta_1)(1 - \eta_2)(1 - \eta_3)\right] \times 100
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Generalized Result (for n stages in series):**
> $$\boxed{\eta_{overall} = \left[1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - \eta_i)\right] \times 100}$$
> The symbol $\prod$ denotes the **product (multiplication)** of all terms from $i=1$ to $n$.

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Illustration

A municipal plant has:
- Primary clarifier: η₁ = 35 % → (1 − η₁) = 0.65
- Activated sludge: η₂ = 92 % → (1 − η₂) = 0.08
- Tertiary filtration: η₃ = 60 % → (1 − η₃) = 0.40

Overall BOD removal:

$$
\eta_{overall} = \left[1 - (0.65)(0.08)(0.40)\right] \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{overall} = \left[1 - 0.0208\right] \times 100 = 97.92 \,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 92 % biological stage may look "good enough," but the tertiary stage pushes the cumulative removal above **97.9 %**, which is the **discharge/reuse threshold**. This justifies the cost of tertiary treatment.

## 3.2 Derivation: Disinfection Log-Removal

### Step 1: Definition
**Log removal** expresses the order-of-magnitude reduction in microbial count. A "1-log removal" means 90 % killed, "2-log" means 99 % killed, "3-log" means 99.9 % killed, and so on.

### Step 2: Mathematical Definition

$$
\text{Log Removal} = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_{in}}{N_{out}}\right)
$$

where $N_{in}$ and $N_{out}$ are the influent and effluent microbial counts (per 100 mL for coliforms).

### Step 3: Equivalent Percent Removal

Using the property $\log_{10}(10) = 1$:

$$
\text{Percent Removal} = \left(1 - 10^{-\text{Log Removal}}\right) \times 100
$$

### Step 4: Numerical Illustration
Secondary effluent has fecal coliform count = **10⁶ MPN / 100 mL**. Disinfection target = **200 MPN / 100 mL** (KSPCB irrigation norm).

$$
\text{Log Removal Required} = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{10^6}{200}\right) = \log_{10}(5000) = 3.699
$$

So a **3.7-log** reduction is required. This corresponds to:

$$
\text{Percent Removal} = \left(1 - 10^{-3.699}\right) \times 100 = 99.98 \,\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Whenever a disinfection problem is given, ALWAYS convert percent removal to log-removal (or vice versa) — examiners love this conversion. The **decimal log scale** is non-negotiable in environmental engineering.

## 3.3 Derivation: Steady-State CSTR Equation for Tertiary Reactor

A tertiary reactor (e.g., an ozone contactor) operates as a Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR). For a first-order decay reaction $r = -kC$, derive the effluent concentration.

### Step 1: Mass Balance on the Reactor
At steady state (no accumulation):

$$
Q \cdot C_{in} - Q \cdot C_{out} + r \cdot V = 0
$$

where $Q$ = flow rate, $V$ = reactor volume, $r$ = rate of reaction, $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ are influent and effluent concentrations.

### Step 2: Substitute the First-Order Rate
$r = -kC_{out}$ (for a CSTR, the concentration inside the reactor equals the effluent concentration).

$$
Q \cdot C_{in} - Q \cdot C_{out} - k \cdot C_{out} \cdot V = 0
$$

### Step 3: Define Hydraulic Residence Time (HRT)
$\tau = V / Q$ (in minutes or hours).

$$
C_{in} - C_{out} - k \cdot C_{out} \cdot \tau = 0
$$

### Step 4: Solve for $C_{out}$

$$
C_{in} = C_{out}(1 + k\tau)
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{out} = \dfrac{C_{in}}{1 + k\tau}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **classical Chick-Watson CSTR model** for disinfection kinetics. The corresponding percent removal is:
> $$\eta = \dfrac{k\tau}{1 + k\tau} \times 100 \,\%$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Treatment Train (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sewage Influent] --> B[Stage 1 Preliminary]
    B --> B1[Screening and Grit Removal]
    B1 --> C[Stage 2 Primary]
    C --> C1[Primary Clarifier]
    C1 --> D[Stage 3 Secondary Biological]
    D --> D1[Activated Sludge or MBBR or SBR]
    D1 --> D2[Secondary Clarifier]
    D2 --> E[Stage 4 Tertiary Advanced]
    E --> E1[Pillar 1 Residual Solids Removal]
    E1 --> E1a[Granular Media Filtration]
    E1 --> E1b[Membrane Microfiltration UF]
    E --> E2[Pillar 2 Refractory and Nutrient Removal]
    E2 --> E2a[Advanced Oxidation AOPs]
    E2 --> E2b[Activated Carbon Adsorption]
    E2 --> E2c[Nitrification Denitrification]
    E2 --> E2d[Chemical Phosphorus Precipitation]
    E --> E3[Pillar 3 Disinfection]
    E3 --> E3a[Chlorination]
    E3 --> E3b[UV Irradiation]
    E3 --> E3c[Ozonation]
    E1a --> F[Polished Reuse-Ready Effluent]
    E1b --> F
    E2a --> F
    E2b --> F
    E2c --> F
    E2d --> F
    E3a --> F
    E3b --> F
    E3c --> F
    F --> G[Discharge to Sensitive Water Body]
    F --> H[Reuse for Industrial Cooling]
    F --> I[Reuse for Landscape Irrigation]
    F --> J[Groundwater Recharge]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Logic for Selecting a Tertiary Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Secondary Effluent In] --> P1{Target Pollutant Class}
    P1 -- Residual TSS --> P2a{Particle Size}
    P2a -- Greater than 5 micron --> P3a1[Granular Media Filtration Sand or Dual Media]
    P2a -- Smaller than 5 micron --> P3a2[Membrane Microfiltration or Ultrafiltration]
    P1 -- Refractory Organics --> P2b{BOD to COD Ratio}
    P2b -- BOD by COD less than 0.1 --> P3b1[Advanced Oxidation AOPs]
    P2b -- BOD by COD between 0.1 and 0.3 --> P3b2[Granular Activated Carbon GAC]
    P1 -- Nutrients N and P --> P2c{Treatment Mode}
    P2c -- Nitrogen --> P3c1[Biological Nitrification Denitrification]
    P2c -- Phosphorus --> P3c2[Chemical Precipitation with Alum or Fe]
    P1 -- Pathogens --> P2d{Reuse End Use}
    P2d -- Irrigation --> P3d1[Chlorination at 0.5 mg per L residual]
    P2d -- Industrial Reuse --> P3d2[UV Irradiation at 40 mJ per cm2]
    P2d -- Indirect Potable --> P3d3[Ozonation plus UV plus Chlorine]
    P3a1 --> OUT1[Treated Effluent]
    P3a2 --> OUT1
    P3b1 --> OUT1
    P3b2 --> OUT1
    P3c1 --> OUT1
    P3c2 --> OUT1
    P3d1 --> OUT1
    P3d2 --> OUT1
    P3d3 --> OUT1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation for KTU Exam:** The Mermaid `flowchart TD` syntax uses top-down direction. The `{}` braces denote decision nodes, the `[]` braces denote process nodes. These are standard, beginner-friendly shapes that examiners accept as schematic representations when physical drawings are not feasible.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Mass-Balance Concept Map for the Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Influent Boundary]
        Inf1[Influent BOD]
        Inf2[Influent COD]
        Inf3[Influent TSS]
        Inf4[Influent Coliforms]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS[Tertiary Process Boundary]
        T1[Filtration]
        T2[AOPs]
        T3[Disinfection]
    end
```
