The ·OH radical abstracts a hydrogen atom from saturated C–H, N–H, or O–H bonds in aliphatic organics, generating a carbon-centered radical and water.

$$
\mathrm{R{-}H + \cdot OH \rightarrow R\cdot + H_2O}
$$

**2. Electron Transfer (ET) Mechanism**
The ·OH radical accepts a single electron from a reducing agent (e.g., a phenoxide ion, an amine, or a metal ion) to yield a hydroxide ion and an oxidized substrate radical.

$$
\mathrm{ArO^- + \cdot OH \rightarrow ArO\cdot + OH^-}
$$

**3. Radical Addition (RA) Mechanism**
The ·OH radical adds to unsaturated bonds (C=C, C≡C, aromatic rings) to form a hydroxylated carbon-centered radical.

$$
\mathrm{C_6H_6 + \cdot OH \rightarrow \cdot C_6H_6OH}
$$

**Significance:** All three mechanisms lead to the formation of a **carbon-centered radical R·**, which combines with dissolved O₂ to form a **peroxyl radical ROO·**. This initiates a cascade of oxidation steps that ultimately mineralizes the substrate. The hydroxyl radical's **E° = +2.80 V vs NHE** and **k = 10⁸ – 10¹⁰ M⁻¹s⁻¹** make it a uniquely powerful, non-selective oxidant.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to mention the *propagation step* — i.e., reaction of R· with dissolved O₂ to form ROO·. Examiners deduct up to 1 mark for this omission.

---

**Q. A (b)** Discuss the **Fenton process in detail** with reaction chemistry, operating parameters, and limitations. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Fenton Process — Reaction Chemistry**

The Fenton process uses the reaction between **ferrous ion (Fe²⁺)** and **hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂)** in acidic medium (pH 2.5 – 4.0) to generate ·OH radicals.

$$
\mathrm{Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 \rightarrow Fe^{3+} + OH^- + \cdot OH} \quad (k = 63 - 76 \; \mathrm{M^{-1}s^{-1}})
$$

The Fe³⁺ produced is then recycled back to Fe²⁺ by a much slower reaction:

$$
\mathrm{Fe^{3+} + H_2O_2 \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + HOO\cdot + H^+} \quad (k = 0.001 - 0.01 \; \mathrm{M^{-1}s^{-1}})
$$

The sum cycle is the **catalytic Fenton system**. The carbon-centered radicals formed by ·OH attack react with O₂ to eventually yield CO₂, H₂O, and Fe(OH)₃ sludge.

**Operating Parameters**

| Parameter | Optimum Value |
|---|---|
| pH | **2.8 – 3.2** |
| [Fe²⁺] : [H₂O₂] ratio | 1 : 5 to 1 : 10 (mass) |
| Reaction time | 30 – 60 minutes |
| Temperature | 25 – 40 °C |
| Initial COD | Up to 5000 mg/L (typical) |

**Limitations of Fenton Process**

1. **Narrow pH window (2.5 – 3.5):** above pH 4, Fe(III) precipitates as Fe(OH)₃ and loses catalytic activity; below pH 2, H₂O₂ is protonated to H₃O₂⁺ and becomes a poor oxidant.
2. **Iron sludge production:** up to 2 – 4 kg of Fe(OH)₃ per kg of H₂O₂ consumed, requiring disposal.
3. **Slow Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺ recycle:** this is the rate-limiting step in dark Fenton; addressed by photo-Fenton.
4. **Excess H₂O₂ scavenging:** unreacted H₂O₂ consumes ·OH radicals:
   $\mathrm{\cdot OH + H_2O_2 \rightarrow HOO\cdot + H_2O}$
5. **Cl⁻ interference:** at high chloride, Fe²⁺ forms chloro-complexes that reduce ·OH yield.
6. **Not effective on highly chlorinated, fluorinated, or saturated aliphatic compounds** alone — they often need UV assistance (photo-Fenton).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss point:** Students write "Fenton works at pH 7" — this is **wrong**. Lose 1 mark. Fenton's window is pH 2.5–3.5, period.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**Q. B (a)** Explain **ozone-based Advanced Oxidation Processes** including peroxone, O₃/UV, and O₃/catalyst systems. Compare their ·OH yield and energy efficiency. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Ozone-Based AOPs**

**1. Peroxone (O₃ / H₂O₂):** Hydrogen peroxide accelerates the decomposition of ozone into hydroxyl radicals through a chain-reaction mechanism.

$$
\mathrm{H_2O_2 + 2O_3 \rightarrow 2\cdot OH + 3O_2}
$$

The mechanism proceeds via superoxide (O₂·⁻) formation:

$$
\mathrm{O_3 + OH^- \rightarrow HO_2^- + O_2}
$$
$$
\mathrm{HO_2^- + O_3 \rightarrow HO_2\cdot + O_3^{\cdot-}}
$$
$$
\mathrm{O_3^{\cdot-} + H_2O \rightarrow \cdot OH + OH^- + O_2}
$$

Optimum dose: H₂O₂/O₃ molar ratio = **0.5 – 1.0**.

**2. O₃ / UV Photolysis:** UV photons at 254 nm decompose ozone to atomic oxygen which then reacts with water:

$$
\mathrm{O_3 + h\nu \rightarrow O_2 + O(^1D)}
$$
$$
\mathrm{O(^1D) + H_2O \rightarrow 2\cdot OH}
$$

**3. O₃ / Solid Catalyst (e.g., TiO₂, Fe-Oxide, activated carbon):** Heterogeneous catalysts accelerate ozone decomposition at the catalyst surface:

$$
\mathrm{O_3 + Surface \rightarrow Surface{-}O\cdot}
$$
$$
\mathrm{Surface{-}O\cdot + H_2O \rightarrow Surface + \cdot OH + OH^-}
$$

**Comparison of Ozone-Based AOPs**

| Feature | Peroxone | O₃ / UV | O₃ / Catalyst |
|---|---|---|---|
| ·OH yield (qualitative) | High | High | Moderate–High |
| E_EO (kWh m⁻³ order⁻¹) | 0.5 – 5 | 1 – 10 | 2 – 20 |
| Operating cost | Low (no UV lamp) | High (lamp + electricity) | Moderate |
| Best for | High-COD colored water | Trace organics, low-TDS | Continuous flow with low-cost catalyst |

**Engineering note:** Peroxone is the most popular because it avoids the high capital cost of UV lamps while still delivering ·OH-driven non-selective oxidation.

---

**Q. B (b)** With neat sketches, describe **TiO₂/UV photocatalysis** for wastewater treatment. Discuss the role of band-gap, hole, and electron, and the fate of charge carriers. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**TiO₂ / UV Photocatalysis — Mechanism**

Titanium dioxide (anatase form) is the most widely used photocatalyst in AOPs. It is **non-toxic, chemically stable, insoluble in water, and reusable**.

**1. Photon Absorption and Charge Separation**

When UV light of energy **≥ 3.2 eV** (wavelength **≤ 385 nm**) strikes TiO₂, a valence-band electron is promoted to the conduction band, leaving behind a positive hole (h⁺) in the valence band.

$$
\mathrm{TiO_2 + h\nu \rightarrow TiO_2\,(e^-_{cb} + h^+_{vb})}
$$

**2. Fate of the Conduction-Band Electron (e⁻_cb)**

The electron reduces adsorbed O₂ to superoxide radical, preventing electron-hole recombination.

$$
\mathrm{e^-_{cb} + O_2 \rightarrow O_2^{\cdot-}}
$$
$$
\mathrm{O_2^{\cdot-} + H^+ \rightarrow HOO\cdot}
$$
$$
\mathrm{2HOO\cdot \rightarrow H_2O_2 + O_2}
$$
$$
\mathrm{H_2O_2 + e^-_{cb} \rightarrow \cdot OH + OH^-}
$$

**3. Fate of the Valence-Band Hole (h⁺_vb)**

The hole oxidizes adsorbed water (or hydroxide) to produce the **hydroxyl radical**:

$$
\mathrm{h^+_{vb} + H_2O \rightarrow \cdot OH + H^+}
$$

The hydroxyl radical then attacks organic pollutants in the bulk solution, mineralizing them to CO₂ and H₂O.

**Block Diagram Sketch (text version):**

```
   UV Lamp (λ ≤ 385 nm)
         |
         v
   +-------------+        e⁻   +   O₂   →  O₂·⁻
   |  TiO₂ Film  |   ──────────────────────────► superoxide
   |  (anatase)  |
   |  Eg = 3.2 eV|        h⁺  +  H₂O  →  ·OH + H⁺
   +-------------+
         |
         v
   Pollutant (P) + ·OH → P· → P-OO· → ... → CO₂ + H₂O
```

**Operating Parameters**

- pH optimum: 5 – 7 (close to TiO₂ pHzpc ≈ 6.2)
- Catalyst loading: 0.5 – 2.0 g/L
- UV intensity: ≥ 5 mW/cm²
- Reactor form: slurry (most common), immobilized thin-film, or optical-fiber reactor

**Advantages**
- Uses sunlight — no electricity for photons in sunny regions.
- Catalyst is reusable for thousands of cycles.
- No chemical addition (only air + UV).

**Limitations**
- Rapid e⁻/h⁺ recombination (within ns) reduces quantum efficiency.
- Band gap limits to UV; visible-light catalysts (doped TiO₂, BiVO₄) are still under research.
- Catalyst recovery from slurry reactors is expensive.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write "TiO₂ + UV → ·OH directly". This misses the **e⁻/h⁺ step**. Examiners deduct 2 marks unless the e⁻/h⁺ separation is explicitly described. Always include the **e⁻, h⁺, and Eg** in your photocatalysis answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this as a 5-minute pre-exam revision tool.**

- **AOP Definition:** Any process generating ·OH in situ for water treatment (Glaze, Kang, Chapin, 1987).
- **Central species:** Hydroxyl radical ·OH.
- **Standard oxidation potential of ·OH:** **+2.80 V (vs NHE)** — second only to F₂ (+3.06 V).
- **Second-order rate constant of ·OH with organics:** **10⁸ – 10¹⁰ M⁻¹s⁻¹**.
- **Three attack mechanisms:** HAT (hydrogen abstraction), ET (electron transfer), RA (radical addition).
- **Propagation step:** R· + O₂ → ROO· → chain propagation → CO₂ + H₂O.
- **Fenton process:** Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ → Fe³⁺ + OH⁻ + ·OH; pH 2.5 – 3.5; k = 63 – 76 M⁻¹s⁻¹.
- **Photo-Fenton advantage:** UV regenerates Fe²⁺ from Fe³⁺, sustaining ·OH production.
- **Peroxone:** O₃ + H₂O₂ → ·OH; H₂O₂/O₃ molar ratio 0.5 – 1.0.
- **O₃/UV:** O₃ + hν + H₂O → H₂O₂ + O₂; quantum yield φ = 0.52 at 254 nm.
- **H₂O₂/UV:** H₂O₂ + hν → 2·OH; quantum yield φ = 0.5 at 254 nm.
- **TiO₂/UV:** Eg = 3.2 eV (anatase); produces e⁻/h⁺ pair; h⁺ + H₂O → ·OH + H⁺.
- **Performance metric:** E_EO in kWh m⁻³ order⁻¹; < 1 is excellent, > 100 is uneconomical.
- **Kinetic model:** Pseudo-first-order, ln(C₀/C) = k_app · t; half-life t₁/₂ = 0.693/k_app.
- **Rct concept:** ∫[·OH]dt; typical Rct = 10⁻¹⁰ – 10⁻⁹ M·s for efficient AOPs.
- **Comparison rule:** O₃ alone is selective (AOP only with H₂O₂ or UV); ·OH is non-selective.
- **Selection of AOP:** pH → Fenton; coloured/COD → Peroxone; saline → Electro-Fenton; trace organics → O₃/UV.
- **Limitations of Fenton:** narrow pH, iron sludge, Cl⁻ interference, H₂O₂ scavenging of ·OH.
- **pHzpc of TiO₂:** 6.2 – 6.8; optimum pH for photocatalysis.
- **pKa of ·OH:** 11.9.
- **Bond dissociation energy of O–H:** 498 kJ/mol.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Warning:** In KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, part-(a) carries **7 marks** and part-(b) carries **7 marks**. The most common mark-loss errors are: (i) forgetting the propagation step, (ii) stating Fenton pH = 7, (iii) skipping the e⁻/h⁺ step in TiO₂ photocatalysis, and (iv) failing to mention **E° = +2.80 V** for ·OH. Read the question carefully, draw a labelled block diagram, and **state all units explicitly**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Oxidizing Agents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Oxidizing Agents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Oxidizing Agents (Oxidants) in Advanced Wastewater Treatment:**
> Oxidizing agents are chemical species that gain electrons (undergo reduction) during a chemical reaction, thereby causing the oxidation of target pollutants. In advanced wastewater treatment, they are deployed to degrade recalcitrant organic compounds, inactivate pathogens, oxidize ammonia/cyanide/sulfides, and remove color, odor, and trace micropollutants that survive conventional biological treatment.

The strength of an oxidant is quantified by its **Standard Electrode Potential (E°)** in volts — higher E° implies stronger oxidizing power and faster reaction kinetics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Disinfectant Toolbox":**
> Imagine a chemist is a *firefighter* and the pollutants are *stubborn fires* (non-biodegradable organics, pathogens, ammonia). Some fires can be doused with water (mild oxidation), but the truly persistent ones need *specialized extinguishers* — foam, CO₂, dry chemical powder. Each oxidant is like a different type of extinguisher:
> - **Chlorine** = foam extinguisher (cheap, broad use, but produces toxic by-products).
> - **Ozone** = CO₂ extinguisher (powerful, leaves no residue, but expensive and unstable).
> - **Hydrogen Peroxide** = water mist (clean, but needs a catalyst to be powerful).
> - **Permanganate** = dry chemical powder (selective for certain "fires" like Mn²⁺/Fe²⁺ and phenols).
>
> The engineer must pick the *right extinguisher* based on the type of fire, cost, and safety.

### 1.3 Classification Overview

The principal oxidants used in advanced wastewater treatment can be grouped as:

| Family | Examples | Typical E° (V) |
|---|---|---|
| Halogen-based | Cl₂, ClO₂, HOCl, OCl⁻, NaOCl | **0.80 – 1.36** |
| Oxygen-based | O₂, O₃, H₂O₂ | **0.68 – 2.07** |
| Metal-based | KMnO₄ | **0.59 – 1.51** |
| Hybrid / AOPs | O₃/H₂O₂, H₂O₂/Fe²⁺ (Fenton), UV/H₂O₂ | **> 2.00 (with •OH radical)** |

> [!NOTE]
> The hydroxyl radical (**•OH**) generated in AOPs has the highest oxidation potential in water (**E° = 2.80 V**) and is the *de facto benchmark* against which all other oxidants are compared.

### 1.4 Why Oxidizing Agents are Needed in Advanced Treatment

Conventional biological treatment (ASP, SBR, MBBR) cannot effectively remove:
- **Recalcitrant organics** (pesticides, pharmaceuticals, endocrine disruptors)
- **Color & odor** compounds
- **Reduced inorganic species** (NH₃, H₂S, CN⁻, Fe²⁺, Mn²⁺)
- **Pathogens of concern** (Cryptosporidium, Giardia cysts)

Chemical oxidants bridge this gap.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Classification of Oxidizing Agents

#### A. Chlorine and Chlorine Compounds

- **Molecular Chlorine (Cl₂):** In water, forms HOCl and OCl⁻ depending on pH.

$$\text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{HOCl} + \text{HCl}$$

- **Hypochlorous Acid (HOCl)** is **~80× more effective** than OCl⁻ as a disinfectant.
- **Chlorine Dioxide (ClO₂):** Unique — does **not** form chlorinated organics (THMs).

$$\text{ClO}_2 \rightarrow \text{ClO}_2^- + e^- \quad (E° = 0.95\,V)$$

- **Sodium Hypochlorite (NaOCl):** Liquid, easy to dose, used in small STPs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The HOCl/OCl⁻ equilibrium is pH-controlled. At pH < 7.5, HOCl dominates (stronger); at pH > 7.5, OCl⁻ dominates. Below pH 4, dissolved Cl₂ escapes as toxic gas.

#### B. Ozone (O₃)

- Generated on-site by **corona discharge** (dry air or pure O₂ feed).
- Powerful — second only to •OH among common oxidants.

$$\text{O}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{HO}_2^- + \text{O}_2 + \text{H}^+ \quad \text{(in alkaline pH)}$$

- **Direct oxidation** (molecular O₃) — selective, attacks C=C, aromatic amines, phenols.
- **Indirect oxidation** (•OH radical via O₃ decomposition) — non-selective, mineralizes organics to CO₂ and H₂O.

> [!WARNING]
> Ozonation **does not produce THMs** but may form **bromate (BrO₃⁻)** if bromide is present — a regulated carcinogen.

#### C. Hydrogen Peroxide (H₂O₂)

- Clean oxidant — by-products are only H₂O and O₂.
- Mild alone (E° = 1.78 V), but very powerful when activated.

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \quad (E° = 1.78\,V)$$

- Activated by: **Fe²⁺ (Fenton), UV, O₃ (Peroxone), or Fe⁰/Fe³⁺-coated catalysts.**

#### D. Potassium Permanganate (KMnO₄)

- Solid, easy to handle, used in pre-oxidation.
- Reaction depends on pH:

$$ \text{MnO}_4^- + 8\text{H}^+ + 5e^- \rightarrow \text{Mn}^{2+} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (E° = 1.51\,V, \text{acidic})$$

$$ \text{MnO}_4^- + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3e^- \rightarrow \text{MnO}_2 + 4\text{OH}^- \quad (E° = 0.59\,V, \text{neutral/alkaline})$$

- MnO₂ precipitate is a **coagulant aid** — removes turbidity and Fe/Mn synergistically.

#### E. Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)

| AOP | Reaction Initiator | Key Radical |
|---|---|---|
| Fenton | Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ | •OH |
| Photo-Fenton | UV/Vis + Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ | •OH |
| UV/H₂O₂ | UV (254 nm) + H₂O₂ | •OH |
| O₃/H₂O₂ (Peroxone) | O₃ + H₂O₂ at high pH | •OH, HO₂• |
| O₃/UV | UV + O₃ | •OH |
| TiO₂/UV | Photocatalysis | •OH, O₂⁻• |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Fenton's Reaction is **board-favorite** in KTU examinations — memorize the rate-controlling step.

$$ \text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{OH}^- + \bullet\text{OH} \quad (k_1 \approx 63-76\,M^{-1}s^{-1}) $$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Oxidant Dose (mass) | $D = C \times Q$ | $C$ = mg/L, $Q$ = m³/d, $D$ in g/d |
| 2 | Moles of Oxidant | $n = \dfrac{C \times Q}{M_r}$ | $M_r$ = molecular weight |
| 3 | Chlorine Requirement (Breakpoint) | $\text{Cl}_2 : \text{NH}_3 = 7.6 : 1$ by mass | After full breakpoint, all NH₃ → N₂ |
| 4 | Fenton H₂O₂ : COD | $1.0 - 2.0\,$kg H₂O₂ per kg COD | Typical range |
| 5 | Fenton Fe²⁺ : H₂O₂ | $1 : 5$ to $1 : 10$ (mass) | Catalytic ratio |
| 6 | Ozone Yield | $Y = \dfrac{[\text{O}_3]_{out}}{[\text{O}_3]_{in}} \times 100$ | % transfer efficiency |
| 7 | Ozone Dose for Color | $D_{\text{O}_3} = 0.5 - 3.0\,$mg O₃/mg color units | Empirical |
| 8 | KMnO₄ Dose | $2\,\text{mg/L}$ for Fe/Mn, $1-4\,$mg/L for organics | Common plant practice |
| 9 | H₂O₂ Residual Test | Absorbance at 240 nm (ε = 40 M⁻¹cm⁻¹) | Spectrophotometric |
| 10 | HOCl/OCl⁻ pKₐ | $pK_a = 7.5$ at 25 °C | At 0 °C, pKₐ = 7.8 |
| 11 | Ozone Henry's Constant | $H_{cc} = 0.082$ at 20 °C | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Standard potentials ranking | F⁻ < Cl₂ < O₂ < H₂O₂ < ClO₂ < MnO₄⁻ < O₃ < •OH | Increasing E° |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal STPs:** Cl₂ for disinfection; KMnO₄ for Fe/Mn removal; O₃ for micropollutants (tertiary).
- **Industrial ETPs (textile, pharma, petrochemical):** Fenton for COD; O₃ for color; AOPs for non-biodegradables.
- **Drinking Water:** ClO₂ and O₃ to avoid THMs; UV/H₂O₂ for taste & odor (geosmin, 2-MIB).
- **Landfill Leachate:** Fenton + Ozone combined for high-strength recalcitrant leachate.
- **Aquaculture:** Ozone for pathogen control without chlorinated residues.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation: Breakpoint Chlorination Stoichiometry

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic 7-mark derivation** in KTU 2019–2024 papers.

**Step 1 — Formation of Chloramines (first step, pH > 7)**

$$ \text{HOCl} + \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{Cl} (\text{monochloramine}) + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Step 2 — Dichloramine formation**

$$ \text{HOCl} + \text{NH}_2\text{Cl} \rightarrow \text{NHCl}_2 (\text{dichloramine}) + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Step 3 — Nitrogen trichloride (at low pH)**

$$ \text{HOCl} + \text{NHCl}_2 \rightarrow \text{NCl}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Step 4 — Breakpoint (destruction of chloramines)**

$$ 2\text{NHCl}_2 + \text{HOCl} \rightarrow \text{N}_2\uparrow + 3\text{HCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Step 5 — Net breakpoint reaction (adding Steps 1 to 4 in proper ratio)**

Multiply Step 1 by 2 to balance; summing all 4 gives:

$$ 2\text{NH}_3 + 3\text{HOCl} \rightarrow \text{N}_2\uparrow + 3\text{HCl} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Step 6 — Convert HOCl → Cl₂ equivalent**

Since 1 mol Cl₂ produces 1 mol HOCl, the **mass ratio** is:

$$ \dfrac{\text{Mass of Cl}_2}{\text{Mass of NH}_3\text{-N}} = \dfrac{3 \times 71}{2 \times 14} = \dfrac{213}{28} \approx 7.6 $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** To completely oxidize 1 kg of NH₃-N by breakpoint chlorination, **~7.6 kg of Cl₂** is theoretically required. Field practice uses **8–10 kg Cl₂/kg N** to account for side reactions (oxidation of organics, Fe²⁺, Mn²⁺, H₂S).

### 3.2 Worked Derivation: Fenton Process Dose Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> Typical 7-mark KTU question on Fenton design.

**Given:**
- Flow, $Q = 100\,$m³/d
- Influent COD = 1500 mg/L
- Target COD removal efficiency = 70%
- Assume H₂O₂ : COD = 1.5 (mass ratio) and Fe²⁺ : H₂O₂ = 1 : 8

**Step 1 — Daily COD load to be removed**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COD load} &= Q \times \text{COD} \times \eta \\
&= 100\,\text{m}^3/\text{d} \times 1500\,\text{g/m}^3 \times 0.70 \\
&= 105{,}000\,\text{g/d} = 105\,\text{kg/d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — H₂O₂ required per day**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \text{ dose} &= 105\,\text{kg/d} \times 1.5 \\
&= 157.5\,\text{kg/d of 100\% H}_2\text{O}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

If commercial H₂O₂ is 50% w/w:

$$ \text{Commercial H}_2\text{O}_2 = \dfrac{157.5}{0.50} = 315\,\text{L/d} \text{ (assuming density } \rho \approx 1.0\, \text{kg/L)} $$

**Step 3 — Fe²⁺ (as FeSO₄·7H₂O) required**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fe}^{2+} &= \dfrac{\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \text{ mass}}{8} = \dfrac{157.5}{8} = 19.7\,\text{kg/d} \\
\text{FeSO}_4 \cdot 7\text{H}_2\text{O} \text{ mass} &= 19.7 \times \dfrac{278}{56} = 97.8\,\text{kg/d}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimum pH for Fenton: 2.5 – 3.5.** Above pH 4, Fe³⁺ precipitates as Fe(OH)₃ sludge; below pH 2, •OH is scavenged by H⁺.

### 3.3 Worked Derivation: Ozone Transfer Efficiency & Specific Dose

**Given:**
- Inlet COD = 500 mg/L, target 60% removal by ozonation
- Specific ozone dose, $\phi = 1.5\,$mg O₃/mg COD removed
- Ozone generator output = 5 kg/h
- Gas flow to contactor = 80 Nm³/h
- Inlet gas concentration = 120 g O₃/Nm³

**Step 1 — Required ozone mass rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COD removed per hour} &= Q_{\text{ww}} \times \text{COD} \times \eta \\
\text{O}_3 \text{ required} &= Q_{\text{ww}} \times 500 \times 0.6 \times 1.5 \\
&= 450 \times Q_{\text{ww}}\,\text{(m}^3/\text{h)}\,\text{mg/L of O}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

(For numeric answer, take $Q_{\text{ww}} = 50\,$m³/h)

$$ \text{O}_3 \text{ required} = 50 \times 500 \times 0.6 \times 1.5 = 22{,}500\,\text{g/h} = 22.5\,\text{kg/h} $$

**Step 2 — Ozone generator capacity check**

$$ \text{Inlet O}_3 = 80 \times 120 = 9.6\,\text{kg/h} \;\; \text{(only)} $$

Generator is **undersized** — need to increase either gas flow or feed-gas O₃ concentration, or install two generators.

**Step 3 — Required inlet O₃ concentration (at fixed 80 Nm³/h)**

$$ C_{\text{in, O}_3} = \dfrac{22.5\,\text{kg/h}}{80\,\text{Nm}^3/\text{h}} = 0.281\,\text{kg/Nm}^3 = 281\,\text{g/Nm}^3 $$

This exceeds typical generator output (~140–180 g/Nm³ with O₂ feed), so a **second generator or pure O₂ feed** is necessary.

### 3.4 Comparative Matrix of Major Oxidants

| Property | Cl₂ | ClO₂ | NaOCl | O₃ | H₂O₂ | KMnO₄ | Fenton (H₂O₂/Fe²⁺) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| E° (V) | 1.36 | 0.95 | 1.48 | 2.07 | 1.78 | 1.51 | 2.80 (•OH) |
| Form at dose | Gas | Gas | Liquid | Gas | Liquid | Solid | Liquid |
| THM formation | **Yes** | No | Yes | No | No | No | No |
| Bromate formation | No | No | No | **Yes** | No | No | No |
| Optimum pH | 6–7.5 | 6–8 | 6–7.5 | 6–8 | 7 | 7–8 | 2.5–3.5 |
| Residual | Long | Moderate | Long | None | Short | MnO₂ ppt | None |
| Sludge | None | None | None | None | None | MnO₂ | Fe(OH)₃ |
| Cost (relative) | 1 | 3 | 1.5 | 5 | 2 | 4 | 3 |
| On-site generation | No | Yes | No | **Yes** | No | No | No |

### 3.5 Selection Algorithm for KTU Numerical/Design Problems

```
Step 1 → Identify target pollutant
         (organics? ammonia? Fe/Mn? color? pathogens?)
Step 2 → Check influent matrix (pH, Cl⁻, Br⁻, alkalinity, COD)
Step 3 → Match pollutant to oxidant preference:
         • NH₃ removal  →  Cl₂ (breakpoint) / breakpoint ClO₂
         • Fe/Mn         →  KMnO₄ (neutral) or ClO₂
         • Color         →  O₃ or Fenton
         • COD refractory→  Fenton / O₃ / O₃-H₂O₂ / UV-H₂O₂
         • Disinfection  →  Cl₂ / ClO₂ / O₃ / UV
         • Micropollutants → AOPs (•OH-based)
Step 4 → Apply dose formula & safety factor (×1.2 – 1.5)
Step 5 → Check by-product constraints (THM, BrO₃⁻, ClO₃⁻, MnO₂)
Step 6 → Economic analysis (LCC over 20 yr)
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture: Oxidant Selection & Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Influent Wastewater] --> B[Characterization: pH, COD, NH3, Fe/Mn, Color]
    B --> C{Target Pollutant?}
    C -->|NH3-N| D1[Breakpoint Chlorination]
    C -->|Fe/Mn| D2[KMnO4 Pre-oxidation]
    C -->|Color| D3[Ozonation]
    C -->|Refractory COD| D4[Fenton / AOP]
    C -->|Pathogens| D5[Cl2 / ClO2 / O3 / UV]
    D1 --> E1[HOCl + NH3 Reaction Sequence]
    D2 --> E2[MnO4- + Fe2+ → Fe3+ + MnO2]
    D3 --> E3[O3 + H2O → OH radical]
    D4 --> E4[Fe2+ + H2O2 → OH radical]
    D5 --> E5[Direct Cell-wall Oxidation]
    E1 --> F[Treated Effluent + Sludge]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G{By-Product Check}
    G -->|Pass| H[Discharge / Reuse]
    G -->|Fail| I[Additional Polishing Stage]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: pH-Controlled Chlorine Speciation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cl2 gas] -->|H2O| B[HOCl]
    B -->|pH 7.5 acidic| C[HOCl dominant]
    B -->|pH 7.5 alkaline| D[OCl- dominant]
    C --> E[Strong Disinfection]
    D --> F[Weak Disinfection]
    A -->|pH below 4| G[Cl2 degassing TOXIC]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Fenton Process Working Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[H2O2 Dosing] --> B[Fe2+ Catalyst]
    B --> C[OH Radical Generation]
    C --> D[Organic Pollutant Attack]
    D --> E[CO2 + H2O]
    C --> F[Fe3+ produced]
    F -->|H2O2| G[Fe2+ regenerated slow]
    F -->|pH above 4| H[Fe OH 3 Sludge]
    H --> I[Dewatering and Disposal]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:**
> - The first diagram (4.1) is a **decision-support flowchart** to help students solve design problems — identify pollutant first, then select oxidant.
> - The second diagram (4.2) shows the **pH-dependent chlorine chemistry** — critical for understanding why breakpoint chlorination is most effective near pH 7.
> - The third diagram (4.3) illustrates the **Fenton cycle**, including the Fe³⁺/Fe²⁺ regeneration and sludge formation pathway.

### 4.4 Comparative Performance Diagram (Radar Style — Textual)

For visual learners, the relative efficacy (1 = poor, 5 = excellent) of common oxidants against major wastewater parameters:

```
                  COD   NH3   Color  Fe/Mn  Pathogens  Micropoll.
   Cl₂            2     5     1      2      4          1
   ClO₂           2     3     3      4      4          2
   O₃             4     2     5      2      5          4
   H₂O₂ (alone)   1     1     2      1      2          1
   KMnO₄          2     1     3      5      3          2
   Fenton         5     1     5      2      3          5
   UV/H₂O₂        4     1     4      1      4          5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**

**Q1. List any three oxidizing agents used in advanced wastewater treatment and state the specific application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Ozone (O₃):** Used for color removal, micropollutant degradation, and disinfection; also breaks down recalcitrant aromatics via •OH radical pathway.
2. **Potassium Permanganate (KMnO₄):** Used for Fe²⁺/Mn²⁺ oxidation and color/odor control; produces MnO₂ which acts as a coagulant aid.
3. **Fenton's Reagent (Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂):** Used for high-COD industrial wastewater (>1000 mg/L) — generates •OH radicals to mineralize refractory organics.

*(1 mark each: name + correct application)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Why is chlorine dioxide (ClO₂) preferred over chlorine (Cl₂) in advanced wastewater treatment?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. ClO₂ does **not** react with ammonia to form chloramines and hence does not produce THMs (trihalomethanes) — major drawback of Cl₂.
2. ClO₂ is an effective disinfectant over a wide pH range (4–10) and is particularly effective against *Cryptosporidium* and *Giardia* cysts.
3. ClO₂'s oxidation by-products (ClO₂⁻, ClO₃⁻) are regulated but generally less harmful than THMs, and ClO₂ does not react with bromide to form bromate.

*(1 mark per valid point)*

---

### 📕 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> **Instructions for students:** Attempt **any ONE** of the two full questions. Each question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Chlorine and Fenton Focus*

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With neat reaction equations, explain **breakpoint chlorination**. Calculate the theoretical chlorine dose (kg/d) required to completely oxidize 25 kg/d of ammonia-nitrogen in a municipal STP. (7 marks)

**(b)** List the **advantages and limitations** of Fenton's process for industrial wastewater treatment. With a labelled sketch, describe the working of a **continuous Fenton reactor**. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark)**
Breakpoint chlorination is the addition of chlorine in sufficient quantity to fully oxidize ammonia nitrogen to nitrogen gas, beyond which free residual chlorine appears.

**Step 2 — Reaction sequence (3 marks)**

$$ \text{HOCl} + \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{NH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{...(1)} $$

$$ \text{HOCl} + \text{NH}_2\text{Cl} \rightarrow \text{NHCl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{...(2)} $$

$$ 2\text{NHCl}_2 + \text{HOCl} \rightarrow \text{N}_2\uparrow + 3\text{HCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{...(3)} $$

**Step 3 — Net reaction (1 mark)**

$$ 2\text{NH}_3 + 3\text{HOCl} \rightarrow \text{N}_2\uparrow + 3\text{HCl} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric dose calculation (2 marks)**

$$ \dfrac{\text{Cl}_2}{\text{NH}_3\text{-N}} = \dfrac{3 \times 71}{2 \times 14} = 7.6 $$

$$ \text{Theoretical Cl}_2 \text{ dose} = 25\,\text{kg/d} \times 7.6 = \mathbf{190\,kg/d} $$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Advantages of Fenton Process (3.5 marks):**

1. Reagents are cheap, readily available, and easy to handle.
2. Operates at ambient temperature and pressure.
3. Produces hydroxyl radicals (E° = 2.80 V) — non-selective, mineralizes a wide range of organics.
4. Adds iron sludge which can act as a coagulant.
5. Compact reactor; short reaction time (30–120 min).

**Limitations (2.5 marks):**

1. Strict pH window (2.5–3.5); requires acid/base dosing upstream and downstream.
2. Significant Fe(OH)₃ sludge generation → disposal cost.
3. Excess H₂O₂ residual can harm downstream biological treatment; quenching needed.
4. Cannot tolerate high alkalinity or phosphate (Fe³⁺ precipitation issues).
5. Limited to moderate COD loads; high-COD generates exothermic heat.

**Continuous Fenton Reactor (1 mark — sketch description):**
- Coagulation tank (pH adjustment to 3) → Fenton reactor (Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ injection, HRT 60 min) → Neutralization tank (lime/NaOH to pH 7–8) → Lamella clarifier (Fe(OH)₃ removal) → Polishing filter.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Ozone and Permanganate Focus*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **direct and indirect oxidation mechanisms of ozone** in wastewater treatment. List the key operating parameters that affect ozonation efficiency. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare the performance of **KMnO₄ and ClO₂** for Fe and Mn removal from groundwater. Support your answer with stoichiometric equations and a 14-mark structured discussion. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Direct Oxidation (Molecular O₃) (3 marks):**
- Selective electrophilic attack on specific functional groups — C=C double bonds, aromatic amines, phenols, sulfides, anilines.
- Slow kinetics for saturated aliphatic compounds.
- Example:
$$ \text{O}_3 + \text{R-CH=CH-R'} \rightarrow \text{R-COOH} + \text{R'-CHO} + \text{O}_2 $$

**Indirect Oxidation (•OH Radical Pathway) (2 marks):**
- At high pH, O₃ decomposes to •OH radicals via H₂O₂ intermediate:

$$ \text{O}_3 + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{HO}_2^- + \text{O}_2 $$

$$ \text{O}_3 + \text{HO}_2^- \rightarrow \bullet\text{OH} + \text{O}_2^{\bullet-} + \text{O}_2 $$

- Non-selective, reacts at near diffusion-controlled rates (k = 10⁸–10¹⁰ M⁻¹s⁻¹).
- Mineralizes organics to CO₂ and H₂O.

**Operating Parameters (2 marks):**
1. **Ozone dose** (mg O₃/L) — 5 to 50 mg/L depending on target.
2. **pH** — higher pH favours •OH pathway.
3. **Contact time & HRT** — typical 5–30 min.
4. **T temperature** — higher T reduces O₃ solubility.
5. **TRO₃ (transferred ozone) yield & gas–liquid mass transfer.**

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**KMnO₄ for Fe/Mn Removal (3 marks):**

$$ 3\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{MnO}_4^- + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 3\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{MnO}_2\downarrow + 4\text{OH}^- $$

$$ 2\text{Mn}^{2+} + 2\text{MnO}_4^- + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 5\text{MnO}_2\downarrow + 4\text{H}^+ $$

- Effective at pH 7–8 (no acid needed).
- Produces MnO₂ precipitate — itself oxidizes Fe²⁺/Mn²⁺ catalytically (autocatalytic).
- Practical dose: 1–4 mg/L.

**ClO₂ for Fe/Mn Removal (2.5 marks):**

$$ 5\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{ClO}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ \rightarrow 5\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{Cl}^- + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

$$ 2\text{Mn}^{2+} + 2\text{ClO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{MnO}_2\downarrow + \text{Cl}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ $$

- Effective over wide pH (4–10).
- By-product Cl⁻ is acceptable in drinking water.

**Comparison Table (1.5 marks):**

| Parameter | KMnO₄ | ClO₂ |
|---|---|---|
| State | Solid | Gas (on-site) |
| pH range | 7–8 optimal | 4–10 |
| By-product | MnO₂ sludge | Cl⁻ (harmless) |
| Cost (per kg Fe) | Lower | Higher |
| Equipment | Simple tank | Generator + dosing |
| THM formation | None | None |

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Wrong stoichiometry in breakpoint chlorination:** Many students write the net reaction as $2\text{NH}_3 + 4\text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + 8\text{HCl}$ — **WRONG**. The correct is via HOCl (3 mol HOCl for 2 mol NH₃).
> 2. **Forgetting the safety factor:** Field dose = theoretical × 1.2–1.5. Always mention this for full marks in design questions.
> 3. **Confusing pH optimum:** Fenton works at pH 2.5–3.5 (NOT 7). MnO₄⁻ works best at neutral pH (NOT acidic).
> 4. **Missing the catalyst role in Fenton:** Many students write H₂O₂ alone generates •OH — **NO**. Fe²⁺ catalyst is mandatory.
> 5. **Units slip-up:** When dose = mg/L × m³/d, the result is g/d, NOT kg/d. Convert carefully.
> 6. **Ozonation by-products:** If you recommend O₃, ALWAYS check for bromide; if present, mention bromate control (e.g., add H₂O₂, suppress pH).
> 7. **No "residual" oxidant → No residual disinfection:** This is a KTU favorite 2-mark question — Chlorine provides residual; Ozone does not.
> 8. **Standard potential values:** Examiner checks for correct E° values ± 0.05 V. Memorize: Fenton •OH = 2.80, O₃ = 2.07, H₂O₂ = 1.78, KMnO₄ = 1.51, Cl₂ = 1.36, ClO₂ = 0.95 V.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save This for Last-Night Prep!)**

- ✅ **Strongest practical oxidant** in wastewater is **•OH radical (E° = 2.80 V)** — generated in AOPs.
- ✅ **Breakpoint chlorination ratio** = **7.6 kg Cl₂ per kg NH₃-N** (theoretical); field uses 8–10.
- ✅ **HOCl : OCl⁻ speciation** is governed by pKₐ = 7.5; HOCl is the active biocide.
- ✅ **Fenton pH window** is **2.5–3.5**; outside this, Fe(OH)₃ precipitates or H⁺ scavenges •OH.
- ✅ **Typical Fenton H₂O₂ : COD** = 1.0–2.0 (mass) and **Fe²⁺ : H₂O₂** = 1 : 5 to 1 : 10.
- ✅ **Ozone is generated on-site** by corona discharge; does NOT leave residual; forms bromate if Br⁻ present.
- ✅ **KMnO₄** is preferred for Fe/Mn and color removal; byproduct is MnO₂ (acts as coagulant aid).
- ✅ **ClO₂** is unique — does NOT form THMs, effective over pH 4–10.
- ✅ **H₂O₂ alone is mild**; requires activation (Fe²⁺ / UV / O₃) for high redox activity.
- ✅ **Selection key**: match oxidant to **target pollutant**, **pH**, **by-product tolerance**, and **cost**.
- ✅ **Standard potentials (descending):** •OH (2.80) > O₃ (2.07) > H₂O₂ (1.78) > KMnO₄ (1.51) > Cl₂ (1.36) > ClO₂ (0.95).
- ✅ **THMs** = Cl₂ drawback; **Bromate** = O₃ drawback; **MnO₂ sludge** = KMnO₄ drawback; **Fe(OH)₃ sludge** = Fenton drawback.
- ✅ **Continuous Fenton sequence**: pH adjust → mix (Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂) → react (60 min) → neutralize → settle → polish.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ozone Based and Non-Ozone Based Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT725 - ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ozone Based and Non-Ozone Based Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs) – The Foundation

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)** are defined as a set of chemical treatment procedures designed to remove organic and inorganic materials in water and wastewater by oxidation through reactions with **hydroxyl radicals (•OH)**. As per KTU 2024 Module 4, these processes are categorized into **Ozone-Based Processes** and **Non-Ozone Based Processes**, depending on whether molecular ozone (O₃) is employed as the primary oxidant precursor or as a supplementary radical generator.

The **hydroxyl radical (•OH)** is the second strongest oxidizing agent known after fluorine, with a standard redox potential of:

$$E^0_{OH/H_2O} = 2.80 \, V \, \text{vs. NHE (at pH 0)}$$

$$E^0_{OH/OH^-} = 1.99 \, V \, \text{vs. NHE (at pH 14)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** The •OH radical is **non-selective**, reacting with most dissolved organics at near-diffusion-controlled rates of **10⁸ – 10¹⁰ M⁻¹ s⁻¹**, ultimately mineralizing them to **CO₂, H₂O, and inorganic salts**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy – "The Molecular Demolition Crew":**
> Think of a wastewater treatment plant as a city full of stubborn, hard-to-destroy buildings (recalcitrant organic pollutants like pesticides, pharmaceuticals, dyes). Conventional chlorine or biological treatment is like a manual laborer who can only dismantle bricks (simple organics). AOPs are like calling in a **specialized demolition crew carrying high-energy explosives (•OH radicals)** that can pulverize the toughest structures (complex aromatics, POPs) into rubble (CO₂ + H₂O).
> - **Ozone (O₃)** is the "explosives manufacturing unit" that produces these radicals.
> - **Non-Ozone Processes (UV, H₂O₂, Fenton)** are alternative "manufacturing units" producing the same demolition crew through different chemical routes.

### Classification as per KTU 2024 Module 4

| Category | Sub-Processes |
|----------|--------------|
| **Ozone-Based** | O₃ alone; O₃/H₂O₂ (Peroxone); O₃/UV; O₃ at elevated pH; O₃ with catalyst |
| **Non-Ozone Based** | Fenton (Fe²⁺/H₂O₂); Photo-Fenton; UV/H₂O₂; TiO₂/UV Photocatalysis; Wet Air Oxidation (WAO); Supercritical Water Oxidation (SCWO); Electrochemical Oxidation; Sonolysis (US) |

### Standard Performance Metrics

- **Electrical Energy per Order (Eₑₒ):** kWh m⁻³ order⁻¹ — energy required to degrade a contaminant by one order of magnitude.
- **Oxidation-Reduction Potential (ORP):** **+2.07 V** for O₃; **+2.80 V** for •OH.
- **Ozone Yield:** typically **5–12% by weight** from air-fed corona discharge; **12–18%** from oxygen-fed systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ozone-Based Processes

### 2.1.1 Ozone (O₃) Alone

**Chemistry:** In aqueous solution, ozone decomposes through a complex chain mechanism (Staehelin, Bühler, Hoigné). At low pH (< 4), direct molecular ozone reactions dominate; at high pH (> 9), indirect radical pathways dominate.

**Direct Reaction (Selective):**

$$O_3 + R \rightarrow R_{ox} \quad (k = 1 - 10^3 \, M^{-1}s^{-1})$$

**Indirect Radical Pathway (Non-selective):**

$$O_3 + OH^- \rightarrow HO_2^- + O_2$$

$$HO_2^- + O_3 \rightarrow HO_2^{\bullet} + O_3^{\bullet-}$$

$$O_3^{\bullet-} + H^+ \rightarrow HO_3^{\bullet} \rightarrow {}^{\bullet}OH + O_2$$

### 2.1.2 O₃ / H₂O₂ (Peroxone Process)

The addition of H₂O₂ accelerates ozone decomposition and radical formation:

$$H_2O_2 + 2O_3 \rightarrow 3O_2 + 2{}^{\bullet}OH$$

$$H_2O_2 + O_3 \rightarrow HO_2^- + O_2 + H^+$$

$$HO_2^- + O_3 \rightarrow HO_2^{\bullet} + O_3^{\bullet-}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimum Dose Ratio:** H₂O₂ : O₃ = **0.3 – 0.5 (mass basis)**. Excess H₂O₂ acts as a •OH scavenger.

### 2.1.3 O₃ / UV Process

UV photolysis of ozone produces hydrogen peroxide and hydroxyl radicals:

$$O_3 + h\nu + H_2O \rightarrow H_2O_2 + O_2$$

$$H_2O_2 + h\nu \rightarrow 2{}^{\bullet}OH$$

The **quantum yield (Φ)** for O₃ photolysis at 254 nm is approximately **0.48 mol Einstein⁻¹**.

### 2.1.4 O₃ / Catalyst (Heterogeneous Catalytic Ozonation)

Catalysts used: **Metal oxides (MnO₂, TiO₂, Fe₂O₃), zeolites, activated carbon, cerium oxide.**

Mechanism involves surface hydroxyl groups:

$$S-OH + O_3 \rightarrow S-O^{\bullet} + HO_3^{\bullet}$$

$$S-O^{\bullet} + H_2O \rightarrow S-OH + {}^{\bullet}OH$$

## 2.2 Non-Ozone Based Processes

### 2.2.1 Fenton Process (Fe²⁺ / H₂O₂)

Discovered by H.J.H. Fenton (1894). Operates under acidic conditions (**pH 2.8 – 3.5**).

**Core Reactions:**

$$Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 \rightarrow Fe^{3+} + {}^{\bullet}OH + OH^- \quad (k = 63 - 76 \, M^{-1}s^{-1})$$

$$Fe^{3+} + H_2O_2 \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + HO_2^{\bullet} + H^+ \quad (k = 0.001 - 0.01 \, M^{-1}s^{-1})$$

**Regeneration (Rate-Limiting Step):**

$$Fe^{3+} + HO_2^{\bullet} \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + O_2 + H^+$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Limitation:** Excess Fe²⁺ acts as a **scavenger**: $Fe^{2+} + {}^{\bullet}OH \rightarrow Fe^{3+} + OH^-$ (wastes radicals). Hence optimum [Fe²⁺]/[H₂O₂] ratio = **1 : 5 to 1 : 10**.

### 2.2.2 Photo-Fenton Process

UV/visible light accelerates Fe³⁺ reduction (the rate-limiting step), reducing iron sludge by **up to 90%**:

$$Fe(OH)^{2+} + h\nu \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + {}^{\bullet}OH \quad (\lambda < 580 \, nm)$$

### 2.2.3 UV / H₂O₂ Process

Direct photolysis of hydrogen peroxide at 254 nm:

$$H_2O_2 + h\nu \rightarrow 2{}^{\bullet}OH \quad (\Phi_{254nm} = 0.5 \, \text{mol Einstein}^{-1})$$

**Molar absorption coefficient:** $\varepsilon_{H_2O_2,254nm} = 18.6 \, M^{-1}cm^{-1}$ (low) — hence requires high H₂O₂ doses or long path lengths.

### 2.2.4 TiO₂ / UV Photocatalysis (Heterogeneous)

Uses semiconductor TiO₂ (anatase form, bandgap **E_g = 3.2 eV**, λ < 387 nm).

$$TiO_2 + h\nu \rightarrow e^-_{CB} + h^+_{VB}$$

$$h^+_{VB} + H_2O \rightarrow {}^{\bullet}OH + H^+$$

$$h^+_{VB} + OH^-_{surface} \rightarrow {}^{\bullet}OH$$

$$O_2 + e^-_{CB} \rightarrow O_2^{\bullet-}$$

### 2.2.5 Wet Air Oxidation (WAO)

Operates at **125–320 °C** and **0.5–20 MPa** with dissolved O₂.

General rate expression (pseudo-first-order):

$$-\frac{dC}{dt} = k_0 \cdot [C] \cdot [O_2]^n \quad (n = 0.5 - 1.0)$$

### 2.2.6 Supercritical Water Oxidation (SCWO)

Operates above the critical point of water (**T_c = 374 °C, P_c = 22.1 MPa**). Organics become completely miscible with O₂, leading to rapid, complete oxidation:

$$C_xH_yO_z + (x + y/4 - z/2) O_2 \rightarrow xCO_2 + (y/2) H_2O$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Process | Key Reaction | Operating Condition | Rate Constant / Yield | Limitation |
|---------|--------------|---------------------|------------------------|------------|
| O₃ alone | $O_3 \rightarrow {}^{\bullet}OH$ via OH⁻ | pH > 9 preferred | $k_{O_3} \approx 10^3 - 10^5 \, M^{-1}s^{-1}$ | Selective at low pH |
| O₃/H₂O₂ | $H_2O_2 + 2O_3 \rightarrow 3O_2 + 2{}^{\bullet}OH$ | pH 7–8 | Eₑₒ = 2–10 kWh m⁻³ | Excess H₂O₂ scavenges |
| O₃/UV | $O_3 + h\nu + H_2O \rightarrow H_2O_2 + O_2$ | 254 nm | Φ = 0.48 | High energy |
| Fenton | $Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 \rightarrow Fe^{3+} + {}^{\bullet}OH + OH^-$ | pH 2.8–3.5 | $k = 70 \, M^{-1}s^{-1}$ | Sludge generation |
| Photo-Fenton | $Fe(OH)^{2+} + h\nu \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + {}^{\bullet}OH$ | pH 2.5–3 | λ < 580 nm | Solar applicability |
| UV/H₂O₂ | $H_2O_2 + h\nu \rightarrow 2{}^{\bullet}OH$ | 254 nm | Φ = 0.5 | High H₂O₂ dose |
| TiO₂/UV | $TiO_2 + h\nu \rightarrow e^- + h^+$ | pH 3–11, anatase | E_g = 3.2 eV | Catalyst recovery |
| WAO | $R-O + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O$ | 125–320 °C, 0.5–20 MPa | Pseudo 1st order | Reactor corrosion |
| SCWO | Above supercritical point | >374 °C, >22.1 MPa | Complete mineralization | Capital cost |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical wastewater:** Fenton and Photo-Fenton used for CIP (ciprofloxacin), tetracycline removal (>95% TOC removal achievable).
- **Textile dye effluent:** Ozone-based systems decolorize >95% in <30 min.
- **Petrochemical:** WAO/SCWO for spent caustic and refinery sludges.
- **Endocrine disruptors & PFAS:** AOPs as polishing step post-MBR.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pseudo-First-Order Kinetics for UV/H₂O₂ System

The UV/H₂O₂ system is often the most commonly asked numerical problem in KTU. Let us derive the rate law from fundamentals.

### Step 1: Photolysis Rate of H₂O₂

The rate of •OH radical generation from H₂O₂ photolysis is given by:

$$R_{OH} = \Phi_{H_2O_2} \cdot I_0 \cdot \varepsilon_{H_2O_2} \cdot C_{H_2O_2} \cdot l \cdot U_\lambda$$

Where:
- $\Phi_{H_2O_2}$ = quantum yield = **0.5 mol Einstein⁻¹**
- $I_0$ = incident photon fluence rate (Einstein L⁻¹ s⁻¹)
- $\varepsilon_{H_2O_2}$ = molar absorptivity = **18.6 M⁻¹ cm⁻¹**
- $l$ = optical path length (cm)
- $U_\lambda$ = fraction of UV absorbed by H₂O₂ (dimensionless)

### Step 2: Mass Balance for Contaminant (C)

For a target pollutant C reacting with •OH:

$$\frac{d[C]}{dt} = -k_{OH,C} \cdot [{}^{\bullet}OH] \cdot [C]$$

Assuming **steady-state for •OH** ([•OH]_ss = constant):

$$\frac{d[C]}{dt} = -k_{app} \cdot [C]$$

Where $k_{app} = k_{OH,C} \cdot [{}^{\bullet}OH]_{ss}$

### Step 3: Integrated Form

Integrating from C₀ to C:

$$\int_{C_0}^{C_t} \frac{dC}{C} = -k_{app} \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{C_t}{C_0}\right) = -k_{app} \cdot t$$

$$C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-k_{app} \cdot t}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Demonstration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Pattern:** Given $k_{OH,C} = 5 \times 10^9 \, M^{-1}s^{-1}$, $[\bullet OH]_{ss} = 10^{-13} \, M$, $t = 600 \, s$. Calculate % removal.

**Solution:**

$$k_{app} = (5 \times 10^9) \times (10^{-13}) = 5 \times 10^{-3} \, s^{-1}$$

$$k_{app} \cdot t = (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 600 = 3.0$$

$$\frac{C_t}{C_0} = e^{-3.0} = 0.0498$$

$$\% \text{ Removal} = (1 - 0.0498) \times 100 = 95.02\%$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Eₑₒ (Electrical Energy per Order)

KTU frequently asks Eₑₒ for UV-based AOPs.

For a pseudo-first-order process:

$$\ln\left(\frac{C_0}{C}\right) = k_{app} \cdot t$$

The **Electrical Energy per Order** is defined as the energy required to degrade a pollutant by **one order of magnitude (90%)** in 1 m³ of water:

$$E_{EO} = \frac{P \cdot t \cdot 1000}{V \cdot \log(C_0/C)} \quad [\text{kWh m}^{-3} \text{ order}^{-1}]$$

For 90% removal ($\log(C_0/C) = 1$):

$$E_{EO} = \frac{P \cdot t \cdot 1000}{V} \quad [\text{kWh m}^{-3} \text{ order}^{-1}]$$

### Numerical Example

A 100 W UV lamp treats 10 L of wastewater containing atrazine. 90% removal is achieved in 5 minutes. Calculate Eₑₒ.

**Solution:**

$$E_{EO} = \frac{100 \, W \times (5 \times 60) \, s \times 1000}{10 \, L \times 1}$$

$$E_{EO} = \frac{100 \times 300 \times 1000}{10} = \frac{3 \times 10^7}{10} = 3 \times 10^6 \, J \, m^{-3}$$

Converting to kWh:

$$E_{EO} = \frac{3 \times 10^6}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 0.833 \, \text{kWh m}^{-3} \text{ order}^{-1}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Stoichiometric Ozone Dose for Peroxone

For a contaminant CₐHᵦOᵧ, complete mineralization requires:

$$C_aH_bO_c + (2a + b/2 - c) O_3 \rightarrow aCO_2 + (b/2) H_2O + (2a + b/2 - c) O_2$$

**Worked Example for Phenol (C₆H₅OH, a=6, b=6, c=1):**

$$2a + b/2 - c = 12 + 3 - 1 = 14$$

Hence 14 moles of O₃ are required per mole of phenol. Mass ratio:

$$\frac{M_{O_3}}{M_{phenol}} \times 14 = \frac{48}{94} \times 14 = 7.15 \, g \, O_3 / g \, \text{phenol}$$

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Ozone-Based | Non-Ozone (Fenton/UV-H₂O₂) | SCWO |
|-----------|-------------|------------------------------|------|
| Capital Cost | Moderate | Low-Moderate | Very High |
| Operating Cost | High (electricity for O₂ generator) | Moderate | High T&P |
| Selectivity | Selective at low pH | Non-selective | Non-selective |
| pH Range | 7–12 | 2.8–3.5 (Fenton), 5–9 (UV/H₂O₂) | Acidic |
| Mineralization | High | High (Photo-Fenton) | >99.99% |
| Sludge/Byproduct | Bromate (issue) | Iron sludge | Salts |
| Industrial Scale | Common | Common | Limited |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q: Define Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs) and explain the role of hydroxyl radical (•OH) in wastewater treatment.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs) are a class of oxidation-based treatment technologies that generate highly reactive **hydroxyl radicals (•OH)** as the primary oxidizing species. The hydroxyl radical has a redox potential of **+2.80 V (at pH 0)**, second only to fluorine. It reacts non-selectively with recalcitrant organic pollutants at **diffusion-controlled rates (10⁸ – 10¹⁰ M⁻¹s⁻¹)**, leading to complete mineralization into **CO₂, H₂O, and inorganic salts**. AOPs are particularly effective for organics resistant to conventional biological treatment, such as pharmaceuticals, pesticides, dyes, and endocrine-disrupting compounds.

---

### Question 2
**Q: Differentiate between direct and indirect reactions of ozone in aqueous solution.** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
**Direct Reaction:** Molecular ozone (O₃) directly attacks organic molecules. This reaction is **selective**, prefers unsaturated/aromatic compounds, and dominates at **low pH (< 4)**. Rate constants are typically **1 – 10³ M⁻¹s⁻¹**.

**Indirect Reaction:** Ozone decomposes to form •OH radicals, which then oxidize the organics. This reaction is **non-selective** and dominates at **high pH (> 9)**. Rate constants are very high, **10⁸ – 10¹⁰ M⁻¹s⁻¹**. The indirect pathway is more desirable for destroying recalcitrant compounds.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q: (a) Describe the Fenton Process in detail. Write the main chemical reactions involved and explain how pH influences the process.** `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### Model Solution (a):

The Fenton Process, discovered by H.J.H. Fenton in 1894, utilizes the catalytic decomposition of hydrogen peroxide by ferrous ions in acidic conditions.

**Main Chemical Reactions:**

$$Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 \rightarrow Fe^{3+} + {}^{\bullet}OH + OH^- \quad (k_1 = 63 - 76 \, M^{-1}s^{-1})$$

$$Fe^{3+} + H_2O_2 \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + HO_2^{\bullet} + H^+ \quad (k_2 = 0.001 - 0.01 \, M^{-1}s^{-1})$$

**Initiation step:**
$$H_2O_2 + {}^{\bullet}OH \rightarrow HO_2^{\bullet} + H_2O$$

**Regeneration of Fe²⁺ (rate-limiting):**
$$Fe^{3+} + HO_2^{\bullet} \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + O_2 + H^+$$

**Termination / Scavenging (undesirable):**
$$Fe^{2+} + {}^{\bullet}OH \rightarrow Fe^{3+} + OH^-$$

**Influence of pH:**

- **Optimum pH = 2.8 – 3.5:** Below pH 2, Fe²⁺ forms complexes and reacts slowly with H₂O₂. Above pH 4, Fe³⁺ precipitates as **Fe(OH)₃**, reducing catalytic activity.
- At pH > 4, the scavenging reaction (Fe²⁺ + •OH) dominates, wasting radicals.
- A high buffering capacity is essential because the Fenton reaction consumes protons.

---

**(b) Compare the Fenton, Photo-Fenton, and UV/H₂O₂ processes. Discuss the advantages of Photo-Fenton over conventional Fenton.** `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### Model Solution (b):

| Feature | Fenton | Photo-Fenton | UV/H₂O₂ |
|---------|--------|--------------|---------|
| Reagents | Fe²⁺, H₂O₂ | Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺, H₂O₂, UV-Vis | H₂O₂, UV (254 nm) |
| pH Range | 2.8–3.5 | 2.5–4.0 | 5–9 |
| Light Source | None | UV-Vis (λ < 580 nm) | LP-Hg lamp (254 nm) |
| Iron Sludge | High | Reduced ~90% | No iron |
| Mineralization | Good | Excellent | Moderate |

**Advantages of Photo-Fenton over Conventional Fenton:**

1. **Reduced Iron Sludge:** UV/visible light accelerates the photoreduction of Fe³⁺ to Fe²⁺:
$$Fe(OH)^{2+} + h\nu \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + {}^{\bullet}OH$$
This reduces the iron dose by 80–90%, drastically minimizing sludge disposal costs.

2. **Faster Reaction:** Rate constants improve 2-10 times due to additional •OH generation pathways.

3. **Solar Compatibility:** Solar Photo-Fenton (using λ up to 580 nm) reduces energy cost significantly.

4. **Higher Mineralization:** Carboxylic acid intermediates (often problematic in dark Fenton) are further oxidized under UV.

5. **Lower Fe Concentration:** Operable with [Fe] < 5 mg/L compared to 50–80 mg/L in dark Fenton.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**Q: (a) With neat reaction equations, explain the Peroxone (O₃/H₂O₂) process. Discuss the role of H₂O₂ in enhancing ozone decomposition.** `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`

#### Model Solution (a):

The **Peroxone Process** combines ozone with hydrogen peroxide to enhance the formation of •OH radicals.

**Initiation:**
$$H_2O_2 \rightleftharpoons HO_2^- + H^+$$

$$HO_2^- + O_3 \rightarrow HO_2^{\bullet} + O_3^{\bullet-}$$

**Radical Propagation:**
$$O_3^{\bullet-} + H^+ \rightarrow HO_3^{\bullet} \rightarrow {}^{\bullet}OH + O_2$$

$$HO_2^{\bullet} \rightleftharpoons O_2^{\bullet-} + H^+$$

$$O_2^{\bullet-} + O_3 \rightarrow O_3^{\bullet-} + O_2$$

**Overall Reaction:**
$$H_2O_2 + 2O_3 \rightarrow 3O_2 + 2{}^{\bullet}OH$$

**Role of H₂O₂:**

1. **Hydroperoxide ion (HO₂⁻) formation:** H₂O₂ acts as a source of HO₂⁻, the key initiator that reacts with O₃ at much faster rates (10⁶ M⁻¹s⁻¹) than OH⁻ alone.

2. **Chain propagation:** HO₂⁻ propagates the radical chain by converting O₃ into O₃•⁻ which rapidly yields •OH.

3. **Optimum Dosage:** H₂O₂ : O₃ mass ratio of **0.3 – 0.5** is optimal. Higher doses cause scavenging:
$${}^{\bullet}OH + H_2O_2 \rightarrow HO_2^{\bullet} + H_2O$$

**Advantages:**
- Better than O₃ alone for saturated organics, alcohols, and amines.
- Operates effectively at near-neutral pH (7–8), unlike alkaline ozonation.

---

**(b) Explain the operating principle of Supercritical Water Oxidation (SCWO). State its merits and demerits.** `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`

#### Model Solution (b):

**Operating Principle:**
SCWO operates at temperatures and pressures **above the critical point of water (T > 374 °C, P > 22.1 MPa)**. Above this point, water exists as a **single supercritical fluid** with unique properties:
- Low dielectric constant (like organic solvents) → organic compounds and oxygen become **completely miscible** with water.
- High diffusivity and low viscosity → mass transfer is excellent.
- High solubility of O₂ in the supercritical phase.

**Process Reactions:**

$$C_xH_yO_z + (x + y/4 - z/2) O_2 \rightarrow xCO_2 + (y/2) H_2O$$

**For Ammonia (a typical recalcitrant):**
$$NH_3 + 2O_2 \rightarrow HNO_3 + H_2O$$

**Merits:**
1. **>99.99% destruction efficiency** for hazardous organics.
2. **Short residence time** (< 1 minute) due to rapid kinetics.
3. **No off-gas emissions** (CO₂, H₂O, salts only).
4. **Compact reactor** footprint.
5. **Applicable to hazardous waste** including PCBs, TNT, nerve agents.

**Demerits:**
1. **Very high capital cost** — reactor must withstand high T and P with corrosion-resistant alloys (Inconel, titanium).
2. **Salt deposition/plugging** at cooler zones — requires specialized reactor design (transpiring wall, cooled reactor).
3. **High energy input** for heating and pressurization.
4. **Corrosion:** chloride and sulfur compounds accelerate corrosion.
5. **Safety concerns** — risk of reactor rupture.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "direct ozonation" with "indirect ozonation"** — Examiners deduct 2–3 marks if you mix up pH dependence. Memorize: **low pH → direct, high pH → indirect**.
> 2. **Forgetting stoichiometric ratios** in ozone dose calculations — always convert to mass basis (g O₃ / g pollutant).
> 3. **Writing Fenton reaction without specifying pH** — the pH range 2.8–3.5 must be mentioned explicitly.
> 4. **Not mentioning quantum yield (Φ) values** in UV/H₂O₂ — Examiner expects **Φ = 0.5**.
> 5. **Confusing Photo-Fenton with photocatalysis (TiO₂/UV)** — Photo-Fenton uses homogeneous iron catalyst; TiO₂ photocatalysis uses heterogeneous TiO₂.
> 6. **Skipping units** in Eₑₒ derivation — always specify kWh m⁻³ order⁻¹.
> 7. **In SCWO**, many students forget to mention the critical constants **T_c = 374 °C** and **P_c = 22.1 MPa**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Recall Bullet Summary

- **AOPs** are defined by •OH radical generation (E° = **+2.80 V**).
- **Classification:** Ozone-based (O₃, O₃/H₂O₂, O₃/UV, O₃/catalyst) vs. Non-Ozone (Fenton, Photo-Fenton, UV/H₂O₂, TiO₂/UV, WAO, SCWO).
- **Fenton optimum pH = 2.8–3.5**; Fenton rate constant = **70 M⁻¹s⁻¹**.
- **Photo-Fenton** reduces iron sludge by 90% via Fe(OH)²⁺ photoreduction.
- **Peroxone** mass ratio H₂O₂:O₃ = **0.3–0.5**.
- **UV/H₂O₂** quantum yield = **0.5 mol Einstein⁻¹**.
- **TiO₂** bandgap = **3.2 eV** (anatase, λ < 387 nm).
- **SCWO** operates above **T_c = 374 °C, P_c = 22.1 MPa**.
- **WAO** operates at **125–320 °C, 0.5–20 MPa**.
- **Eₑₒ** unit = **kWh m⁻³ order⁻¹**.
- **•OH scavenging** is undesirable — control scavenger (Fe²⁺, excess H₂O₂, carbonate, bicarbonate) doses.
- **Direct ozone reactions:** selective, low pH, low rate constants.
- **Indirect ozone reactions:** non-selective, high pH, very high rate constants.
- **Stoichiometric ozone dose** for mineralization follows the formula $(2a + b/2 - c)$ per mole of CₐHᵦOᵧ.
- **Bromate (BrO₃⁻) formation** is a critical drawback of ozonation of bromide-containing water.
- **SCWO** destruction efficiency **>99.99%**; WAO typically achieves **75–95%** TOC removal.
- **Fenton sludge (Fe(OH)₃)** requires additional handling cost — Photo-Fenton mitigates this.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always structure answers as: **(i) Principle, (ii) Reactions with stoichiometry, (iii) Operating parameters (pH, T, P, dose), (iv) Merits & Demerits, (v) Applications**. Examiners allocate marks proportionally — missing any one section costs 2–3 marks.

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fenton Oxidation** is an Advanced Oxidation Process (AOP) that employs **Fenton's reagent** — a homogeneous catalytic mixture of **ferrous ions (Fe²⁺)** and **hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂)** — operating under strongly acidic conditions (typically **pH = 2.8 – 3.5**) to generate highly reactive, non-selective **hydroxyl radicals (•OH)** as the primary oxidizing species. The standard potential of •OH is **E° = +2.80 V (vs. NHE)**, making it the second strongest oxidant after fluorine.

**Photo-Fenton Oxidation** is the enhanced version in which the Fenton system is irradiated with **UV (λ < 400 nm)** or **solar visible light**, promoting additional pathways that reduce Fe³⁺ back to Fe²⁺ (photoreduction) and photolyze Fe³⁺-hydroxo complexes, thereby accelerating the catalytic cycle and •OH production.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Fenton and Photo-Fenton are categorized under **chemical Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)** in Module 4. They are commonly asked either as a standalone 14-mark Part B question or as a 7-mark sub-part of a broader AOP question.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a locked kitchen drawer full of stubborn, oily, hard-to-clean junk (the **refractory organic pollutants** like phenols, dyes, pesticides, and pharmaceuticals). 

- **Normal soap and water (conventional treatment)** cannot reach inside the drawer to break down the grime.
- **Fenton's Reagent** is like pouring a powerful **acidic cleaning foam (H₂O₂)** into the drawer along with a small amount of **iron filings (Fe²⁺)**. The moment these two meet, they release millions of tiny, hyperactive "molecular Pac-Man" — the **hydroxyl radicals (•OH)** — that chew up the grime into harmless CO₂, H₂O, and mineral salts.
- **Photo-Fenton** adds a **bright UV lamp** shining into the drawer, which recharges the iron filings instantly (Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺), making the Pac-Man army regenerate faster and clean the drawer far more efficiently and at lower iron doses.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Symbol / Value |
|---|---|
| Standard redox potential of •OH | **E° = +2.80 V (vs. NHE)** |
| Second-order rate constant (Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂) | **k₁ ≈ 63 – 76 L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹** |
| Rate constant (Fe³⁺ + H₂O₂) | **k₂ ≈ 0.001 – 0.01 L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹** |
| Optimum pH | **2.8 – 3.5** |
| Optimum temperature | **25 – 40 °C** |
| Typical H₂O₂ : Fe²⁺ molar ratio | **10 : 1 to 50 : 1** |

> [!NOTE]
> The radical •OH is **non-selective** — it attacks virtually any organic molecule at near-diffusion-controlled rates (10⁸ – 10¹⁰ L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹), which is both its **strength** (broad-spectrum oxidation) and its **limitation** (scavenging by background matrix).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Haber–Weiss Mechanistic Chain

The Fenton reaction proceeds via a **radical chain mechanism** originally proposed by Haber and Weiss (1934). The core "engine" of the process is the redox cycling between Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺.

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Initiation (Catalytic Step):** Fe²⁺ reacts with H₂O₂ to produce the first •OH radical and oxidizes Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺. This is the rate-limiting generation step.
2. **Propagation:** The newly formed •OH attacks dissolved organic matter (RH), abstracting a hydrogen atom to form a carbon-centered radical (R•), which rapidly reacts with dissolved O₂ to form peroxyl radicals (ROO•), propagating the chain.
3. **Catalytic Regeneration (Slow):** Fe³⁺ can be slowly reduced back to Fe²⁺ by another molecule of H₂O₂, regenerating the catalyst. This step is **10⁴ – 10⁵ times slower** than Step 1, creating a kinetic bottleneck.
4. **Termination:** Two radicals combine (e.g., •OH + •OH → H₂O₂) or radicals are scavenged by excess H₂O₂ itself, terminating the chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Kinetic Bottleneck (Key Insight for Examiners):** Because the regeneration of Fe²⁺ from Fe³⁺ (Step 3) is much slower than its consumption (Step 1), **Fe²⁺ accumulates as Fe³⁺** within minutes, and •OH generation effectively halts unless additional energy (UV light) or a chemical reductant is supplied. **This is exactly why Photo-Fenton exists.**

## 2.2 Photo-Fenton Enhancement Mechanism

When UV/solar photons are introduced, two additional pathways are activated:

- **Photolysis of Fe³⁺-aquo/hydroxo complexes:** Fe(OH)²⁺ absorbs light strongly at λ ≈ 300 nm and undergoes ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT), yielding an extra •OH and regenerating Fe²⁺ in one step.
- **Photolysis of H₂O₂:** Direct photolytic cleavage of H₂O₂ also yields 2 •OH per photon (low quantum yield ~ 0.5 at 254 nm).

This shifts the **rate-limiting step** from Step 3 (Fe³⁺ reduction) to a much faster photochemical pathway, **increasing the overall rate of •OH generation by 3–10 times** under solar irradiation compared to dark Fenton.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fenton initiation | $$\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{\textbullet OH} + \text{OH}^-$$ | k₁ ≈ 70 L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹ |
| 2 | Fe³⁺ reduction (dark) | $$\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{\textbullet OOH} + \text{H}^+$$ | k₂ ≈ 0.002 L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹ |
| 3 | Photo-reduction | $$\text{Fe(OH)}^{2+} + h\nu \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{\textbullet OH}$$ | λ < 400 nm |
| 4 | H₂O₂ photolysis | $$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow 2\,\text{\textbullet OH}$$ | Quantum yield Φ ≈ 0.5 |
| 5 | Pseudo-1st-order kinetics | $$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_t}{C_0}\right) = -k_{\text{obs}}\, t$$ | k_obs in min⁻¹ |
| 6 | Stoichiometric H₂O₂ demand | $$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \text{ (g)} = 0.03125 \times \text{COD (mg/L)} \times V \text{ (L)}$$ | Based on ½O₂ : 1 H₂O₂ |
| 7 | Optimum molar ratio | $$R = \frac{[\text{H}_2\text{O}_2]_0}{[\text{Fe}^{2+}]_0} = 10 - 50$$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Fe(OH)₃ precipitation boundary | $$\text{Fe}^{3+} + 3\,\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_3 \downarrow$$ | Occurs above pH ≈ 4 |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fenton and Photo-Fenton are widely deployed as:
- **Pre-treatment** for industrial wastewater (pharma, textile, pesticide, landfill leachate) before biological treatment, to break recalcitrant compounds into biodegradable fragments (**BOD₅/COD ratio improvement**).
- **Post-treatment (polishing step)** after biological treatment to meet stringent discharge COD limits.
- **Soil and groundwater remediation** via in-situ chemical oxidation (ISCO) using injected Fenton's reagent.
- **Solar Photo-Fenton** in raceway or compound parabolic collector (CPC) reactors in regions with high solar irradiance — Kerala's tropical climate makes this highly relevant for decentralized industrial effluent plants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pseudo-First-Order Rate Law for COD Removal

In a well-buffered Fenton batch reactor at fixed [Fe²⁺]₀ and [H₂O₂]₀, the concentration of generated •OH rapidly reaches a quasi-steady state. The consumption of organic substrate R (measured as COD) follows:

**Step 1 — Write the elementary rate of •OH attack on organics:**

$$\frac{d[\text{R}]}{dt} = -k_{\text{\textbullet OH},R}\,[\text{\textbullet OH}]\,[\text{R}]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Quasi-Steady-State Approximation (QSSA) on •OH:**

Because •OH is extremely reactive (lifetime ≈ 10⁻⁹ s in water), its concentration is essentially constant:

$$\frac{d[\text{\textbullet OH}]}{dt} \approx 0$$

**Step 3 — Equate generation to consumption of •OH:**

The dominant generation channel is the Fenton initiation step:

$$\text{Generation} = k_1 [\text{Fe}^{2+}][\text{H}_2\text{O}_2]$$

$$= k_{\text{\textbullet OH},R}[\text{\textbullet OH}]_{\text{ss}}[\text{R}] + k_s [\text{\textbullet OH}]_{\text{ss}}[\text{S}]$$

where [S] represents scavengers (Cl⁻, HCO₃⁻, excess H₂O₂). Solving for [•OH]ₛₛ:

$$[\text{\textbullet OH}]_{\text{ss}} = \frac{k_1 [\text{Fe}^{2+}][\text{H}_2\text{O}_2]}{k_{\text{\textbullet OH},R}[\text{R}] + k_s [\text{S}]}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back and recognize that when [Fe²⁺]₀ and [H₂O₂]₀ are constant, [•OH]ₛₛ ≈ constant = K:**

$$\frac{d[\text{R}]}{dt} = -K\,[\text{R}]$$

**Step 5 — Integrate with [R]₀ at t = 0:**

$$\int_{[\text{R}]_0}^{[\text{R}]} \frac{d[\text{R}]}{[\text{R}]} = -\int_0^{t} K\,dt$$

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{[\text{R}]_t}{[\text{R}]_0}\right) = -k_{\text{obs}}\, t\;}$$

where k_obs = K = k_{•OH,R} · [•OH]ₛₛ is the observed pseudo-first-order rate constant (units: min⁻¹).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A textile wastewater has initial COD₀ = 1000 mg/L. A Fenton process with [Fe²⁺] = 50 mg/L and [H₂O₂] = 500 mg/L is applied at pH 3.0. The pseudo-first-order rate constant was determined experimentally as k_obs = 0.08 min⁻¹. Find the COD after 30 minutes and the % removal.

**Step 1 — Apply pseudo-first-order equation:**

$$[\text{COD}]_t = [\text{COD}]_0 \cdot e^{-k_{\text{obs}}\, t}$$

$$[\text{COD}]_{30} = 1000 \times e^{-(0.08)(30)}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the exponent:**

$$(0.08)(30) = 2.4$$

$$[\text{COD}]_{30} = 1000 \times e^{-2.4}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation (e⁻²·⁴ ≈ 0.0907):**

$$[\text{COD}]_{30} = 1000 \times 0.0907 = 90.7 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 4 — Compute % removal:**

$$\%\text{Removal} = \frac{1000 - 90.7}{1000} \times 100 = 90.93\%$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check stoichiometric H₂O₂ demand:**

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \text{ required} = 0.03125 \times (1000 - 90.7) \times 1 = 28.4 \text{ mg/L theoretical}$$

Since 500 mg/L was dosed (excess), residual H₂O₂ must be quenched (e.g., Na₂S₂O₃ or MnO₂) before discharge.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Dark Fenton vs. Photo-Fenton

| Parameter | Dark Fenton | Photo-Fenton |
|---|---|---|
| Energy input | None (chemical only) | UV lamp or solar |
| Rate-limiting step | Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺ regeneration | Shifted to photoreduction |
| Optimum pH | 2.8 – 3.5 | 2.8 – 3.5 (can extend to ~5 with Fe³⁺-ligand) |
| Fe²⁺ dose required | Higher (50 – 200 mg/L) | Lower (5 – 30 mg/L) |
| H₂O₂ consumption | Higher | 30 – 60 % lower |
| Reaction time | 30 – 120 min | 10 – 45 min |
| Operational cost | Lower CAPEX, higher reagent | Higher CAPEX, lower operating cost |
| Solar compatibility | No | Yes (Kerala, India) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fenton Process Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Wastewater Influent] --> B[Equalization Tank pH Adjustment to 2.8 - 3.5]
    B --> C[Acid Dosing H2SO4 Tank]
    B --> D[Fe2+ Dosing FeSO4 Solution]
    B --> E[H2O2 Dosing 30 - 50 Percent w/v]
    D --> F[Fenton Reactor Stirred Tank Reactor]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Radical Generation •OH Production]
    G --> H[Oxidation of Organics CO2 plus H2O plus Salts]
    H --> I[Neutralization Tank pH 7 to 8 NaOH]
    I --> J[Coagulation Flocculation Fe OH 3 Sludge]
    J --> K[Sedimentation Clarifier]
    K --> L[Treated Effluent Discharge]
    J --> M[Sludge Handling Filter Press]

    F --> N[Photo Reactor Option UV Lamp or Solar CPC]
    N --> O[Enhanced Fe3 plus to Fe2 plus Regeneration]
    O --> G
```

## 4.2 Reaction Cycle & Mechanism Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Fe2 plus] -- Step 1 k1 equals 70 --> B1[Fe3 plus]
    B1 -- Step 2 k2 equals 0.002 dark slow --> A1
    B1 -- Step 3 Photo h nu less than 400 nm --> A1
    A1 -- Step 4 H2O2 --> C1[•OH Radical]
    C1 -- Step 5 Attack RH --> D1[R• Carbon Radical]
    D1 -- Step 6 O2 --> E1[ROO• Peroxyl Radical]
    E1 -- Step 7 Chain Propagation --> C1
    C1 -- Step 8 Termination •OH plus •OH --> F1[H2O2]
    C1 -- Step 9 Attack Organics --> G1[CO2 plus H2O]
```

## 4.3 Operational Sequence & Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Characterization COD BOD pH TSS] --> S2{High COD greater than 500}
    S2 -- No --> S3[Use Biological Treatment]
    S2 -- Yes --> S4{Refractory Compounds Present}
    S4 -- No --> S5[Use Conventional Coagulation]
    S4 -- Yes --> S6{High Solar Irradiance Available}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Choose Solar Photo Fenton]
    S6 -- No --> S8[Choose Dark Fenton plus UV Lamp]
    S7 --> S9[Optimize pH 3 Fe2 plus H2O2 Ratio]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Run Jar Tests then Pilot]
    S10 --> S11[Full Scale Implementation]
```
