# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Fenton's reagent. Write the reaction for the generation of hydroxyl radicals from Fenton's reagent.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Fenton's reagent is a homogeneous mixture of **ferrous sulphate (FeSO₄)** as a source of **Fe²⁺ ions** and **hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂)** in acidic medium (pH 2.8 – 3.5).

**Reaction (2 marks):** When Fe²⁺ reacts with H₂O₂, it generates the hydroxyl radical (•OH):

$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{\textbullet OH} + \text{OH}^-$$

The hydroxyl radical (E° = +2.80 V) is a powerful, non-selective oxidant that mineralizes refractory organics into CO₂ and H₂O.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is the optimum pH for the Fenton process around 3? What happens if pH is above 5 or below 2?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Optimum pH ≈ 3 (1 mark):** At pH 2.8 – 3.5, Fe²⁺ remains soluble, the Fe³⁺-catalyzed decomposition of H₂O₂ to •OH is maximized, and scavenging by •OH self-quenching is minimized.

**Above pH 5 (1 mark):** Fe³⁺ precipitates as ferric hydroxide Fe(OH)₃ sludge, removing the catalyst from solution and stopping •OH production. The reaction rate drops drastically.

**Below pH 2 (1 mark):** Excess H⁺ ions scavenge •OH (•OH + H⁺ + e⁻ → H₂O), and H₂O₂ becomes stabilized as the oxonium ion (H₃O₂⁺), reducing its reactivity with Fe²⁺. Hence the process is inhibited.

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(a) Explain with a neat reaction mechanism the chemistry of Fenton and Photo-Fenton processes for wastewater treatment. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Fenton Process (3 marks):**
The Fenton process is based on the **Haber-Weiss mechanism**, where Fe²⁺ catalyzes the decomposition of H₂O₂ into hydroxyl radicals (•OH).

**Initiation (Fenton reaction):**

$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{\textbullet OH} + \text{OH}^- \quad (k_1 \approx 70 \text{ L mol}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1})$$

**Propagation (•OH attacks organics):**

$$\text{\textbullet OH} + \text{RH} \rightarrow \text{R\textbullet} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{R\textbullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{ROO\textbullet} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Catalytic regeneration (slow):**

$$\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{\textbullet OOH} + \text{H}^+$$

The rate-limiting bottleneck is Fe²⁺ regeneration from Fe³⁺ because the rate constant (k₂ ≈ 0.001 L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹) is roughly 4–5 orders of magnitude smaller than k₁.

**Photo-Fenton Process (4 marks):**
When UV/solar irradiation is applied, additional •OH-generating pathways become available:

**Photo-reduction of Fe³⁺:**

$$\text{Fe(OH)}^{2+} + h\nu \, (\lambda < 400 \text{ nm}) \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{\textbullet OH}$$

**Photolysis of H₂O₂:**

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow 2\,\text{\textbullet OH}$$

**Net benefits of Photo-Fenton:**
- Faster Fe²⁺ regeneration (overcomes rate-limiting step).
- Lower Fe²⁺ dose requirement (5–30 mg/L vs. 50–200 mg/L for dark Fenton).
- Lower H₂O₂ consumption (30–60 % reduction).
- Higher overall COD removal (typically 85 – 95 %).
- Compatibility with solar CPC reactors for sustainable operation.

---

**(b) Discuss the influence of operating parameters on Fenton process efficiency. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) pH (2 marks):**
Optimum pH = 2.8 – 3.5. Below pH 2, H⁺ scavenges •OH and H₂O₂ stabilization occurs. Above pH 4, Fe(OH)₃ precipitation removes the catalyst. Performance follows a bell-shaped curve with peak at pH 3.

**(ii) H₂O₂ dose (2 marks):**
Increasing [H₂O₂] enhances •OH generation up to a point. Excess H₂O₂ acts as a •OH scavenger:

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + \text{\textbullet OH} \rightarrow \text{\textbullet OOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Hence optimum H₂O₂:Fe²⁺ molar ratio is 10:1 – 50:1.

**(iii) Fe²⁺ catalyst dose (1 mark):**
Higher Fe²⁺ accelerates •OH production, but excess Fe²⁺ causes brown color in effluent and increases sludge. Optimum dose is determined by jar tests.

**(iv) Temperature (1 mark):**
Higher temperature increases reaction rate (Arrhenius behavior), but excessive heating (> 50 °C) accelerates H₂O₂ decomposition to O₂ + H₂O without •OH formation. Optimum: 25 – 40 °C.

**(v) Reaction time and matrix effects (1 mark):**
Contact time of 30 – 120 min is typical. Background Cl⁻, HCO₃⁻, and NOM scavenge •OH and must be accounted for in dose optimization.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) Compare dark Fenton and solar Photo-Fenton processes. Discuss their merits, demerits, and reactor configurations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Dark Fenton | Solar Photo-Fenton |
|---|---|---|
| Energy input | None | Solar / UV |
| Optimum pH | 2.8 – 3.5 | 2.8 – 3.5 (extendable with Fe³⁺ ligands) |
| Fe²⁺ dose | 50 – 200 mg/L | 5 – 30 mg/L |
| H₂O₂ consumption | High | 30 – 60 % lower |
| Reaction time | 30 – 120 min | 10 – 45 min |
| CAPEX | Low | High (CPC reactors) |
| OPEX | High reagent | Low (free solar energy) |
| COD removal | 60 – 80 % | 85 – 95 % |
| Mineralization | Partial | Near-complete |

**Merits of Solar Photo-Fenton (2 marks):**
1. Uses free, renewable solar energy → drastically reduces operating cost.
2. Faster kinetics due to photoreduction of Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺.
3. Lower reagent consumption and sludge production.
4. Suitable for tropical regions with high insolation (highly applicable to Kerala).

**Demerits (2 marks):**
1. Higher initial CAPEX for CPC/raceway reactors.
2. Requires post-treatment (neutralization, Fe(OH)₃ removal).
3. Diurnal variability of solar energy (requires H₂O₂ feed modulation).
4. Reactor land footprint can be significant.

**Reactor Configurations (2 marks):**
- **Raceway pond reactors:** Shallow, mechanically stirred ponds exposing large surface area to sunlight. Low cost, suitable for rural plants.
- **Compound Parabolic Collectors (CPC):** Tubular reactors with parabolic reflectors concentrating diffuse UV/visible light. Higher photonic efficiency, ideal for industrial effluents.
- **Falling-film / thin-film reactors:** Used for higher concentrations of recalcitrants.

---

**(b) A pharmaceutical industry effluent has COD = 2500 mg/L. Design the Fenton reagent dose and predict the COD after 45 minutes using k_obs = 0.06 min⁻¹. Also compute the theoretical stoichiometric H₂O₂ requirement. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Optimum dose selection from standard practice (1 mark):**
Adopt molar ratio H₂O₂ : Fe²⁺ = 20 : 1.

$$[\text{H}_2\text{O}_2]_0 = 20 \times [\text{Fe}^{2+}]_0$$

For high-COD industrial wastewater, typical Fe²⁺ dose = 100 mg/L.

$$[\text{Fe}^{2+}]_0 = 100 \text{ mg/L}, \quad [\text{H}_2\text{O}_2]_0 = 20 \times 100 = 2000 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 2 — Apply pseudo-first-order equation (3 marks):**

$$[\text{COD}]_t = [\text{COD}]_0 \times e^{-k_{\text{obs}}\,t}$$

$$[\text{COD}]_{45} = 2500 \times e^{-(0.06)(45)}$$

$$(0.06)(45) = 2.7$$

$$[\text{COD}]_{45} = 2500 \times e^{-2.7} = 2500 \times 0.0672 = 168.0 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3 — Compute % COD removal (1 mark):**

$$\%\text{Removal} = \frac{2500 - 168}{2500} \times 100 = 93.3\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute stoichiometric H₂O₂ for COD removed (2 marks):**
Each mole of O₂ (32 g) is replaced stoichiometrically by 1 mole of H₂O₂ (34 g) for COD oxidation. Therefore:

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \text{ (mg/L)} = \frac{34}{32} \times \text{COD removed (mg/L)} \times \frac{1}{2}$$

$$= 0.53125 \times 2332 = 1238.8 \text{ mg/L theoretical}$$

Since 2000 mg/L was added, **excess H₂O₂ = 761.2 mg/L** must be catalytically destroyed before discharge (using MnO₂ or UV/H₂O₂ polishing).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Fenton / Photo-Fenton Questions):**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems — always write k_obs in min⁻¹ and t in the same unit (min).
> 2. **Confusing Fe²⁺ with Fe³⁺** in the regeneration step. Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺ is the *catalyst regeneration*, not the catalyst itself.
> 3. **Not stating the optimum pH range** — many students give a single value (3) instead of a range (2.8–3.5). Examiners deduct marks for this imprecision.
> 4. **Skipping the rate constants** k₁ ≈ 70 and k₂ ≈ 0.002 — quoting them shows depth and fetches full marks in mechanism questions.
> 5. **Ignoring residual H₂O₂** in numerical design problems. Always compute the excess and recommend quenching — this is a frequently asked follow-up.
> 6. **Writing Photo-Fenton without photon wavelength** — examiners expect "λ < 400 nm" or "UV/visible" qualifier.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Fenton reagent = Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ in acidic medium (pH 2.8 – 3.5).**
- ✅ **Primary oxidant: hydroxyl radical (•OH), E° = +2.80 V.**
- ✅ **Initiation:** Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ → Fe³⁺ + •OH + OH⁻ (k₁ ≈ 70 L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹).
- ✅ **Rate-limiting step:** Fe³⁺ + H₂O₂ → Fe²⁺ + •OOH + H⁺ (k₂ ≈ 0.002).
- ✅ **Photo-Fenton adds UV/solar light** to overcome the Fe²⁺ regeneration bottleneck via photoreduction of Fe(OH)²⁺ and H₂O₂ photolysis.
- ✅ **Optimum operating envelope:** pH 2.8 – 3.5, temperature 25 – 40 °C, H₂O₂/Fe²⁺ molar ratio 10–50.
- ✅ **Pseudo-first-order kinetics:** ln(C_t/C_0) = −k_obs · t.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric H₂O₂:** 0.03125 × COD (mg/L) per liter for 100 % removal.
- ✅ **Above pH 4:** Fe(OH)₃ precipitates → catalyst loss.
- ✅ **Below pH 2:** •OH scavenging by H⁺ → inhibition.
- ✅ **Applications:** Pre-treatment of refractory industrial wastewater (pharma, textile, landfill leachate), soil/groundwater remediation (ISCO), polishing step post biological treatment.
- ✅ **Solar Photo-Fenton (CPC, raceway pond)** is highly suitable for Kerala's tropical climate.
- ✅ **Residual H₂O₂ must be quenched** (MnO₂, Na₂S₂O₃) before discharge.
- ✅ **COD removal efficiencies:** Dark Fenton 60 – 80 %; Photo-Fenton 85 – 95 %.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Solar Photocatalytic Treatment** is an **Advanced Oxidation Process (AOP)** that harnesses solar photons to activate a semiconductor catalyst (typically **TiO₂**), generating highly reactive transitory species — primarily the **hydroxyl radical (•OH)**, superoxide radical (O₂•⁻), and photogenerated holes (h⁺) — which non-selectively mineralize recalcitrant organic pollutants, pathogens, and emerging micropollutants in wastewater into CO₂, H₂O, and innocuous mineral acids.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framing, this technology lies at the intersection of **renewable-energy-driven water treatment** and **heterogeneous photocatalysis**, classified under solar AOPs alongside photo-Fenton, UV/H₂O₂, and UV/O₃ systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Solar Photo Catalytic Treatment Systems encompass photocatalyst chemistry (TiO₂, ZnO, doped/composite semiconductors), reaction mechanism (e⁻/h⁺ pair dynamics), solar reactor design (CPC, PTC, slurry, immobilized), and kinetic modeling using the **Langmuir–Hinshelwood** framework.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sun-Powered Detergent"

Imagine a stubborn grease stain on your kitchen counter. An ordinary detergent (conventional oxidants like chlorine) only dissolves a thin top layer. Now imagine a *sun-charged, nano-sized cleaning crew* that, upon receiving sunlight, splits into aggressive "scrubber radicals" (•OH) that attack **every carbon-hydrogen bond** indiscriminately until the stain disappears completely — even converting the grime into harmless CO₂ and water vapor. That is exactly what a TiO₂ nanoparticle does under solar UV: sunlight charges it, and the resulting hydroxyl radicals demolish complex organics that resist biological treatment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Differentiator:** Unlike chlorination (selective, leaves by-products) or biological treatment (slow, fails on recalcitrant compounds), solar photocatalysis achieves **complete mineralization** at ambient temperature using a **renewable, zero-carbon energy input**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are indispensable for KTU numerical problems:

| Parameter | Symbol | Value / Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of light | c | **3.00 × 10⁸** | m·s⁻¹ |
| Planck's constant | h | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴** | J·s |
| Avogadro's number | N_A | **6.022 × 10²³** | mol⁻¹ |
| TiO₂ band-gap (anatase) | E_g | **3.20** | eV |
| TiO₂ band-gap (rutile) | E_g | **3.02** | eV |
| Threshold wavelength (anatase) | λ_max | **≤ 387** | nm |
| Solar constant (AM 1.5) | G_sc | **1361** | W·m⁻² |
| UV fraction in terrestrial sunlight | — | **3 – 5** | % |
| Hydroxyl radical standard potential | E⁰(•OH/H₂O) | **+2.80** | V vs NHE |
| Quantum yield (TiO₂/Degussa P25) | Φ | 0.01 – 0.10 | — |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Why and How

### Step 1 — Photon Absorption (Energy Threshold Exceedance)
A semiconductor photocatalyst is characterized by a **valence band (VB)** filled with electrons and an **empty conduction band (CB)**, separated by a forbidden energy gap (band-gap, E_g). A solar photon is absorbed **only if** its energy (E_p = hν) is **greater than or equal to E_g**.

> **Why it matters:** Solar UV-B (280–315 nm) and UV-A (315–400 nm) carry enough energy; visible photons (≥ 400 nm) generally do *not* activate pristine anatase TiO₂ — this is the central limitation that drives **doping and visible-light catalyst** research.

### Step 2 — Electron–Hole Pair Generation
Upon absorption, an electron is promoted from VB → CB, leaving behind a positively charged **hole (h⁺)**. The pair (e⁻_CB / h⁺_VB) is the engine of all subsequent chemistry.

> **How:** The excited electron is a powerful reductant (E⁰ ≈ −0.5 V vs NHE for TiO₂), while the hole is a powerful oxidant (E⁰ ≈ +2.7 V vs NHE).

### Step 3 — Charge Separation vs Recombination
The e⁻/h⁺ pair has a lifetime of **nanoseconds to microseconds**. Competing fates:
- **Migration to catalyst surface** → drives productive redox reactions
- **Bulk/surface recombination** → releases heat; unproductive (this is the main efficiency loss)

### Step 4 — Surface Redox Reactions (Generation of •OH)
- **Valence-band hole path:** h⁺ + H₂O → **•OH + H⁺**
- **CB electron path:** e⁻ + O₂ → **O₂•⁻ → •OOH → H₂O₂ → •OH**
- **Direct oxidation:** h⁺ + Pollutant → Pollutant•⁺ → mineralized fragments

### Step 5 — Mineralization (Non-selective Attack)
•OH radicals (E⁰ = +2.80 V, second only to fluorine) attack organic molecules by:
- **Hydrogen abstraction** (aliphatics)
- **Electrophilic addition** (aromatics → dihydroxylated rings)
- **Electron transfer** (electron-rich species)

The end-products are **CO₂, H₂O, NO₃⁻, PO₄³⁻, halide ions** — total mineralization.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | E_g = h·c ÷ λ | Band-gap energy, Planck const., speed of light, wavelength | J or eV | Threshold λ for activation |
| 2 | λ_threshold (nm) = 1240 ÷ E_g (eV) | — | nm | Quick check: TiO₂ (3.2 eV) → 387 nm |
| 3 | r = k·K·C ÷ (1 + K·C) | Langmuir–Hinshelwood rate; k = rate const., K = ads. const., C = substrate conc. | mg·L⁻¹·min⁻¹ | General photocatalytic kinetics |
| 4 | ln(C₀ ÷ C) + K·(C₀ − C) = k·K·t | Integrated L-H form (closed reactor) | — | Full-range numerical |
| 5 | ln(C₀ ÷ C) = k_app·t (when K·C ≪ 1) | Pseudo-first-order; k_app = k·K | min⁻¹ | Most common simplified form |
| 6 | t_{1/2} = ln 2 ÷ k_app | Half-life of pollutant | min | Plot-based or graphical problems |
| 7 | Φ = (moles product) ÷ (moles photons absorbed) | Quantum yield | — | Efficiency calculation |
| 8 | η_stoich = (•OH generated) ÷ (h⁺ consumed) | Stoichiometric efficiency | — | Reactor diagnostics |
| 9 | G_sc·τ_a·A_coll = Q_UV | Useful UV energy collected; τ_a = atmospheric transmit., A_coll = collector area | W | Solar reactor sizing |
| 10 | % Mineralization = (TOC₀ − TOC_t) ÷ TOC₀ × 100 | Total Organic Carbon removal | % | Performance reporting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize for KTU:** Equations 1, 2, 3, 5, and 6 appear in **at least one numerical** in every recent KTU ESE paper for advanced treatment courses. Equation 5 (pseudo-first-order) is the most frequently tested.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Decentralized rural treatment** in Kerala's coastal and hill districts using CPC reactors (Eliodomestico-style solar systems).
- **Pharmaceutical effluent polishing** — India is the world's 3rd-largest pharma producer; solar photocatalysis destroys antibiotics (ciprofloxacin, sulfamethoxazole) that pass through STPs.
- **Textile dye degradation** — Malappuram & Tirur textile clusters: solar/TiO₂ removes azo dyes refractory to biological oxidation.
- **Pathogen inactivation** — E. coli, *Legionella*, and antibiotic-resistant bacteria are inactivated by •OH attack on cell membranes and DNA.
- **Combination with biotreatment** — solar photocatalysis as a *pre-treatment* (breaking recalcitrant bonds) followed by conventional activated sludge.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Threshold Wavelength from Band-Gap

We start from the photon-energy relation and convert from joules to electron-volts.

$$
E_{\text{photon}} = \frac{h \cdot c}{\lambda}
$$

Step 1 — Substitute the constants numerically.

$$
E = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34}\,\text{J·s})\,(3.00 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/s})}{\lambda\,(\text{m})}
$$

Step 2 — Use the conversion **1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J**, so for E in eV and λ in nm:

$$
E\,(\text{eV}) = \frac{1240}{\lambda\,(\text{nm})}
$$

Step 3 — For activation, the photon energy must satisfy E_photon ≥ E_g. Hence the **maximum usable wavelength** is:

$$
\lambda_{\text{max}} = \frac{1240}{E_g\,(\text{eV})}\,\text{nm}
$$

Step 4 — Apply to anatase TiO₂ (E_g = 3.20 eV):

$$
\lambda_{\text{max}} = \frac{1240}{3.20} = 387.5\;\text{nm}
$$

**Conclusion:** Only UV-B and short UV-A photons (λ < 387 nm) — roughly **3–5 % of incident solar energy** — can activate pristine TiO₂. This single numerical result drives most solar photocatalysis research toward *visible-light extension*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Langmuir–Hinshelwood Integrated Rate Law

This is the **central kinetic model** of heterogeneous photocatalysis and the most-tested derivation in KTU ESE.

### 3.2.1 Starting Postulate

Adsorption of substrate on the catalyst surface follows the **Langmuir isotherm**, and the surface reaction is the rate-determining step.

$$
\theta = \frac{K \cdot C}{1 + K \cdot C}
$$

where θ = fractional surface coverage, K = Langmuir adsorption constant (L·mg⁻¹), C = bulk concentration (mg·L⁻¹).

### 3.2.2 Rate Expression

The observed degradation rate is proportional to θ:

$$
r = -\frac{dC}{dt} = k \cdot \theta = \frac{k \cdot K \cdot C}{1 + K \cdot C}
$$

Step-by-step manipulation:

Step 1 — Separate variables.

$$
-\frac{(1 + K \cdot C)\,dC}{K \cdot C} = k \cdot dt
$$

Step 2 — Expand the numerator.

$$
-\frac{dC}{K \cdot C} - dC = k \cdot dt
$$

Step 3 — Integrate both sides from t = 0 (C = C₀) to t = t (C = C_t).

$$
\int_{C_0}^{C_t}\!\left(-\frac{1}{K\,C} - 1\right)dC = \int_{0}^{t}\! k\,dt
$$

Step 4 — Carry out the integration.

$$
\left[-\frac{1}{K}\ln C - C\right]_{C_0}^{C_t} = k \cdot t
$$

Step 5 — Evaluate at the bounds.

$$
\left(-\frac{1}{K}\ln C_t - C_t\right) - \left(-\frac{1}{K}\ln C_0 - C_0\right) = k \cdot t
$$

Step 6 — Rearrange (multiply by −1 and group logs).

$$
\frac{1}{K}\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_t}\right) + (C_0 - C_t) = k \cdot t
$$

Step 7 — Multiply through by K to obtain the **canonical L-H form**.

$$
\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_t}\right) + K \cdot (C_0 - C_t) = k \cdot K \cdot t\;}
$$

### 3.2.3 Limiting Case 1 — Low Concentration (K·C ≪ 1)

When KC is small, the term K·(C₀ − C_t) is negligible, and we recover **pseudo-first-order kinetics**:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_t}\right) = k \cdot K \cdot t = k_{\text{app}} \cdot t
$$

The half-life follows:

$$
t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_{\text{app}}}
$$

### 3.2.4 Limiting Case 2 — High Concentration (K·C ≫ 1)

The denominator (1 + K·C) ≈ K·C, and the rate becomes constant:

$$
r \approx k \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{zero-order kinetics:}\quad C_0 - C_t = k \cdot t
$$

This explains why **dilute wastewater (μg·L⁻¹ micropollutant range) follows first-order behavior**, while concentrated industrial streams exhibit near-zero-order.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Quantum Yield under Solar Irradiance

Step 1 — Moles of photons incident per unit time on area A.

$$
N_{\text{photon}} = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{E_{\text{photon}}} = \frac{G_{\text{UV}} \cdot A \cdot t}{N_A \cdot h \cdot c \, / \, \lambda}
$$

Step 2 — Moles of pollutant degraded (from TOC loss or C_t measurement).

$$
n_{\text{degraded}} = \frac{(C_0 - C_t) \cdot V}{M}
$$

Step 3 — Quantum yield (single-electron-transfer approximation).

$$
\Phi = \frac{n_{\text{degraded}}}{N_{\text{photon}}}
$$

For a Degussa P25 TiO₂ reactor degrading 10 mg·L⁻¹ of phenol under noon-equivalent solar UV (G_UV ≈ 25 W·m⁻²) on 0.1 m² for 60 min in 1 L, you obtain Φ ≈ 0.04, which is the typical KTU-cited benchmark.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical (KTU-Style, 7-Mark Pattern)

> **Problem:** A solar CPC reactor (catalyst = 0.5 g·L⁻¹ TiO₂ P25, V = 2 L) treats a phenol solution (C₀ = 20 mg·L⁻¹). After 90 min solar exposure, C_t = 4 mg·L⁻¹. The Langmuir constant K = 0.08 L·mg⁻¹. Verify whether pseudo-first-order assumption is valid and compute the apparent rate constant.

**Step 1 — Check K·C₀:**

$$
K \cdot C_0 = 0.08 \times 20 = 1.6
$$

Since K·C₀ > 0.1, the strict L-H form should ideally be used, but for KTU problems we will *first* test the pseudo-first-order form to illustrate the procedure.

**Step 2 — Apply pseudo-first-order:**

$$
k_{\text{app}} = \frac{1}{t}\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_t}\right) = \frac{1}{90}\ln\!\left(\frac{20}{4}\right)
$$

$$
k_{\text{app}} = \frac{\ln 5}{90} = \frac{1.6094}{90} = 0.01788\;\text{min}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Check with full L-H integrated form:**

$$
\text{LHS} = \ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_t}\right) + K\cdot(C_0 - C_t) = 1.6094 + 0.08 \times 16 = 1.6094 + 1.28 = 2.8894
$$

$$
k = \frac{2.8894}{K \cdot t} = \frac{2.8894}{0.08 \times 90} = \frac{2.8894}{7.2} = 0.4013\;\text{min}^{-1}
$$

Step 4 — Verification: when K·C ≪ 1, k_app = k·K = 0.4013 × 0.08 = 0.0321. This differs from the crude 0.0179, showing that the simple pseudo-first-order is **not strictly valid** for this concentration range. The KTU-marked answer would be the rigorous L-H form.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuator's Tip:** Whenever K·C₀ > 0.1 in the problem, **always use the full L-H integrated form** for full credit (7 marks). Dropping to pseudo-first-order without justification costs 2–3 marks.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Mechanism of Solar Photocatalysis on TiO₂

The following Mermaid block renders the **electron–hole dynamics** at the semiconductor–water interface, the most-frequently-asked schematic in KTU ESE.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Solar Photon h nu"]:::input --> B["TiO2 Semiconductor Surface"]
    B --> C["Electron Excited to Conduction Band"]
    B --> D["Hole Left in Valence Band"]
    C --> E["eCB + O2 dissolved"]:::redox
    D --> F["hVB + H2O adsorbed"]:::redox
    E --> G["Superoxide O2 minus"]
    G --> H["Hydroperoxyl HOO"]
    H --> I["Hydrogen Peroxide H2O2"]
    I --> J["Hydroxyl Radical OH"]
    F --> J
    J --> K["Organic Pollutant R H"]
    K --> L["Mineralized Products CO2 H2O ions"]
    D --> M["Direct Hole Oxidation of Pollutant"]:::altpath
    M --> L
    C --> N["Surface Recombination Heat Loss"]:::loss
    D --> N

    classDef input fill:#FFD580,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef redox fill:#B5E2FF,stroke:#0066CC,color:#000
    classDef altpath fill:#D5F5D5,stroke:#2E8B57,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#CC0000,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **central trunk** (A → B → C/D → E/F → G → H → I → J → K → L) is the canonical •OH radical pathway.
- The **green side-branch** (D → M → L) is the direct-hole oxidation route, important for species that cannot generate •OH.
- The **red loss path** (C/D → N) represents the ~80–90 % recombination loss that limits quantum yield.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    S1 --> EQ["Equalization Basin"]

    DIS --> OUT["Reuse or Discharge to Water Body"]

```

**Key engineering observations from the flow diagram:**

- Catalyst recovery (cyclone or membrane filter) is **non-negotiable** in slurry systems; recycling loop (CAT → SP) preserves the TiO₂ inventory.
- The biological pre-treatment is **optional**; for purely recalcitrant streams (e.g., textile or pharma), the reactor can act as a *stand-alone* process.
- Solar field tracking is required for **CPC** (1-axis) and **PTC** (2-axis) but not for fixed flat-plate reactors.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define solar photocatalysis. List any two semiconductor photocatalysts used in wastewater treatment with their band-gap energies.**

**Model Answer:**
Solar photocatalysis is an advanced oxidation process (AOP) in which a semiconductor catalyst (e.g., TiO₂, ZnO, Fe₂O₃) is activated by solar photons of energy ≥ band-gap, generating electron–hole pairs that produce reactive oxygen species (•OH, O₂•⁻) which mineralize organic pollutants.

Two catalysts:
1. **TiO₂ (anatase)** — E_g = 3.20 eV, λ_max ≈ 387 nm.
2. **ZnO** — E_g = 3.37 eV, λ_max ≈ 368 nm.

(Alternative acceptable: WO₃ 2.7 eV, Fe₂O₃ 2.2 eV, BiVO₄ 2.4 eV.)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of electron acceptors such as H₂O₂ and persulfate in solar photocatalytic systems?**

**Model Answer:**
Electron acceptors (H₂O₂, S₂O₈²⁻, O₂) capture the conduction-band electron, thereby:
1. **Suppressing e⁻/h⁺ recombination**, increasing the lifetime of photogenerated holes.
2. **Generating additional reactive species** — H₂O₂ + e⁻ → •OH + OH⁻; S₂O₈²⁻ + e⁻ → SO₄•⁻ + SO₄²⁻ (sulfate radical, E⁰ = +2.5 V).
3. **Enhancing mineralization rates** typically by a factor of 1.5–4 in solar CPC reactors.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) `[7 Marks]` Explain with a neat sketch the mechanism of solar photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants on TiO₂. Discuss the role of hydroxyl and superoxide radicals.

**Model Answer (Stepwise):**

**1. Photon absorption and charge generation (2 marks):**
- TiO₂ has filled VB and empty CB separated by E_g = 3.2 eV.
- A solar UV photon (λ < 387 nm) is absorbed: TiO₂ + hν → e⁻_CB + h⁺_VB.

**2. Surface redox reactions (2 marks):**
- **Valence-band hole (oxidation):** h⁺_VB + H₂O → •OH + H⁺
- **Conduction-band electron (reduction):** e⁻_CB + O₂ → O₂•⁻

**3. Reactive species cascade (1.5 marks):**
- O₂•⁻ + H⁺ → •OOH
- 2 •OOH → H₂O₂ + O₂
- H₂O₂ + e⁻_CB → •OH + OH⁻
- Direct hole attack: h⁺ + RH → R• + H⁺

**4. Mineralization by •OH (1.5 marks):**
- •OH (E⁰ = +2.80 V) is non-selective.
- It attacks organics via H-abstraction, electrophilic addition, and electron transfer.
- End products: CO₂, H₂O, NO₃⁻, PO₄³⁻, Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻.

**Sketch:** (Refer to Diagram 1 in Section 4 — show e⁻ in CB, h⁺ in VB, both reacting with O₂ and H₂O, generation of •OH and O₂•⁻, attack on pollutant, and recombination loss pathway.)

#### (b) `[7 Marks]` Discuss the design features and operating parameters of a solar Compound Parabolic Collector (CPC) photocatalytic reactor. Compare it with a parabolic trough reactor.

**Model Answer:**

**CPC Reactor Design (3.5 marks):**
- Geometry: **truncated parabolic mirrors** that focus diffuse and direct solar radiation onto a tubular absorber without tracking the sun (acceptance angle ≈ 90°).
- UV-transparent borosilicate or Teflon tubes carry the TiO₂ slurry.
- Concentration ratio: **1 sun (non-concentrating)** — purely radiative gain, no thermal hot spots.
- Typical reactor volume: 5–50 L for pilot CPC plants.
- Static installation ⇒ **low O&M cost**, ideal for Kerala's rural deployment.

**Operating Parameters (1.5 marks):**
- Catalyst loading: 0.1–2.0 g·L⁻¹ (optimum ~ 0.5 g·L⁻¹ for TiO₂ P25).
- pH: 5–9 (TiO₂ pHpzc ≈ 6.2; pollutant speciation matters).
- Flow regime: turbulent (Re > 4000) to overcome mass-transfer limitations.
- Dissolved O₂ ≥ 6 mg·L⁻¹.

**Comparison with Parabolic Trough (PTC) Reactor (2 marks):**

| Feature | CPC | PTC |
|---|---|---|
| Concentration ratio | 1 sun | 30–80 suns |
| Tracking | None (static) | 2-axis continuous |
| Operating temperature | Ambient | 60–150 °C |
| Catalyst suitability | TiO₂ slurry / fixed film | Thermally stable catalysts |
| Cooling requirement | None | Mandatory (avoid hot spots) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Application | Wastewater | Syngas, high-T reactions |

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) `[7 Marks]` Derive the Langmuir–Hinshelwood rate equation for solar photocatalytic degradation. Show how pseudo-first-order kinetics emerges as a limiting case.

**Model Answer:**

(Use the derivation in Section 3.2, reproduced in answer form.)

**1. Langmuir adsorption assumption (1 mark):**
- A monolayer of substrate adsorbs on identical active sites.
- Fractional coverage: θ = (K·C) ÷ (1 + K·C).

**2. Rate-determining step is surface reaction (1 mark):**
- r = k·θ = k·K·C ÷ (1 + K·C)

**3. Variable separation and integration (3 marks):**
- −dC/dt = k·K·C ÷ (1 + K·C)
- (1 + K·C)·(−dC)/C = k·K·dt
- −(1/K)·(dC/C) − dC = k·K·dt
- Integrating from C₀ to C_t and 0 to t:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C_t}\right) + K\cdot(C_0 - C_t) = k \cdot K \cdot t
$$

**4. Limiting case — pseudo-first-order (2 marks):**
- When K·C ≪ 1, the term K·(C₀ − C_t) → 0.
- Hence: ln(C₀/C_t) = k·K·t = k_app·t.
- Plot of ln(C₀/C_t) vs t is a straight line with slope = k_app = k·K.

**Numerical example (bonus 1 mark):**
- Given C₀ = 10 mg·L⁻¹, C_t = 2 mg·L⁻¹, t = 60 min ⇒ k_app = ln(5)/60 = 0.0268 min⁻¹; t_{1/2} = 25.8 min.

#### (b) `[7 Marks]` Critically evaluate the limitations of TiO₂-based solar photocatalysis and explain how visible-light extension strategies (doping, heterojunctions, Z-scheme) overcome them.

**Model Answer:**

**Limitations of pristine TiO₂ (3 marks):**
1. **Wide band-gap (3.2 eV)** → only UV-active; < 5 % of solar spectrum utilized.
2. **Rapid e⁻/h⁺ recombination** (lifetimes ~ ns) → low quantum yield (~1–10 %).
3. **Catalyst recovery problem** in slurry systems (post-treatment filtration cost).
4. **Mass transfer limitations** in immobilized systems.
5. **Catalyst deactivation** by intermediate poisoning (chlorinated/aromatic by-products).
6. **No activity in dark or low-irradiance periods** (cloudy/monsoon days in Kerala).

**Visible-light extension strategies (4 marks):**

1. **Doping (cationic):** N, C, S, F substitute O in TiO₂ lattice → introduces mid-gap states, lowering effective E_g to ~2.0–2.5 eV.
2. **Doping (metallic):** Fe³⁺, Cu²⁺, Ag⁺ act as e⁻ traps, suppressing recombination.
3. **Semiconductor heterojunctions:** Coupling TiO₂ with narrow-E_g semiconductors (CdS, BiVO₄, g-C₃N₄, Fe₂O₃) → type-II band alignment separates charges, broadening absorption to visible range.
4. **Z-scheme photocatalysts:** Mimicking natural photosynthesis; two semiconductors with redox mediators (or solid-state electron mediators like Ag, Au, RGO) transfer electrons in a Z-path, retaining strong redox potentials.
5. **Surface plasmon resonance (SPR):** Deposition of Au/Ag nanoparticles on TiO₂ → strong visible-light absorption via collective e⁻ oscillations.
6. **Carbon-based composites:** Graphene oxide (GO) or carbon dots act as e⁻ acceptors and visible sensitizers.

**Conclusion (any 2 of the above strategies is acceptable for full marks):** Combined strategies can extend TiO₂'s response into the visible range, raising solar-energy utilization from < 5 % to **15–25 %**, and simultaneously suppressing recombination by a factor of 3–10.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps (2024 Scheme Examiners' Reports):**
> 1. **Confusing "photocatalysis" with "photo-Fenton"** — they are *not* the same. Photocatalysis uses a *solid semiconductor* catalyst; photo-Fenton uses *dissolved iron salts* with H₂O₂. (−2 marks)
> 2. **Stating TiO₂ is activated by *visible light* without qualification** — pristine TiO₂ is *UV-only*; visible response requires doping. (−1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** (k_app in min⁻¹, C in mg·L⁻¹). (−1 mark)
> 4. **Writing L-H kinetics without showing the integration step** — KTU markers expect at least the separation of variables. (−2 marks)
> 5. **Calling •OH a "free radical" without its redox potential** (E⁰ = +2.80 V) — examiners look for this number. (−1 mark)
> 6. **Confusing CPC (non-concentrating) with PTC (concentrating)** — opposite! CPC = Compound Parabolic Collector = 1 sun; PTC = Parabolic Trough Collector = high concentration. (−2 marks)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Photocatalysis = semiconductor + photon ≥ band-gap → e⁻/h⁺ pair → •OH radical → mineralization of organics.**
- **TiO₂ anatase band-gap = 3.20 eV; threshold wavelength ≈ 387 nm;** only ~3–5 % of solar spectrum is usable.
- **Three core rate equations to memorize:** L-H general, integrated L-H, and pseudo-first-order.
- **Most common kinetic form in KTU problems:** pseudo-first-order `ln(C₀/C) = k_app·t`; always check if K·C ≪ 1 holds.
- **Reactor types ranked by cost-effectiveness for wastewater:** flat plate < CPC < immobilized thin film < slurry CPC.
- **CPC reactors are the workhorse for solar water treatment in tropical climates** (high diffuse fraction, no tracking needed).
- **Hole oxidizes water; electron reduces O₂;** both pathways converge on •OH, the actual "active species."
- **Recombination is the #1 efficiency killer** (90 % loss typical); doping/heterojunctions mitigate it.
- **Visible-light strategies** (N-doping, CdS coupling, Z-scheme, plasmonic Au/Ag) push solar utilization to 15–25 %.
- **E⁰(•OH/H₂O) = +2.80 V vs NHE** — the second-highest oxidant after fluorine; write this number when asked.
- **Catalyst recovery (cyclone / membrane) is mandatory** for slurry systems — examiners love this practical point.
- **Solar photocatalysis works best on:** dyes, phenols, pesticides, pharmaceuticals, endocrine disruptors, and pathogens — *not* on high-TDS brines.
- **Combination is powerful:** solar photocatalysis as pre-treatment (to break recalcitrant structures) → biological polishing (for cost-effective COD/BOD reduction).
- **For Kerala context:** CPC reactors with TiO₂ P25 slurry are the recommended pilot configuration due to high solar insolation (4.5–5.5 kWh·m⁻²·day⁻¹) and prolonged dry season.
